title: Sets and functions
blurb: Sets, membership, subsets, unions/intersections, functions as single-valued relations, injections and surjections.

title: Cartesian products and power sets
blurb: Products $A\times B$, function spaces $B^A$, and the power set $\mathcal{P}(A)\cong\{0,1\}^A$ — the basic constructions behind tuples, graphs of functions, and indicator functions.

title: Equivalence relations and quotients
blurb: Equivalence relations partition sets; the quotient set X/∼ as the universal recipient of functions constant on classes.

title: Countability
blurb: Countable vs uncountable, Cantor's diagonal argument, and why |R| > |Q|.

title: Axiom of choice
blurb: A choice function exists for every family of non-empty sets. Equivalent to Zorn's lemma and the well-ordering theorem; behind every basis, maximal ideal, and Tychonoff product.

q: If $|A| = 7$, $|B| = 9$, and $|A \cap B| = 4$, what is $|A \cup B|$?
text: If $|A| = 7$, $|B| = 9$, and $|A \cap B| = 4$, what is $|A \cup B|$? Inclusion–exclusion: $|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B| = 7 + 9 - 4 = 12$.

q: Let $f\colon \{1,2,3,4\} \to \{a,b,c\}$ be given by $1 \mapsto a,\ 2 \mapsto b,\ 3 \mapsto a,\ 4 \mapsto c$. Which is true?
text: Let $f\colon \{1,2,3,4\} \to \{a,b,c\}$ be given by $1 \mapsto a,\ 2 \mapsto b,\ 3 \mapsto a,\ 4 \mapsto c$. Which is true? The image is $\{a,b,c\}$, the whole codomain, so $f$ is surjective. But $f(1) = f(3) = a$ shows $f$ is not injective. With $|A|=4 > 3 = |B|$ no function $A \to B$ can be injective (pigeonhole).

q: How many subsets does a set of size $6$ have? (That is, compute $|\mathcal{P}(A)|$ for $|A| = 6$.)
text: How many subsets does a set of size $6$ have? (That is, compute $|\mathcal{P}(A)|$ for $|A| = 6$.) Each of the $6$ elements is independently in or out of a subset, so $|\mathcal{P}(A)| = 2^6 = 64$.

q: Let $f\colon A \to B$ and $g\colon B \to C$. Which is the correct chain of implications among injectivity and surjectivity of compositions?
text: Let $f\colon A \to B$ and $g\colon B \to C$. Which is the correct chain of implications among injectivity and surjectivity of compositions? If $g\circ f$ is injective and $f(a)=f(a')$, then $g(f(a))=g(f(a'))$, so $a=a'$; thus $f$ is injective. Conversely $g$ need not be (e.g. $g$ can collapse elements outside $\operatorname{im} f$). If $g\circ f$ is surjective then every $c\in C$ is hit, so $g$ is surjective; $f$ need not be.

q: Sets $A$, $B$, $C$ satisfy $|A|=10$, $|B|=8$, $|C|=6$, $|A\cap B|=3$, $|A\cap C|=2$, $|B\cap C|=4$, $|A\cap B\cap C|=1$. Compute $|A\cup B\cup C|$.
text: Sets $A$, $B$, $C$ satisfy $|A|=10$, $|B|=8$, $|C|=6$, $|A\cap B|=3$, $|A\cap C|=2$, $|B\cap C|=4$, $|A\cap B\cap C|=1$. Compute $|A\cup B\cup C|$. Three-set inclusion–exclusion: $|A\cup B\cup C|=10+8+6-3-2-4+1=16$.

q: Which is a correct counterexample showing that $f^{-1}(f(S)) = S$ can fail?
text: Which is a correct counterexample showing that $f^{-1}(f(S)) = S$ can fail? $f^{-1}(f(S))\supseteq S$ always, with equality iff $f$ is injective on the relevant domain. A non-injective $f$ can collapse elements outside $S$ into $f(S)$, enlarging the preimage. The example above is the minimal witness.

q: If $|A| = 5$ and $|B| = 4$, how many functions are there from $A$ to $B$? (That is, compute $|B^A|$.)
text: If $|A| = 5$ and $|B| = 4$, how many functions are there from $A$ to $B$? (That is, compute $|B^A|$.) Each of the $|A|=5$ elements picks a value in $B$ independently, so $|B^A| = |B|^{|A|} = 4^5 = 1024$.

q: Let $A$ be a set. Under which bijection is $\mathcal{P}(A) \cong \{0,1\}^A$?
text: Let $A$ be a set. Under which bijection is $\mathcal{P}(A) \cong \{0,1\}^A$? A subset $S\subseteq A$ is determined by, and determines, its indicator function $\mathbf{1}_S\colon A\to\{0,1\}$. This bijection is also why $|\mathcal{P}(A)|=2^{|A|}$.

q: Let $|A|=3$ and $|B|=2$. How many elements are in $\mathcal{P}(A\times B)$?
text: Let $|A|=3$ and $|B|=2$. How many elements are in $\mathcal{P}(A\times B)$? $|A\times B| = 3\cdot 2 = 6$, so $|\mathcal{P}(A\times B)| = 2^6 = 64$.

q: Compute $|\mathcal{P}(\mathcal{P}(\{a,b\}))|$, i.e. the size of the power set of the power set of a $2$-element set.
text: Compute $|\mathcal{P}(\mathcal{P}(\{a,b\}))|$, i.e. the size of the power set of the power set of a $2$-element set. $|\{a,b\}|=2$, so $|\mathcal{P}(\{a,b\})|=4$, and $|\mathcal{P}(\mathcal{P}(\{a,b\}))|=2^4=16$.

q: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between $A\times B$ and $B\times A$?
text: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between $A\times B$ and $B\times A$? The swap map $(a,b)\mapsto(b,a)$ is a bijection $A\times B\to B\times A$, so they have the same cardinality. However, as sets of ordered pairs they differ: $(a,b)\in A\times B$ has first coordinate in $A$, while $(b,a)\in B\times A$ has first coordinate in $B$. If $A\cap B=\emptyset$ and both are non-empty, no element of $A\times B$ belongs to $B\times A$.

q: How many injective functions are there from a $3$-element set to a $5$-element set?
text: How many injective functions are there from a $3$-element set to a $5$-element set? The first element has $5$ choices, the second $4$, the third $3$: $5\cdot 4\cdot 3=60$.

q: How many equivalence classes does the relation '$a \sim b$ iff $5 \mid a - b$' on $\mathbb{Z}$ have?
text: How many equivalence classes does the relation '$a \sim b$ iff $5 \mid a - b$' on $\mathbb{Z}$ have? The classes are $[0], [1], [2], [3], [4]$ — the residue classes modulo $5$. So $|\mathbb{Z}/5\mathbb{Z}| = 5$.

q: Which of the following relations on $\mathbb{Z}$ is an equivalence relation?
text: Which of the following relations on $\mathbb{Z}$ is an equivalence relation? $a \sim b \iff a^2 = b^2$ is reflexive ($a^2 = a^2$), symmetric ($a^2 = b^2 \Rightarrow b^2 = a^2$), and transitive. The others fail: $\le$ is not symmetric, $\ne$ is not reflexive (and not transitive), and $\mid$ is not symmetric.

q: Let $\pi\colon X \to X/{\sim}$ be the quotient map. Which statement correctly expresses the universal property of the quotient?
text: Let $\pi\colon X \to X/{\sim}$ be the quotient map. Which statement correctly expresses the universal property of the quotient? The universal property: $f\colon X \to Y$ descends to a (unique) $\bar f\colon X/{\sim} \to Y$ with $\bar f \circ \pi = f$ exactly when $f$ respects the equivalence — i.e. $x \sim x' \Rightarrow f(x) = f(x')$.

q: A relation $R$ on a set $A$ is reflexive and transitive but fails symmetry. Which of the following is a correct counterexample?
text: A relation $R$ on a set $A$ is reflexive and transitive but fails symmetry. Which of the following is a correct counterexample? Divisibility on $\mathbb{N}_{>0}$ is a partial order (reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive) but not symmetric: $2\mid 4$ yet $4\nmid 2$. It therefore shows that reflexivity + transitivity do not force symmetry.

q: Which is the correct order of steps in proving that the kernel equivalence $x\sim_f y\iff f(x)=f(y)$ yields the canonical factorisation $f = \bar f\circ\pi$?
text: Which is the correct order of steps in proving that the kernel equivalence $x\sim_f y\iff f(x)=f(y)$ yields the canonical factorisation $f = \bar f\circ\pi$? Standard proof: first define $\bar f([x])=f(x)$, then verify well-definedness ($x\sim_f y\Rightarrow f(x)=f(y)$), then observe $\bar f(\pi(x))=\bar f([x])=f(x)$. Injectivity of $\bar f$ follows because $\bar f([x])=\bar f([y])$ implies $f(x)=f(y)$ implies $[x]=[y]$.

q: The relation $\sim$ on $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$ is defined by $a\sim b\iff 3\mid a-b$. How many equivalence classes are there, and what is the size of the class $[2]$? Enter the size of $[2]$.
text: The relation $\sim$ on $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$ is defined by $a\sim b\iff 3\mid a-b$. How many equivalence classes are there, and what is the size of the class $[2]$? Enter the size of $[2]$. There are $3$ classes: $[0]=\{3,6\}$, $[1]=\{1,4\}$, $[2]=\{2,5\}$. The class $[2]=\{2,5\}$ has size $2$.

q: Which of the following sets is uncountable?
text: Which of the following sets is uncountable? By Cantor's theorem $|\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})| > |\mathbb{N}|$, so $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})$ is uncountable (in fact $|\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})| = 2^{\aleph_0} = \mathfrak{c} = |\mathbb{R}|$). The other three are countable: $\mathbb{Q}$ injects into $\mathbb{Z}^2$; $\mathbb{Z}^3$ is a finite product of countables; the algebraic numbers are a countable union of finite root-sets of integer polynomials.

q: Let $s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots$ be an enumeration of binary sequences with $s_0 = 0110\dots$, $s_1 = 1001\dots$, $s_2 = 0011\dots$, $s_3 = 1111\dots$. What are the first four bits of the Cantor diagonal sequence $d$ defined by $d_n = 1 - s_n(n)$?
text: Let $s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots$ be an enumeration of binary sequences with $s_0 = 0110\dots$, $s_1 = 1001\dots$, $s_2 = 0011\dots$, $s_3 = 1111\dots$. What are the first four bits of the Cantor diagonal sequence $d$ defined by $d_n = 1 - s_n(n)$? The diagonal entries are $s_0(0) = 0$, $s_1(1) = 0$, $s_2(2) = 1$, $s_3(3) = 1$. Flipping each bit gives $d = 1100\dots$, which differs from each $s_n$ in position $n$.

q: Under the Cantor zigzag pairing $\langle i, j \rangle = \tfrac{(i+j)(i+j+1)}{2} + i$ (with $i, j \ge 0$), what is $\langle 2, 1 \rangle$?
text: Under the Cantor zigzag pairing $\langle i, j \rangle = \tfrac{(i+j)(i+j+1)}{2} + i$ (with $i, j \ge 0$), what is $\langle 2, 1 \rangle$? $\langle 2, 1 \rangle = \tfrac{(2+1)(2+1+1)}{2} + 2 = \tfrac{3 \cdot 4}{2} + 2 = 6 + 2 = 8$.

q: Cantor's theorem states $|A| < |\mathcal{P}(A)|$ for every set $A$. Which step in the diagonal proof is the critical one that produces a contradiction?
text: Cantor's theorem states $|A| < |\mathcal{P}(A)|$ for every set $A$. Which step in the diagonal proof is the critical one that produces a contradiction? Assume for contradiction $f\colon A\to\mathcal{P}(A)$ is surjective. Form the diagonal set $D=\{a\in A\mid a\notin f(a)\}$. Since $f$ is surjective, $D=f(d)$ for some $d$. Then $d\in D\iff d\notin f(d)=D$, a contradiction. Hence no surjection $A\to\mathcal{P}(A)$ exists. Build a subset guaranteed to differ from every $f(a)$ on the element $a$ itself.

q: Which is a correct proof-step ordering for showing $\mathbb{Q}$ is countable via the zigzag enumeration?
text: Which is a correct proof-step ordering for showing $\mathbb{Q}$ is countable via the zigzag enumeration? Writing each rational in lowest terms gives an injection $\mathbb{Q}\hookrightarrow\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}_{>0}$. A countable product of countable sets is countable (shown by the zigzag/Cantor pairing), so $\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}_{>0}$ is countable, and any subset of a countable set is countable.

q: Using the Cantor zigzag pairing $\langle i,j\rangle=\frac{(i+j)(i+j+1)}{2}+i$, compute $\langle 3,0\rangle$.
text: Using the Cantor zigzag pairing $\langle i,j\rangle=\frac{(i+j)(i+j+1)}{2}+i$, compute $\langle 3,0\rangle$. $\langle 3,0\rangle=\frac{(3+0)(3+0+1)}{2}+3=\frac{3\cdot 4}{2}+3=6+0=6$.

q: Which of the following is equivalent to the axiom of choice over ZF?
text: Which of the following is equivalent to the axiom of choice over ZF? Zorn's lemma is one of the standard equivalents of AC, alongside the well-ordering theorem and Tukey's lemma. The other choices are theorems of ZF or ZFC but are not equivalent to AC — the continuum hypothesis is independent of ZFC, and Cantor's theorem is provable in ZF with no choice at all.

q: Russell's distinction between shoes and socks illustrates which point?
text: Russell's distinction between shoes and socks illustrates which point? With shoes there is a uniform rule ('pick the left one'), so no AC is needed. With socks there is no such rule, yet AC still asserts the product $\prod_i A_i$ is non-empty. The point is that for infinite families we can always produce a choice function under AC, even when we cannot write down an explicit one.

q: Which of these standard results is not known to require (some form of) the axiom of choice?
text: Which of these standard results is not known to require (some form of) the axiom of choice? The first three all use full AC (or Zorn's lemma, which is equivalent). The countable-union-of-countable-sets statement only needs the weaker countable choice , and over ZF plus countable choice it is provable — but in ZF alone it is not. The question asks for the statement that is 'not known to require AC ' (i.e. full choice).

q: Which is the correct logical chain showing AC $\Rightarrow$ Well-Ordering Theorem $\Rightarrow$ Zorn's Lemma $\Rightarrow$ AC (the classical equivalence circle)?
text: Which is the correct logical chain showing AC $\Rightarrow$ Well-Ordering Theorem $\Rightarrow$ Zorn's Lemma $\Rightarrow$ AC (the classical equivalence circle)? All three are equivalent over ZF. AC → Well-Ordering: use the choice function to pick the least element at each transfinite stage. Well-Ordering → Zorn: in a well-ordered poset every chain has an upper bound (its supremum in the well-order), so a maximal element exists. Zorn → AC: apply Zorn to the poset of partial choice functions ordered by extension.

q: In ZF without AC, which of the following can be proved?
text: In ZF without AC, which of the following can be proved? Cantor's diagonal theorem requires no choice whatsoever — the diagonal set $D=\{a\mid a\notin f(a)\}$ is defined explicitly. The existence of a countably infinite subset of every infinite set requires (at least) Dependent Choice; well-ordering $\mathbb{R}$ needs full AC; and right inverses for surjections are equivalent to AC.

q: Vitali's construction of a non-measurable subset of $[0,1]$ uses AC in which essential step?
text: Vitali's construction of a non-measurable subset of $[0,1]$ uses AC in which essential step? The Vitali set $V$ is built by picking exactly one element from each equivalence class of $x\sim y\iff x-y\in\mathbb{Q}$ restricted to $[0,1]$. There are uncountably many classes and no canonical choice; AC is invoked to guarantee a simultaneous selection. Assuming $V$ is measurable then leads to a contradiction with countable additivity.

title: Groups, rings, and fields
blurb: Core algebraic structures package operations with axioms and examples from symmetries to polynomial arithmetic.

title: Cayley tables and subgroups
blurb: Finite groups can be read off from their multiplication table, with subgroup and normality patterns visible combinatorially.

title: Quotients and finite fields
blurb: Homomorphisms, quotient structures, and finite fields connect symmetry algebra to arithmetic and coding theory.

title: Ideals and quotient rings
blurb: An ideal I of a commutative ring R is the kernel of some homomorphism out of R; quotienting by it produces R/I, and prime versus maximal ideals distinguish integral domains from fields.

title: Field extensions and minimal polynomials
blurb: Adjoining a root of an irreducible polynomial m(x) over F produces the extension F[x]/(m), a field of degree deg m; algebraic elements carry a unique monic minimal polynomial.

title: Subgroups, cosets, and Lagrange
blurb: Left cosets gH partition G into blocks all of size |H|, so |H| divides |G| and the index [G:H] counts how many cosets there are; normality is the equality of left and right coset partitions.

title: Quotient groups and isomorphism theorems
blurb: When N is normal in G the coset set G/N inherits a group structure; the three isomorphism theorems describe how homomorphisms, intersections, and chains of normal subgroups interact with quotients.

title: Group actions, orbits, and Burnside
blurb: A group action splits a set into orbits whose sizes are indices of stabilizers (orbit-stabilizer); Burnside's lemma then counts orbits as the average number of fixed points across all group elements.

title: Sylow theorems
blurb: For |G| = p^k m with p prime and gcd(p,m)=1, Sylow p-subgroups of order p^k always exist, are all conjugate, and their count n_p divides m and is congruent to 1 mod p — frequently forcing normality.

title: Finite abelian groups
blurb: Every finite abelian group is uniquely a direct sum of cyclic groups of prime-power order; the count of abelian groups of order n is a product over primes p | n of the number of partitions of v_p(n).

title: Polynomial rings and irreducibility
blurb: Over a field k, k[x] is a Euclidean domain whose irreducibles are the building blocks of extensions; Eisenstein's criterion, rational-root testing, and Gauss's lemma give practical tools to detect irreducibility.

title: Semidirect products and short exact sequences
blurb: A split short exact sequence 1 → N → G → H → 1 reconstructs G as a semidirect product N ⋊_φ H, with the homomorphism φ: H → Aut(N) controlling how H twists N; φ trivial recovers the direct product.

q: Which statement is true in any field $F$?
text: Which statement is true in any field $F$? A field requires that $F^{\times}=F\setminus\{0\}$ is a group under multiplication, so each nonzero element is invertible.

q: In the additive group $(\mathbb{Z}/12\mathbb{Z},+)$, what is the order of the element $4$?
text: In the additive group $(\mathbb{Z}/12\mathbb{Z},+)$, what is the order of the element $4$? The order is $12/\gcd(12,4)=3$, since $3\cdot4\equiv0\pmod{12}$.

q: Which structure models symmetry most directly?
text: Which structure models symmetry most directly? Symmetries compose and invert, exactly the group axioms.

q: Every field is a ring, but not every ring is a field. Which of the following rings is also a field?
text: Every field is a ring, but not every ring is a field. Which of the following rings is also a field? $\mathbb{Z}/7\mathbb{Z}$ is a field because $7$ is prime, so every nonzero element is invertible. $\mathbb{Z}$ lacks inverses for integers other than $\pm1$; $\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$ has zero-divisors; $\mathbb{Z}[x]$ is not a field since e.g. $x$ is not invertible.

q: Let $G$ be a group of order $35$. By Lagrange's theorem, every element's order divides $|G|=35$. If no element has order $35$, the possible nontrivial orders are $5$ and $7$. How many elements of order $5$ must exist if there is exactly one subgroup of order $7$?
text: Let $G$ be a group of order $35$. By Lagrange's theorem, every element's order divides $|G|=35$. If no element has order $35$, the possible nontrivial orders are $5$ and $7$. How many elements of order $5$ must exist if there is exactly one subgroup of order $7$? The unique subgroup of order $7$ contributes $6$ elements of order $7$ plus the identity. The remaining $35-7=28$ non-identity elements all have order $5$ (since their order must divide $35$ and cannot be $7$ or $35$). So there are $28$ elements of order $5$. Count which elements are used up by the order-$7$ subgroup; the rest must have order $5$.

q: Which of the following is a counterexample showing that a subring of a field need not itself be a field?
text: Which of the following is a counterexample showing that a subring of a field need not itself be a field? $\mathbb{Z}$ is a subring of the field $\mathbb{Q}$ but fails to be a field because, for instance, $2\in\mathbb{Z}$ has no multiplicative inverse in $\mathbb{Z}$. The other options either involve two fields or are not counterexamples.

q: Select all of the following groups that are abelian.
text: Select all of the following groups that are abelian. $(\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z},+)$ and $(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z})^{\times}=\{1,3\}$ are both abelian (additive/cyclic and order-$2$ respectively). $S_3$ is the smallest nonabelian group (e.g. $(12)(13)\neq(13)(12)$); $D_4$ contains a rotation and a reflection that don't commute; and matrix multiplication in $GL_2(\mathbb{R})$ is famously noncommutative. Additive groups of $\mathbb{Z}/n$ and their unit groups are always abelian; nonabelian examples usually involve permutations or matrices.

q: In a Cayley table for a finite group $G$, each row is a permutation of $G$ primarily because of:
text: In a Cayley table for a finite group $G$, each row is a permutation of $G$ primarily because of: For fixed $g$, the map $h\mapsto gh$ is bijective with inverse $h\mapsto g^{-1}h$, so each row lists each element once.

q: If $|G|=24$ and $|H|=6$, how many left cosets does $H$ have in $G$?
text: If $|G|=24$ and $|H|=6$, how many left cosets does $H$ have in $G$? By Lagrange, the index is $[G:H]=|G|/|H|=24/6=4$.

q: A subgroup $H\le G$ is normal exactly when:
text: A subgroup $H\le G$ is normal exactly when: Normality means left and right cosets coincide for every group element.

q: The dihedral group $D_4=\langle r,s\mid r^4=s^2=e,\,srs=r^{-1}\rangle$ has order $8$. Select all of the listed proper subgroups that are normal in $D_4$.
text: The dihedral group $D_4=\langle r,s\mid r^4=s^2=e,\,srs=r^{-1}\rangle$ has order $8$. Select all of the listed proper subgroups that are normal in $D_4$. $\{e,r^2\}$ is the center, automatically normal. The three order-$4$ subgroups $\langle r\rangle$, $\{e,r^2,s,r^2s\}$, and $\{e,r^2,rs,r^3s\}$ all have index $2$, hence normal. The order-$2$ subgroup $\langle s\rangle$ has index $4$ and is not normal: $rsr^{-1}=r\cdot s\cdot r^3 = r^2 s\notin\langle s\rangle$. So exactly the first four are normal. Index-$2$ subgroups are always normal; the center is always normal; a subgroup of order $2$ generated by one reflection has index $4$, so check conjugation by $r$.

q: Using the Cayley table for $\mathbb{Z}/8\mathbb{Z}$, the element $3$ has an order. Meanwhile the number of subgroups of $\mathbb{Z}/8\mathbb{Z}$ equals the number of divisors of $8$. What is that count?
text: Using the Cayley table for $\mathbb{Z}/8\mathbb{Z}$, the element $3$ has an order. Meanwhile the number of subgroups of $\mathbb{Z}/8\mathbb{Z}$ equals the number of divisors of $8$. What is that count? The divisors of $8$ are $1,2,4,8$, giving four subgroups: $\{0\}$, $\{0,4\}$, $\{0,2,4,6\}$, and $\mathbb{Z}/8\mathbb{Z}$ itself. Cyclic groups of order $n$ have exactly one subgroup per divisor of $n$.

q: Let $G$ be a finite group of order $|G|=105$, and let $H,K\le G$ be subgroups with $|H|=15$ and $|K|=35$. What is $|H\cap K|$?
text: Let $G$ be a finite group of order $|G|=105$, and let $H,K\le G$ be subgroups with $|H|=15$ and $|K|=35$. What is $|H\cap K|$? By Lagrange applied inside $H$ and inside $K$, $|H\cap K|$ divides $\gcd(|H|,|K|)=\gcd(15,35)=5$, so $|H\cap K|\in\{1,5\}$. If $|H\cap K|=1$, then $|HK|=|H|\cdot|K|/|H\cap K|=15\cdot 35=525>105=|G|$, impossible. So $|H\cap K|=5$. $H\cap K$ is a subgroup of both $H$ and $K$; use Lagrange twice, then rule out the trivial case via a counting argument in $G$.

q: The First Isomorphism Theorem says for a homomorphism $\varphi:G\to H$:
text: The First Isomorphism Theorem says for a homomorphism $\varphi:G\to H$: Quotienting by the kernel identifies exactly elements with equal image.

q: How many elements does $\mathbb{F}_{3^2}$ have?
text: How many elements does $\mathbb{F}_{3^2}$ have? A finite field $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ has exactly $p^n$ elements.

q: In characteristic $p$, the Frobenius map on $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ is:
text: In characteristic $p$, the Frobenius map on $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ is: The Frobenius endomorphism raises each element to the $p$th power.

q: Let $\varphi:\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$ be the quotient map. By the First Isomorphism Theorem, $\mathbb{Z}/\ker\varphi\cong\operatorname{im}\varphi$. Which statement about this isomorphism is correct?
text: Let $\varphi:\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$ be the quotient map. By the First Isomorphism Theorem, $\mathbb{Z}/\ker\varphi\cong\operatorname{im}\varphi$. Which statement about this isomorphism is correct? The kernel of the quotient map $n\mapsto\bar{n}$ is $6\mathbb{Z}=(6)$, and the map is surjective, so $\operatorname{im}\varphi=\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$. The First Isomorphism Theorem then gives $\mathbb{Z}/(6)\cong\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$, matching the original codomain exactly.

q: The Frobenius on $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ generates the Galois group $\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{F}_{p^n}/\mathbb{F}_p)$. How many elements does this Galois group have when $p=2,\ n=4$?
text: The Frobenius on $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ generates the Galois group $\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{F}_{p^n}/\mathbb{F}_p)$. How many elements does this Galois group have when $p=2,\ n=4$? $\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{F}_{2^4}/\mathbb{F}_2)\cong\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}$, cyclic of order $n=4$. It is generated by the Frobenius $x\mapsto x^2$.

q: Which map is a group homomorphism from $(\mathbb{R},+)$ to $(\mathbb{R}^{>0},\times)$ with kernel $\{0\}$?
text: Which map is a group homomorphism from $(\mathbb{R},+)$ to $(\mathbb{R}^{>0},\times)$ with kernel $\{0\}$? $e^{x+y}=e^x e^y$, so $x\mapsto e^x$ is a homomorphism $(\mathbb{R},+)\to(\mathbb{R}^{>0},\times)$. Its kernel is $\{x:e^x=1\}=\{0\}$, confirming injectivity. The other options either map to the wrong codomain or fail the homomorphism property.

q: A proper ideal $\mathfrak{m}\subsetneq R$ of a commutative ring with $1$ is maximal exactly when:
text: A proper ideal $\mathfrak{m}\subsetneq R$ of a commutative ring with $1$ is maximal exactly when: By the correspondence theorem, ideals of $R/\mathfrak{m}$ correspond to ideals of $R$ containing $\mathfrak{m}$. Maximality means the only such ideals are $\mathfrak{m}$ and $R$, i.e. $R/\mathfrak{m}$ has only $(0)$ and itself as ideals — which is exactly the field condition.

q: In $\mathbb{Z}$, how many maximal ideals contain the ideal $(30)$?
text: In $\mathbb{Z}$, how many maximal ideals contain the ideal $(30)$? Maximal ideals of $\mathbb{Z}$ are $(p)$ for $p$ prime. $(30)\subseteq(p)$ iff $p\mid 30$, so $p\in\{2,3,5\}$ — three of them.

q: Which ring is not an integral domain?
text: Which ring is not an integral domain? In $\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$, $\overline{2}\cdot\overline{3}=\overline{0}$ with neither factor zero, so $(6)$ is not prime. The other three are domains.

q: In $\mathbb{Z}[x]$, the ideal $(2,x)$ is maximal. Which quotient ring witnesses this, and what does it equal?
text: In $\mathbb{Z}[x]$, the ideal $(2,x)$ is maximal. Which quotient ring witnesses this, and what does it equal? Quotienting by $(x)$ first gives $\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x)\cong\mathbb{Z}$; then quotienting by the image of $(2)$ gives $\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\cong\mathbb{F}_2$. Since $\mathbb{F}_2$ is a field, $(2,x)$ is maximal. This chains ideal/quotient ring reasoning with the maximal $\Leftrightarrow$ field criterion. Quotient by one generator at a time; the order doesn't matter.

q: Let $R=\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)\cong\mathbb{Z}[i]$ (Gaussian integers). How many units does $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ have? (A unit $u$ satisfies $u\cdot v=1$ for some $v\in\mathbb{Z}[i]$.)
text: Let $R=\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)\cong\mathbb{Z}[i]$ (Gaussian integers). How many units does $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ have? (A unit $u$ satisfies $u\cdot v=1$ for some $v\in\mathbb{Z}[i]$.) The norm $N(a+bi)=a^2+b^2$ is multiplicative. $u$ is a unit iff $N(u)=1$, i.e. $a^2+b^2=1$ over $\mathbb{Z}$. Solutions: $(\pm1,0),(0,\pm1)$, giving units $\{1,-1,i,-i\}$ — exactly $4$.

q: A prime ideal $\mathfrak{p}$ in a commutative ring $R$ satisfies: $ab\in\mathfrak{p}\Rightarrow a\in\mathfrak{p}$ or $b\in\mathfrak{p}$. Which is a correct counterexample showing a prime ideal need not be maximal?
text: A prime ideal $\mathfrak{p}$ in a commutative ring $R$ satisfies: $ab\in\mathfrak{p}\Rightarrow a\in\mathfrak{p}$ or $b\in\mathfrak{p}$. Which is a correct counterexample showing a prime ideal need not be maximal? $(0)\subset\mathbb{Z}$ is prime because $\mathbb{Z}$ is a domain ($ab=0\Rightarrow a=0$ or $b=0$), but $(0)$ is not maximal since it is properly contained in $(2),(3),(5),\ldots$ This shows prime $\not\Rightarrow$ maximal in general. Option B gives a maximal-prime example (not a counterexample). Option C is wrong: $(x)$ in $\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2)$ is not prime since $x\cdot x=x^2=0\in(x)$ but $x\notin(0)$.

q: What is $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3}):\mathbb{Q}]$?
text: What is $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3}):\mathbb{Q}]$? $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}):\mathbb{Q}]=2$ (minimal poly $x^2-2$), and $\sqrt{3}\notin\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$, so $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3}):\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})]=2$. The Tower Law gives $2\cdot 2=4$.

q: The minimal polynomial $m_\alpha\in F[x]$ of an algebraic element $\alpha$ is characterized by:
text: The minimal polynomial $m_\alpha\in F[x]$ of an algebraic element $\alpha$ is characterized by: The ideal $\{p\in F[x]:p(\alpha)=0\}$ is principal in the PID $F[x]$, generated by a unique monic irreducible — that generator is the minimal polynomial.

q: Let $\alpha=\sqrt[3]{2}$. What is $[\mathbb{Q}(\alpha):\mathbb{Q}]$?
text: Let $\alpha=\sqrt[3]{2}$. What is $[\mathbb{Q}(\alpha):\mathbb{Q}]$? The minimal polynomial of $\sqrt[3]{2}$ over $\mathbb{Q}$ is $x^3-2$, irreducible by Eisenstein at $p=2$. Its degree $3$ is the extension degree.

q: Let $\alpha$ be a root of the irreducible $p(x)=x^4-2x^2-2\in\mathbb{Q}[x]$ (irreducible by Eisenstein shifted: $p(x+1)=x^4+4x^3+4x^2-4$ — or confirm by rational-root test plus irreducibility mod $3$). What is $[\mathbb{Q}(\alpha):\mathbb{Q}]$?
text: Let $\alpha$ be a root of the irreducible $p(x)=x^4-2x^2-2\in\mathbb{Q}[x]$ (irreducible by Eisenstein shifted: $p(x+1)=x^4+4x^3+4x^2-4$ — or confirm by rational-root test plus irreducibility mod $3$). What is $[\mathbb{Q}(\alpha):\mathbb{Q}]$? Since $p(x)$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{Q}$ and $\alpha$ is a root, $\mathbb{Q}(\alpha)\cong\mathbb{Q}[x]/(p(x))$ and $[\mathbb{Q}(\alpha):\mathbb{Q}]=\deg p=4$. The Tower Law is not needed here — the extension degree equals the degree of the minimal polynomial.

q: The Tower Law says $[L:F]=[L:K][K:F]$ for $F\subseteq K\subseteq L$. Which chain gives $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt[3]{5}):\mathbb{Q}]=6$?
text: The Tower Law says $[L:F]=[L:K][K:F]$ for $F\subseteq K\subseteq L$. Which chain gives $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt[3]{5}):\mathbb{Q}]=6$? $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}):\mathbb{Q}]=2$. Since $\sqrt[3]{5}$ has minimal polynomial $x^3-5$ over $\mathbb{Q}$ and $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}):\mathbb{Q}]=2$ is coprime to $3$, the polynomial $x^3-5$ remains irreducible over $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$, giving degree-$3$ second step. Tower Law: $2\times 3=6$. A degree-3 polynomial stays irreducible over a degree-2 extension when $\gcd(2,3)=1$.

q: The finite field $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ can be viewed as a field extension of $\mathbb{F}_p$ of degree $n$. Combining this with the minimal-polynomial characterization: what is the minimal polynomial over $\mathbb{F}_2$ of a primitive element $\alpha\in\mathbb{F}_{2^3}$ (i.e., an element generating the multiplicative group of order $7$)?
text: The finite field $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ can be viewed as a field extension of $\mathbb{F}_p$ of degree $n$. Combining this with the minimal-polynomial characterization: what is the minimal polynomial over $\mathbb{F}_2$ of a primitive element $\alpha\in\mathbb{F}_{2^3}$ (i.e., an element generating the multiplicative group of order $7$)? $[\mathbb{F}_{2^3}:\mathbb{F}_2]=3$, so the minimal polynomial of any primitive element must have degree exactly $3$. The polynomial $x^3+x+1$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{F}_2$ (no roots in $\mathbb{F}_2$) and serves as a minimal polynomial for a generator. Degree-$7$ would make no sense as a minimal poly; degree $2$ gives $\mathbb{F}_4\neq\mathbb{F}_8$.

q: If $|G|=60$ and $H\le G$ with $|H|=12$, how many left cosets $gH$ does $H$ have in $G$?
text: If $|G|=60$ and $H\le G$ with $|H|=12$, how many left cosets $gH$ does $H$ have in $G$? By Lagrange, $[G:H]=|G|/|H|=60/12=5$.

q: Let $H\le G$ and $g\in G$. Which statement about the left coset $gH$ is always true?
text: Let $H\le G$ and $g\in G$. Which statement about the left coset $gH$ is always true? Left multiplication by $g$ is a bijection of $G$, so it sends $H$ to a set of the same cardinality. Cosets are not subgroups unless $g\in H$ (then $gH=H$ contains $e$); left and right cosets coincide only when $H$ is normal.

q: Select all statements equivalent to $H\trianglelefteq G$ (i.e., $H$ is normal in $G$).
text: Select all statements equivalent to $H\trianglelefteq G$ (i.e., $H$ is normal in $G$). Coset equality $gH=Hg$, closure under conjugation $gHg^{-1}\subseteq H$, and being the kernel of a homomorphism are the three standard equivalent definitions of normality. Prime index and abelianness can occur with non-normal or with normal subgroups; neither implies normality in general (though index $2$ always does). Normality is intrinsic to the $H\hookrightarrow G$ embedding; it doesn't depend on internal structure of $H$.

q: Let $H,K\le G$ be finite subgroups with $\gcd(|H|,|K|)=1$. What is $|H\cap K|$?
text: Let $H,K\le G$ be finite subgroups with $\gcd(|H|,|K|)=1$. What is $|H\cap K|$? Because $H\cap K\le H$, $|H\cap K|$ divides $|H|$; similarly it divides $|K|$. So $|H\cap K|\mid\gcd(|H|,|K|)=1$, forcing $|H\cap K|=1$. $H\cap K$ is a subgroup of both $H$ and $K$; apply Lagrange twice.

q: Consider $H=\langle (12)\rangle\le S_3$, of order $2$. Is $H$ normal in $S_3$?
text: Consider $H=\langle (12)\rangle\le S_3$, of order $2$. Is $H$ normal in $S_3$? $(13)(12)(13)^{-1}=(13)(12)(13)=(23)$, which lies outside $H=\{e,(12)\}$. Hence $H$ is not normal. Only index-$2$ subgroups are automatically normal; prime index alone is not enough when the index is $>2$. Normal subgroups must be closed under conjugation by every group element.

q: Let $G$ be a finite group and suppose $H\le G$ with $[G:H]=3$. The left cosets partition $G$ into $3$ blocks each of size $|H|$. If additionally $|G|=12$, what is $|H|$?
text: Let $G$ be a finite group and suppose $H\le G$ with $[G:H]=3$. The left cosets partition $G$ into $3$ blocks each of size $|H|$. If additionally $|G|=12$, what is $|H|$? $|H|=|G|/[G:H]=12/3=4$.

q: When is the coset multiplication $(aN)(bN)=(ab)N$ well-defined on $G/N$?
text: When is the coset multiplication $(aN)(bN)=(ab)N$ well-defined on $G/N$? The product of cosets is independent of representatives iff $aN=Na$ for all $a$, which is the defining property of a normal subgroup.

q: What is $|G/N|$ when $|G|=24$ and $|N|=6$ (with $N\trianglelefteq G$)?
text: What is $|G/N|$ when $|G|=24$ and $|N|=6$ (with $N\trianglelefteq G$)? $|G/N|=[G:N]=|G|/|N|=24/6=4$.

q: The first isomorphism theorem says that for a surjective homomorphism $\varphi:G\twoheadrightarrow H$,
text: The first isomorphism theorem says that for a surjective homomorphism $\varphi:G\twoheadrightarrow H$, Quotienting by the kernel identifies exactly those pairs that $\varphi$ sends to the same element, so $G/\ker\varphi\to\mathrm{im}\,\varphi=H$ is an isomorphism.

q: Let $H\le G$ and $N\trianglelefteq G$. The second isomorphism theorem gives an isomorphism involving the subgroup $HN$ and the quotient $H/(H\cap N)$. Which is it?
text: Let $H\le G$ and $N\trianglelefteq G$. The second isomorphism theorem gives an isomorphism involving the subgroup $HN$ and the quotient $H/(H\cap N)$. Which is it? Consider $\varphi:H\to HN/N$, $h\mapsto hN$. It is surjective (every coset of $N$ in $HN$ has an $H$-representative) and $\ker\varphi=H\cap N$. The first iso theorem gives $H/(H\cap N)\cong HN/N$. The inclusion $H\hookrightarrow HN$ composed with the projection $HN\twoheadrightarrow HN/N$ has kernel $H\cap N$.

q: Using the third isomorphism theorem with $G=\mathbb{Z}$, $N=6\mathbb{Z}$, $M=2\mathbb{Z}\supseteq N$, we have $(G/N)/(M/N)\cong G/M$. What is $|(G/N)/(M/N)|$?
text: Using the third isomorphism theorem with $G=\mathbb{Z}$, $N=6\mathbb{Z}$, $M=2\mathbb{Z}\supseteq N$, we have $(G/N)/(M/N)\cong G/M$. What is $|(G/N)/(M/N)|$? $G/M=\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}$, of order $2$. The third isomorphism theorem matches $|(G/N)/(M/N)|=|G/M|=2$. $G/M=\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}$.

q: Why is the alternating group $A_n$ (for $n\ge 5$) a witness to failure of the converse of Lagrange for quotients?
text: Why is the alternating group $A_n$ (for $n\ge 5$) a witness to failure of the converse of Lagrange for quotients? For $n\ge 5$, $A_n$ is simple, so its only normal subgroups are $\{e\}$ and $A_n$ itself. It still contains subgroups of many orders dividing $|A_n|$, but none of them are normal, so none can be used to form a nontrivial proper quotient.

q: Let $G=S_4$ act on the set $X=\{1,2,3,4\}$ by permutation. The orbit of $1\in X$ has size $4$, so by orbit-stabilizer, $|\mathrm{Stab}(1)|=|G|/4=?$
text: Let $G=S_4$ act on the set $X=\{1,2,3,4\}$ by permutation. The orbit of $1\in X$ has size $4$, so by orbit-stabilizer, $|\mathrm{Stab}(1)|=|G|/4=?$ $|G|/|\mathrm{orbit}(1)|=24/4=6$. The stabilizer of $1$ is the subgroup of permutations fixing $1$, isomorphic to $S_3$ on $\{2,3,4\}$ — of order $6$.

q: Burnside's lemma says the number of orbits of $G$ on $X$ equals
text: Burnside's lemma says the number of orbits of $G$ on $X$ equals Burnside (Cauchy–Frobenius): the number of orbits is the average number of fixed points across all group elements.

q: Apply Burnside to $G=\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}$ acting on $2$-colorings of a $3$-bead necklace by rotation. There are $2^3=8$ colorings. The identity fixes all $8$; each nontrivial rotation fixes $2$ (constant colorings). The orbit count is $(8+2+2)/3=?$
text: Apply Burnside to $G=\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}$ acting on $2$-colorings of a $3$-bead necklace by rotation. There are $2^3=8$ colorings. The identity fixes all $8$; each nontrivial rotation fixes $2$ (constant colorings). The orbit count is $(8+2+2)/3=?$ $(8+2+2)/3=12/3=4$. The four orbits correspond to distinct necklaces up to rotation.

q: Count the distinct $2$-colorings of the $4$ vertices of a square under the full dihedral group $D_4$ ($|D_4|=8$). Fixed counts: $e{:}16$, $r{:}2$, $r^2{:}4$, $r^3{:}2$, two diagonal reflections each fix $2^3=8$, two edge-midpoint reflections each fix $2^2=4$. Apply Burnside:
text: Count the distinct $2$-colorings of the $4$ vertices of a square under the full dihedral group $D_4$ ($|D_4|=8$). Fixed counts: $e{:}16$, $r{:}2$, $r^2{:}4$, $r^3{:}2$, two diagonal reflections each fix $2^3=8$, two edge-midpoint reflections each fix $2^2=4$. Apply Burnside: Sum: $16+2+4+2+8+8+4+4=48$. By Burnside, the number of orbits is $48/8=6$. Sum the fixed counts, then divide by $|D_4|=8$.

q: Why can Burnside's lemma fail for an infinite group $G$ acting on a finite set $X$?
text: Why can Burnside's lemma fail for an infinite group $G$ acting on a finite set $X$? Burnside's lemma relies on averaging fixed-point counts over a finite group. For infinite $G$, both numerator and denominator can be infinite. Infinite groups certainly can act on finite sets — e.g. $\mathbb{Z}$ acts on $\{0,1\}$ via $n\mapsto n\bmod 2$. Burnside is an averaging argument; averaging over an infinite group needs the group to be (at least) locally finite or measure-preserving.

q: By orbit-stabilizer, if $G$ acts on $X$ and $|G|=30$, $|\mathrm{orbit}(x)|=10$, what is $|\mathrm{Stab}(x)|$?
text: By orbit-stabilizer, if $G$ acts on $X$ and $|G|=30$, $|\mathrm{orbit}(x)|=10$, what is $|\mathrm{Stab}(x)|$? $|\mathrm{Stab}(x)|=|G|/|\mathrm{orbit}(x)|=30/10=3$.

q: For a group of order $|G|=15=3\cdot 5$, the number $n_3$ of Sylow $3$-subgroups satisfies $n_3\mid 5$ and $n_3\equiv 1\pmod 3$. What must $n_3$ be?
text: For a group of order $|G|=15=3\cdot 5$, the number $n_3$ of Sylow $3$-subgroups satisfies $n_3\mid 5$ and $n_3\equiv 1\pmod 3$. What must $n_3$ be? Divisors of $5$ are $1,5$. Only $1\equiv 1\pmod 3$, so $n_3=1$ — a unique Sylow $3$-subgroup, hence normal. Similarly $n_5=1$, and $G\cong\mathbb{Z}/15\mathbb{Z}$.

q: For $|G|=56=2^3\cdot 7$, what is the maximal possible order of a Sylow $2$-subgroup?
text: For $|G|=56=2^3\cdot 7$, what is the maximal possible order of a Sylow $2$-subgroup? A Sylow $p$-subgroup of a group of order $p^k m$ with $\gcd(p,m)=1$ has order exactly $p^k$. Here $p=2$, $k=3$, so order $2^3=8$.

q: Which of the following are true about Sylow $p$-subgroups of a finite group $G$ (with $|G|=p^k m$, $\gcd(p,m)=1$)?
text: Which of the following are true about Sylow $p$-subgroups of a finite group $G$ (with $|G|=p^k m$, $\gcd(p,m)=1$)? All five are part of the Sylow theorems. Existence gives order $p^k$; Sylow II gives conjugacy; Sylow III gives the divisibility and congruence on $n_p$; a unique Sylow subgroup is necessarily closed under conjugation, hence normal.

q: Let $|G|=30=2\cdot 3\cdot 5$. Which argument shows that $G$ cannot be simple?
text: Let $|G|=30=2\cdot 3\cdot 5$. Which argument shows that $G$ cannot be simple? $n_3\in\{1,10\}$ and $n_5\in\{1,6\}$. If $n_3=10$ and $n_5=6$, that's $10\cdot 2=20$ elements of order $3$ plus $6\cdot 4=24$ elements of order $5$, for $44>30$ elements — contradiction. So at least one Sylow subgroup is unique, hence normal, and $G$ isn't simple. If $n_3=10$ and $n_5=6$, count the elements of order $3$ and $5$: there would be more than $30$ total.

q: Let $p<q$ be primes with $p\nmid(q-1)$, and $|G|=pq$. Which conclusion is correct?
text: Let $p<q$ be primes with $p\nmid(q-1)$, and $|G|=pq$. Which conclusion is correct? $n_q\mid p$ and $n_q\equiv 1\pmod q$; since $p<q$, $n_q=1$. $n_p\mid q$ and $n_p\equiv 1\pmod p$; the divisors of $q$ are $1,q$, and $q\equiv 1\pmod p$ would force $p\mid(q-1)$, contradicting the hypothesis. Hence $n_p=1$. Both Sylows are normal and meet trivially, so $G\cong\mathbb{Z}/p\times\mathbb{Z}/q\cong\mathbb{Z}/pq$. Show $n_p=1$ by combining Sylow's divisibility and congruence with the hypothesis $p\nmid(q-1)$.

q: For $|G|=56=2^3\cdot 7$, list the divisors of $8$ congruent to $1\pmod 7$; this tells you the possible values of $n_7$. The count of such divisors is:
text: For $|G|=56=2^3\cdot 7$, list the divisors of $8$ congruent to $1\pmod 7$; this tells you the possible values of $n_7$. The count of such divisors is: Among $\{1,2,4,8\}$, the ones $\equiv 1\pmod 7$ are $1$ and $8$, so $n_7\in\{1,8\}$ — two possibilities. (A classical counting argument then forces $n_7=1$, but the raw Sylow constraint allows two.) Divisors of $8$: $1,2,4,8$. Which are $\equiv 1\pmod 7$?

q: How many abelian groups of order $8$ are there, up to isomorphism?
text: How many abelian groups of order $8$ are there, up to isomorphism? The partitions of $3$ (the $2$-adic valuation) are $3$, $2+1$, $1+1+1$, giving $\mathbb{Z}/8$, $\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2$, and $(\mathbb{Z}/2)^3$ — three groups.

q: How many abelian groups of order $12$ are there?
text: How many abelian groups of order $12$ are there? $12=2^2\cdot 3$. Partitions of $2$ (for $p=2$): $2$ or $1+1$; partitions of $1$ (for $p=3$): $1$. Product: $2\cdot 1=2$ groups — $\mathbb{Z}/12\cong\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/3$ and $\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/3$.

q: Which is the invariant-factor form of $\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/6$?
text: Which is the invariant-factor form of $\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/6$? Decompose into prime-power parts: $\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/6\cong\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/3$. Recombine as invariant factors $d_1\mid d_2$: pair smaller prime powers into $\mathbb{Z}/2$, and larger into $\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/3\cong\mathbb{Z}/12$. So $\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/12$.

q: How many abelian groups of order $72=2^3\cdot 3^2$ are there up to isomorphism?
text: How many abelian groups of order $72=2^3\cdot 3^2$ are there up to isomorphism? Partitions of $3$: $3,\,2+1,\,1+1+1$ — three of them. Partitions of $2$: $2,\,1+1$ — two. Product: $3\cdot 2=6$. Multiply the number of partitions of $3$ by the number of partitions of $2$.

q: Which abelian group has invariant factors $(2,4,12)$ (i.e., is isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/12$)?
text: Which abelian group has invariant factors $(2,4,12)$ (i.e., is isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/12$)? $\mathbb{Z}/12\cong\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/3$. So $\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/12\cong\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/3$. The order is $2\cdot 4\cdot 12=96$. The invariant-factor tuple $(2,4,12)$ is valid because $2\mid 4\mid 12$. Decompose each $\mathbb{Z}/d_i$ into prime-power cyclic factors and combine.

q: Why is the classification uniquely determined by the elementary divisors (i.e., the multiset of prime-power cyclic summands)?
text: Why is the classification uniquely determined by the elementary divisors (i.e., the multiset of prime-power cyclic summands)? The structure theorem for finitely generated modules over a PID, specialized to $R=\mathbb{Z}$, gives a canonical decomposition; the multisets of elementary divisors (or equivalently invariant factors) are invariants of the isomorphism class.

q: Apply Eisenstein's criterion at $p=3$ to $f(x)=x^3+3x+3\in\mathbb{Z}[x]$. Which conditions hold, showing irreducibility?
text:
Apply Eisenstein's criterion at $p=3$ to $f(x)=x^3+3x+3\in\mathbb{Z}[x]$. Which conditions hold, showing irreducibility? Eisenstein needs: $p
mid a_n$ (leading), $p\mid a_i$ for $i<n$, and $p^2
mid a_0$. With $p=3$: $3
mid 1$, $3\mid 0$ and $3\mid 3$, and $9
mid 3$. So $f$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{Q}$ (and over $\mathbb{Z}$ by Gauss's lemma).

q: What are the rational-root candidates for $f(x)=2x^2+3x+1$ from the rational-root test?
text: What are the rational-root candidates for $f(x)=2x^2+3x+1$ from the rational-root test? Candidates are $\pm(\text{divisors of }a_0)/(\text{divisors of }a_n)=\pm(1)/\pm(1,2)=\pm 1, \pm\tfrac{1}{2}$. Testing: $f(-1)=0$ and $f(-\tfrac{1}{2})=0$, so $f=(2x+1)(x+1)$.

q: If $f\in k[x]$ has degree $5$ and $g\in k[x]$ has degree $3$, what is $\deg(fg)$?
text: If $f\in k[x]$ has degree $5$ and $g\in k[x]$ has degree $3$, what is $\deg(fg)$? In a polynomial ring over a field (or any integral domain), $\deg(fg)=\deg f+\deg g=5+3=8$.

q: Consider $f(x)=x^4+1\in\mathbb{Q}[x]$. Which of the following is correct?
text: Consider $f(x)=x^4+1\in\mathbb{Q}[x]$. Which of the following is correct? $f(x+1)=(x+1)^4+1=x^4+4x^3+6x^2+4x+2$. Eisenstein at $p=2$: $2\nmid 1$, $2\mid 4,6,4,2$, $4\nmid 2$. So $f(x+1)$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{Q}$, hence so is $f(x)$. The factorization into $(x^2\pm\sqrt{2}x+1)$ exists only over $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$, not $\mathbb{Q}$. Use Eisenstein after the shift $x\mapsto x+1$.

q: Gauss's lemma states: if $f\in\mathbb{Z}[x]$ is primitive (gcd of coefficients is $1$) and factors as $f=gh$ in $\mathbb{Q}[x]$, then ...
text: Gauss's lemma states: if $f\in\mathbb{Z}[x]$ is primitive (gcd of coefficients is $1$) and factors as $f=gh$ in $\mathbb{Q}[x]$, then ... Gauss's lemma: the product of primitive polynomials is primitive, and consequently a factorization in $\mathbb{Q}[x]$ of a primitive integer polynomial can be rescaled to a factorization in $\mathbb{Z}[x]$. This is why irreducibility in $\mathbb{Q}[x]$ and in $\mathbb{Z}[x]$ agree for primitive polynomials. The point is that primitivity is stable under multiplication.

q: Reduce $f(x)=x^3+x+1$ mod $2$. How many roots does it have in $\mathbb{F}_2=\{0,1\}$?
text: Reduce $f(x)=x^3+x+1$ mod $2$. How many roots does it have in $\mathbb{F}_2=\{0,1\}$? $f(0)=1$, $f(1)=1+1+1=1$ in $\mathbb{F}_2$. No roots, so $\bar{f}\in\mathbb{F}_2[x]$ has no linear factors, and since it's degree $3$, it is irreducible over $\mathbb{F}_2$. This lifts to show $f$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{Z}$ (and $\mathbb{Q}$) too. Evaluate at $0$ and at $1$ in $\mathbb{F}_2$.

q: For a semidirect product $G=N\rtimes_\varphi H$ with $|N|=8$ and $|H|=3$, what is $|G|$?
text: For a semidirect product $G=N\rtimes_\varphi H$ with $|N|=8$ and $|H|=3$, what is $|G|$? $|G|=|N|\cdot|H|=8\cdot 3=24$, independent of $\varphi$.

q: Which group is $\mathbb{Z}/n\rtimes_\varphi\mathbb{Z}/2$ when $\varphi$ sends the generator of $\mathbb{Z}/2$ to the inversion automorphism $a\mapsto -a$ of $\mathbb{Z}/n$?
text: Which group is $\mathbb{Z}/n\rtimes_\varphi\mathbb{Z}/2$ when $\varphi$ sends the generator of $\mathbb{Z}/2$ to the inversion automorphism $a\mapsto -a$ of $\mathbb{Z}/n$? By construction $D_n=\langle r,s\mid r^n=s^2=e,\,srs=r^{-1}\rangle$; with $N=\langle r\rangle\cong\mathbb{Z}/n$ and $H=\langle s\rangle\cong\mathbb{Z}/2$ and $\varphi(s)=$ inversion, the twisted product reproduces the dihedral relations exactly.

q: A short exact sequence $1\to N\to G\to H\to 1$ splits when
text: A short exact sequence $1\to N\to G\to H\to 1$ splits when Splitting means the quotient map has a homomorphism section. Equivalently, $G$ is isomorphic to a semidirect product $N\rtimes H$.

q: Let $|G|=2p$ with $p$ an odd prime. Classify $G$ up to isomorphism.
text: Let $|G|=2p$ with $p$ an odd prime. Classify $G$ up to isomorphism. $n_p\mid 2$ and $n_p\equiv 1\pmod p$, so $n_p=1$ (since $p>2$ implies $2\not\equiv 1\pmod p$). So $N$ is normal; complement $\mathbb{Z}/2$ exists (Schur-Zassenhaus or Sylow argument). The only homomorphisms $\mathbb{Z}/2\to\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}/p)\cong\mathbb{Z}/(p-1)$ send the generator to either the identity (giving $\mathbb{Z}/p\times\mathbb{Z}/2=\mathbb{Z}/(2p)$) or to inversion (giving $D_p$). Sylow gives a unique normal Sylow $p$-subgroup $N$; then $G=N\rtimes\mathbb{Z}/2$, and $\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}/p)\cong\mathbb{Z}/(p-1)$ has a unique order-$2$ element (inversion).

q: When is an internal semidirect product $G=N\rtimes H$ actually a direct product $N\times H$?
text: When is an internal semidirect product $G=N\rtimes H$ actually a direct product $N\times H$? By definition $(n_1,h_1)(n_2,h_2)=(n_1\varphi_{h_1}(n_2),h_1 h_2)$; this collapses to componentwise multiplication iff $\varphi_{h}$ is the identity for all $h$, i.e. $\varphi$ is trivial. Coprimality and abelianness are red herrings. The multiplication in $N\rtimes_\varphi H$ reduces to that of $N\times H$ precisely when $\varphi$ is trivial.

q: How many nonisomorphic semidirect products $\mathbb{Z}/7\rtimes_\varphi\mathbb{Z}/3$ are there (counting both trivial and nontrivial $\varphi$)?
text: How many nonisomorphic semidirect products $\mathbb{Z}/7\rtimes_\varphi\mathbb{Z}/3$ are there (counting both trivial and nontrivial $\varphi$)? Homomorphisms $\mathbb{Z}/3\to\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}/7)\cong\mathbb{Z}/6$ correspond to elements of order dividing $3$ in $\mathbb{Z}/6$, which form the unique subgroup of order $3$. So there are $3$ homomorphisms, but two of the nontrivial ones give isomorphic semidirect products (they differ by an automorphism of $\mathbb{Z}/3$). Thus there are $2$ isomorphism classes: the trivial one ($\mathbb{Z}/21$) and the nonabelian one (order $21$). $\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}/7)\cong\mathbb{Z}/6$. How many subgroups of order dividing $3$?

title: Categories and morphisms
blurb: A category organizes objects and arrows with associative composition and identities.

title: Functors and natural transformations
blurb: Functors map structure between categories, and natural transformations compare functors componentwise.

title: Yoneda, limits, and adjunctions
blurb: Yoneda and adjunctions turn universal properties into a computational language used across modern mathematics.

title: Universal properties and representability
blurb: A universal property pins an object down as the representing object of a Hom-functor; every standard construction (product, free group, limit, adjoint) is a universal property in disguise.

title: Monoidal categories
blurb: A monoidal category has an associative bifunctor and a unit, up to coherent natural isomorphism; it is the setting for tensor products, braidings, and algebraic structures internal to a category.

title: The Yoneda lemma
blurb: $\mathrm{Nat}(h_A,F)\cong F(A)$: every natural transformation out of a representable is pinned by a single element, and every object is determined by its functor of points.

title: Limits and colimits
blurb: A (co)limit is the universal (co)cone on a diagram; products, equalizers, pullbacks and their duals are all special cases of the same representability problem.

title: Adjunctions: unit, counit, triangle
blurb: An adjunction $F\dashv G$ is a natural Hom-isomorphism, equivalently unit/counit satisfying the triangle identities; left adjoints preserve colimits, right adjoints preserve limits.

title: Monads and their algebras
blurb: A monad $(T,\mu,\eta)$ is a monoid in endofunctors; Kleisli and Eilenberg–Moore categories recover the adjunction and classify its algebras.

title: Kan extensions
blurb: Left and right Kan extensions along a functor extend structure as freely (or cofreely) as possible; Mac Lane's aphorism ‘all concepts are Kan extensions’ is literally true.

title: 2-categories preview
blurb: A 2-category has objects, 1-morphisms, and 2-morphisms between 1-morphisms; $\mathsf{Cat}$ is the archetype, with functors as 1-cells and natural transformations as 2-cells.

title: Enriched categories preview
blurb: Enrichment replaces Hom-sets by objects in a monoidal $\mathcal{V}$; Ab-enriched gives additive categories, and Lawvere showed a metric space is exactly a $([0,\infty],+,0)$-enriched category.

q: Which equation is NOT part of the definition of a category?
text: Which equation is NOT part of the definition of a category? Composition in a category is associative and unital, but not commutative. In $\mathsf{Set}$ already, $g\circ f\ne f\circ g$ in general.

q: In a category $\mathcal{C}$, an isomorphism $f\colon A\to B$ is a morphism such that:
text: In a category $\mathcal{C}$, an isomorphism $f\colon A\to B$ is a morphism such that: Isomorphism is a categorical notion: a morphism with a two-sided inverse. Only in $\mathsf{Set}$ does this specialize to bijection.

q: How many morphisms does the category $\mathbf{2}=(\bullet\xrightarrow{f}\bullet)$ with two objects and one non-identity arrow have in total (including identities)?
text: How many morphisms does the category $\mathbf{2}=(\bullet\xrightarrow{f}\bullet)$ with two objects and one non-identity arrow have in total (including identities)? Two identities $\mathrm{id}_0,\mathrm{id}_1$ plus the single arrow $f$ gives $3$ morphisms.

q: A morphism $f\colon A\to B$ in a category is a monomorphism if and only if:
text: A morphism $f\colon A\to B$ in a category is a monomorphism if and only if: A monomorphism is left-cancellable: $f\circ g=f\circ h\Rightarrow g=h$. This is the categorical generalisation of injectivity. Having a left inverse implies mono, but not conversely — e.g. inclusions in $\mathsf{Top}$ can be mono without being split.

q: Consider the category $\mathcal{B}G$ associated to a group $G$ (one object $*$, morphisms = elements of $G$, composition = multiplication). A functor $F\colon\mathcal{B}G\to\mathsf{Set}$ is the same as:
text: Consider the category $\mathcal{B}G$ associated to a group $G$ (one object $*$, morphisms = elements of $G$, composition = multiplication). A functor $F\colon\mathcal{B}G\to\mathsf{Set}$ is the same as: $F$ must send $*$ to some set $S=F(*)$ and each $g\in G$ to a function $S\to S$, preserving composition and identity. This is precisely the data of a left $G$-set (group action on $S$).

q: The category of a poset $(P,\le)$ has a morphism $x\to y$ iff $x\le y$. In the poset $\{0<1<2\}$, how many morphisms (including identities) are there in total?
text: The category of a poset $(P,\le)$ has a morphism $x\to y$ iff $x\le y$. In the poset $\{0<1<2\}$, how many morphisms (including identities) are there in total? Identities: $0\to 0$, $1\to 1$, $2\to 2$ (3 morphisms). Non-identity morphisms: $0\to 1$, $1\to 2$, $0\to 2$ (3 morphisms). Total: $6$.

q: A functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ must satisfy which property on composition?
text: A functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ must satisfy which property on composition? Covariant functors preserve the order of composition: $F(g\circ f)=F(g)\circ F(f)$. Contravariant functors reverse it — those are functors out of $\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}$.

q: A natural transformation $\eta\colon F\Rightarrow G$ between $F,G\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ consists of components $\eta_A\colon F(A)\to G(A)$ satisfying, for every $f\colon A\to B$ in $\mathcal{C}$:
text: A natural transformation $\eta\colon F\Rightarrow G$ between $F,G\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ consists of components $\eta_A\colon F(A)\to G(A)$ satisfying, for every $f\colon A\to B$ in $\mathcal{C}$: The naturality square: $G(f)\circ\eta_A=\eta_B\circ F(f)$. The transformation commutes with the action of $f$ on both sides.

q: How many functors are there from the terminal category $\mathbf{1}$ (one object, only identity) to a category $\mathcal{C}$ with exactly $5$ objects?
text: How many functors are there from the terminal category $\mathbf{1}$ (one object, only identity) to a category $\mathcal{C}$ with exactly $5$ objects? A functor $\mathbf{1}\to\mathcal{C}$ just picks an object of $\mathcal{C}$, so the count is $|\mathrm{Ob}(\mathcal{C})|=5$.

q: The evaluation map $\mathrm{ev}_V\colon V\to V^{**}$, $v\mapsto(\varphi\mapsto\varphi(v))$, is a natural transformation $\mathrm{id}_{\mathsf{Vect}_k}\Rightarrow(-)^{**}$. Why is there NO natural isomorphism $\mathrm{id}\Rightarrow(-)^*$ (single dual) on all of $\mathsf{Vect}_k$?
text: The evaluation map $\mathrm{ev}_V\colon V\to V^{**}$, $v\mapsto(\varphi\mapsto\varphi(v))$, is a natural transformation $\mathrm{id}_{\mathsf{Vect}_k}\Rightarrow(-)^{**}$. Why is there NO natural isomorphism $\mathrm{id}\Rightarrow(-)^*$ (single dual) on all of $\mathsf{Vect}_k$? $(-)^*=\operatorname{Hom}(-,k)$ is contravariant: it reverses the direction of linear maps. A natural transformation requires components in the same ambient functor category, which forces both functors to have the same variance. Since $\mathrm{id}$ is covariant and $(-)^*$ is contravariant, no natural transformation — let alone isomorphism — between them can be written down. What direction does $(-)^*$ send a linear map $f\colon V\to W$?

q: Arrange the steps to verify that a family $\eta=\{\eta_A\}$ is a natural transformation $F\Rightarrow G$. Which ordering is logically correct?
text: Arrange the steps to verify that a family $\eta=\{\eta_A\}$ is a natural transformation $F\Rightarrow G$. Which ordering is logically correct? One must first define all components $\eta_A$, then verify the naturality square for each morphism $f$. Step (i) fixes an arbitrary morphism; steps (ii–iii) spell out both paths around the square; step (iv) proves them equal using the definitions of $F$, $G$, and $\eta$. Isomorphism is not required for a natural transformation.

q: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be the discrete category on $3$ objects (no non-identity morphisms). How many natural transformations are there from the identity functor $\mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{C}}$ to itself?
text: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be the discrete category on $3$ objects (no non-identity morphisms). How many natural transformations are there from the identity functor $\mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{C}}$ to itself? A natural transformation $\eta\colon\mathrm{id}\Rightarrow\mathrm{id}$ needs, for each object $A$, a morphism $\eta_A\colon A\to A$. In a discrete category the only morphism $A\to A$ is $\mathrm{id}_A$. So there is exactly $1$ natural transformation (all components forced to be identities).

q: The Yoneda lemma states that for a locally small category $\mathcal{C}$, a functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$, and $X\in\mathcal{C}$, the natural transformations $h_X\Rightarrow F$ are in bijection with:
text: The Yoneda lemma states that for a locally small category $\mathcal{C}$, a functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$, and $X\in\mathcal{C}$, the natural transformations $h_X\Rightarrow F$ are in bijection with: Yoneda: $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X,F)\cong F(X)$ naturally in $X$ and $F$. Evaluating at $\mathrm{id}_X$ recovers the element, and any $x\in F(X)$ determines the transformation $f\mapsto F(f)(x)$.

q: If $F\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ is left adjoint to $G\colon\mathcal{D}\to\mathcal{C}$, which is true?
text: If $F\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ is left adjoint to $G\colon\mathcal{D}\to\mathcal{C}$, which is true? Left adjoints preserve colimits (LAPC); right adjoints preserve limits (RAPL). The adjunction $\operatorname{Hom}(F(-),-)\cong\operatorname{Hom}(-,G(-))$ together with Yoneda makes this immediate.

q: In $\mathsf{Set}$, the product (= categorical limit of the discrete two-object diagram) of $\{a,b,c\}$ and $\{0,1\}$ has how many elements?
text: In $\mathsf{Set}$, the product (= categorical limit of the discrete two-object diagram) of $\{a,b,c\}$ and $\{0,1\}$ has how many elements? The product in $\mathsf{Set}$ is the Cartesian product, so $|A\times B|=|A|\cdot|B|=3\cdot 2=6$.

q: The Yoneda embedding $h_{(-)}\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathsf{Fun}(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}},\mathsf{Set})$ is fully faithful. What does full faithfulness imply about objects of $\mathcal{C}$?
text: The Yoneda embedding $h_{(-)}\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathsf{Fun}(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}},\mathsf{Set})$ is fully faithful. What does full faithfulness imply about objects of $\mathcal{C}$? Full faithfulness gives $\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(X,Y)\cong\operatorname{Nat}(h_X,h_Y)$ (by Yoneda with $F=h_Y$). In particular $X\cong Y\Leftrightarrow h_X\cong h_Y$: objects are determined up to isomorphism by their functor of points. Not every presheaf need be representable (that would make the embedding an equivalence, which is usually false).

q: For the free–forgetful adjunction $F\dashv U\colon\mathsf{Grp}\to\mathsf{Set}$, the unit $\eta_X\colon X\to UF(X)$ is the insertion-of-generators map. What does the counit $\varepsilon_G\colon FU(G)\to G$ do?
text: For the free–forgetful adjunction $F\dashv U\colon\mathsf{Grp}\to\mathsf{Set}$, the unit $\eta_X\colon X\to UF(X)$ is the insertion-of-generators map. What does the counit $\varepsilon_G\colon FU(G)\to G$ do? The counit $\varepsilon_G$ is the evaluation map: $FU(G)$ is the free group on the underlying set of $G$, and $\varepsilon_G$ is the unique homomorphism sending each generator $g\in U(G)$ to $g\in G$ — i.e. 'reduce words in elements of $G$ using $G$'s own multiplication.' This is the universal property of the free group applied to $\mathrm{id}_{U(G)}$. Apply the universal property of the free group to the identity function on $U(G)$.

q: In $\mathsf{Set}$, the coproduct (categorical colimit) of $\{a,b\}$ and $\{0,1,2\}$ has how many elements?
text: In $\mathsf{Set}$, the coproduct (categorical colimit) of $\{a,b\}$ and $\{0,1,2\}$ has how many elements? The coproduct in $\mathsf{Set}$ is the disjoint union, so $|A\sqcup B|=|A|+|B|=2+3=5$.

q: A functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$ is called representable if:
text: A functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$ is called representable if: Representability means $F\cong h_X$ for some object $X$; by Yoneda this $X$ is unique up to unique iso.

q: The tensor product $M\otimes_R N$ of two $R$-modules represents which functor (on $R$-modules $P$)?
text: The tensor product $M\otimes_R N$ of two $R$-modules represents which functor (on $R$-modules $P$)? By definition, $M\otimes_R N$ represents $P\mapsto\operatorname{Bilin}_R(M\times N, P)$: every $R$-bilinear map $M\times N\to P$ factors uniquely through the canonical $\otimes\colon M\times N\to M\otimes N$.

q: By Yoneda, how many objects (up to isomorphism) satisfy the universal property of the product $A\times B$ in a given category (assuming at least one exists)?
text: By Yoneda, how many objects (up to isomorphism) satisfy the universal property of the product $A\times B$ in a given category (assuming at least one exists)? Representing objects are unique up to unique isomorphism — exactly $1$ isomorphism class.

q: Arrange the canonical steps of a proof that products in a category are unique up to unique isomorphism. Let $P=(A\times B,\pi_1,\pi_2)$ and $Q=(A\times B,\rho_1,\rho_2)$ be two candidate products of the same objects $A$ and $B$.
text: Arrange the canonical steps of a proof that products in a category are unique up to unique isomorphism. Let $P=(A\times B,\pi_1,\pi_2)$ and $Q=(A\times B,\rho_1,\rho_2)$ be two candidate products of the same objects $A$ and $B$. First produce $u$ and $v$ in either order (A, then B). Then verify $vu=\mathrm{id}_P$ via uniqueness inside $P$'s universal property (C), and $uv=\mathrm{id}_Q$ via uniqueness inside $Q$'s (D). The proof is symmetric between $P$ and $Q$, which is the hallmark of a universal-property argument.

q: Which of the following is a counterexample showing that a left adjoint need not reflect limits?
text: Which of the following is a counterexample showing that a left adjoint need not reflect limits? Left adjoints preserve colimits but need not preserve — or reflect — limits. The free group functor $F\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Grp}$ sends a two-element product $X\times Y$ to $F(X\times Y)$, the free group on $X\times Y$, which is generally not the product $F(X)\times F(Y)$ in $\mathsf{Grp}$. This is a concrete illustration that LAPC (left adjoints preserve colimits) has no analogue for limits. Which direction (limits vs colimits) does a LEFT adjoint preserve?

q: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be a category with exactly one object $*$ and $\operatorname{Hom}(*,*)=\{\mathrm{id}\}$. How many distinct functors $F\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{C}$ are there?
text: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be a category with exactly one object $*$ and $\operatorname{Hom}(*,*)=\{\mathrm{id}\}$. How many distinct functors $F\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{C}$ are there? There is only one object, so $F(*)=*$ is forced. The only morphism $\mathrm{id}_*$ must map to a morphism $*\to*$, i.e. to $\mathrm{id}_*$ itself. So the only functor is the identity — exactly $1$.

q: A monoid object in the monoidal category $(\mathsf{Ab},\otimes_{\mathbb Z},\mathbb Z)$ is:
text: A monoid object in the monoidal category $(\mathsf{Ab},\otimes_{\mathbb Z},\mathbb Z)$ is: An abelian group $R$ together with a $\mathbb Z$-bilinear multiplication $R\otimes_{\mathbb Z} R\to R$ (associative) and a unit $\mathbb Z\to R$ is exactly the data of a unital ring.

q: Which coherence diagram must commute in every monoidal category?
text: Which coherence diagram must commute in every monoidal category? Mac Lane's pentagon is one of the two required coherence diagrams (the other is the triangle). The hexagon/naturality of $\sigma$ are extra data for braided/symmetric monoidal categories.

q: How many distinct bracketings of a fourfold tensor product $A\otimes B\otimes C\otimes D$ are there (Catalan number $C_3$)?
text: How many distinct bracketings of a fourfold tensor product $A\otimes B\otimes C\otimes D$ are there (Catalan number $C_3$)? The Catalan number $C_3 = 5$ counts full binary trees with $4$ leaves — $((AB)C)D,\ (A(BC))D,\ (AB)(CD),\ A((BC)D),\ A(B(CD))$. Mac Lane's pentagon says all $5$ become equal under $\alpha$.

q: Mac Lane's coherence theorem for monoidal categories states that:
text: Mac Lane's coherence theorem for monoidal categories states that: Mac Lane's coherence theorem says every monoidal category is monoidally equivalent to a strict one (where $\otimes$ is strictly associative and $I\otimes A=A=A\otimes I$ on the nose). This justifies treating parenthesizations as equal 'in practice': any diagram built from associators and unit isomorphisms commutes.

q: A commutative monoid object in a symmetric monoidal category $(\mathcal{C},\otimes,I)$ is a monoid $(M,\mu,\eta)$ satisfying additionally $\mu\circ\sigma_{M,M}=\mu$, where $\sigma$ is the symmetry. In $(\mathsf{Ab},\otimes_{\mathbb Z},\mathbb Z)$, what additional structure does this give over an ordinary ring?
text: A commutative monoid object in a symmetric monoidal category $(\mathcal{C},\otimes,I)$ is a monoid $(M,\mu,\eta)$ satisfying additionally $\mu\circ\sigma_{M,M}=\mu$, where $\sigma$ is the symmetry. In $(\mathsf{Ab},\otimes_{\mathbb Z},\mathbb Z)$, what additional structure does this give over an ordinary ring? Commutativity of the monoid with respect to the symmetry isomorphism $\sigma$ translates exactly to $ab=ba$ in the ring $M$, i.e. $M$ is a commutative ring.

q: The Catalan number $C_n$ counts the bracketings of an $(n{+}1)$-fold tensor. Using $C_n=\binom{2n}{n}/(n+1)$, compute $C_4$ (the number of bracketings of a $5$-fold tensor $A\otimes B\otimes C\otimes D\otimes E$).
text: The Catalan number $C_n$ counts the bracketings of an $(n{+}1)$-fold tensor. Using $C_n=\binom{2n}{n}/(n+1)$, compute $C_4$ (the number of bracketings of a $5$-fold tensor $A\otimes B\otimes C\otimes D\otimes E$). $C_4=\binom{8}{4}/5=70/5=14$. These $14$ bracketings all become equal under iterated application of the associator $\alpha$, by Mac Lane's coherence theorem.

q: The Yoneda lemma asserts a bijection $\mathrm{Nat}(h_A,F)\cong F(A)$. The forward map sends a natural transformation $\eta\colon h_A\Rightarrow F$ to:
text: The Yoneda lemma asserts a bijection $\mathrm{Nat}(h_A,F)\cong F(A)$. The forward map sends a natural transformation $\eta\colon h_A\Rightarrow F$ to: Evaluate $\eta$'s $A$-component at the distinguished element $\mathrm{id}_A\in h_A(A)=\mathrm{Hom}(A,A)$. That single value determines every other component via naturality.

q: Select every statement that is an immediate corollary of the Yoneda lemma.
text: Select every statement that is an immediate corollary of the Yoneda lemma. Taking $F=h_Y$ gives $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X,h_Y)\cong\mathrm{Hom}(X,Y)$ — full faithfulness. Isomorphism of functors then pulls back to isomorphism of objects. Unique representability is the Yoneda-level uniqueness statement. Not every presheaf is representable (that would be an equivalence, which generally fails).

q: In the poset category on $\{0<1<2\}$ viewed as a category, the representable $h_1=\mathrm{Hom}(-,1)$ satisfies $h_1(C)=\{\ast\}$ iff $C\le 1$, else $\emptyset$. How many natural transformations $h_1\Rightarrow F$ are there for the functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$ with $|F(1)|=4$?
text: In the poset category on $\{0<1<2\}$ viewed as a category, the representable $h_1=\mathrm{Hom}(-,1)$ satisfies $h_1(C)=\{\ast\}$ iff $C\le 1$, else $\emptyset$. How many natural transformations $h_1\Rightarrow F$ are there for the functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$ with $|F(1)|=4$? Yoneda: $\mathrm{Nat}(h_1,F)\cong F(1)$, so the count is $|F(1)|=4$.

q: Prove $\mathrm{Nat}(h_A,F)\to F(A)$, $\eta\mapsto\eta_A(\mathrm{id}_A)$, is injective. Pick the correct next step.
text: Prove $\mathrm{Nat}(h_A,F)\to F(A)$, $\eta\mapsto\eta_A(\mathrm{id}_A)$, is injective. Pick the correct next step. Naturality says $\eta_C(\varphi)=F(\varphi)(\eta_A(\mathrm{id}_A))=F(\varphi)(x)$, same for $\eta'$. So components agree on every $C$ and every $\varphi$, giving $\eta=\eta'$. $\varphi=\mathrm{id}_A\circ\varphi$: push it through the naturality square.

q: An object $X\in\mathcal{C}$ is called a $\textit{projective generator}$ if $\mathrm{Hom}(X,-)$ is faithful and preserves epimorphisms. By Yoneda, this is equivalent to saying:
text: An object $X\in\mathcal{C}$ is called a $\textit{projective generator}$ if $\mathrm{Hom}(X,-)$ is faithful and preserves epimorphisms. By Yoneda, this is equivalent to saying: Faithfulness of $\mathrm{Hom}(X,-)$ forces $h_X$ to separate morphisms, and preservation of epis under a representable makes $X$ projective. Together they are the standard 'enough projectives' condition.

q: Let $G$ be a finite group and $\mathcal{B}G$ its one-object category. A functor $F\colon\mathcal{B}G^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$ is a right $G$-set. By Yoneda, $\mathrm{Nat}(h_\ast,F)\cong F(\ast)$; for $G=S_3$ and $F(\ast)$ the regular right $S_3$-set, how many natural transformations are there?
text: Let $G$ be a finite group and $\mathcal{B}G$ its one-object category. A functor $F\colon\mathcal{B}G^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$ is a right $G$-set. By Yoneda, $\mathrm{Nat}(h_\ast,F)\cong F(\ast)$; for $G=S_3$ and $F(\ast)$ the regular right $S_3$-set, how many natural transformations are there? $|S_3|=6$, so the regular $S_3$-set has $6$ elements and Yoneda gives $|\mathrm{Nat}(h_\ast,F)|=|F(\ast)|=6$.

q: Match each diagram shape $\mathcal{J}$ with the corresponding limit in $\mathcal{C}$.
text: Match each diagram shape $\mathcal{J}$ with the corresponding limit in $\mathcal{C}$. Each limit is the universal cone on the corresponding shape: empty → terminal, two-point discrete → product, parallel pair → equalizer, cospan → pullback.

q: In $\mathsf{Set}$, the pullback of $f\colon X\to Z$ and $g\colon Y\to Z$ is:
text: In $\mathsf{Set}$, the pullback of $f\colon X\to Z$ and $g\colon Y\to Z$ is: The pullback is the set of pairs that agree in $Z$ — exactly the universal cone over the cospan.

q: Let $X=\{1,2,3,4\}$, $Y=\{a,b\}$, $Z=\{0,1\}$, with $f\colon X\to Z$ defined $f(1)=f(2)=0$, $f(3)=f(4)=1$, and $g\colon Y\to Z$ defined $g(a)=0$, $g(b)=1$. Compute $|X\times_Z Y|$.
text: Let $X=\{1,2,3,4\}$, $Y=\{a,b\}$, $Z=\{0,1\}$, with $f\colon X\to Z$ defined $f(1)=f(2)=0$, $f(3)=f(4)=1$, and $g\colon Y\to Z$ defined $g(a)=0$, $g(b)=1$. Compute $|X\times_Z Y|$. Fiber over $0$: $\{1,2\}\times\{a\}$ gives $2$ pairs; fiber over $1$: $\{3,4\}\times\{b\}$ gives $2$ pairs. Total: $4$.

q: A functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ $\textit{preserves}$ a limit $L$ of a diagram $D$ if $F(L)$ is a limit of $F\circ D$; it $\textit{reflects}$ limits if every cone $(c_j)$ in $\mathcal{C}$ whose image is a limit cone in $\mathcal{D}$ was already a limit cone. Which statement is false?
text: A functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ $\textit{preserves}$ a limit $L$ of a diagram $D$ if $F(L)$ is a limit of $F\circ D$; it $\textit{reflects}$ limits if every cone $(c_j)$ in $\mathcal{C}$ whose image is a limit cone in $\mathcal{D}$ was already a limit cone. Which statement is false? Faithful functors reflect isomorphisms but not general limits. E.g. $U\colon\mathsf{Top}\to\mathsf{Set}$ is faithful yet does not reflect products (a non-product topology on $X\times Y$ still has product underlying set). A faithful functor can forget just enough structure to reflect isos but not reflect whole limit cones.

q: Arrange the steps to show the pullback $X\times_Z Y$ exists in $\mathsf{Set}$.
text: Arrange the steps to show the pullback $X\times_Z Y$ exists in $\mathsf{Set}$. Construct the set, equip it with projections, produce the universal map, verify uniqueness.

q: In $\mathsf{Set}$, the coequalizer of $f,g\colon A\rightrightarrows B$ with $A=\{1,2,3\}$, $B=\{x,y,z,w\}$, $f(1)=x,f(2)=y,f(3)=z$ and $g(1)=y,g(2)=z,g(3)=w$ is $B/{\sim}$ where $\sim$ is generated by $f(a)\sim g(a)$. How many elements does the coequalizer have?
text: In $\mathsf{Set}$, the coequalizer of $f,g\colon A\rightrightarrows B$ with $A=\{1,2,3\}$, $B=\{x,y,z,w\}$, $f(1)=x,f(2)=y,f(3)=z$ and $g(1)=y,g(2)=z,g(3)=w$ is $B/{\sim}$ where $\sim$ is generated by $f(a)\sim g(a)$. How many elements does the coequalizer have? The relations $x\sim y$, $y\sim z$, $z\sim w$ collapse all of $B$ to a single equivalence class.

q: $F\dashv G$ means, by definition, that there is an isomorphism, natural in $A$ and $B$:
text: $F\dashv G$ means, by definition, that there is an isomorphism, natural in $A$ and $B$: The defining Hom-isomorphism: mapping out of $F(A)$ in $\mathcal{D}$ is the same data as mapping into $G(B)$ in $\mathcal{C}$.

q: Select every example of an adjunction.
text: Select every example of an adjunction. The first three are textbook adjunctions. $(-)^{**}$ on $\mathsf{Grp}$ isn't even functorial in an obvious way — and there's certainly no natural Hom-isomorphism with the identity.

q: For the free–forgetful adjunction $F\dashv U\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Grp}$, compute $|\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{Grp}}(F(\{a\}),\mathbb{Z}/5)|$.
text: For the free–forgetful adjunction $F\dashv U\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Grp}$, compute $|\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{Grp}}(F(\{a\}),\mathbb{Z}/5)|$. By the Hom-iso, $|\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{Grp}}(F(\{a\}),\mathbb{Z}/5)|=|\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{Set}}(\{a\},\mathbb{Z}/5)|=|\mathbb{Z}/5|=5$.

q: The triangle identities for an adjunction $F\dashv G$ with unit $\eta$ and counit $\varepsilon$ are:
text: The triangle identities for an adjunction $F\dashv G$ with unit $\eta$ and counit $\varepsilon$ are: The triangles say 'whisker on one side, then whisker back, and you're home'. Neither $\eta$ nor $\varepsilon$ need be an iso — e.g. $F\dashv U$ for free groups has non-iso $\eta$ and non-iso $\varepsilon$.

q: $F\dashv G$ and $F$ preserves all colimits that exist. Which is the correct slogan?
text: $F\dashv G$ and $F$ preserves all colimits that exist. Which is the correct slogan? $\mathrm{Hom}(-,X)$ turns colimits into limits, so $\mathrm{Hom}(F-,-)\cong\mathrm{Hom}(-,G-)$ combined with Yoneda forces $F$ to preserve colimits and $G$ to preserve limits. Left adjoint to $G$ means 'left side of a hom-iso' — which side of $\mathrm{Hom}$ is covariant in its second argument and commutes with limits?

q: Let $D\dashv U$ where $D\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Top}$ gives the discrete topology and $U$ is underlying set. For $X=\{0,1\}$ and $Y$ a three-point space with the indiscrete topology, compute $|\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{Top}}(D(X),Y)|$.
text: Let $D\dashv U$ where $D\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Top}$ gives the discrete topology and $U$ is underlying set. For $X=\{0,1\}$ and $Y$ a three-point space with the indiscrete topology, compute $|\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{Top}}(D(X),Y)|$. By the adjunction, $\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{Top}}(D(X),Y)\cong\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{Set}}(X,U(Y))$ — every function $X\to U(Y)$ is automatically continuous out of a discrete domain. So the count is $|U(Y)|^{|X|}=3^2=9$.

q: A monad on $\mathcal{C}$ is a triple $(T,\mu,\eta)$ where $T\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{C}$ is a functor, $\mu\colon T^2\Rightarrow T$ and $\eta\colon\mathrm{id}\Rightarrow T$ are natural transformations. Which identities must hold?
text: A monad on $\mathcal{C}$ is a triple $(T,\mu,\eta)$ where $T\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{C}$ is a functor, $\mu\colon T^2\Rightarrow T$ and $\eta\colon\mathrm{id}\Rightarrow T$ are natural transformations. Which identities must hold? Associativity of multiplication and the left/right unit laws — the 'monoid-in-endofunctors' axioms.

q: The $\mathrm{Maybe}$ monad on $\mathsf{Set}$ sends $X\mapsto X\sqcup\{\bot\}$. The Kleisli composition $g\circ_K f$ of $f\colon X\to\mathrm{Maybe}\,Y$ and $g\colon Y\to\mathrm{Maybe}\,Z$ is:
text: The $\mathrm{Maybe}$ monad on $\mathsf{Set}$ sends $X\mapsto X\sqcup\{\bot\}$. The Kleisli composition $g\circ_K f$ of $f\colon X\to\mathrm{Maybe}\,Y$ and $g\colon Y\to\mathrm{Maybe}\,Z$ is: Kleisli composition is 'bind then map': unwrap the option from $f$, run $g$ on success, propagate $\bot$ on failure. This is exactly $\mu_Z\circ\mathrm{Maybe}(g)\circ f$.

q: Every adjunction $F\dashv U$ induces a monad $T=UF$ on the source category. For $F\dashv U\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Mon}$ (free monoid / underlying set), what is $|T(\{a\})|$ restricted to words of length $\le 3$ (including the empty word)?
text: Every adjunction $F\dashv U$ induces a monad $T=UF$ on the source category. For $F\dashv U\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Mon}$ (free monoid / underlying set), what is $|T(\{a\})|$ restricted to words of length $\le 3$ (including the empty word)? Words in $\{a\}$ of length $0,1,2,3$ are $\varepsilon, a, aa, aaa$ — exactly $4$ elements.

q: A $T$-algebra for a monad $T$ is a pair $(A,\alpha)$ with $\alpha\colon T(A)\to A$ satisfying unit and associativity axioms. For the free-monoid monad $T\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Set}$, a $T$-algebra is:
text: A $T$-algebra for a monad $T$ is a pair $(A,\alpha)$ with $\alpha\colon T(A)\to A$ satisfying unit and associativity axioms. For the free-monoid monad $T\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Set}$, a $T$-algebra is: The structure map $\alpha\colon T(A)\to A$ assigns a product to every finite word; the axioms force this to be associative and unital — i.e. make $A$ a monoid.
