q: Krull dimension of $k[x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4]$?
text: Krull dimension of $k[x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4]$? $\dim k[x_1,\dots,x_n]=n$; chain $(0)\subsetneq(x_1)\subsetneq\cdots\subsetneq(x_1,\dots,x_4)$.

q: $\dim k[x,y]/(xy)$?
text: $\dim k[x,y]/(xy)$? Two coordinate axes; longest chain is (generic of one axis) $\subsetneq$ (origin): dim $1$.

q: $\dim\mathbb{Z}[x]$?
text: $\dim\mathbb{Z}[x]$? $\dim A[x]=\dim A+1$ for Noetherian $A$. $\dim\mathbb{Z}=1$, so $\dim\mathbb{Z}[x]=2$. Chain: $(0)\subsetneq(p)\subsetneq(p,x)$.

q: Compute $\dim k[x,y,z]/(xz-y^2, yz-x^3, z^2-x^2 y)$ (twisted cubic).
text: Compute $\dim k[x,y,z]/(xz-y^2, yz-x^3, z^2-x^2 y)$ (twisted cubic). Twisted cubic is a $1$-dim curve $t\mapsto(t,t^2,t^3)$.

q: Which is a Noetherian but non-catenary ring?
text: Which is a Noetherian but non-catenary ring? Nagata built a Noetherian domain with chains of two different maximal lengths. Polynomial rings, fields, and $\mathbb{Z}$ are universally catenary.

q: Compute $\dim \mathbb{R}[x,y,z]/(x^2+y^2+z^2-1)$.
text: Compute $\dim \mathbb{R}[x,y,z]/(x^2+y^2+z^2-1)$. $2$-sphere is $2$-dim irreducible variety; $\dim$ = tr.deg = $2$.

q: The transcendence degree $\mathrm{tr.deg}_k(k(x,y))$ of the field of rational functions in two variables is:
text: The transcendence degree $\mathrm{tr.deg}_k(k(x,y))$ of the field of rational functions in two variables is: $\{x,y\}$ is algebraically independent over $k$, so $\mathrm{tr.deg}=2$. This is the same as the Krull dimension of $k[x,y]$.

q: Let $B = k[x,y,z]/(z - xy)$. Compute $\mathrm{tr.deg}_k\,\mathrm{Frac}(B)$.
text: Let $B = k[x,y,z]/(z - xy)$. Compute $\mathrm{tr.deg}_k\,\mathrm{Frac}(B)$. The relation $z=xy$ makes $z$ algebraically dependent on $x,y$. $\mathrm{Frac}(B)=k(x,y)$, with tr.deg $2$. (Matches $\dim B = 2$.)

q: For a finitely generated integral domain $B$ over an algebraically closed field $k$, which theorem asserts $\dim B = \mathrm{tr.deg}_k\,\mathrm{Frac}(B)$?
text: For a finitely generated integral domain $B$ over an algebraically closed field $k$, which theorem asserts $\dim B = \mathrm{tr.deg}_k\,\mathrm{Frac}(B)$? Noether normalization gives a finite map $B\twoheadleftarrow k[y_1,\dots,y_d]$ with $d=\mathrm{tr.deg}$; going-up preserves dimension; $\dim k[y_1,\dots,y_d]=d$.

q: Let $f\colon X\to Y$ be a dominant morphism of irreducible varieties over $\overline k$ with $\dim X > \dim Y$. Pick the correct generic-fiber statement.
text: Let $f\colon X\to Y$ be a dominant morphism of irreducible varieties over $\overline k$ with $\dim X > \dim Y$. Pick the correct generic-fiber statement. The dimension-of-fiber theorem: generic fiber has dim $=\dim X - \dim Y$; special fibers can only jump up. E.g. blow-up has generic fiber a point but exceptional fiber a curve. Upper-semicontinuity of fiber dimension.

q: Compute $\mathrm{tr.deg}_{\mathbb{Q}}(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}, \pi, e))$ assuming $\pi, e$ are algebraically independent over $\mathbb{Q}$ (this is open, but assume it).
text: Compute $\mathrm{tr.deg}_{\mathbb{Q}}(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}, \pi, e))$ assuming $\pi, e$ are algebraically independent over $\mathbb{Q}$ (this is open, but assume it). $\sqrt{2}$ is algebraic over $\mathbb{Q}$ (contributes $0$ to tr.deg). $\pi, e$ assumed algebraically independent contribute $2$. Total: $2$.

q: For a purely transcendental extension $k\subset k(t_1,\dots,t_n)$, which is true?
text: For a purely transcendental extension $k\subset k(t_1,\dots,t_n)$, which is true? Purely transcendental means a transcendence basis generates the extension. tr.deg = $n$; the field is $k(t_1,\dots,t_n) = \mathrm{Frac}(k[t_1,\dots,t_n])$.

title: Chain complexes and homology
blurb: Complexes encode algebraic data with differentials, and homology measures failure of exactness.

title: Exact sequences
blurb: Exactness at B means im f = ker g; short exact sequences 0→A→B→C→0 are the atomic building blocks of homological algebra.

title: The snake lemma
blurb: A commutative diagram with exact rows yields a connecting map δ:ker γ → coker α and a six-term exact sequence linking kernels to cokernels.

title: The five lemma
blurb: In a commutative ladder of exact rows, if the four outer vertical maps are isomorphisms then so is the middle one — the workhorse diagram-chase for recognizing isomorphisms.

title: Long exact sequence in homology
blurb: A short exact sequence of chain complexes induces a long exact sequence in homology, built by iterating the snake-lemma connecting map in each degree.

title: Projective modules
blurb: A module P is projective iff every surjection onto P splits; equivalently P is a direct summand of a free module and makes Hom(P,−) exact.

title: Injective modules
blurb: Dual to projective: I is injective iff every injection of I extends; Baer's criterion and divisibility characterize injectives over ℤ.

title: Flat modules
blurb: M is flat iff −⊗M preserves exactness; localizations are flat, projectives are flat, and over ℤ flatness coincides with torsion-freeness.

title: Derived functors and Ext/Tor
blurb: Projective or injective resolutions define Ext and Tor as computable invariants; the Ext/Tor tables pin down extensions and torsion.

title: Resolutions: projective, injective, flat
blurb: Every module has projective and injective resolutions; their lengths measure the global dimension of the ring.

title: Abelian categories and triangulated preview
blurb: Abelian categories are the native habitat of homological algebra; triangulated categories encode long exact sequences as distinguished triangles.

title: Spectral sequences
blurb: Spectral sequences stage difficult computations into successive approximation pages.

q: A chain complex $(C_\bullet, \partial)$ is a sequence of abelian groups with maps $\partial_n : C_n \to C_{n-1}$ satisfying:
text: A chain complex $(C_\bullet, \partial)$ is a sequence of abelian groups with maps $\partial_n : C_n \to C_{n-1}$ satisfying: The defining condition of a chain complex is $\partial^2=0$, i.e. $\partial_{n-1}\circ\partial_n=0$. This guarantees $\operatorname{im}\partial_{n+1}\subseteq \ker\partial_n$, so $H_n=\ker\partial_n/\operatorname{im}\partial_{n+1}$ is well-defined.

q: Let $C_\bullet$ be the simplicial chain complex of the standard triangle (boundary of a $2$-simplex) with $C_0=\mathbb{Q}^3,\ C_1=\mathbb{Q}^3$. What is $\dim_\mathbb{Q} H_1$?
text: Let $C_\bullet$ be the simplicial chain complex of the standard triangle (boundary of a $2$-simplex) with $C_0=\mathbb{Q}^3,\ C_1=\mathbb{Q}^3$. What is $\dim_\mathbb{Q} H_1$? The triangle is homotopy equivalent to $S^1$, so $H_0=\mathbb{Q}$ and $H_1=\mathbb{Q}$. Its one independent $1$-cycle is the loop around the triangle.

q: For the torus $T^2$ with rational Betti numbers $(b_0,b_1,b_2)=(1,2,1)$, compute the Euler characteristic $\chi(T^2)=b_0-b_1+b_2$.
text: For the torus $T^2$ with rational Betti numbers $(b_0,b_1,b_2)=(1,2,1)$, compute the Euler characteristic $\chi(T^2)=b_0-b_1+b_2$. $\chi(T^2)=1-2+1=0$. Every closed orientable surface of genus $g$ has $\chi=2-2g$; here $g=1$.

q: Which of the following is a valid chain complex over $\mathbb{Z}$?
text:
Which of the following is a valid chain complex over $\mathbb{Z}$? We need $\partial_{n-1}\circ\partial_n=0$. For option B: $0\circ 2=0$. For A: $3\circ 2=6
e 0$. For C: $\mathrm{id}\circ\mathrm{id}=\mathrm{id}
e 0$. For D: $2\circ 3=6
e 0$.

q: Let $C_\bullet$ be the chain complex $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 3}\mathbb{Z}\to 0$ concentrated in degrees $1$ and $0$. Compute $|H_0(C_\bullet)|$ (the order of $H_0$).
text: Let $C_\bullet$ be the chain complex $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 3}\mathbb{Z}\to 0$ concentrated in degrees $1$ and $0$. Compute $|H_0(C_\bullet)|$ (the order of $H_0$). $H_0=\ker(C_{-1}\to C_{-2})/\operatorname{im}(\partial_1)=\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}$, which has order $3$.

q: A chain map $f_\bullet:C_\bullet\to D_\bullet$ consists of maps $f_n:C_n\to D_n$ satisfying which condition?
text: A chain map $f_\bullet:C_\bullet\to D_\bullet$ consists of maps $f_n:C_n\to D_n$ satisfying which condition? A chain map must commute with the boundary maps: $\partial^D\circ f=f\circ\partial^C$. This ensures $f$ descends to a well-defined map on homology groups.

q: A sequence $A \xrightarrow{f} B \xrightarrow{g} C$ is exact at $B$ when:
text: A sequence $A \xrightarrow{f} B \xrightarrow{g} C$ is exact at $B$ when: Exactness at $B$ is the equality $\operatorname{im} f = \ker g$. The weaker condition $g\circ f=0$ only gives $\operatorname{im} f\subseteq\ker g$; the reverse inclusion is the substantive content.

q: For $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot n}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/n\to 0$, which summary is correct?
text: For $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot n}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/n\to 0$, which summary is correct? $\operatorname{im}(\cdot n)=n\mathbb{Z}=\ker(\text{mod }n)$, so it is short exact. A splitting would require a homomorphism $\mathbb{Z}/n\to\mathbb{Z}$, and there is none for $n\ge 2$ (any image is torsion-free). So it splits iff $n=1$, where the sequence is trivial.

q: For a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ of finite abelian groups with $|A|=6$ and $|C|=10$, compute $|B|$.
text: For a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ of finite abelian groups with $|A|=6$ and $|C|=10$, compute $|B|$. Orders multiply across a short exact sequence: $|B|=|A|\cdot|C|=6\cdot 10=60$. This is the group-theoretic analogue of $\dim B = \dim A + \dim C$ for vector spaces.

q: Which of the following is a counterexample showing that a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ need not split?
text: Which of the following is a counterexample showing that a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ need not split? $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 2}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$ is short exact but does not split: a splitting would require a homomorphism $\mathbb{Z}/2\to\mathbb{Z}$, which is impossible since $\mathbb{Z}$ is torsion-free.

q: In the long exact sequence arising from $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$, suppose $H_1(B)=0$, $H_1(C)=\mathbb{Z}^2$, $H_0(A)=\mathbb{Z}^3$, and the connecting map $\partial:H_1(C)\to H_0(A)$ is injective. What is $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}}\operatorname{coker}(\partial)$?
text: In the long exact sequence arising from $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$, suppose $H_1(B)=0$, $H_1(C)=\mathbb{Z}^2$, $H_0(A)=\mathbb{Z}^3$, and the connecting map $\partial:H_1(C)\to H_0(A)$ is injective. What is $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}}\operatorname{coker}(\partial)$? $\partial:\mathbb{Z}^2\hookrightarrow\mathbb{Z}^3$ is injective (since $H_1(B)=0$ forces exactness). Its cokernel has rank $3-2=1$ over $\mathbb{Q}$.

q: The five-lemma: in a commutative diagram with exact rows, if the four outer vertical maps are isomorphisms, the middle map is:
text: The five-lemma: in a commutative diagram with exact rows, if the four outer vertical maps are isomorphisms, the middle map is: The five-lemma: with exact rows and the four outer verticals being isomorphisms, a standard diagram chase (injectivity first, then surjectivity) proves the middle map is also an isomorphism. Two diagram chases — injectivity then surjectivity — both ride on the outer isomorphisms.

q: Given the snake-lemma diagram with exact rows and vertical maps $\alpha,\beta,\gamma$, the connecting map $\delta$ has which source and target?
text: Given the snake-lemma diagram with exact rows and vertical maps $\alpha,\beta,\gamma$, the connecting map $\delta$ has which source and target? The snake connects $\ker\gamma$ (on the right of the top row) to $\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ (on the left of the bottom row) via the recipe lift $\to$ $\beta$ $\to$ pull back along $i'$.

q: Which six-term exact sequence does the snake lemma produce?
text: Which six-term exact sequence does the snake lemma produce? Kernels first (reading left-to-right), then $\delta$, then cokernels (again left-to-right). The 0 on the left encodes 'left-most kernel arrow is injective' and the 0 on the right encodes 'right-most cokernel arrow is surjective'.

q: In the widget's example ($\alpha=0, \beta=\cdot 2, \gamma=0$ on $\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/4,\mathbb{Z}/2$), what is $\delta(1)\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha=\mathbb{Z}/2$?
text: In the widget's example ($\alpha=0, \beta=\cdot 2, \gamma=0$ on $\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/4,\mathbb{Z}/2$), what is $\delta(1)\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha=\mathbb{Z}/2$? Lift $c=1$ to $b=1\in\mathbb{Z}/4$; $\beta(b)=2$; pull back along $i':a'\mapsto 2a'$ to get $a'=1\in\mathbb{Z}/2$. Since $\operatorname{im}\alpha=0$, $\delta(1)=1$.

q: In the snake-lemma diagram, which is the correct order of steps to construct $\delta(c)\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ from $c\in\ker\gamma$?
text: In the snake-lemma diagram, which is the correct order of steps to construct $\delta(c)\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ from $c\in\ker\gamma$? The snake recipe: (1) lift $c$ to some $b\in B$ using surjectivity from $j$, (2) compute $\beta(b)\in B'$, (3) since $j'(\beta(b))=\gamma(j(b))=\gamma(c)=0$, exactness gives $\beta(b)=i'(a')$ for a unique $a'\in A'$, (4) $\delta(c)=[a']\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha$. Independence of lift follows from $\partial^2=0$.

q: Which condition on $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ forces $\delta=0$ identically?
text: Which condition on $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ forces $\delta=0$ identically? If $\alpha$ is surjective then $\operatorname{coker}\alpha=0$, so $\delta$ must be the zero map. Alternatively, if $\gamma$ is injective then $\ker\gamma=0$ and $\delta$ has trivial domain.

q: In a snake-lemma setup with $\alpha=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$, $\beta=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$, $\gamma=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$ and exact rows being the identity sequence, what is $|\ker\delta|$?
text: In a snake-lemma setup with $\alpha=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$, $\beta=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$, $\gamma=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$ and exact rows being the identity sequence, what is $|\ker\delta|$? With all vertical maps being identities, $\ker\gamma=0$ (since $\gamma=\mathrm{id}$, every element is in the image, kernel is trivial — but actually $\ker(\mathrm{id})=0$). Wait: $\ker\delta$ is the kernel of $\delta:\ker\gamma\to\operatorname{coker}\alpha$. Since $\ker\gamma=0$ (identity is injective), the domain is $0$ and $\ker\delta=0$. But if rows are $0\to\mathbb{Z}/4\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id}}\mathbb{Z}/4\to 0$, then $\ker\gamma=\mathbb{Z}/4$, $\operatorname{coker}\alpha=0$, and $\delta=0$ with $\ker\delta=\mathbb{Z}/4$, so $|\ker\delta|=4$.

q: A student is building the connecting map $\delta:\ker\gamma\to\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ in the snake lemma. Exactly one step contains a logical flaw — find it.
text: A student is building the connecting map $\delta:\ker\gamma\to\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ in the snake lemma. Exactly one step contains a logical flaw — find it. Step 4 has the bug. Defining $\delta(c)=a'$ in $A'$ itself is not well-defined because the lift $b$ was a choice, and $a'$ depends on that choice by an element of $\operatorname{im}\alpha$ (as step 5 correctly notes). The fix is exactly what step 5 sets up: define $\delta(c)=[a']\in A'/\operatorname{im}\alpha=\operatorname{coker}\alpha$, which then is well-defined. Compare target spaces: $\delta$ must land in a quotient, not in $A'$ itself.

q: The five lemma concerns a commutative diagram with exact rows $A_1\to A_2\to A_3\to A_4\to A_5$ and $B_1\to B_2\to B_3\to B_4\to B_5$, with vertical maps $f_1,\dots,f_5$. What is its conclusion?
text: The five lemma concerns a commutative diagram with exact rows $A_1\to A_2\to A_3\to A_4\to A_5$ and $B_1\to B_2\to B_3\to B_4\to B_5$, with vertical maps $f_1,\dots,f_5$. What is its conclusion? The five lemma: outer four $f_1,f_2,f_4,f_5$ isomorphisms + exact rows $\Rightarrow$ middle $f_3$ is an isomorphism. Proof is the standard two-step diagram chase (injectivity, then surjectivity).

q: In the four lemma (one half of five), which hypotheses imply $f_3$ is surjective?
text: In the four lemma (one half of five), which hypotheses imply $f_3$ is surjective? The surjectivity half of the five lemma: $f_2$ surjective and $f_4$ injective force $f_3$ surjective. The injectivity half requires $f_2$ injective and $f_4$ surjective — dual conditions, split across the two halves.

q: In a ladder with exact rows, $f_1,f_2,f_4,f_5$ are isomorphisms and $\dim A_3=\dim B_3=7$ (vector spaces). What is the rank of $f_3$?
text: In a ladder with exact rows, $f_1,f_2,f_4,f_5$ are isomorphisms and $\dim A_3=\dim B_3=7$ (vector spaces). What is the rank of $f_3$? By the five lemma $f_3$ is an isomorphism, so its rank equals $\dim A_3=7$.

q: Injectivity half of the five lemma: assume rows exact, $f_1$ surjective, $f_2,f_4$ injective. Show $f_3$ is injective. Suppose $f_3(a_3)=0$. Pick the correct next step.
text: Injectivity half of the five lemma: assume rows exact, $f_1$ surjective, $f_2,f_4$ injective. Show $f_3$ is injective. Suppose $f_3(a_3)=0$. Pick the correct next step. Injectivity of $f_4$ gives $a_4=0$, i.e. $d^A_3(a_3)=0$, so $a_3\in\ker d^A_3=\operatorname{im}d^A_2$. Write $a_3=d^A_2(a_2)$. Continue the chase with $f_2(a_2)$ and use $f_1$ surjective to finish. You have $f_4(a_4)=0$; the injectivity of $f_4$ kills $a_4$, which unlocks exactness at $A_3$.

q: Which configuration is a counterexample showing the five lemma fails if $f_4$ is merely surjective (not injective), while $f_1,f_2,f_5$ remain isomorphisms?
text: Which configuration is a counterexample showing the five lemma fails if $f_4$ is merely surjective (not injective), while $f_1,f_2,f_5$ remain isomorphisms? The five lemma is sharp: weakening any of the four outer hypotheses can break the conclusion. One can construct a concrete diagram where $f_4$ is surjective (not injective), the other three are isomorphisms, yet $f_3$ fails injectivity because a kernel element of $f_4$ hides in $A_4$ but is not the image of anything in $A_3$. You need a map out of $A_4$ that kills a summand of $B_3$, letting $f_3$ miss torsion.

q: The five lemma in an abelian category is proved by:
text: The five lemma in an abelian category is proved by: In an abelian category one either uses element-free chases with generalized elements (morphisms out of a 'test object') or, via Freyd–Mitchell embedding, reduces to $\mathsf{Ab}$ where ordinary element chases work. Mitchell's theorem lets you run the same chase you'd do in $\mathsf{Ab}$.

q: A short exact sequence of chain complexes $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$ induces a long exact sequence whose connecting map $\partial$ has which degree?
text: A short exact sequence of chain complexes $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$ induces a long exact sequence whose connecting map $\partial$ has which degree? The snake lemma applied degree-by-degree produces $\partial:H_n(C)\to H_{n-1}(A)$, lowering homological degree by 1 (for a cohomologically indexed complex the analogous map raises degree by 1).

q: Use the Mayer–Vietoris long exact sequence and induction to compute $\dim_\mathbb{Q} H_n(S^n;\mathbb{Q})$ for $n\ge 1$.
text: Use the Mayer–Vietoris long exact sequence and induction to compute $\dim_\mathbb{Q} H_n(S^n;\mathbb{Q})$ for $n\ge 1$. Covering $S^n$ by two contractible hemispheres $U,V$ with $U\cap V\simeq S^{n-1}$, the MV sequence gives $H_n(S^n)\cong H_{n-1}(S^{n-1})$ for $n\ge 2$, and $H_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ directly. Inductively $\dim H_n(S^n;\mathbb{Q})=1$.

q: If $B_\bullet$ is acyclic ($H_n(B)=0$ for all $n$) in $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$, the long exact sequence collapses to:
text: If $B_\bullet$ is acyclic ($H_n(B)=0$ for all $n$) in $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$, the long exact sequence collapses to: With the middle terms $H_n(B)=H_{n-1}(B)=0$ the sequence $0\to H_n(C)\xrightarrow{\partial}H_{n-1}(A)\to 0$ forces $\partial$ to be an isomorphism. Acyclic complexes are 'invisible' except for the degree shift they impose.

q: The Mayer–Vietoris sequence for $X=A\cup B$ has connecting map $\partial:H_n(A\cup B)\to H_{n-1}(A\cap B)$. Which description of the connecting map is correct?
text: The Mayer–Vietoris sequence for $X=A\cup B$ has connecting map $\partial:H_n(A\cup B)\to H_{n-1}(A\cap B)$. Which description of the connecting map is correct? A cycle $z$ on $X$ can be written $z=a+b$ (with chains supported in $A$ and $B$ respectively). Then $\partial a = -\partial b$ is a cycle in $A\cap B$; its homology class is $\partial[z]$. This is the snake-lemma connecting map applied to the Mayer–Vietoris short exact sequence of chain complexes.

q: Using the long exact sequence of the pair $(D^2, S^1)$ and the fact that $D^2$ is contractible, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} H_1(D^2,S^1;\mathbb{Q})$.
text: Using the long exact sequence of the pair $(D^2, S^1)$ and the fact that $D^2$ is contractible, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} H_1(D^2,S^1;\mathbb{Q})$. The LES gives $H_1(D^2)\to H_1(D^2,S^1)\to H_0(S^1)\to H_0(D^2)$. Since $D^2$ is contractible, $H_1(D^2)=0$ and the map $H_0(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Q}\to H_0(D^2)\cong\mathbb{Q}$ is an isomorphism. So $H_1(D^2,S^1)\cong\mathbb{Q}$, dimension $1$. (By excision this equals $\tilde{H}_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Q}$.)

q: Which of the following is the correct proof-step ordering to show naturality of the connecting map $\partial$ in the long exact sequence?
text: Which of the following is the correct proof-step ordering to show naturality of the connecting map $\partial$ in the long exact sequence? Naturality is proved by chasing the snake-lemma diagram for the naturality square: a map of short exact sequences of chain complexes induces a map of long exact sequences, and the connecting homomorphisms commute with the induced maps because each step of the snake construction is natural.

q: Which of the following is equivalent to $P$ being projective as a module over a ring $R$?
text: Which of the following is equivalent to $P$ being projective as a module over a ring $R$? The characterizations: $P$ projective $\iff$ every surjection $M\twoheadrightarrow P$ splits $\iff$ $\operatorname{Hom}(P,-)$ is exact (both left and right) $\iff$ $P$ is a direct summand of a free module. Free is strictly stronger in general (though not over a PID).

q: Given $\varphi\colon M\twoheadrightarrow N$ and a map $f\colon P\to N$ with $P$ projective, the lifting property says:
text: Given $\varphi\colon M\twoheadrightarrow N$ and a map $f\colon P\to N$ with $P$ projective, the lifting property says: Projectivity: for any surjection $\varphi\colon M\twoheadrightarrow N$ and any $f\colon P\to N$, there exists a lift $\tilde f\colon P\to M$ with $\varphi\tilde f=f$. This is exactly the dual of the 'extension' property defining injectives.

q: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, which of the following is NOT projective?
text: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, which of the following is NOT projective? Over a PID, projective $\iff$ free. $\mathbb{Z}/2$ is not free (it has torsion), and the surjection $\mathbb{Z}\twoheadrightarrow\mathbb{Z}/2$ does not split (no homomorphism $\mathbb{Z}/2\to\mathbb{Z}$). Hence $\mathbb{Z}/2$ is not projective.

q: Over $R=\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(xy)$, the module $R/(x)\cong\mathbb{Z}[y]$ is projective but not free. Which invariant distinguishes projective-not-free?
text: Over $R=\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(xy)$, the module $R/(x)\cong\mathbb{Z}[y]$ is projective but not free. Which invariant distinguishes projective-not-free? Over rings with nontrivial Picard group (like $\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(xy)$, whose Spec has two components), projectives can be direct summands of free modules without themselves being free. The canonical example: rank-$1$ projectives $\leftrightarrow$ invertible modules $\leftrightarrow$ elements of $\mathrm{Pic}(R)$.

q: Let $P$ be a module. Which statement is FALSE?
text: Let $P$ be a module. Which statement is FALSE? Direct sums and summands of projectives are projective, and projectivity is equivalent to every SES ending in $P$ splitting. But infinite products of projectives need not be projective: e.g. $\prod_{\mathbb{N}}\mathbb{Z}$ is not a free (or projective) $\mathbb{Z}$-module (Baer–Specker group). Sums of projectives are fine; products are not.

q: How many isomorphism classes of projective modules of rank $1$ are there over a Dedekind domain with class number $h=3$?
text: How many isomorphism classes of projective modules of rank $1$ are there over a Dedekind domain with class number $h=3$? Over a Dedekind domain, rank-$1$ projectives are classified by the ideal class group $\mathrm{Cl}(R)$. If $h=|\mathrm{Cl}(R)|=3$, there are exactly $3$ isomorphism classes (the trivial class corresponds to the free module $R$; the other two are non-free rank-$1$ projectives).

q: $I$ is injective iff:
text: $I$ is injective iff: Injectivity: for every injection $A\hookrightarrow B$ and map $A\to I$, there is an extension $B\to I$. Equivalently $\operatorname{Hom}(-,I)$ is exact (both left and right). The 'every injection from $I$ splits' form is also an equivalent formulation.

q: Baer's criterion says: to verify $I$ is injective over a ring $R$, it suffices to check the extension property for:
text: Baer's criterion says: to verify $I$ is injective over a ring $R$, it suffices to check the extension property for: Baer's criterion: $I$ is injective $\iff$ every map from an ideal $\mathfrak{a}\subseteq R$ to $I$ extends to $R\to I$. This reduces an a priori enormous check (all injections of all modules) to a single, tractable one.

q: Which abelian group is NOT injective as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module?
text: Which abelian group is NOT injective as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module? Over $\mathbb{Z}$, injective $\iff$ divisible. $\mathbb{Q}$ and $\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ and Prüfer groups $\mathbb{Z}/p^\infty$ are divisible; $\mathbb{Z}/2$ is not ($2\cdot\mathbb{Z}/2 = 0\ne \mathbb{Z}/2$).

q: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, divisibility characterizes injectivity. Why does Baer's criterion immediately yield this?
text: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, divisibility characterizes injectivity. Why does Baer's criterion immediately yield this? Baer: extension to $\mathbb{Z}$ of a map $(n)\to I$ sending $n\mapsto a$ requires $x\in I$ with $nx = a$. That is exactly divisibility of $a\in I$ by $n$. Since every ideal of $\mathbb{Z}$ is some $(n)$, injectivity $\iff$ divisibility. Lift the generator of $(n)$ and see what's needed.

q: Which statement about injective hulls is correct?
text: Which statement about injective hulls is correct? Every module has an injective hull (or envelope) $M\hookrightarrow E(M)$: the minimal injective extension, unique up to (non-canonical) isomorphism. Over $\mathbb{Z}$, $E(\mathbb{Z}) = \mathbb{Q}$ and $E(\mathbb{Z}/p) = \mathbb{Z}/p^\infty$.

q: Let $I=\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ over $\mathbb{Z}$. How many extensions $\mathbb{Z}\to I$ exist of a given map $f\colon(2)\to I$ with $f(2)=1/4\in\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$?
text: Let $I=\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ over $\mathbb{Z}$. How many extensions $\mathbb{Z}\to I$ exist of a given map $f\colon(2)\to I$ with $f(2)=1/4\in\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$? We need $\tilde f(1)\in\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ with $2\tilde f(1)=1/4$, i.e. $\tilde f(1)\in\{1/8, 5/8\}\pmod{1}$. Both are valid extensions; there are exactly $2$.

q: $M$ is a flat $R$-module iff:
text: $M$ is a flat $R$-module iff: Flat $=$ tensoring with $M$ preserves exactness. Equivalently preserves injectivity (the right-exactness of $-\otimes M$ holds automatically; flatness is the extra condition that injective maps stay injective after tensoring).

q: Which implication chain is correct for modules over a ring?
text: Which implication chain is correct for modules over a ring? Free $\Rightarrow$ projective (trivial: free is projective); projective $\Rightarrow$ flat (direct summand of free, and free is obviously flat). None of the reverse implications hold over general rings.

q: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, which of the following is NOT flat?
text: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, which of the following is NOT flat? Over a PID, flat $\iff$ torsion-free. $\mathbb{Z}/2$ has torsion; concretely, tensoring $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 2}\mathbb{Z}$ with $\mathbb{Z}/2$ gives $\mathbb{Z}/2\xrightarrow{0}\mathbb{Z}/2$, which is not injective. So $\mathbb{Z}/2$ is not flat.

q: A key source of flat modules: localization. Which is the correct statement?
text: A key source of flat modules: localization. Which is the correct statement? Localization is the archetypal non-projective flat module: $S^{-1}R$ is always flat over $R$ (tensor product with localization preserves exact sequences), but usually not projective (e.g. $\mathbb{Q}$ over $\mathbb{Z}$ is flat, not projective). This is why flatness is the 'right' local notion.

q: Lazard's theorem characterizes flat modules as:
text: Lazard's theorem characterizes flat modules as: Lazard's theorem: $M$ is flat $\iff$ $M$ is a filtered colimit of finitely generated free modules. This is why flatness is 'locally free' in a categorical sense, and why tensor products preserve filtered colimits commute cleanly.

q: Let $M = \mathbb{Z}/6$ as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module. Tensor the SES $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 4}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/4\to 0$ with $M$. What is the order of the kernel of the induced map $\mathbb{Z}/6\to\mathbb{Z}/6$?
text: Let $M = \mathbb{Z}/6$ as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module. Tensor the SES $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 4}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/4\to 0$ with $M$. What is the order of the kernel of the induced map $\mathbb{Z}/6\to\mathbb{Z}/6$? After tensoring with $\mathbb{Z}/6$, the map $\cdot 4\colon\mathbb{Z}/6\to\mathbb{Z}/6$ has kernel $\{x\in\mathbb{Z}/6 : 4x\equiv 0\pmod 6\}=\{0,3\}$, of order $2$. This measures the failure of flatness of $\mathbb{Z}/6$: $\operatorname{Tor}_1^\mathbb{Z}(\mathbb{Z}/6,\mathbb{Z}/4)=\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(6,4)=\mathbb{Z}/2$.

q: To compute the right derived functors $R^iF(M)$ of a left exact functor $F$, the standard recipe is:
text: To compute the right derived functors $R^iF(M)$ of a left exact functor $F$, the standard recipe is: Right derived functors of a left exact $F$ are computed from an injective resolution $0\to M\to I^0\to I^1\to\cdots$, applying $F$ and taking cohomology of $F(I^\bullet)$. The answer is independent of the resolution.

q: Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_2} \operatorname{Ext}^1_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)$.
text: Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_2} \operatorname{Ext}^1_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)$. From $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$, applying $\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathbb{Z}/2)$ gives $\mathbb{Z}/2\xrightarrow{\times 2=0}\mathbb{Z}/2$, whose cokernel is $\mathbb{Z}/2$. So $\operatorname{Ext}^1_\mathbb{Z}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)\cong\mathbb{Z}/2$, dimension $1$ over $\mathbb{F}_2$.

q: What is $\operatorname{Tor}^\mathbb{Z}_1(\mathbb{Z}/6,\mathbb{Z}/4)$ as $\mathbb{Z}/d$? Give $d$.
text: What is $\operatorname{Tor}^\mathbb{Z}_1(\mathbb{Z}/6,\mathbb{Z}/4)$ as $\mathbb{Z}/d$? Give $d$. $\operatorname{Tor}^\mathbb{Z}_1(\mathbb{Z}/m,\mathbb{Z}/n)\cong\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)$. Here $\gcd(6,4)=2$.

q: To compute $\operatorname{Ext}^n_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/p,M)$ via a projective resolution of $\mathbb{Z}/p$, the correct resolution is:
text: To compute $\operatorname{Ext}^n_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/p,M)$ via a projective resolution of $\mathbb{Z}/p$, the correct resolution is: $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot p}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/p\to 0$ is a free resolution of $\mathbb{Z}/p$ of length $1$. Applying $\operatorname{Hom}(-,M)$ and taking cohomology gives $\operatorname{Ext}^0=M^p$ (fixed by $p$) and $\operatorname{Ext}^1=M/pM$; higher Ext groups vanish.

q: Compute $|\operatorname{Tor}^{\mathbb{Z}}_1(\mathbb{Z}/12,\mathbb{Z}/8)|$ (the order of the group).
text: Compute $|\operatorname{Tor}^{\mathbb{Z}}_1(\mathbb{Z}/12,\mathbb{Z}/8)|$ (the order of the group). $\operatorname{Tor}^\mathbb{Z}_1(\mathbb{Z}/m,\mathbb{Z}/n)\cong\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)$. Here $\gcd(12,8)=4$, so the group is $\mathbb{Z}/4$ with order $4$.

q: Which of the following functors is NOT right exact (and hence genuinely requires derived functors to fix)?
text: Which of the following functors is NOT right exact (and hence genuinely requires derived functors to fix)? $\operatorname{Hom}_R(M,-)$ is left exact but not right exact in general: $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$ applied with $M=\mathbb{Z}$ gives $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}$, which fails surjectivity. Its right derived functors are the $\operatorname{Ext}^n$ groups.

q: A projective resolution of a module $M$ is an exact sequence:
text: A projective resolution of a module $M$ is an exact sequence: Projective resolutions go from the left: $\cdots\to P_1\to P_0\twoheadrightarrow M\to 0$, exact at every spot. They are used to compute left-derived functors like $\operatorname{Tor}$ and $L_iF$ for right-exact $F$.

q: $\mathbb{Z}/6$ has what length-$1$ free resolution? Enter the single invariant factor appearing in the presentation matrix (a $1\times 1$ matrix).
text: $\mathbb{Z}/6$ has what length-$1$ free resolution? Enter the single invariant factor appearing in the presentation matrix (a $1\times 1$ matrix). The resolution is $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 6}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/6\to 0$. The presentation matrix is the $1\times 1$ matrix $[6]$.

q: What is the global dimension of the polynomial ring $k[x_1,\dots,x_n]$ over a field $k$?
text: What is the global dimension of the polynomial ring $k[x_1,\dots,x_n]$ over a field $k$? Hilbert's syzygy theorem: the global dimension of $k[x_1,\dots,x_n]$ is exactly $n$. Equivalently, every finitely generated module has a free resolution of length $\le n$, and there exist modules requiring length exactly $n$.

q: Over the ring $R=k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)$ (dual numbers), the module $k=R/(\varepsilon)$ has which minimal free resolution?
text: Over the ring $R=k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)$ (dual numbers), the module $k=R/(\varepsilon)$ has which minimal free resolution? $R=k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)$ has global dimension $\infty$: $k$ has an infinite $2$-periodic free resolution $\cdots\xrightarrow{\varepsilon}R\xrightarrow{\varepsilon}R\to k$. Consequently $\operatorname{Ext}^i_R(k,k)=k$ for all $i\ge 0$ (not eventually zero). This is a typical non-regular local ring.

q: Over a PID $R$, every finitely generated module has a free resolution of length at most what?
text: Over a PID $R$, every finitely generated module has a free resolution of length at most what? Over a PID, submodules of free modules are free (Smith normal form), so a finitely generated module $M$ has a resolution $0\to R^r\to R^s\to M\to 0$ of length $1$. Hence global dimension of a PID is $\le 1$.

q: Which statement about flat resolutions is correct?
text: Which statement about flat resolutions is correct? $\operatorname{Tor}_i(M,N)$ can be computed from a flat resolution of $M$ (since $-\otimes N$ is right-exact and flat modules give the acyclicity needed). $\operatorname{Ext}$ requires projective (or injective) resolutions — flat is not enough.

q: Which of the following is NOT required of an abelian category?
text: Which of the following is NOT required of an abelian category? Abelian categories need a zero object, kernels/cokernels, biproducts, and every mono/epi to be normal (a kernel/cokernel respectively). They do NOT need every object to be projective — in fact $\mathsf{Ab}$ is abelian and most objects there aren't projective.

q: The Freyd–Mitchell embedding theorem says:
text: The Freyd–Mitchell embedding theorem says: Freyd–Mitchell: any small abelian category $\mathcal{A}$ admits a full exact embedding $\mathcal{A}\hookrightarrow R\text{-Mod}$ for some ring $R$. The point: element-chases in $\mathsf{Ab}$ transfer to any abelian category.

q: In the derived category $D(\mathcal{A})$, a distinguished triangle $X\to Y\to Z\to X[1]$ replaces what in ordinary abelian categories?
text: In the derived category $D(\mathcal{A})$, a distinguished triangle $X\to Y\to Z\to X[1]$ replaces what in ordinary abelian categories? Short exact sequences in $\mathcal{A}$ lift to distinguished triangles in $D(\mathcal{A})$; the rotation/shift structure of triangles encodes long exact sequences of cohomology objects. This is the fundamental move toward derived categories.

q: Which example is an abelian category?
text: Which example is an abelian category? Sheaves of abelian groups form an abelian category (kernels are taken section-wise; cokernels require sheafification). The other examples fail: in non-abelian groups every subgroup isn't normal; in Top monos-that-aren't-kernels exist; fields lack zero objects / biproducts. Abelianness is inherited objectwise in a sheaf setting.

q: Which axiom of a triangulated category fails in a general abelian category $\mathcal{A}$, forcing us to pass to $D(\mathcal{A})$?
text: Which axiom of a triangulated category fails in a general abelian category $\mathcal{A}$, forcing us to pass to $D(\mathcal{A})$? An abelian category is not triangulated (except the trivial one): it lacks a natural shift and distinguished triangle structure. The chain complex category $\mathrm{Ch}(\mathcal{A})$, localized at quasi-isomorphisms, becomes the derived category $D(\mathcal{A})$ — now genuinely triangulated.

q: Which statement about $\operatorname{Ext}$ in the derived category is correct?
text: Which statement about $\operatorname{Ext}$ in the derived category is correct? The fundamental identification: $\operatorname{Hom}_{D(\mathcal{A})}(X,Y[i])\cong\operatorname{Ext}^i_\mathcal{A}(X,Y)$ for $X,Y\in\mathcal{A}$. This is why the derived category is 'the right place' to do homological algebra: all Ext/Tor appear as hom-sets after shifts. Derived category maps read off all Ext groups via shifts.

q: On the $E_r$ page of a (cohomological) spectral sequence, the differential $d_r$ has bidegree:
text: On the $E_r$ page of a (cohomological) spectral sequence, the differential $d_r$ has bidegree: In the standard cohomological convention $d_r:E_r^{p,q}\to E_r^{p+r,q-r+1}$, i.e. bidegree $(r,1-r)$. The next page is defined as $E_{r+1}=H(E_r,d_r)$.

q: A spectral sequence $E_2^{p,q}\Rightarrow H^{p+q}$ is said to degenerate at $E_2$ if:
text: A spectral sequence $E_2^{p,q}\Rightarrow H^{p+q}$ is said to degenerate at $E_2$ if: Degeneration at $E_2$ means $d_r=0$ for all $r\ge 2$, so $E_\infty=E_2$. The abutment $H^n$ is then the associated graded of some filtration with graded pieces $E_2^{p,n-p}$ — you still have an extension problem, but the entries are pinned down.

q: Consider the double complex $C^{0,0}=C^{1,0}=C^{0,1}=C^{1,1}=\mathbb{Z}$, with horizontal maps multiplication by $2$ and vertical maps multiplication by $3$. Taking horizontal homology first, what is the rank of $E_1^{0,0}$ as an abelian group?
text: Consider the double complex $C^{0,0}=C^{1,0}=C^{0,1}=C^{1,1}=\mathbb{Z}$, with horizontal maps multiplication by $2$ and vertical maps multiplication by $3$. Taking horizontal homology first, what is the rank of $E_1^{0,0}$ as an abelian group? The horizontal map $\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}$ is injective, so its kernel is $0$. Thus $E_1^{0,0}=\ker(2)=0$, which has rank $0$ (and is in fact the zero group).

q: The Serre spectral sequence for a fibration $F\to E\to B$ has $E_2$-page $E_2^{p,q}=H^p(B;H^q(F))$. If $F=S^1$ and $B=S^2$, which groups are the only potentially nonzero entries on $E_2$?
text: The Serre spectral sequence for a fibration $F\to E\to B$ has $E_2$-page $E_2^{p,q}=H^p(B;H^q(F))$. If $F=S^1$ and $B=S^2$, which groups are the only potentially nonzero entries on $E_2$? $H^p(S^2)$ is $\mathbb{Z}$ for $p=0,2$ and $0$ otherwise. $H^q(S^1)$ is $\mathbb{Z}$ for $q=0,1$ and $0$ otherwise. So the only nonzero corners of the $E_2$ page are $(p,q)\in\{(0,0),(2,0),(0,1),(2,1)\}$.

q: In the Serre spectral sequence for $S^1\to E\to S^2$ converging to $H^*(E)$, the only potentially nontrivial differential on the $E_2$ page is $d_2:E_2^{0,1}\to E_2^{2,0}$. If $E=S^3$ (Hopf fibration), this map is:
text: In the Serre spectral sequence for $S^1\to E\to S^2$ converging to $H^*(E)$, the only potentially nontrivial differential on the $E_2$ page is $d_2:E_2^{0,1}\to E_2^{2,0}$. If $E=S^3$ (Hopf fibration), this map is: For the Hopf fibration $S^1\to S^3\to S^2$, the spectral sequence must give $H^*(S^3)$: $\mathbb{Z}$ in degrees $0$ and $3$. To kill $E_\infty^{0,1}$ and $E_\infty^{2,0}$ we need $d_2:E_2^{0,1}\cong\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\sim}E_2^{2,0}\cong\mathbb{Z}$ to be an isomorphism.

q: For the Leray–Hirsch spectral sequence of the trivial fibration $E=F\times B$ with $F=S^2,B=S^2$, the spectral sequence degenerates at $E_2$. Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} H^2(E;\mathbb{Q})$.
text: For the Leray–Hirsch spectral sequence of the trivial fibration $E=F\times B$ with $F=S^2,B=S^2$, the spectral sequence degenerates at $E_2$. Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} H^2(E;\mathbb{Q})$. Degeneration gives $H^n(E)\cong\bigoplus_{p+q=n}H^p(B)\otimes H^q(F)$. For $n=2$: $(p,q)=(2,0)$ contributes $H^2(S^2)\otimes H^0(S^2)=\mathbb{Q}$, and $(0,2)$ contributes $H^0(S^2)\otimes H^2(S^2)=\mathbb{Q}$. Total $\dim=2$.

title: What is a topos?
blurb: An elementary topos is a finitely complete cartesian closed category with a subobject classifier $\Omega$. The three pieces — limits, exponentials, $\Omega$ — together let a category function as a universe of generalized sets in which set-theoretic constructions and intuitionist logic make sense.

title: The subobject classifier $\Omega$
blurb: The subobject classifier $\Omega$ is an object equipped with a map $\mathrm{true}\colon 1 \to \Omega$ such that every monomorphism $S \hookrightarrow X$ is the pullback of $\mathrm{true}$ along a unique characteristic map $\chi_S\colon X \to \Omega$. In $\mathbf{Set}$, $\Omega = \{\bot, \top\}$ and $\chi_S$ is the indicator function.

title: Characteristic maps as truth values
blurb: Every subobject of $X$ is named by exactly one map $X \to \Omega$, so $\Omega$ doubles as the object of "truth values" of the topos. Logical operations on subobjects (intersection, union, implication) become operations on $\Omega$ that the topos sees natively.

title: Power objects $P(A)$
blurb: The power object $P(A) = \Omega^A$ represents subobjects of $A$ in the same sense $\mathcal{P}(A)$ does in $\mathbf{Set}$: maps $X \to P(A)$ correspond to subobjects of $X \times A$. Power objects make every topos a model of the basic comprehension principle.

title: Presheaf toposes $\hat{C} = [C^{\mathrm{op}}, \mathbf{Set}]$
blurb: For any small category $C$, the functor category $\hat{C}$ is a topos. Limits and exponentials are computed pointwise; $\Omega(c)$ classifies sieves on $c$. Presheaf toposes are the source of most topos-theoretic intuition outside $\mathbf{Set}$.

title: The topos of $G$-sets
blurb: For a group $G$, the category of left $G$-sets is a topos: it's the presheaf topos on $\mathbf{B}G$ (the one-object groupoid). Its subobject classifier is the lattice of subgroups, generalizing $\{\bot,\top\}$ in $\mathbf{Set}$.

title: Geometric morphisms
blurb: A geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ is an adjoint pair $f^* \dashv f_*$ with $f^*$ left exact. They are the natural notion of "map between toposes," generalizing continuous maps between spaces and ring homomorphisms in the algebraic-geometric direction.

q: Select all categories that are elementary toposes.
text: Select all categories that are elementary toposes. $\mathbf{Set}$ is the prototypical topos. $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set} = [\mathbf{B}G^{\mathrm{op}}, \mathbf{Set}]$ is a presheaf topos. Any presheaf category is a topos. $\mathbf{Top}$ fails cartesian closure (function spaces aren't always topologisable). $\mathbf{Ab}$ has no subobject classifier (a non-trivial $\Omega$ would have to be additive, forcing collapse). A topos requires finite limits, cartesian closure, and a subobject classifier. Which fail in $\mathbf{Top}$ and $\mathbf{Ab}$?

q: Which of the following is NOT one of the three defining axioms of an elementary topos?
text: Which of the following is NOT one of the three defining axioms of an elementary topos? Elementary toposes need only finite limits, not arbitrary colimits. Grothendieck toposes are additionally cocomplete and have a small generator, but elementary toposes are weaker. Compare 'elementary topos' (Lawvere–Tierney) vs 'Grothendieck topos'.

q: By the Mikkelsen / Paré–Tierney theorem, an elementary topos can equivalently be defined as a finitely complete category with which extra structure?
text: By the Mikkelsen / Paré–Tierney theorem, an elementary topos can equivalently be defined as a finitely complete category with which extra structure? A finitely complete category with power objects automatically has exponentials and a subobject classifier $\Omega = P(1)$, hence is a topos. This is the second equivalent definition. The defining feature 'beyond limits' that recovers exponentials and $\Omega$.

q: Match each topos to its subobject classifier $\Omega$.
text: Match each topos to its subobject classifier $\Omega$. $\mathbf{Set}$ has $\Omega = \{\bot, \top\}$ (classical truth values). Presheaf toposes have $\Omega(c) = \mathrm{Sieves}(c)$. Sheaves on a topological space $X$ have $\Omega(U) = $ open subsets of $U$. The topos $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set}$ is a presheaf topos on $\mathbf{B}G$ and its $\Omega$ unwinds to subgroups of $G$ with conjugation action. Each generalises 'truth values'. In a presheaf topos a 'truth value at $c$' is a sieve.

q: Which property uniquely characterises $\Omega$ up to canonical isomorphism in $\mathcal{E}$?
text: Which property uniquely characterises $\Omega$ up to canonical isomorphism in $\mathcal{E}$? $\Omega$ represents $\mathrm{Sub}(-)$: there is a natural bijection $\mathrm{Sub}(X) \cong \mathrm{Hom}(X, \Omega)$. By Yoneda this determines $\Omega$ up to canonical iso. Counter-examples: in non-classical toposes $\Omega$ may have more than two global sections (e.g. presheaf toposes), so option C fails. Yoneda: a representable functor determines its representing object up to unique iso.

q: In the presheaf topos $\hat{C}$ where $C = \{a \le b\}$ is the walking arrow (the two-element chain), how many global sections does $\Omega$ have, i.e. how many maps $1 \to \Omega$?
text: In the presheaf topos $\hat{C}$ where $C = \{a \le b\}$ is the walking arrow (the two-element chain), how many global sections does $\Omega$ have, i.e. how many maps $1 \to \Omega$? Global sections of $\Omega$ in $\hat{C}$ correspond to subobjects of the terminal presheaf $1$, which are sieves on the maximal element compatible with restrictions. For the walking arrow these are: $\emptyset$, $\{a\}$ (down-closed since $a \le b$ is the only non-trivial inclusion and $\{a\}$ is closed under going down), and the whole $\{a, b\}$. So 3 — the topos is 3-valued. The global sections of $\Omega$ are the 'truth values' of the topos. For a poset, count the down-sets.

q: Why does $\mathbf{Ab}$ (the category of abelian groups) fail to have a subobject classifier?
text: Why does $\mathbf{Ab}$ (the category of abelian groups) fail to have a subobject classifier? Subobjects in $\mathbf{Ab}$ are kernels, named by quotient maps $X \to X/S$ — and the codomain depends on $S$, so they cannot all be classified by a single $X \to \Omega$. Concretely, any candidate $\Omega$ in $\mathbf{Ab}$ would be an abelian group, with maps to it given by abelian group homomorphisms, which is far too restrictive. Option C is partially right but misstates the symptom; D is the cleanest articulation. Think about how subobjects in $\mathbf{Ab}$ are usually 'named.'

q: Let $C = \{a, b\}$ be the discrete category with two objects (no non-identity morphisms). What is $\Omega$ in $\hat{C} = \mathbf{Set}^C$?
text: Let $C = \{a, b\}$ be the discrete category with two objects (no non-identity morphisms). What is $\Omega$ in $\hat{C} = \mathbf{Set}^C$? Sieves on $a$ in a discrete category are subsets of the empty set of non-identity arrows into $a$, plus the choice of including or excluding the identity — so $\Omega(a) = \{\emptyset, \{\mathrm{id}_a\}\} = \{\bot,\top\}$. Same for $b$. The topos $\mathbf{Set}^{\{a,b\}} = \mathbf{Set} \times \mathbf{Set}$ has $\Omega = \{\bot,\top\}\times\{\bot,\top\}$, with 4 global sections. A discrete category with two objects gives a product topos $\mathbf{Set} \times \mathbf{Set}$.

q: In $\mathbf{Set}$, let $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $S = \{2, 4\}$. The characteristic map $\chi_S\colon X \to \Omega = \{\bot, \top\}$ sends:
text: In $\mathbf{Set}$, let $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $S = \{2, 4\}$. The characteristic map $\chi_S\colon X \to \Omega = \{\bot, \top\}$ sends: The characteristic map of $S \subseteq X$ sends $x \in S$ to $\top$ and $x \notin S$ to $\bot$ — the indicator function. Here $S = \{2, 4\}$ so $\chi_S(2) = \chi_S(4) = \top$ and $\chi_S(1) = \chi_S(3) = \bot$. In $\mathbf{Set}$, $\chi_S$ is the indicator function of $S$.

q: In a topos, the internal conjunction $\wedge\colon \Omega \times \Omega \to \Omega$ is defined as the characteristic map of which subobject of $\Omega \times \Omega$?
text: In a topos, the internal conjunction $\wedge\colon \Omega \times \Omega \to \Omega$ is defined as the characteristic map of which subobject of $\Omega \times \Omega$? $\wedge$ should classify pairs $(p, q)$ with $p = q = \top$. That subobject of $\Omega \times \Omega$ is exactly $\langle \mathrm{true},\mathrm{true}\rangle\colon 1 \hookrightarrow \Omega \times \Omega$. Its characteristic map is $\wedge$. $p \wedge q = \top$ iff both $p$ and $q$ are $\top$. Which subobject of $\Omega \times \Omega$ does that pick out?

q: Order the following steps in the construction of internal conjunction $\wedge\colon \Omega \times \Omega \to \Omega$.
text: Order the following steps in the construction of internal conjunction $\wedge\colon \Omega \times \Omega \to \Omega$. First identify the subobject $\{(\top,\top)\}$ of $\Omega \times \Omega$, then invoke the universal property of $\Omega$ to get its unique classifying map, verify it does what it should via pullback, and name it $\wedge$. Identify the subobject first, then apply the universal property.

q: In $\mathbf{Set}$, let $A = \{a, b, c, d\}$. Compute $|P(A)|$.
text: In $\mathbf{Set}$, let $A = \{a, b, c, d\}$. Compute $|P(A)|$. $P(A) = \Omega^A$ in $\mathbf{Set}$ is the set of functions $A \to \{\bot, \top\}$, i.e. the power set $\mathcal{P}(A)$. $|P(A)| = 2^{|A|} = 2^4 = 16$. $|P(A)| = 2^{|A|}$ in $\mathbf{Set}$.

q: The bijection $\mathrm{Hom}(X \times A, \Omega) \cong \mathrm{Hom}(X, P(A))$ comes from which categorical principle?
text: The bijection $\mathrm{Hom}(X \times A, \Omega) \cong \mathrm{Hom}(X, P(A))$ comes from which categorical principle? Cartesian closure provides $\mathrm{Hom}(X \times A, B) \cong \mathrm{Hom}(X, B^A)$ for every $B$. Plugging $B = \Omega$ gives the displayed bijection with $P(A) = \Omega^A$. It's currying — the right-adjoint of $(-) \times A$.

q: What is the universal property of the membership relation $\in_A \hookrightarrow A \times P(A)$?
text: What is the universal property of the membership relation $\in_A \hookrightarrow A \times P(A)$? $\in_A$ is the universal subobject of $A \times P(A)$: it is classified by the evaluation map $\mathrm{ev}\colon A \times P(A) \to \Omega$ obtained by uncurrying the identity $\mathrm{id}_{P(A)}\colon P(A) \to P(A) = \Omega^A$. Every other subobject $R \hookrightarrow A \times P(A)$ pulls back from $\in_A$ along $\mathrm{id} \times \hat\chi$. The membership relation should be the 'universal' subobject — the one all others pull back from.

q: Let $C = \{a \le b \le c\}$ be the chain of length 3. How many sieves are there on $c$?
text: Let $C = \{a \le b \le c\}$ be the chain of length 3. How many sieves are there on $c$? Sieves on $c$ in a poset are down-closed subsets of $\{a, b, c\}$. The down-sets are: $\emptyset, \{a\}, \{a, b\}, \{a, b, c\}$. So $|\Omega(c)| = 4$. In a poset, sieves on $c$ are down-closed subsets of $\{x : x \le c\}$.

q: In the presheaf topos $\hat{C}$ for $C$ a small category, exponentials $G^F$ are computed by which formula?
text: In the presheaf topos $\hat{C}$ for $C$ a small category, exponentials $G^F$ are computed by which formula? Pointwise definitions don't give the right adjoint. The correct formula uses the representable $よ(c) = \mathrm{Hom}(-, c)$ and natural transformations: $G^F(c) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\hat C}(F \times \mathrm{Hom}(-, c), G)$. Limits in $\hat C$ are pointwise; exponentials are not. Limits in $\hat C$ are pointwise. Exponentials need Yoneda.

q: Which of the following is NOT a presheaf topos?
text: Which of the following is NOT a presheaf topos? Sheaves on a non-discrete space $X$ are a *reflective subcategory* of presheaves on $\mathrm{Open}(X)$, but they are not themselves a presheaf topos in general. Sheafification is needed. The other three are all presheaf categories. Sheaves are presheaves satisfying a gluing condition — and that's a strict subcategory.

q: Let $C$ be the walking-isomorphism category — two objects $a, b$ with arrows $f\colon a \to b$, $g\colon b \to a$, and $g\circ f = \mathrm{id}_a$, $f \circ g = \mathrm{id}_b$. What is $\hat{C}$ equivalent to?
text: Let $C$ be the walking-isomorphism category — two objects $a, b$ with arrows $f\colon a \to b$, $g\colon b \to a$, and $g\circ f = \mathrm{id}_a$, $f \circ g = \mathrm{id}_b$. What is $\hat{C}$ equivalent to? When $C$ is equivalent to a one-object category, so is $\hat C$. The walking iso is equivalent to the terminal category (one object, one identity), so $\hat C \simeq \mathbf{Set}$. Equivalences of categories induce equivalences of presheaf categories. What category is the walking iso equivalent to?

q: In a presheaf topos $\hat{C}$, what is $\Omega$ at the level of 'global elements,' i.e. how many maps $1 \to \Omega$ are there in $\hat{C}$?
text: In a presheaf topos $\hat{C}$, what is $\Omega$ at the level of 'global elements,' i.e. how many maps $1 \to \Omega$ are there in $\hat{C}$? $\mathrm{Hom}_{\hat C}(1, \Omega) = \mathrm{Sub}_{\hat C}(1)$: subobjects of the terminal presheaf. These correspond to *closed sieves on the whole category* — equivalently, idempotent natural transformations $1 \to 1$. For $C = \mathbf{B}G$ this gives 2 (only $\emptyset$ and the maximal sieve survive); for a poset it gives the number of down-closed subsets of $\mathrm{Ob}(C)$. $\mathrm{Hom}(1, \Omega) = \mathrm{Sub}(1)$ — subobjects of the terminal.

q: Let $G = \mathbb{Z}/6$. Which of the following is the lattice of subgroups of $G$ (and hence the lattice of subobjects of $G/1$ in $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set}$)?
text: Let $G = \mathbb{Z}/6$. Which of the following is the lattice of subgroups of $G$ (and hence the lattice of subobjects of $G/1$ in $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set}$)? $\mathbb{Z}/6$ has subgroups $\{0\}, \langle 3 \rangle = \{0, 3\} \cong \mathbb{Z}/2, \langle 2 \rangle = \{0, 2, 4\} \cong \mathbb{Z}/3$, and $\mathbb{Z}/6$ itself. The lattice is a diamond: trivial at the bottom, $\mathbb{Z}/2$ and $\mathbb{Z}/3$ as incomparable middle elements, $\mathbb{Z}/6$ on top. By Lagrange, divisors of 6 are $1, 2, 3, 6$; cyclic groups have one subgroup per divisor.

q: In $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set}$, the subobject classifier $\Omega$ as a $G$-set has underlying set:
text: In $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set}$, the subobject classifier $\Omega$ as a $G$-set has underlying set: $\Omega$ in $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set} = [\mathbf{B}G^{\mathrm{op}}, \mathbf{Set}]$ at the unique object $* \in \mathbf{B}G$ is the set of sieves on $*$, which unwinds to subgroups of $G$. The presheaf-restriction action becomes conjugation: $g \cdot H = gHg^{-1}$. All subgroups appear, not just normal ones. Sieves on the unique object of $\mathbf{B}G$ are exactly subgroups of $G$ (downward closure becomes the subgroup property).

q: When does the topos $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set}$ collapse to (be equivalent to) ordinary $\mathbf{Set}$?
text: When does the topos $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set}$ collapse to (be equivalent to) ordinary $\mathbf{Set}$? When $G$ is trivial, $\mathbf{B}G$ is the terminal category, so $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set} = [\mathbf{B}\{e\}^{\mathrm{op}}, \mathbf{Set}] \simeq \mathbf{Set}$. For any non-trivial $G$, $\Omega$ has more than 2 elements (the lattice of subgroups), so the topos cannot be $\mathbf{Set}$. $\Omega$ is the lattice of subgroups. When does that have only two elements?

q: A geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ consists of an adjoint pair $f^* \dashv f_*$. Which is the inverse-image, which is the direct-image, and which is the constraint?
text: A geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ consists of an adjoint pair $f^* \dashv f_*$. Which is the inverse-image, which is the direct-image, and which is the constraint? By convention the geometric morphism's named direction is the same as $f_*$ (direct image): $f_*\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ goes the same way as $f$. The inverse-image $f^*\colon \mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{F}$ goes the other way and is required to be left exact (preserve finite limits). Right adjoints are automatically left exact, but the constraint here is on the *left* adjoint $f^*$, which is non-trivial. The 'direct image' is in the same direction as $f$ itself. The other one carries the exactness requirement.

q: For a continuous map $f\colon X \to Y$ of topological spaces, the induced geometric morphism on sheaves goes which direction, and what plays the role of $f^*$?
text: For a continuous map $f\colon X \to Y$ of topological spaces, the induced geometric morphism on sheaves goes which direction, and what plays the role of $f^*$? A continuous $f\colon X \to Y$ induces a geometric morphism $\mathrm{Sh}(X) \to \mathrm{Sh}(Y)$ in the same direction as $f$. The inverse-image functor $f^*\colon \mathrm{Sh}(Y) \to \mathrm{Sh}(X)$ is sheaf-pullback (left exact because $f^{-1}$ preserves intersections of opens); $f_*$ is pushforward (often called direct image of sheaves). Topology: the map of *opens* goes the opposite way of $f$, but the geometric morphism still 'points like $f$.'

q: A *point* of a topos $\mathcal{E}$ is by definition a geometric morphism with which source and target?
text: A *point* of a topos $\mathcal{E}$ is by definition a geometric morphism with which source and target? Points of $\mathcal{E}$ are geometric morphisms $\mathbf{Set} \to \mathcal{E}$, mirroring continuous maps $\{*\} \to X$ for spaces. The data is a left-exact left adjoint $p^*\colon \mathcal{E} \to \mathbf{Set}$ (the 'stalk' functor) plus its right adjoint $p_*\colon \mathbf{Set} \to \mathcal{E}$ (skyscraper). A point is the 'morphism from a single point' — but the source/target convention follows the geometric-morphism convention.

q: The inclusion $\mathrm{Sh}(C, J) \hookrightarrow \hat{C}$ of sheaves into presheaves is a geometric morphism. In which direction does the morphism go, and what is $f^*$?
text: The inclusion $\mathrm{Sh}(C, J) \hookrightarrow \hat{C}$ of sheaves into presheaves is a geometric morphism. In which direction does the morphism go, and what is $f^*$? Sheaves form a *left-exact reflective* subcategory of presheaves. The geometric morphism is $\mathrm{Sh}(C, J) \to \hat{C}$ with $f^* = $ sheafification $a$ (left exact — this is what 'left-exact reflective' means and is the content of the sheafification theorem), and $f_* = $ the inclusion (right adjoint to sheafification). Embedding-as-direct-image, sheafification-as-inverse-image. Sheafification is a *left adjoint* (of the inclusion) and is left exact. That makes it $f^*$.

q: An *essential* geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ is one for which $f^*$ has an additional left adjoint $f_!$. Which of the following is NOT generally true of essential geometric morphisms?
text: An *essential* geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ is one for which $f^*$ has an additional left adjoint $f_!$. Which of the following is NOT generally true of essential geometric morphisms? Being a left adjoint, $f_!$ preserves all colimits — but it is *not* generally left exact. Essential morphisms in fact tend to be the ones where $f^*$ has both adjoints precisely because it has an even nicer 'Kan extension' structure on both sides. Forgetting left exactness of $f_!$ is exactly what allows $f^*$ to remain left exact while $f_!$ does the colimit work. $f_!$ is a *left* adjoint — preserving colimits is automatic. What's special about $f^*$ being left exact, that $f_!$ doesn't share?

title: Heyting algebras
blurb: A Heyting algebra is a bounded lattice with a binary operation $\Rightarrow$ satisfying $a \wedge b \le c \iff a \le b \Rightarrow c$. Boolean algebras are the special case where double-negation is the identity; topological open-set lattices are generic Heyting algebras.

title: $\Omega$ as an internal Heyting algebra
blurb: In any topos, the subobject classifier $\Omega$ carries an internal Heyting-algebra structure: maps $\Omega \times \Omega \to \Omega$ implementing $\wedge, \vee, \Rightarrow$. Subobject lattices $\mathrm{Sub}(X)$ become external Heyting algebras with these operations applied pointwise.

title: The internal Mitchell–Bénabou language
blurb: Every topos has an internal first-order language: types are objects, terms are morphisms, formulas are characteristic maps into $\Omega$. Provability in this language tracks subobject inclusion, and the resulting logic is intuitionist (LEM may fail).

title: Kripke–Joyal forcing semantics
blurb: Kripke–Joyal semantics interprets the internal language stage-by-stage: an object $X$ "forces" a formula $\varphi$ when the corresponding subobject contains all of $X$. In presheaf toposes this collapses to Kripke's classical possible-world semantics for intuitionist logic.

title: Why LEM fails: double negation in toposes
blurb:
Most toposes are not Boolean: $
eg
eg p = p$ generally fails internally. The double-negation topology on a topos extracts the largest Boolean sub-topos and is the algebraic shadow of "forcing $p \vee 
eg p$".

title: Geometric morphisms preserve geometric logic
blurb: Inverse image functors $f^*$ preserve finite limits and arbitrary colimits — exactly the connectives of geometric logic ($\exists, \wedge, \bigvee$). This is why geometric formulas transport across geometric morphisms while implication and $\forall$ generally do not.

q: On the open-set lattice $\tau = \{\varnothing, \{x\}, \{y\}, \{x,y\}, X\}$ of $X = \{x,y,z\}$, what is $\{x\} \Rightarrow \{y\}$?
text: On the open-set lattice $\tau = \{\varnothing, \{x\}, \{y\}, \{x,y\}, X\}$ of $X = \{x,y,z\}$, what is $\{x\} \Rightarrow \{y\}$? $a \Rightarrow b$ is the largest $c$ with $a \wedge c \le b$. Test each candidate: $\{x\} \cap \{y\} = \varnothing \subseteq \{y\}$ ✓, $\{x\} \cap \{x,y\} = \{x\} \not\subseteq \{y\}$, $\{x\}\cap X = \{x\}\not\subseteq\{y\}$. So the largest $c$ that works is $\{y\}$ itself. Compute $\{x\} \cap c$ for each $c$ in the lattice and check which lie inside $\{y\}$ — pick the largest.

q: Which of the following finite lattices are Heyting algebras?
text: Which of the following finite lattices are Heyting algebras? A finite lattice is Heyting iff it is distributive (then $b\Rightarrow c$ exists as the join of all $x$ with $x\wedge b\le c$). $N_5$ and $M_3$ are the two forbidden non-distributive sublattices of Birkhoff's theorem, so they are not Heyting. Boolean algebras are distributive (in fact complemented). Finite chains are distributive. Heyting ⇔ distributive (for finite lattices). The two non-distributive 5-element lattices are exactly $N_5$ and $M_3$.

q: In a Heyting algebra, which identity holds for every element $a$?
text:
In a Heyting algebra, which identity holds for every element $a$? $
eg
eg
eg a = 
eg a$ is provable in intuitionist logic: from $a \le 
eg
eg a$ apply $
eg$ (which reverses) to get $
eg
eg
eg a \le 
eg a$; combine with the dual instance. The first two are LEM/DNE — equivalent to being Boolean. The fourth fails when $a 
e  ot$. Triple negation collapses to single negation in any Heyting algebra — but double negation does not collapse to identity.

q: What is the subobject classifier $\Omega$ in the topos $\mathbf{Set}$?
text: What is the subobject classifier $\Omega$ in the topos $\mathbf{Set}$? In $\mathbf{Set}$, subobjects of $X$ are subsets, and a subset is determined by its indicator function $X \to \{\bot,\top\}$. So $\Omega = \{\bot,\top\}$ with $\mathrm{true}(*)=\top$. Two truth values — exactly the data needed for classical Boolean logic. Subobjects of a set are subsets; an indicator function takes how many values?

q: In the topos $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$ of left $G$-sets, $\Omega$ as a $G$-set has underlying set:
text: In the topos $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$ of left $G$-sets, $\Omega$ as a $G$-set has underlying set: A subobject of the terminal $G$-set $\{*\}$ is a $G$-stable subset of $\{*\}$ — but the elements of $\Omega$ in $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$ are sieves on the unique object of the one-object groupoid $G$, which correspond to subgroups of $G$. The action of $g$ on $H \le G$ is conjugation $H \mapsto gHg^{-1}$. Think of $G$ as a one-object groupoid; $\Omega(c)$ in a presheaf topos is the sieves on $c$.

q: Match each Heyting operation on $\Omega$ with the subobject of $\Omega \times \Omega$ it classifies.
text: Match each Heyting operation on $\Omega$ with the subobject of $\Omega \times \Omega$ it classifies. $\wedge$ classifies the singleton $(\top,\top)$; $\vee$ classifies the union of the two true-lines; $\Rightarrow$ classifies the order $\le$. See Mac Lane–Moerdijk IV.8. Conjunction is true only when both coordinates are true; implication is true when the first coordinate is below the second.

q: In $\mathrm{Sh}(\mathbb{R})$, the section $\Omega(U)$ over an open $U \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is:
text: In $\mathrm{Sh}(\mathbb{R})$, the section $\Omega(U)$ over an open $U \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is: In $\mathrm{Sh}(X)$, $\Omega$ is the sheaf $U \mapsto \{\text{open subsets of } U\}$ with $\mathrm{true}_U = U$. A subobject of a sheaf $F$ over $U$ is a sub-sheaf, picked out by 'on which open does each section live.' This makes $\Omega(U)$ a Heyting algebra under $\cap, \cup,$ and the formula $V \Rightarrow W = \mathrm{int}(U \setminus V \cup W)$. Sub-sheaves of the terminal sheaf over $U$ are determined by the maximal open on which the section is defined.

q: Which morphism implements the operation $\neg\colon \Omega \to \Omega$?
text: Which morphism implements the operation $\neg\colon \Omega \to \Omega$? $\neg p = p \Rightarrow \bot$. The map pairs $p$ with the constant $\bot$ (via the unique $\Omega \to 1$ then $\bot\colon 1 \to \Omega$) and applies $\Rightarrow$. There is no canonical 'swap automorphism' on $\Omega$ in non-Boolean toposes. Negation is implication against falsehood.

q: Match each piece of the internal language to its categorical interpretation.
text: Match each piece of the internal language to its categorical interpretation. Closed terms are global sections; formulas are characteristic maps (or equivalently subobjects); $\exists$ is the image / left adjoint; $\forall$ is the right adjoint to pullback. This is the standard Lawvere adjoint correspondence for quantifiers. $\exists$ is image-along-projection; $\forall$ is dual.

q: The formula $\forall x.\,(P(x) \to Q(x))$ is interpreted in $\mathrm{Sub}(1)$ as:
text: The formula $\forall x.\,(P(x) \to Q(x))$ is interpreted in $\mathrm{Sub}(1)$ as: First compute $\{P \to Q\} = \{P\} \Rightarrow \{Q\}$ in $\mathrm{Sub}(A)$. Then quantify universally: $\forall x.\,$ becomes $\Pi_\pi$, the right adjoint to $\pi^*\colon \mathrm{Sub}(1) \to \mathrm{Sub}(A)$. So the answer is $\Pi_\pi(\{P\} \Rightarrow \{Q\})$. Implication first, then $\forall$ as right adjoint to pullback.

q: Provability $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$ in the Mitchell–Bénabou logic translates to which categorical condition?
text: Provability $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$ in the Mitchell–Bénabou logic translates to which categorical condition? $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$ means: in the context $\Gamma$, $\varphi$ is always true. In subobject terms, the subobject $\{\Gamma \mid \varphi\}$ is everything — equal to the top of $\mathrm{Sub}(\llbracket\Gamma\rrbracket)$. The classifying map $\llbracket\varphi\rrbracket$ is then $\mathrm{true}\circ !\colon \llbracket\Gamma\rrbracket \to 1 \to \Omega$. A formula provable from $\Gamma$ holds on the entire context object.

q: On the 2-world Kripke model with $C = (a \to b)$ and valuation $\llbracket p\rrbracket(a)=\varnothing$, $\llbracket p\rrbracket(b)=\{*\}$, which of the following are forced at world $b$?
text:
On the 2-world Kripke model with $C = (a 	o b)$ and valuation $\llbracket prbracket(a)=\varnothing$, $\llbracket prbracket(b)=\{*\}$, which of the following are forced at world $b$? At $b$: (0) $b \Vdash p$ since $\llbracket prbracket(b) 
e \varnothing$. (1) $b

q: The Kripke–Joyal clause for implication $\varphi \to \psi$ at $X$ is:
text: The Kripke–Joyal clause for implication $\varphi \to \psi$ at $X$ is: Kripke–Joyal $\to$ quantifies over every generalised element $Y \to X$ — this is the persistence/stability condition. Without this universal closure, $\to$ would not be invariant under restriction along arrows, and the clauses wouldn't be sound for the topos's internal logic. Implication in intuitionist Kripke semantics persists along the accessibility relation — the clause must quantify over all accessible worlds.

q: In a sheaf topos $\mathrm{Sh}(X)$, the Kripke–Joyal clause for $\exists y.\,\varphi$ at an open $U$ becomes:
text: In a sheaf topos $\mathrm{Sh}(X)$, the Kripke–Joyal clause for $\exists y.\,\varphi$ at an open $U$ becomes: Existential quantification in sheaf forcing is 'locally a witness exists': there is a covering family on which the witness can be chosen. This is why constructive analysis builds objects locally and glues them by descent rather than picking global sections. Sheaves are about local data; existential is local existence of a witness.

q: Consider the 3-world model $C$ with objects $a, b, c$ and arrows $a \to b, a \to c$ (no arrow $b \leftrightarrow c$). Let $\llbracket p\rrbracket(b) = \{*\}$, $\llbracket p\rrbracket(a) = \llbracket p\rrbracket(c) = \varnothing$. Where does $\neg\neg p$ hold?
text:
Consider the 3-world model $C$ with objects $a, b, c$ and arrows $a 	o b, a 	o c$ (no arrow $b \leftrightarrow c$). Let $\llbracket prbracket(b) = \{*\}$, $\llbracket prbracket(a) = \llbracket prbracket(c) = \varnothing$. Where does $
eg

eg

ot\Vdash

q: The Kripke–Joyal clause for disjunction in a topos requires a cover $\{f_i\colon X_i \to X\}$. In a presheaf topos $\widehat{C}$, what is a cover of a representable $X = y(c)$?
text: The Kripke–Joyal clause for disjunction in a topos requires a cover $\{f_i\colon X_i \to X\}$. In a presheaf topos $\widehat{C}$, what is a cover of a representable $X = y(c)$? In a presheaf topos $\widehat{C}$ (no Grothendieck topology), the only cover of a representable $y(c)$ is the trivial cover $\{\mathrm{id}\}$. This is why Kripke–Joyal forcing on $\widehat{C}$ collapses to the classical Kripke clauses (no genuine 'covering' refinement). Switching to sheaves $\mathrm{Sh}(C, J)$ on a non-trivial site adds genuine covers and is what enables descent. Presheaf toposes have no Grothendieck topology beyond the trivial one — covers are trivial.

q: In which presheaf topos does LEM fail ?
text: In which presheaf topos does LEM fail ? $\widehat{*}$ is just $\mathbf{Set}$ (Boolean). $\widehat{G}$ is $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$, also Boolean (since $G$ is a groupoid). $\widehat{(a\to b)}$ is non-Boolean: $\Omega(a)$ has 3 elements (the sieves $\varnothing, \{a\}, \{a, a\to b\}$ — really the latter two), and the 3-element chain is the smallest non-Boolean Heyting algebra. LEM fails. Theorem: $\widehat{C}$ is Boolean iff $C$ is a groupoid. $\widehat{C}$ is Boolean iff $C$ is a groupoid — the arrow $a \to b$ is not invertible.

q: Select all toposes in which the law of excluded middle holds (i.e. all Boolean toposes).

The double-negation topology $
eg
eg\colon \Omega 	o \Omega$ on a topos $\mathcal{E}$ produces a subtopos $\mathrm{Sh}_{
eg
eg}(\mathcal{E}) \hookrightarrow \mathcal{E}$. What is its defining property?
text:
The double-negation topology $
eg
eg\colon \Omega 	o \Omega$ on a topos $\mathcal{E}$ produces a subtopos $\mathrm{Sh}_{
eg
eg}(\mathcal{E}) \hookrightarrow \mathcal{E}$. What is its defining property? $\mathrm{Sh}_{
eg
eg}(\mathcal{E})$ is the largest Boolean subtopos of $\mathcal{E}$ (Mac Lane–Moerdijk V.2 / VI.9). When $\mathcal{E}$ is the topos of $ eta$-names for a forcing condition, $\mathrm{Sh}_{
eg
eg}$ is the model of ZFC obtained by Cohen forcing — the topos-theoretic shadow of forcing. Double-negation 'rounds down' all subobjects to their $
eg
eg$-stable parts, exactly the ones that obey LEM.

q: Which subobjects of $\mathbb{R}$ in $\mathrm{Sh}(\mathbb{R})$ are $\neg\neg$-stable (i.e. equal to their double-negation)?
text:
Which subobjects of $\mathbb{R}$ in $\mathrm{Sh}(\mathbb{R})$ are $
eg
eg$-stable (i.e. equal to their double-negation)? $
eg
eg U = \mathrm{int}(\overline{U})$ in the open-set lattice. So $U = 
eg
eg U$ iff $U = \mathrm{int}(\overline{U})$, the definition of a regular open set. The regular opens form a complete Boolean algebra, the Booleanisation of the Heyting algebra of opens. $
eg U = \mathrm{int}(U^c)$, so $
eg
eg U = \mathrm{int}(\mathrm{int}(U^c)^c) = \mathrm{int}(\overline{U})$.

q: The classical theorem 'every set has a cardinality' (every set is in bijection with an initial ordinal) requires the axiom of choice. In a topos $\mathcal{E}$, what is the relationship between AC and LEM?
text: The classical theorem 'every set has a cardinality' (every set is in bijection with an initial ordinal) requires the axiom of choice. In a topos $\mathcal{E}$, what is the relationship between AC and LEM? Diaconescu's theorem (1975): if every epi splits internally in a topos $\mathcal{E}$, then $\mathcal{E}$ is Boolean. Constructive proof: from a 2-element quotient $\{0,1\}/{\sim}$ where $\sim$ depends on a proposition $\varphi$, internal AC gives a section that decides $\varphi$. The converse (LEM ⇒ AC) is false: $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$ is Boolean but AC fails. There's a famous theorem of Diaconescu connecting choice and excluded middle in any topos.

q: Which of the following logical connectives are always preserved by the inverse image $f^*$ of a geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$?
text: Which of the following logical connectives are always preserved by the inverse image $f^*$ of a geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$? Geometric logic is the fragment $\{\top, \bot, \wedge, \bigvee, \exists, =\}$. $f^*$ preserves finite limits ($\wedge$, $=$) and arbitrary colimits ($\bigvee$, $\exists$ as image). $\Rightarrow$, $\forall$, $\neg$ are right-adjoint constructions; left adjoints don't commute with right adjoints in general. Geometric formulas use $\wedge, \bigvee, \exists, =$ — and only those — because $f^*$ preserves finite limits and arbitrary colimits.

q: Why does $f^*$ preserve $\bigvee$ but not $\bigwedge$ (infinitary conjunction)?
text: Why does $f^*$ preserve $\bigvee$ but not $\bigwedge$ (infinitary conjunction)? $f^* \dashv f_*$, so $f^*$ is a left adjoint and preserves all colimits — including infinitary unions $\bigvee$. It only preserves finite limits, not arbitrary ones; $\bigwedge$ over an infinite index is an infinite limit, not preserved. Left adjoints commute with colimits; $f^*$ is the left adjoint of the geometric morphism.

q: If $\mathcal{T}$ is a geometric theory and $\varphi \vdash_x \psi$ is a geometric sequent provable in the classifying topos $\mathrm{Set}[\mathcal{T}]$, then for any topos $\mathcal{F}$ with a $\mathcal{T}$-model $M$:
text: If $\mathcal{T}$ is a geometric theory and $\varphi \vdash_x \psi$ is a geometric sequent provable in the classifying topos $\mathrm{Set}[\mathcal{T}]$, then for any topos $\mathcal{F}$ with a $\mathcal{T}$-model $M$: This is geometric soundness: a $\mathcal{T}$-model in $\mathcal{F}$ corresponds to a geometric morphism $\mathcal{F} \to \mathrm{Set}[\mathcal{T}]$; $f^*$ preserves geometric formulas, so anything provable geometrically in the classifying topos transports along $f^*$ to $\mathcal{F}$. This is the central technical reason for restricting to geometric logic. The whole point of the geometric fragment: it transports along arbitrary geometric morphisms, including those classifying models.

title: Sieves
blurb: A sieve on $c$ is a downward-closed family of arrows into $c$ — equivalently a subfunctor of the representable presheaf $h_c$. Sieves are the building blocks of Grothendieck topologies and play the role that open covers do for ordinary topology.

title: Grothendieck topology axioms
blurb: A Grothendieck topology on $C$ assigns to each object $c$ a collection of "covering" sieves, satisfying maximality, stability under pullback, and a transitivity axiom. The data $(C, J)$ is called a site; sheaves on a site generalize sheaves on a topological space.

title: Examples: small/large Zariski, étale, fpqc
blurb: The small Zariski site of a scheme has open immersions for covers; the étale site loosens to surjective étale maps; the fpqc site is even coarser. Each gives a different sheaf theory — Zariski-locally trivial $\ne$ étale-locally trivial, the difference detecting Galois descent.

title: Sheaves and sheafification
blurb: A sheaf on $(C, J)$ is a presheaf $F$ such that for every covering sieve $S$ of $c$, $F(c)$ is the limit of $F$ over $S$. The sheafification functor is the left adjoint of the inclusion of sheaves into presheaves; it averages a presheaf over all covering sieves.

title: $\mathrm{Sh}(C, J)$ as a topos
blurb: The category $\mathrm{Sh}(C, J)$ of sheaves on a site is a Grothendieck topos — a particular kind of elementary topos that has all small colimits and a small generating set. Every Grothendieck topos arises this way (Giraud's theorem).

title: Geometric morphisms from morphisms of sites
blurb: A continuous map of sites $f\colon (D, K) \to (C, J)$ induces a geometric morphism $\mathrm{Sh}(D, K) \to \mathrm{Sh}(C, J)$. This is how site theory provides examples of geometric morphisms: pullback along $f$ on the level of sites pulls sheaves back.

q: Let $C$ be the 3-object poset $\{x, y, t\}$ with $x \leq t$ and $y \leq t$ (no relation between $x$ and $y$). Which of the following families of arrows form a sieve on $t$?
text: Let $C$ be the 3-object poset $\{x, y, t\}$ with $x \leq t$ and $y \leq t$ (no relation between $x$ and $y$). Which of the following families of arrows form a sieve on $t$? Every arrow into $t$ has source $x$, $y$, or $t$. Since the only arrows out of $x$, $y$, $t$ that land in $\{x, y, t\}$ via composition are themselves identities, every set listed is precomposition-closed. In a poset, sieves on $c$ are exactly down-sets of objects $\leq c$, and all four sets correspond to legitimate down-sets. A sieve is closed under precomposition; in a poset there are no nontrivial composable predecessors to worry about.

q: How many sieves are there on the top object $t$ of the same 3-object poset $\{x, y, t\}$ with $x \leq t$ and $y \leq t$?
text: How many sieves are there on the top object $t$ of the same 3-object poset $\{x, y, t\}$ with $x \leq t$ and $y \leq t$? Sieves on $t$ correspond bijectively to down-sets of $\{d : d \leq t\} = \{x, y, t\}$. With no relations between $x$ and $y$, every subset is automatically a down-set: $\varnothing$, $\{x\}$, $\{y\}$, $\{x, y\}$ — but a sieve on $t$ must contain $\mathrm{id}_t$ to make sense as a subfunctor of $h_t$ at $d = t$? Careful: actually every subset is a sieve, and the empty set is allowed too. The four sieves are $\varnothing,\ \{x \to t\},\ \{y \to t\},\ \{x \to t, y \to t, \mathrm{id}_t\}$ if we close under precomposition — but in a poset closure is trivial.
