text: The Hausdorff dimension of a set $E$ is: $s\mapsto\mathcal{H}^s(E)$ is $\infty$ for small $s$ and $0$ for large $s$, with a unique transition point $s^*=\dim_H(E)$. At $s^*$ itself, $\mathcal{H}^{s^*}(E)$ may take any value in $[0,\infty]$.

q: Which Hausdorff-measure statement is correct for the $s$-dimensional Sierpiński gasket (three contractions of ratio $1/2$)?
text: Which Hausdorff-measure statement is correct for the $s$-dimensional Sierpiński gasket (three contractions of ratio $1/2$)? Moran: $3\cdot(1/2)^s=1\Rightarrow s=\log 3/\log 2$. The gasket satisfies the Open Set Condition, which implies $0<\mathcal{H}^s(\text{gasket})<\infty$ at $s=\dim_H$. Moran's equation $N\cdot r^s=1$ for $N$ self-similar copies of ratio $r$.

q: For the 4-contraction Koch curve with similarity ratio $1/3$, compute the Hausdorff dimension to 3 decimals. (Moran: $4\cdot(1/3)^s=1$.)
text: For the 4-contraction Koch curve with similarity ratio $1/3$, compute the Hausdorff dimension to 3 decimals. (Moran: $4\cdot(1/3)^s=1$.) $s=\log 4/\log 3\approx 1.2619$. The Koch curve has strictly larger Hausdorff dimension than its topological dimension ($1$), reflecting its fractal crinkliness.

q: Why does $\mathcal{H}^1$ on $\mathbb{R}$ agree with Lebesgue measure $m$?
text: Why does $\mathcal{H}^1$ on $\mathbb{R}$ agree with Lebesgue measure $m$? $\mathcal{H}^1([a,b])=b-a$ because optimal covers by small intervals have diameter-sum approaching $b-a$. Both $\mathcal{H}^1$ and $m$ are translation-invariant, $\sigma$-finite, and agree on a generating ring of intervals, so Carathéodory uniqueness forces $\mathcal{H}^1=m$ on $\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$.

title: Complex numbers
blurb: Arithmetic on $\mathbb{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$ with multiplication; polar form $z = re^{i\theta}$ and the one-point compactification to the Riemann sphere $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$ via stereographic projection.

title: Riemann sphere
blurb: Stereographic projection; ∞ as a point; circles/lines unified.

title: Möbius transformations
blurb: Linear-fractional maps, cross-ratio, automorphisms of Ĉ.

title: Amplitwist
blurb: Needham's picture: a holomorphic $f$ acts on infinitesimal vectors at $z_0$ as rotation by $\arg f'(z_0)$ and scaling by $|f'(z_0)|$; the Jacobian is a similarity matrix.

title: Cauchy–Riemann equations
blurb: A complex-differentiable $f = u + iv$ must have Jacobian commuting with multiplication by $i$; equivalently $u_x = v_y$, $u_y = -v_x$, i.e. $\bar\partial f = 0$.

title: Holomorphic functions
blurb: Complex-differentiable on an open set; infinitely more rigid than real.

title: Domain coloring
blurb: Visualize $f\colon\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{C}$ with hue $=\arg f(z)$ and lightness encoding $|f(z)|$; zeros and poles appear as rainbow winds counting multiplicity.

title: Contour integrals
blurb: Integrate $f\,dz$ along a piecewise smooth $\gamma$; the real 1-forms $u\,dx - v\,dy$ and $v\,dx + u\,dy$ are closed exactly when $f$ is holomorphic.

title: Cauchy's theorem
blurb: ∫_γ f dz = 0 on simply connected domains.

title: Cauchy integral formula
blurb: $f(a) = \tfrac{1}{2\pi i}\oint f(z)/(z-a)\,dz$: boundary values determine interior values on a closed disk, and differentiation under the integral makes holomorphic functions $C^\infty$.

title: Analyticity = holomorphy
blurb: Every holomorphic function is locally a convergent power series.

title: Liouville's theorem
blurb: Bounded entire ⇒ constant.

title: Fundamental theorem of algebra
blurb: Every non-constant polynomial over $\mathbb{C}$ has a root; Liouville's argument applied to $1/p(z)$ makes this a corollary of complex boundedness.

title: Maximum modulus principle
blurb: Non-constant holomorphic f has no interior max of |f|.

title: Schwarz lemma
blurb: For holomorphic $f\colon\mathbb{D}\to\mathbb{D}$ with $f(0)=0$: $|f(z)|\le|z|$ and $|f'(0)|\le 1$; equality forces $f(z)=e^{i\theta}z$, a rigidity extracted from the maximum modulus principle.

title: Laurent series
blurb: Expansions on annuli; principal part captures singular behavior.

title: Classification of singularities
blurb: Removable, pole of order n, essential (Casorati–Weierstrass).

title: Residue theorem
blurb: ∮ f dz = 2πi · Σ Res(f; z_k). Computes real integrals, sums series.

title: Argument principle
blurb: (1/2πi) ∮ f′/f = Z − P; winding of f(γ) counts zeros minus poles.

title: Rouché's theorem
blurb: |g| < |f| on γ ⇒ f and f+g have the same number of zeros inside.

title: Conformal maps
blurb: Angle-preserving, orientation-preserving; locally holomorphic with f′ ≠ 0.

title: Disk automorphisms
blurb: $\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{D})$ consists of the Blaschke factors $e^{i\theta}(z-a)/(1-\bar a z)$; this is the isometry group $\mathrm{PSU}(1,1)$ of the hyperbolic Poincaré metric on the disk.

title: Normal families (Montel)
blurb: Montel: a locally bounded family of holomorphic functions is normal (every sequence has a locally uniformly convergent subsequence); compactness that powers the Riemann mapping theorem.

title: Riemann mapping theorem
blurb: Any simply connected proper open U ⊂ C is conformally equivalent to D.

title: Harmonic functions
blurb: If $f = u + iv$ is holomorphic then $u$ and $v$ satisfy Laplace's equation $\Delta u = 0$; on simply connected domains every harmonic $u$ has a harmonic conjugate recovering a holomorphic lift.

title: Analytic continuation
blurb: The identity theorem lets a holomorphic germ extend uniquely along paths; loops around branch points produce monodromy, and $\log z$ or $\sqrt z$ live naturally on a Riemann surface rather than $\mathbb{C}\setminus\{0\}$.

q: Compute $|3 + 4i|$.
Multiply $(1+i)(2-i)$.
What is $\arg(-1 - i)$ in $(-\pi, \pi]$?
text: What is $\arg(-1 - i)$ in $(-\pi, \pi]$? $-1-i$ lies in the third quadrant. Its modulus is $\sqrt{2}$ and its angle from the positive real axis (measured in $(-\pi,\pi]$) is $-3\pi/4$.

q: On the complex plane below, place a marker at the primitive sixth root of unity $\zeta_6 = e^{i\pi/3}$. Real axis runs left-right; imaginary axis runs up (note the SVG $y$-axis has been flipped so positive imaginary is up). Unit circle is shown.
text: On the complex plane below, place a marker at the primitive sixth root of unity $\zeta_6 = e^{i\pi/3}$. Real axis runs left-right; imaginary axis runs up (note the SVG $y$-axis has been flipped so positive imaginary is up). Unit circle is shown. The primitive sixth root of unity is $\zeta_6 = e^{i\pi/3} = \cos(\pi/3) + i\sin(\pi/3) = 1/2 + i\sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.5 + 0.866 i$. In the SVG viewBox the $y$-axis points down, so the target coordinate is $(0.5, -\sqrt{3}/2)$. $\zeta_6 = \cos(\pi/3) + i\sin(\pi/3) = 1/2 + i\sqrt{3}/2$.

q: Compute the sum of all 4th roots of $-16$ as a single complex number $a + bi$.
text: Compute the sum of all 4th roots of $-16$ as a single complex number $a + bi$. The 4th roots of $-16 = 16 e^{i\pi}$ are $2e^{i(\pi + 2\pi k)/4}$ for $k=0,1,2,3$. Their sum equals $2(e^{i\pi/4}+e^{3i\pi/4}+e^{5i\pi/4}+e^{7i\pi/4}) = 0$, since the roots of any $z^n = c$ sum to $0$ when $n \ge 2$. The roots lie at the vertices of a regular polygon centered at the origin.

q: Which property of $|\cdot|$ on $\mathbb{C}$ fails for the function $f(z) = \operatorname{Re}(z)$?
text: Which property of $|\cdot|$ on $\mathbb{C}$ fails for the function $f(z) = \operatorname{Re}(z)$? $\operatorname{Re}(\lambda z) = \operatorname{Re}(\lambda)\operatorname{Re}(z) - \operatorname{Im}(\lambda)\operatorname{Im}(z)$, which does not equal $|\lambda|\operatorname{Re}(z)$ in general (e.g.~$\lambda = i$, $z = 1$ gives $\operatorname{Re}(i \cdot 1) = 0 \neq 1 \cdot 1$). Also positive-definiteness fails ($f(i) = 0$ but $i \neq 0$), but homogeneity is the cleanest single counterexample structural failure of a norm.

q: How many fixed points does a non-identity Möbius transformation have on $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$?
text: How many fixed points does a non-identity Möbius transformation have on $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$? Solving $T(z) = z$ gives a quadratic $cz^2 + (d-a)z - b = 0$ on $\mathbb{C}$, with $\infty$ a fixed point exactly when $c = 0$. Always exactly two roots in $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$ counted with multiplicity.

q: Let $T(z) = \dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$. Compute $T(0)$.
How many distinct points must you specify the image of in order to uniquely determine a Möbius transformation?
text: How many distinct points must you specify the image of in order to uniquely determine a Möbius transformation? Möbius maps are 3-transitive on $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$: any triple of distinct points can be sent to any other triple by a unique transformation.

q: The cross-ratio $(z_1, z_2; z_3, z_4)$ is invariant under Möbius transformations. If $(0, 1; z, \infty) = 2$, what is $z$?
text: The cross-ratio $(z_1, z_2; z_3, z_4)$ is invariant under Möbius transformations. If $(0, 1; z, \infty) = 2$, what is $z$? The cross-ratio $(z_1,z_2;z_3,z_4)=\frac{(z_1-z_3)(z_2-z_4)}{(z_1-z_4)(z_2-z_3)}$. With $z_4=\infty$ this simplifies to $\frac{z_1-z_3}{z_2-z_3}=\frac{0-z}{1-z}$. Setting equal to $2$: $-z = 2(1-z) = 2-2z$, so $z = 2 - 2z + z \Rightarrow z = 1/2$.

q: A Möbius transformation $T$ has exactly one fixed point in $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$. Which type is $T$?
text: A Möbius transformation $T$ has exactly one fixed point in $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$. Which type is $T$? A non-identity Möbius transformation has two fixed points counted with multiplicity. Exactly one (a double fixed point) occurs precisely when the transformation is parabolic, i.e., conjugate to $z \mapsto z + 1$, and algebraically characterized by $\mathrm{tr}^2 = 4$.

q: Match each Möbius transformation with its geometric role on $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$.
text: Match each Möbius transformation with its geometric role on $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$. $T(z)=z+1$ is translation; $T(z)=1/z$ is the classical inversion $z\mapsto 1/z$ (the map whose fixed points are $\pm 1$ and which swaps $0$ and $\infty$); $T(z)=rz$ is a dilation; $T(z)=e^{i\theta}z$ is a rotation by $\theta$.

q: For $f(z) = z^2$ at $z_0 = 1 + i$, the amplitwist acts on infinitesimal vectors by:
text: For $f(z) = z^2$ at $z_0 = 1 + i$, the amplitwist acts on infinitesimal vectors by: $f'(z) = 2z$ so $f'(1+i) = 2 + 2i$. Then $|f'(z_0)| = 2\sqrt{2}$ and $\arg f'(z_0) = \pi/4$.

q: If $f$ is holomorphic at $z_0$ and $f'(z_0) = 0$, is $f$ conformal (angle-preserving) at $z_0$?
text: If $f$ is holomorphic at $z_0$ and $f'(z_0) = 0$, is $f$ conformal (angle-preserving) at $z_0$? Where $f'(z_0)=0$, $f$ is not a similarity at first order; if $f$ vanishes to order $n$ at $z_0$, angles between curves through $z_0$ get multiplied by $n$.

q: For $f(z) = z^3$ at $z_0 = i$, what is $|f'(z_0)|$?
For $f(z) = z^2 + iz$ near $z_0 = 1 - i$, compute the rotation angle (in radians) that the amplitwist applies to infinitesimal tangent vectors. Give a value in $(-\pi, \pi]$.
text: For $f(z) = z^2 + iz$ near $z_0 = 1 - i$, compute the rotation angle (in radians) that the amplitwist applies to infinitesimal tangent vectors. Give a value in $(-\pi, \pi]$. $f'(z) = 2z + i$, so $f'(1-i) = 2(1-i)+i = 2 - 2i + i = 2 - i$. The rotation angle is $\arg(2-i) = -\arctan(1/2) \approx -0.4636$ radians.

q: Let $f(z) = z^n$ for $n \ge 2$. At $z_0 = 0$ the map $f$ is not conformal. By what factor are angles between smooth curves through $0$ multiplied by $f$?
text: Let $f(z) = z^n$ for $n \ge 2$. At $z_0 = 0$ the map $f$ is not conformal. By what factor are angles between smooth curves through $0$ multiplied by $f$? At a zero of $f'$ of order $n-1$ (i.e., $f(z) = z^n$ has $f'(0) = 0$ to order $n-1$), the map behaves like $z \mapsto z^n$ locally, which multiplies angles by $n$. Two curves meeting at angle $\alpha$ get mapped to curves meeting at angle $n\alpha$.

q: Which of these functions is holomorphic on all of $\mathbb{C}$?
text: Which of these functions is holomorphic on all of $\mathbb{C}$? Anything involving $\bar z$ explicitly fails the Cauchy–Riemann equations. Polynomials in $z$ alone are entire.

q: Let $u(x,y) = x^2 - y^2$. Find the unique harmonic conjugate $v$ with $v(0,0) = 0$ such that $f = u + iv$ is holomorphic, and compute $v(1, 2)$.
text: Let $u(x,y) = x^2 - y^2$. Find the unique harmonic conjugate $v$ with $v(0,0) = 0$ such that $f = u + iv$ is holomorphic, and compute $v(1, 2)$. From $v_y = u_x = 2x$ we get $v = 2xy + g(x)$; then $-v_x = u_y = -2y$ forces $g'(x) = 0$, so $v = 2xy$. Thus $f = z^2$ and $v(1,2) = 4$.

q: Which is the correct form of the Cauchy–Riemann equations for $f = u + iv$?
text: Which is the correct form of the Cauchy–Riemann equations for $f = u + iv$? Equivalently: $\partial_{\bar z} f = 0$, where $\partial_{\bar z} = \tfrac12(\partial_x + i\partial_y)$.

q: Let $f(z)=|z|^2$ on $\mathbb{C}$. Which of the following statements are true?
text: Let $f(z)=|z|^2$ on $\mathbb{C}$. Which of the following statements are true? Writing $f=u+iv$ with $u(x,y)=x^2+y^2$, $v=0$: the CR equations $u_x=v_y$, $u_y=-v_x$ become $2x=0$, $2y=0$, holding only at the origin. At $z=0$ the difference quotient is $\lim_{h\to 0}|h|^2/h=\lim_{h\to 0}\bar h=0$, so $f'(0)$ exists. However, at any $z_0\ne 0$ the CR equations fail, so $f$ is not complex-differentiable there. Holomorphy requires differentiability on an open set, so $f$ is not holomorphic on any neighborhood of $0$, and in particular not entire. Check CR pointwise at $0$; compute the difference quotient at $0$; then ask whether complex-differentiability at a single point is enough for holomorphy.

q: Let $u(x,y) = e^x \cos y$. The harmonic conjugate with $v(0,0) = 0$ is $v(x,y) = e^x \sin y$. Compute $\operatorname{Im}(f(\ln 2 + i\pi/2))$ where $f = u + iv$.
text: Let $u(x,y) = e^x \cos y$. The harmonic conjugate with $v(0,0) = 0$ is $v(x,y) = e^x \sin y$. Compute $\operatorname{Im}(f(\ln 2 + i\pi/2))$ where $f = u + iv$. $f(z) = e^z$ (since $u = \operatorname{Re}(e^z)$, $v = \operatorname{Im}(e^z)$). Then $f(\ln 2 + i\pi/2) = e^{\ln 2}e^{i\pi/2} = 2i$, so $\operatorname{Im}(f) = 2$.

q: Compute $\operatorname{Res}\bigl(\tfrac{1}{z^2+1};\, i\bigr)$.
text: Compute $\operatorname{Res}\bigl(\tfrac{1}{z^2+1};\, i\bigr)$. $1/(z^2+1) = 1/((z-i)(z+i))$, simple pole at $i$ with residue $1/(2i) = -i/2$.

q: Compute $\displaystyle\oint_{|z|=2}\frac{dz}{z^2+1}$.
text: Compute $\displaystyle\oint_{|z|=2}\frac{dz}{z^2+1}$. Both poles $\pm i$ are inside the contour. $\operatorname{Res}(\cdot;i) = -i/2$, $\operatorname{Res}(\cdot;-i) = i/2$, sum $0$. So the integral is $2\pi i \cdot 0 = 0$.

q: For $f(z) = \dfrac{1}{z^2(z-1)}$, compute $\operatorname{Res}(f; 0)$.
text: For $f(z) = \dfrac{1}{z^2(z-1)}$, compute $\operatorname{Res}(f; 0)$. Pole of order 2 at $0$. $\operatorname{Res}(f;0) = \lim_{z\to 0}\frac{d}{dz}\!\left[z^2 f(z)\right] = \frac{d}{dz}\!\left[\frac{1}{z-1}\right]_{z=0} = -\frac{1}{(0-1)^2} = -1$.

q: Use the residue theorem to evaluate $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} \frac{dx}{1+x^4}$. Enter the exact value as a decimal to three places.
text: Use the residue theorem to evaluate $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} \frac{dx}{1+x^4}$. Enter the exact value as a decimal to three places. The integrand $1/(1+z^4)$ has poles at $z_k = e^{i\pi(2k+1)/4}$, $k=0,1,2,3$. For the upper half-plane contour, the poles inside are $z_0 = e^{i\pi/4}$ and $z_1 = e^{3i\pi/4}$. Computing: $\operatorname{Res}(f;z_k) = 1/(4z_k^3) = -z_k/4$. The two residues sum to $-(e^{i\pi/4}+e^{3i\pi/4})/4 = -(i\sqrt{2}/2) \cdot (1/2)$... The standard result is $\int_0^\infty dx/(1+x^4) = \pi/(2\sqrt{2}) \approx 1.1107$. Close the contour in the upper half-plane and use evenness to halve the real-line integral.

q: The function $f(z) = \dfrac{\sin z}{z^3}$ has a singularity at $z=0$. What kind is it, and what is $\operatorname{Res}(f;0)$?
text: The function $f(z) = \dfrac{\sin z}{z^3}$ has a singularity at $z=0$. What kind is it, and what is $\operatorname{Res}(f;0)$? Laurent expand: $\sin z/z^3 = (z - z^3/6 + z^5/120 - \cdots)/z^3 = z^{-2} - 1/6 + z^2/120 - \cdots$. The principal part has lowest term $z^{-2}$, so this is a pole of order 2. The residue is the coefficient of $z^{-1}$, which is $0$ (no $z^{-1}$ term appears in the expansion). Write the Taylor series of $\sin z$ and divide term by term.

q: Arrange the steps of a standard residue-theorem contour-integral proof into the order they appear in the argument.
text: Arrange the steps of a standard residue-theorem contour-integral proof into the order they appear in the argument. The standard playbook proceeds exactly in this order: (1) locate poles of the integrand and decide which are enclosed; (2) close the real axis with a large semicircle so the integral becomes a loop; (3) use Jordan's lemma or a $1/R^k$ decay estimate to kill the arc contribution as $R\to\infty$; (4) apply the residue theorem to the closed contour; (5) since the arc vanished, the loop integral equals the real-line integral, giving the answer. Think of the workflow: classify singularities first, build the contour, estimate the arc, invoke the theorem, then extract the real answer.

title: Banach and Hilbert spaces
blurb: Infinite-dimensional normed spaces provide the analytic home for operators and PDEs.

title: Bounded linear operators and the operator norm
blurb: B(X,Y) is Banach under the operator norm; for linear maps bounded = continuous = Lipschitz, and the norm is the largest singular value in the matrix case.

title: Bounded operators and adjoints
blurb: Linear operators on Hilbert spaces admit operator norms, adjoints, and projection geometry.

title: Adjoint operator on Hilbert space
blurb: Self-adjoint, normal, and unitary operators are detected through identities between T and T*; Pauli matrices make the distinctions concrete in 2×2.

title: The big four theorems
blurb: Hahn–Banach, Open Mapping, Closed Graph, and Banach–Steinhaus organize Banach-space analysis via Baire category and Zorn's lemma.

title: Compact operators and Fredholm alternative
blurb: Compact operators send bounded sets to precompact sets; Riesz–Schauder gives a discrete spectrum accumulating only at 0 and the Fredholm alternative for T−λI.

title: Spectral theorem viewpoint
blurb: Self-adjoint and normal operators are analyzed through spectral decompositions.

title: Spectrum classification: point, continuous, residual
blurb: The spectrum decomposes by which way T−λ fails to be invertible: not injective (point), dense-range-but-not-closed (continuous), or non-dense range (residual).

title: Riesz representation theorem
blurb: Every bounded linear functional on a Hilbert space is inner product with a unique vector; on Lᵖ it is integration against an Lᵩ density.

title: Weak and weak-* topologies
blurb: Coarsen the topology so dual pairings become continuous; Banach–Alaoglu then makes the unit ball of the dual weak-* compact.

title: Reflexivity
blurb: X is reflexive when the canonical embedding X → X** is surjective; Lᵖ (1<p<∞) and Hilbert spaces qualify; L¹, L∞, C[0,1], c₀ do not.

title: Distributions and test functions
blurb: The dual of Cc^∞ is the space of distributions — delta, principal value, tempered — extending differentiation and Fourier analysis beyond functions.

q: Which condition distinguishes a Banach space from an arbitrary normed space?
text: Which condition distinguishes a Banach space from an arbitrary normed space? A Banach space is a normed vector space in which every Cauchy sequence converges. Inner products give Hilbert spaces; compact unit balls characterize finite-dimensional normed spaces.

q: A norm $\|\cdot\|$ on a real vector space arises from an inner product if and only if it satisfies:
text: A norm $\|\cdot\|$ on a real vector space arises from an inner product if and only if it satisfies: The Jordan–von Neumann theorem: a norm comes from an inner product iff it obeys the parallelogram law. The inner product is then recovered by polarization.

q: In $\ell^2$, compute $\|x\|_2$ for $x=(1,2,2,0,0,\ldots)$.
The space $C[0,1]$ with the $L^2$ norm is NOT a Banach space. Which is the correct reason, and which is its completion?
text: The space $C[0,1]$ with the $L^2$ norm is NOT a Banach space. Which is the correct reason, and which is its completion? The functions $f_n(x)=x^n$ form a Cauchy sequence in $C[0,1]$ under $\|\cdot\|_{L^2}$, but their $L^2$ limit is the indicator $\mathbf{1}_{{1}}$, which is not continuous. Thus $C[0,1]$ is not closed under $L^2$ limits; its metric completion is $L^2[0,1]$.

q: Which is the correct order of steps for proving $\ell^2$ is a Hilbert space via the Riesz–Fischer approach?
text: Which is the correct order of steps for proving $\ell^2$ is a Hilbert space via the Riesz–Fischer approach? The standard proof: pass to a fast-Cauchy subsequence (so the telescoping series of norms converges), form the coordinatewise limit $x$, use the dominated convergence-style bound $\sum|x_k|^2\le(\|x_{n_1}\|+\sum 2^{-k})^2<\infty$ to put $x\in\ell^2$, then verify norm convergence. The unit ball of an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space is never compact.

q: Let $e_1,e_2\in\ell^2$ be the first two standard basis vectors. Using the polarization identity $\langle x,y\rangle=\tfrac{1}{4}(\|x+y\|^2-\|x-y\|^2)$ (real case), compute $\langle e_1+2e_2,\, 3e_1-e_2\rangle$.
text: Let $e_1,e_2\in\ell^2$ be the first two standard basis vectors. Using the polarization identity $\langle x,y\rangle=\tfrac{1}{4}(\|x+y\|^2-\|x-y\|^2)$ (real case), compute $\langle e_1+2e_2,\, 3e_1-e_2\rangle$. $\langle e_1+2e_2,3e_1-e_2\rangle=1\cdot 3+2\cdot(-1)=3-2=1$. Verify with polarization: $\|x+y\|^2=\|(4e_1+e_2)\|^2=17$, $\|x-y\|^2=\|(-2e_1+3e_2)\|^2=13$; $(17-13)/4=1$.

q: The operator norm of $T\in B(X,Y)$ is defined by $\|T\|=\sup_{\|x\|\le 1}\|Tx\|$. Which equivalent expression is INCORRECT?
text: The operator norm of $T\in B(X,Y)$ is defined by $\|T\|=\sup_{\|x\|\le 1}\|Tx\|$. Which equivalent expression is INCORRECT? The infimum is $0$ for any linear $T$ (take $x=0$). The first three expressions are all standard equivalent formulas for the operator norm.

q: On $\mathbb{R}^2$ with Euclidean norm, what is the operator norm of the diagonal matrix $T=\mathrm{diag}(3,-4)$?
text: On $\mathbb{R}^2$ with Euclidean norm, what is the operator norm of the diagonal matrix $T=\mathrm{diag}(3,-4)$? For a real diagonal matrix, $\|T\|=\max_i|t_i|$ since the singular values are $|t_i|$. Here $\max(3,4)=4$.

q: Compute the operator norm of $T=\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\2&1\end{pmatrix}$ (symmetric, so $\|T\|=\max|\lambda_i|$).
text: Compute the operator norm of $T=\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\2&1\end{pmatrix}$ (symmetric, so $\|T\|=\max|\lambda_i|$). Eigenvalues of the symmetric matrix satisfy $(1-\lambda)^2-4=0$, so $\lambda=3$ or $\lambda=-1$. The operator norm is $\max(|3|,|-1|)=3$.

q: $B(X,Y)$ is a Banach space whenever $Y$ is a Banach space — even if $X$ is merely normed. Which step of the proof of completeness of $B(X,Y)$ is MOST subtle?
text: $B(X,Y)$ is a Banach space whenever $Y$ is a Banach space — even if $X$ is merely normed. Which step of the proof of completeness of $B(X,Y)$ is MOST subtle? Pointwise convergence $T_n x\to Tx$ is given by completeness of $Y$ and the Cauchy property of $(T_n x)$. Upgrading to operator-norm convergence uses a uniform $\varepsilon$ over $\|x\|\le 1$: for $n,m\ge N$, $\|T_n x-T_m x\|\le\varepsilon\|x\|$; let $m\to\infty$ to get $\|T_n x-Tx\|\le\varepsilon\|x\|$, so $\|T_n-T\|\le\varepsilon$. The nonobvious step is operator-norm convergence, not pointwise convergence.

q: Let $T=\begin{pmatrix}3&4\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$ (Euclidean). Compute $\|T\|$ via the largest singular value.
text: Let $T=\begin{pmatrix}3&4\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$ (Euclidean). Compute $\|T\|$ via the largest singular value. $T^\top T=\begin{pmatrix}9&12\\12&16\end{pmatrix}$ has eigenvalues $25$ and $0$. Largest singular value is $\sqrt{25}=5$. Matches: the single nonzero row has norm $\sqrt{9+16}=5$. Compute $T^\top T$ and find its largest eigenvalue, then take square root.

q: Which operator on $\ell^2$ satisfies $\|T\|=1$ yet has $T^n\to 0$ in the strong operator topology (i.e. $T^n x\to 0$ for every $x$), while $\|T^n\|=1$ for all $n$?
text: Which operator on $\ell^2$ satisfies $\|T\|=1$ yet has $T^n\to 0$ in the strong operator topology (i.e. $T^n x\to 0$ for every $x$), while $\|T^n\|=1$ for all $n$? $S^*$ (left shift) has $\|S^*\|=1$ and $\|(S^*)^n\|=1$ for all $n$, yet $(S^*)^n x\to 0$ for every $x\in\ell^2$ since only the tail past index $n$ remains. This is the prototypical "strongly convergent but not uniformly convergent" example, showing operator-norm convergence is strictly stronger than pointwise/strong.

q: For a linear map $T\colon X\to Y$ between normed spaces, which of the following is NOT equivalent to the others?
text: For a linear map $T\colon X\to Y$ between normed spaces, which of the following is NOT equivalent to the others? Continuity at a point, continuity everywhere, and boundedness are equivalent for linear maps. Injectivity is unrelated: the zero map is bounded and continuous but not injective.

q: The adjoint $T^*$ of a bounded operator on a Hilbert space satisfies the $C^*$-identity:
text: The adjoint $T^*$ of a bounded operator on a Hilbert space satisfies the $C^*$-identity: The defining $C^*$-identity is $\|T^*T\|=\|T\|^2$. Combined with $\|T^*\|=\|T\|$, it pins down the Hilbert-space operator norm.

q: On $\ell^2$, let $S$ be the unilateral (right) shift $S(x_1,x_2,\ldots)=(0,x_1,x_2,\ldots)$. What is the operator norm $\|S\|$?
text: On $\ell^2$, let $S$ be the unilateral (right) shift $S(x_1,x_2,\ldots)=(0,x_1,x_2,\ldots)$. What is the operator norm $\|S\|$? $S$ is an isometry: $\|Sx\|_2=\|x\|_2$ for every $x\in\ell^2$, so $\|S\|=1$.

q: Let $S$ be the unilateral right shift on $\ell^2$ and $S^*$ its adjoint (left shift). Which statement about their composition is correct?
text: Let $S$ be the unilateral right shift on $\ell^2$ and $S^*$ its adjoint (left shift). Which statement about their composition is correct? $S^*$ is the left shift: $S^*(x_1,x_2,\ldots)=(x_2,x_3,\ldots)$. Then $S^*S(x)=S^*(0,x_1,x_2,\ldots)=(x_1,x_2,\ldots)=x$, so $S^*S=I$. But $(SS^*)(x_1,x_2,\ldots)=S(x_2,x_3,\ldots)=(0,x_2,x_3,\ldots)$, missing the first coordinate — so $SS^*=I-P_{e_1}$. $S$ is an isometry but not unitary: it is not surjective. One composition recovers $x$; the other first drops then reinserts a coordinate.

q: The Open Mapping Theorem requires both domain and codomain to be Banach spaces. Which example shows the theorem can fail if the domain is not complete?
text: The Open Mapping Theorem requires both domain and codomain to be Banach spaces. Which example shows the theorem can fail if the domain is not complete? $c_{00}$ (finitely supported sequences) with the $\ell^2$ norm is a normed space but not complete. The inclusion into $\ell^2$ is bounded and injective with dense range; a surjective bounded map from an incomplete space to a Banach space need not be open, so the inverse need not be bounded. The Open Mapping Theorem requires the domain to be Banach.

q: Let $T$ be the $2\times 2$ matrix $\begin{pmatrix}3&4\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$ viewed as an operator $\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2$ with the Euclidean norm. Compute $\|T\|$ (the operator norm), which equals the largest singular value of $T$.
text: Let $T$ be the $2\times 2$ matrix $\begin{pmatrix}3&4\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$ viewed as an operator $\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2$ with the Euclidean norm. Compute $\|T\|$ (the operator norm), which equals the largest singular value of $T$. The singular values of $T$ are the square roots of the eigenvalues of $T^\top T=\begin{pmatrix}9&12\\12&16\end{pmatrix}$. Its eigenvalues are $25$ and $0$, so the largest singular value — and hence the operator norm — is $\sqrt{25}=5$.

q: On a Hilbert space $H$, the adjoint $T^*$ of $T\in B(H)$ is the unique operator satisfying:
text: On a Hilbert space $H$, the adjoint $T^*$ of $T\in B(H)$ is the unique operator satisfying: The defining identity of the adjoint is $\langle Tx,y\rangle=\langle x,T^*y\rangle$. Existence and uniqueness follow from the Riesz representation theorem applied to $y\mapsto\langle Tx,y\rangle$.

q: Select all matrices that are self-adjoint (Hermitian) as operators on $\mathbb{C}^2$.
text: Select all matrices that are self-adjoint (Hermitian) as operators on $\mathbb{C}^2$. All three Pauli matrices are Hermitian (self-adjoint): $\sigma_k^*=\sigma_k$. The skew-symmetric matrix $J=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\-1&0\end{pmatrix}$ satisfies $J^*=-J$, so it is anti-self-adjoint, not self-adjoint.

q: For $T=\begin{pmatrix}2&i\\ 3&0\end{pmatrix}$ on $\mathbb{C}^2$, compute $(T^*)_{12}$ (the top-right entry of the adjoint). Give your answer as a real number (it happens to be real here).
text: For $T=\begin{pmatrix}2&i\\ 3&0\end{pmatrix}$ on $\mathbb{C}^2$, compute $(T^*)_{12}$ (the top-right entry of the adjoint). Give your answer as a real number (it happens to be real here). The adjoint is the conjugate transpose: $T^*=\begin{pmatrix}\bar 2&\bar 3\\ \overline{i}&\bar 0\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}2&3\\-i&0\end{pmatrix}$. So $(T^*)_{12}=3$.

q: Classify the operator $U=\frac{1}{\sqrt 2}\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\1&-1\end{pmatrix}$ (Hadamard matrix) on $\mathbb{R}^2$.
text: Classify the operator $U=\frac{1}{\sqrt 2}\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\1&-1\end{pmatrix}$ (Hadamard matrix) on $\mathbb{R}^2$. $U^\top=U$ (real symmetric, hence self-adjoint). Also $U^\top U=\tfrac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix}2&0\\0&2\end{pmatrix}=I$, so $U$ is unitary. A self-adjoint unitary is called a reflection: $U^2=I$, eigenvalues $\pm 1$. Compute $U^\top U$ and check whether $U^\top=U$.

q: Match each operator class to the defining identity between $T$ and $T^*$.
text: Match each operator class to the defining identity between $T$ and $T^*$. Self-adjoint: $T^*=T$. Skew-adjoint: $T^*=-T$. Normal: $T^*T=TT^*$ (strictly weaker — includes self-adjoint and unitary). Unitary: $T^*T=TT^*=I$ (i.e., $T$ is an isometric bijection).

q: On $L^2[0,1]$, consider the Volterra operator $(Vf)(x)=\int_0^x f(t)\,dt$. What is its adjoint $V^*$?
text: On $L^2[0,1]$, consider the Volterra operator $(Vf)(x)=\int_0^x f(t)\,dt$. What is its adjoint $V^*$? Change the order of integration: $\langle Vf,g\rangle=\int_0^1\int_0^x f(t)g(x)\,dt\,dx=\int_0^1 f(t)\int_t^1 g(x)\,dx\,dt=\langle f,V^*g\rangle$, so $(V^*g)(t)=\int_t^1 g(x)\,dx$. Volterra is NOT self-adjoint; in fact $V+V^*$ is self-adjoint and equals the rank-one operator $(Mf)(x)=\int_0^1 f$ (a constant function).

q: Match each of the big four theorems to its tagline.
text: Match each of the big four theorems to its tagline. Hahn–Banach extends functionals; Open Mapping gives openness (so inverses of bijections are bounded); Closed Graph turns a a-priori estimate into continuity; Banach–Steinhaus (uniform boundedness) upgrades pointwise bounds to norm bounds.

q: Three of the big four theorems are proved via the Baire category theorem. Which one is NOT?
text: Three of the big four theorems are proved via the Baire category theorem. Which one is NOT? Hahn–Banach rests on Zorn's lemma (or the axiom of choice), not on Baire category. The other three all use the Baire category theorem on a Banach space — a complete metric space is not a countable union of nowhere-dense sets.

q: A direct consequence of Hahn–Banach on a normed space $X$ is that $X^*$ separates points, i.e., for every $x\ne 0$ there is $\ell\in X^*$ with $\ell(x)\ne 0$. What is the standard one-line construction?
text: A direct consequence of Hahn–Banach on a normed space $X$ is that $X^*$ separates points, i.e., for every $x\ne 0$ there is $\ell\in X^*$ with $\ell(x)\ne 0$. What is the standard one-line construction? On the 1-dimensional subspace $\mathbb{K}x$, define $\ell_0(\alpha x)=\alpha\|x\|$ so that $|\ell_0|=\|\cdot\|$ there. Hahn–Banach extends $\ell_0$ to $\ell\in X^*$ with $\|\ell\|=1$ and $\ell(x)=\|x\|>0$.

q: The Closed Graph Theorem is often used contrapositively: if $T\colon X\to Y$ is linear between Banach spaces but UNbounded, then its graph is not closed. What does this translate to concretely?
text:
The Closed Graph Theorem is often used contrapositively: if $T\colon X	o Y$ is linear between Banach spaces but UNbounded, then its graph is not closed. What does this translate to concretely? If the graph is not closed, there is a sequence with $x_n	o x$ and $Tx_n	o y$ but $y
e Tx$. Taking $x=0$ gives $x_n	o 0$ with $Tx_n	o y
e T(0)=0$: a bounded $T$ cannot do this. Example: differentiation on $C[0,1]	o C[0,1]$ with domain $C^1[0,1]$ — $f_n(x)=\sin(n^2 x)/n	o 0$ uniformly but $f_n'	o$ nothing uniformly; graph is not closed in $C[0,1]	imes C[0,1]$. Graph-closedness says: if both $x_n	o x$ and $Tx_n	o y$, then $y=Tx$. Failure means $y$ exists but $y
e Tx$.

q: A classical application of Banach–Steinhaus (the uniform boundedness principle): there exists a continuous $2\pi$-periodic function whose Fourier series diverges at a dense set of points. Which functional is 'resonating' in the proof?
text: A classical application of Banach–Steinhaus (the uniform boundedness principle): there exists a continuous $2\pi$-periodic function whose Fourier series diverges at a dense set of points. Which functional is 'resonating' in the proof? Banach–Steinhaus says: if $\sup_N\|L_N\|=\infty$, then $\{f:\sup_N|L_N(f)|<\infty\}$ is meager. The Lebesgue constants $\|L_N\|=\int_{-\pi}^\pi|D_N(t)|dt/(2\pi)$ grow like $\log N$, so the set of $f$ with $S_N f(0)$ bounded is meager — and for generic $f$, the Fourier series diverges at $0$. By translation, divergence occurs on a dense set.

q: Prove: if $T\colon X\to Y$ is a bounded linear bijection between Banach spaces, then $T^{-1}$ is bounded. Which step correctly continues after step 2?
text: Prove: if $T\colon X\to Y$ is a bounded linear bijection between Banach spaces, then $T^{-1}$ is bounded. Which step correctly continues after step 2? Step 3 unpacks: $T^{-1}$ continuous $\iff (T^{-1})^{-1}(U)=T(U)$ is open for every open $U$ — exactly openness of $T$. So openness of $T$ (step 2) IS the boundedness of $T^{-1}$. This is the Inverse Mapping Theorem as a corollary of the Open Mapping Theorem.

q: A bounded operator $T\colon X\to Y$ is compact if:
text: A bounded operator $T\colon X\to Y$ is compact if: A compact operator sends the unit ball (bounded) to a relatively compact (precompact) set. Equivalently, every bounded sequence $(x_n)$ has $(Tx_{n_k})$ norm-convergent. This is far stronger than continuity.

q: Select all operators that are compact on an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space.
text: Select all operators that are compact on an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space. The identity is not compact on an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space (the unit ball is not compact — Riesz's lemma). Finite-rank operators are always compact. Diagonal operators with $\lambda_n\to 0$ are compact (norm-limit of finite-rank truncations). $\lambda_n=1+1/n\to 1\ne 0$, so not compact.

q: The Fredholm alternative says: for compact $T$ and $\lambda\ne 0$, exactly one of the following holds for $(T-\lambda I)x=y$:
text: The Fredholm alternative says: for compact $T$ and $\lambda\ne 0$, exactly one of the following holds for $(T-\lambda I)x=y$: Fredholm alternative dichotomy: for $\lambda\ne 0$ in $\sigma(T)\cup\rho(T)$, either $T-\lambda I$ is a bijection, or $\dim\ker(T-\lambda I)=\dim\mathrm{coker}(T-\lambda I)<\infty$ and solvability is equivalent to a finite family of orthogonality conditions.

q: Which statement about the spectrum of a compact operator $T$ on an infinite-dimensional Banach space is FALSE?
text: Which statement about the spectrum of a compact operator $T$ on an infinite-dimensional Banach space is FALSE? The spectrum of a compact operator is typically disconnected: a discrete sequence $\{\lambda_n\}_n$ accumulating only at $0$, together with $0$ itself. For instance, $T=\mathrm{diag}(1,1/2,1/3,\ldots)$ on $\ell^2$ has $\sigma(T)=\{0,1,1/2,1/3,\ldots\}$, highly disconnected. Compact operators can have two distinct isolated nonzero eigenvalues.

q: Rellich's theorem: the inclusion $H^1_0(\Omega)\hookrightarrow L^2(\Omega)$ for a bounded domain $\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ is compact. Combined with the spectral theorem, what does this give for the Dirichlet Laplacian $-\Delta$ on $\Omega$?
text: Rellich's theorem: the inclusion $H^1_0(\Omega)\hookrightarrow L^2(\Omega)$ for a bounded domain $\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ is compact. Combined with the spectral theorem, what does this give for the Dirichlet Laplacian $-\Delta$ on $\Omega$? The resolvent $(-\Delta)^{-1}$ (Green's operator) is bounded $L^2\to H^1_0$ by Lax–Milgram, and $H^1_0\hookrightarrow L^2$ is compact by Rellich. So $(-\Delta)^{-1}$ is compact self-adjoint on $L^2$; Hilbert–Schmidt gives an ONB of eigenfunctions with eigenvalues of $(-\Delta)^{-1}$ accumulating at $0$, i.e. eigenvalues of $-\Delta$ tending to $+\infty$.

q: Consider the diagonal operator $T$ on $\ell^2$ with $T e_n=\frac{1}{n}e_n$. Compute the Hilbert–Schmidt norm $\|T\|_{HS}^2=\sum_n\|Te_n\|^2=\sum_n 1/n^2$. Give the exact value $\pi^2/6$ numerically.
text: Consider the diagonal operator $T$ on $\ell^2$ with $T e_n=\frac{1}{n}e_n$. Compute the Hilbert–Schmidt norm $\|T\|_{HS}^2=\sum_n\|Te_n\|^2=\sum_n 1/n^2$. Give the exact value $\pi^2/6$ numerically. $\|T\|_{HS}^2=\sum_{n=1}^\infty 1/n^2=\pi^2/6\approx 1.6449$. Since the HS norm is finite, $T$ is Hilbert–Schmidt (hence compact). By contrast, the trace norm $\|T\|_1=\sum 1/n=\infty$, so $T$ is NOT trace-class — the hierarchy trace-class $\subsetneq$ HS $\subsetneq$ compact is strict.

q: The spectrum of a self-adjoint bounded operator $T$ on a Hilbert space is always:
text: The spectrum of a self-adjoint bounded operator $T$ on a Hilbert space is always: Self-adjoint operators have real spectrum. Unitary operators have spectrum on the unit circle; bounded operator spectra on complex Hilbert spaces are always nonempty and compact.

q: The multiplication form of the spectral theorem says every bounded self-adjoint $T$ on a separable Hilbert space is unitarily equivalent to:
text: The multiplication form of the spectral theorem says every bounded self-adjoint $T$ on a separable Hilbert space is unitarily equivalent to: After a unitary change of coordinates, every bounded self-adjoint operator becomes multiplication by a bounded real-valued function on an $L^2$ space.

q: Let $T=\begin{pmatrix}0&-i\\ i&0\end{pmatrix}=\sigma_y$ acting on $\mathbb{C}^2$. Its spectrum is $\{-1,+1\}$. Give the product of its two eigenvalues as $a+bi$.
text: Let $T=\begin{pmatrix}0&-i\\ i&0\end{pmatrix}=\sigma_y$ acting on $\mathbb{C}^2$. Its spectrum is $\{-1,+1\}$. Give the product of its two eigenvalues as $a+bi$. The Pauli matrix $\sigma_y$ has eigenvalues $+1$ and $-1$; their product equals $\det(\sigma_y)=-1=-1+0i$, matching the general fact $\det T=\prod\lambda_i$.

q: Which is the correct sequence of steps to prove that eigenvalues of a self-adjoint operator $T$ on a Hilbert space are real?
text: Which is the correct sequence of steps to prove that eigenvalues of a self-adjoint operator $T$ on a Hilbert space are real? The elementary proof: $\langle Tv,v\rangle=\lambda\|v\|^2$ from the eigenvalue equation, and $\langle Tv,v\rangle=\langle v,T^*v\rangle=\langle v,Tv\rangle=\overline{\lambda}\|v\|^2$ from self-adjointness. So $\lambda=\overline{\lambda}$, i.e., $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$. The spectral theorem proof is circular (it uses real spectrum as input).

q: Let $M_g$ be the multiplication operator $(M_g f)(x)=g(x)f(x)$ on $L^2[0,1]$ with $g(x)=x$. Which statement about its spectrum is correct?
text: Let $M_g$ be the multiplication operator $(M_g f)(x)=g(x)f(x)$ on $L^2[0,1]$ with $g(x)=x$. Which statement about its spectrum is correct? $M_g-\lambda$ is invertible iff $g-\lambda$ is bounded away from $0$ a.e., i.e., $\lambda\notin\operatorname{ess-range}(g)=[0,1]$. So $\sigma(M_g)=[0,1]$. For any $\lambda\in[0,1]$, $M_g-\lambda$ is injective (the set $\{x=\lambda\}$ has measure $0$), so $\lambda$ is not an eigenvalue — yet $\lambda$ is in the spectrum because $M_g-\lambda$ is not bounded below.

q: The spectral projections of $T=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}$ onto eigenspaces $\lambda=+1$ and $\lambda=-1$ are $P_+=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$ and $P_-=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix}$. Compute $\|T\|^2=\|T^*T\|$ using the spectral decomposition $T=P_+-P_-$.
text: The spectral projections of $T=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}$ onto eigenspaces $\lambda=+1$ and $\lambda=-1$ are $P_+=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$ and $P_-=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix}$. Compute $\|T\|^2=\|T^*T\|$ using the spectral decomposition $T=P_+-P_-$. $T^*T=T^2=(P_+-P_-)^2=P_++P_-=I$ (since $P_+P_-=0$ and $P_\pm^2=P_\pm$). Thus $\|T^*T\|=\|I\|=1$, so $\|T\|^2=1$ and $\|T\|=1$. This matches $\|T\|=\max(|+1|,|-1|)=1$.

q: For $T\in B(X)$ and $\lambda\in\sigma(T)$, classify $\lambda$ by WHY $T-\lambda I$ fails to be invertible. Match each row to its definition.
text: For $T\in B(X)$ and $\lambda\in\sigma(T)$, classify $\lambda$ by WHY $T-\lambda I$ fails to be invertible. Match each row to its definition. Point = $\lambda$ is an eigenvalue. Continuous = injective, dense range, not closed (think multiplication by $x$ on $L^2[0,1]$). Residual = injective, range not dense (classic: $\lambda=0$ for the right shift).

q: On $\ell^2$, let $S$ be the right shift. What is $\sigma_p(S)$ (point spectrum)?
text: On $\ell^2$, let $S$ be the right shift. What is $\sigma_p(S)$ (point spectrum)? If $Sx=\lambda x$ with $x\ne 0$, then $(0,x_1,x_2,\ldots)=\lambda(x_1,x_2,\ldots)$ gives $\lambda x_1=0$ and $\lambda x_{n+1}=x_n$. If $\lambda=0$, then $x=0$; if $\lambda\ne 0$, then $x_1=0$ and inductively $x=0$. So $S$ has no eigenvalues — $\sigma_p(S)=\emptyset$, yet $\sigma(S)=\overline{D(0,1)}$.

q: The RESOLVENT set $\rho(T)$ and the spectrum $\sigma(T)$ partition $\mathbb{C}$. Which is TRUE?
text: The RESOLVENT set $\rho(T)$ and the spectrum $\sigma(T)$ partition $\mathbb{C}$. Which is TRUE? The resolvent set is open (perturbing $T-\lambda I$ slightly keeps invertibility by a Neumann-series argument), so the spectrum is closed. It is also bounded by $\|T\|$ (for $|\lambda|>\|T\|$, the Neumann series $\sum(T/\lambda)^n/\lambda$ inverts $T-\lambda I$). Hence $\sigma(T)$ is compact, non-empty, and countable iff dictated by extra structure (e.g., compactness of $T$).

q: On $\ell^2$, the right shift $S$ and the left shift $S^*$. Which statement correctly classifies $\lambda=0$ in each spectrum?
text: On $\ell^2$, the right shift $S$ and the left shift $S^*$. Which statement correctly classifies $\lambda=0$ in each spectrum? $S$ is injective but $\mathrm{ran}(S)=\{x\in\ell^2:x_1=0\}$ is closed but not dense, so $0\in\sigma_r(S)$. $S^*e_1=0$, so $e_1$ is an eigenvector of $S^*$ with eigenvalue $0$, giving $0\in\sigma_p(S^*)$. This is the canonical example where residual spectrum of $T$ = conjugate of point spectrum of $T^*$. Compute the range of $S$ and the kernel of $S^*$ separately.

q: For self-adjoint $T$ on a Hilbert space, which pieces of the spectrum CAN be nonempty?
text: For self-adjoint $T$ on a Hilbert space, which pieces of the spectrum CAN be nonempty? A theorem: self-adjoint operators have empty residual spectrum. Proof: if $\lambda\in\sigma_r(T)$ (real, since $\sigma(T)\subset\mathbb{R}$), then $\mathrm{ran}(T-\lambda)^\perp=\ker(T^*-\lambda)^*=\ker(T-\lambda)\ne\{0\}$, forcing $\lambda\in\sigma_p(T)$ — contradiction. So for self-adjoint $T$, spectrum splits into point $\cup$ continuous. Use the relation $\sigma_r(T)\subseteq\overline{\sigma_p(T^*)}$.

q: On $L^2[0,1]$, the multiplication operator $(M_x f)(x)=xf(x)$ has $\sigma(M_x)=[0,1]$. What is the Lebesgue measure of its POINT spectrum $\sigma_p(M_x)$?
text: On $L^2[0,1]$, the multiplication operator $(M_x f)(x)=xf(x)$ has $\sigma(M_x)=[0,1]$. What is the Lebesgue measure of its POINT spectrum $\sigma_p(M_x)$? $M_x$ has no eigenvalues: $(M_x-\lambda)f=0$ forces $f(x)=0$ for a.e. $x\ne\lambda$, so $f=0$ in $L^2$. Thus $\sigma_p(M_x)=\emptyset$, and its measure is $0$. The entire spectrum $[0,1]$ is continuous spectrum: $M_x-\lambda$ is injective with dense range but not surjective for $\lambda\in[0,1]$.

q: The Riesz representation theorem on a Hilbert space $H$ says every $\ell\in H^*$ has the form $\ell(x)=\langle x,y\rangle$ for a unique $y\in H$. The map $y\mapsto\ell$ is:
text: The Riesz representation theorem on a Hilbert space $H$ says every $\ell\in H^*$ has the form $\ell(x)=\langle x,y\rangle$ for a unique $y\in H$. The map $y\mapsto\ell$ is: Over $\mathbb{C}$, the map is conjugate-linear because $\langle x,\alpha y\rangle=\bar\alpha\langle x,y\rangle$; it is an isometric bijection, giving the canonical antilinear identification $H\cong H^*$.

q: For $1<p<\infty$ and $\tfrac1p+\tfrac1q=1$ on a $\sigma$-finite measure space, the dual $(L^p)^*$ is isometrically identified with:
text: For $1<p<\infty$ and $\tfrac1p+\tfrac1q=1$ on a $\sigma$-finite measure space, the dual $(L^p)^*$ is isometrically identified with: The pairing $\langle f,g\rangle=\int fg\,d\mu$ is bounded by Hölder with constant $\|g\|_q$, and every bounded functional on $L^p$ arises this way — this is the content of the Riesz representation for $L^p$.

q: On $\ell^2$ let $\ell(x)=2x_1+x_2-2x_3$ for $x=(x_1,x_2,x_3,0,\ldots)$. By Riesz, $\ell(x)=\langle x,y\rangle$ for some $y\in\ell^2$. Compute $\|\ell\|=\|y\|_2$.
text: On $\ell^2$ let $\ell(x)=2x_1+x_2-2x_3$ for $x=(x_1,x_2,x_3,0,\ldots)$. By Riesz, $\ell(x)=\langle x,y\rangle$ for some $y\in\ell^2$. Compute $\|\ell\|=\|y\|_2$. The representing vector is $y=(2,1,-2,0,\ldots)$ (real, so no conjugation), and $\|y\|_2=\sqrt{4+1+4}=\sqrt{9}=3$. Riesz makes $\|\ell\|_{H^*}=\|y\|_H$.

q: The Riesz theorem fails for $p=1$: $(L^1)^*\cong L^\infty$ but $(L^\infty)^*\not\cong L^1$. Which is the standard counterexample showing $(L^\infty[0,1])^*$ is strictly larger than $L^1[0,1]$?
text: The Riesz theorem fails for $p=1$: $(L^1)^*\cong L^\infty$ but $(L^\infty)^*\not\cong L^1$. Which is the standard counterexample showing $(L^\infty[0,1])^*$ is strictly larger than $L^1[0,1]$? Point evaluations are well-defined on $L^\infty$ (up to measure-zero sets they can be realized via limits and Banach limits) and give bounded linear functionals. The Banach limit — a positive linear functional on $\ell^\infty$ extending $\lim$ from convergent sequences — is the classical example: it is bounded, not $w^*$-continuous, and lies strictly outside the image of $\ell^1\hookrightarrow(\ell^\infty)^*$.

q: Prove step-by-step that the Riesz vector $y$ in $\ell(x)=\langle x,y\rangle$ is unique. Which argument is correct?
text: Prove step-by-step that the Riesz vector $y$ in $\ell(x)=\langle x,y\rangle$ is unique. Which argument is correct? The standard one-line uniqueness argument: if $\langle x,y_1\rangle=\langle x,y_2\rangle$ for all $x$, then $y:=y_1-y_2$ satisfies $\langle x,y\rangle=0$ for all $x$; taking $x=y$ gives $\|y\|^2=0$, forcing $y=0$. Existence uses the projection theorem onto a closed subspace.

q: On $L^2[0,1]$, the functional $\ell(f)=\int_0^1 (3x^2-1)f(x)\,dx$ is represented by $y(x)=3x^2-1$. Compute $\|\ell\|=\|y\|_{L^2}$ to three decimal places (the answer is $\sqrt{\int_0^1(3x^2-1)^2dx}$).
text: On $L^2[0,1]$, the functional $\ell(f)=\int_0^1 (3x^2-1)f(x)\,dx$ is represented by $y(x)=3x^2-1$. Compute $\|\ell\|=\|y\|_{L^2}$ to three decimal places (the answer is $\sqrt{\int_0^1(3x^2-1)^2dx}$). $\int_0^1(3x^2-1)^2dx=\int_0^1(9x^4-6x^2+1)dx=9/5-6/3+1=9/5-2+1=9/5-1=4/5$. So $\|y\|_{L^2}=\sqrt{4/5}=2/\sqrt{5}\approx 0.6325$.

q: A sequence $(x_n)$ in a Banach space $X$ converges weakly to $x$ when:
text: A sequence $(x_n)$ in a Banach space $X$ converges weakly to $x$ when: Weak convergence $x_n\rightharpoonup x$ means $\ell(x_n)\to\ell(x)$ for every bounded linear functional $\ell$. Norm convergence is strictly stronger.

q: The Banach–Alaoglu theorem states that the closed unit ball of $X^*$ is:
text: The Banach–Alaoglu theorem states that the closed unit ball of $X^*$ is: Banach–Alaoglu: $B_{X^*}$ is compact in the weak-* topology, proved by embedding it in the product $\prod_{x\in X}\overline{D(0,\|x\|)}$ and applying Tychonoff. Norm-compactness of the unit ball fails in every infinite-dimensional Banach space.

q: In $\ell^2$, let $e_n$ be the $n$-th standard basis vector. The sequence $(e_n)$ does NOT converge in norm, but does converge weakly to a unique limit $w\in\ell^2$. Compute $\|w\|_2$.
text: In $\ell^2$, let $e_n$ be the $n$-th standard basis vector. The sequence $(e_n)$ does NOT converge in norm, but does converge weakly to a unique limit $w\in\ell^2$. Compute $\|w\|_2$. For each $y\in\ell^2$, $\langle e_n,y\rangle=\overline{y_n}\to 0$, so $e_n\rightharpoonup 0$. Yet $\|e_n-0\|_2=1$, so there is no norm convergence — a textbook illustration that weak limit $\ne$ strong limit.

q: Mazur's theorem states that for a convex set $C$ in a Banach space, weak closure equals norm closure. Which consequence is most directly useful in optimization?
text: Mazur's theorem states that for a convex set $C$ in a Banach space, weak closure equals norm closure. Which consequence is most directly useful in optimization? Mazur: for convex $C$, weak-closure $=$ norm-closure. The key corollary: if $C$ is convex and norm-closed, it is also weakly closed, so weak limits of sequences in $C$ remain in $C$. This is indispensable in calculus of variations — one finds minimizers as weak limits in a reflexive space, then uses convexity to stay inside feasible sets.

q: Let $X=\ell^1$ and consider its dual $X^*=\ell^\infty$. A sequence $(\ell_n)$ in $B_{\ell^\infty}$ converges weak-* to $\ell$ iff:
text: Let $X=\ell^1$ and consider its dual $X^*=\ell^\infty$. A sequence $(\ell_n)$ in $B_{\ell^\infty}$ converges weak-* to $\ell$ iff: Weak-* convergence in $X^*=\ell^\infty$ is convergence on every $x\in X=\ell^1$. Norm convergence (choice A) is strictly stronger. Convergence on all of $\ell^\infty$ (choice C) would be weak convergence in $\ell^\infty$ itself — a finer topology. Concretely, $\delta_n\rightharpoonup^* 0$ in $\ell^\infty$ since $\delta_n(x)=x_n\to 0$ for every $x\in\ell^1$, but $\|\delta_n\|_\infty=1\not\to 0$.

q: In $L^2[0,2\pi]$, consider $f_n(x)=\sin(nx)$. One can show $f_n\rightharpoonup 0$ weakly. Compute $\lim_{n\to\infty}\int_0^{2\pi}\sin(nx)\cdot x\,dx$ using integration by parts (this gives the value of the weak limit applied to $g(x)=x$, confirming $f_n\rightharpoonup 0$).
text: In $L^2[0,2\pi]$, consider $f_n(x)=\sin(nx)$. One can show $f_n\rightharpoonup 0$ weakly. Compute $\lim_{n\to\infty}\int_0^{2\pi}\sin(nx)\cdot x\,dx$ using integration by parts (this gives the value of the weak limit applied to $g(x)=x$, confirming $f_n\rightharpoonup 0$). $\int_0^{2\pi} x\sin(nx)\,dx=\left[-\frac{x\cos(nx)}{n}\right]_0^{2\pi}+\frac{1}{n}\int_0^{2\pi}\cos(nx)\,dx=-\frac{2\pi}{n}+0\to 0$ as $n\to\infty$. Since this holds for all $g\in L^2[0,2\pi]$ (by Riemann–Lebesgue), $f_n\rightharpoonup 0$ weakly, even though $\|f_n\|_{L^2}=\sqrt{\pi}$ for all $n$.

q: The canonical embedding $J\colon X\to X^{**}$ sends $x$ to the functional $Jx(\ell)=\ell(x)$. This map is always:
text: The canonical embedding $J\colon X\to X^{**}$ sends $x$ to the functional $Jx(\ell)=\ell(x)$. This map is always: Hahn–Banach guarantees enough functionals to isometrically embed $X$ in $X^{**}$: $\|Jx\|=\sup_{\|\ell\|\le 1}|\ell(x)|=\|x\|$. $X$ is reflexive when $J$ is additionally surjective.

q: Which of the following spaces are reflexive?
text: Which of the following spaces are reflexive? $\ell^p,L^p$ for $1<p<\infty$ and Hilbert spaces are reflexive. $L^1$ is not ($L^{1**}=L^{\infty *}\supsetneq L^1$). $C[0,1]$ is not (its dual is measures, whose dual is much larger). $\ell^\infty$ is not (its dual contains Banach limits outside $\ell^1$).

q: An equivalent formulation of reflexivity (Kakutani): $X$ is reflexive iff its closed unit ball is:
text: An equivalent formulation of reflexivity (Kakutani): $X$ is reflexive iff its closed unit ball is: Kakutani's theorem: $X$ is reflexive iff $B_X$ is weakly compact. Norm-compactness happens only in finite dimensions. Eberlein–Šmulian further says this weak compactness can be checked sequentially: $B_X$ weakly compact iff every bounded sequence has a weakly convergent subsequence.

q: Why is $c_0$ (sequences tending to $0$, with sup norm) NOT reflexive? Pick the crispest reason.
text: Why is $c_0$ (sequences tending to $0$, with sup norm) NOT reflexive? Pick the crispest reason. The canonical chain $c_0\hookrightarrow c_0^{**}\cong\ell^\infty$ is strict because sequences not tending to $0$ (e.g., constants) lie in $\ell^\infty\setminus c_0$. So $J$ is not surjective. The sequence $(1,1,\ldots)\in\ell^\infty$ corresponds to a functional on $c_0^*=\ell^1$ that is NOT evaluation at any element of $c_0$. Chase the duals twice.

q: Which theorem about reflexive spaces is the foundation of the direct method in the calculus of variations?
text: Which theorem about reflexive spaces is the foundation of the direct method in the calculus of variations? In a reflexive Banach space, the unit ball is weakly compact, so any bounded sequence has a weakly convergent subsequence. Coupling this with weak lower semicontinuity of a convex energy functional $F$ produces a minimizer: take a minimizing sequence, extract a weak limit $x^*$, and use $F(x^*)\le\liminf F(x_n)$.

q: On any reflexive Banach space $X$, an additional regularity property holds automatically for every convex continuous function. Which one?
text: On any reflexive Banach space $X$, an additional regularity property holds automatically for every convex continuous function. Which one? For a nonempty closed convex $C\subset X$ reflexive and any $x\in X$, the infimum $\inf_{c\in C}\|x-c\|$ is attained. Take a minimizing sequence $(c_n)$; it is bounded so has a weakly convergent subsequence $c_{n_k}\rightharpoonup c^*\in C$ (Mazur + norm-closure of convex $C$); weak lower semicontinuity of the norm gives $\|x-c^*\|\le\liminf\|x-c_{n_k}\|$. Non-reflexive spaces generally lack this. Use weak compactness of bounded sets + weak lower semicontinuity of the norm.

q: A distribution on $\mathbb{R}$ is:
text: A distribution on $\mathbb{R}$ is: The space $\mathcal{D}'(\mathbb{R})=(C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R}))^*$ is, by definition, the continuous linear functionals on test functions (with a specific inductive-limit topology). Every locally integrable function $f$ defines a distribution $\phi\mapsto\int f\phi$, but distributions are strictly more general — they include $\delta$ and its derivatives.

q: The Dirac delta distribution $\delta_0$ is defined by $\langle\delta_0,\phi\rangle=\phi(0)$. Which sequence of $L^1$ functions $f_n$ converges to $\delta_0$ in the distributional sense?
text: The Dirac delta distribution $\delta_0$ is defined by $\langle\delta_0,\phi\rangle=\phi(0)$. Which sequence of $L^1$ functions $f_n$ converges to $\delta_0$ in the distributional sense? $f_n=n\cdot\mathbf{1}_{[0,1/n]}$ has $\int f_n=1$ and mass concentrating at $0$. For $\phi\in C_c^\infty$, $\int f_n\phi=n\int_0^{1/n}\phi(x)dx\to\phi(0)$ by continuity. Hence $f_n\to\delta_0$ in $\mathcal{D}'$.

q: Compute $\langle\delta_0^{(1)},\phi\rangle$ where $\delta_0^{(1)}$ is the distributional derivative of $\delta_0$ and $\phi(x)=x^2-3x+5$. Recall $\langle\delta_0^{(1)},\phi\rangle=-\phi'(0)$.
text: Compute $\langle\delta_0^{(1)},\phi\rangle$ where $\delta_0^{(1)}$ is the distributional derivative of $\delta_0$ and $\phi(x)=x^2-3x+5$. Recall $\langle\delta_0^{(1)},\phi\rangle=-\phi'(0)$. Distributional differentiation: $\langle T',\phi\rangle=-\langle T,\phi'\rangle$. For $T=\delta_0$, $\langle\delta_0',\phi\rangle=-\langle\delta_0,\phi'\rangle=-\phi'(0)$. With $\phi(x)=x^2-3x+5$, $\phi'(x)=2x-3$, $\phi'(0)=-3$, so $-\phi'(0)=3$.

q: The principal value distribution $\mathrm{p.v.}\,\tfrac{1}{x}$ is defined by $\langle\mathrm{p.v.}\tfrac{1}{x},\phi\rangle=\lim_{\varepsilon\to 0^+}\int_{|x|>\varepsilon}\phi(x)/x\,dx$. Which identity relates the distributional derivative of $\log|x|$ to it?
text: The principal value distribution $\mathrm{p.v.}\,\tfrac{1}{x}$ is defined by $\langle\mathrm{p.v.}\tfrac{1}{x},\phi\rangle=\lim_{\varepsilon\to 0^+}\int_{|x|>\varepsilon}\phi(x)/x\,dx$. Which identity relates the distributional derivative of $\log|x|$ to it? For $\phi\in C_c^\infty$: $\langle(\log|x|)',\phi\rangle=-\langle\log|x|,\phi'\rangle=-\lim_{\varepsilon\to 0}\int_{|x|>\varepsilon}\log|x|\phi'(x)dx$. Integrate by parts; boundary terms $\log\varepsilon(\phi(\varepsilon)-\phi(-\varepsilon))\to 0$ because $\phi(\varepsilon)-\phi(-\varepsilon)=O(\varepsilon)$. The remaining integral is $\lim_{\varepsilon\to 0}\int_{|x|>\varepsilon}\phi(x)/x\,dx=\langle\mathrm{p.v.}\tfrac{1}{x},\phi\rangle$. The singularity at $0$ is regularized by the principal-value cancellation. Pair the derivative with a test function and integrate by parts away from $0$, taking a symmetric limit.

q: Tempered distributions $\mathcal{S}'(\mathbb{R}^n)$ are the dual of Schwartz space $\mathcal{S}$ (rapidly decreasing smooth functions). Which property makes them the correct setting for Fourier analysis?
text: Tempered distributions $\mathcal{S}'(\mathbb{R}^n)$ are the dual of Schwartz space $\mathcal{S}$ (rapidly decreasing smooth functions). Which property makes them the correct setting for Fourier analysis? The Fourier transform $\mathcal{F}\colon\mathcal{S}\to\mathcal{S}$ is a topological isomorphism. By duality, $\hat{T}\in\mathcal{S}'$ is defined by $\langle\hat T,\phi\rangle=\langle T,\hat\phi\rangle$. This extends Fourier to objects with no Fourier integral as a function: $\delta$, polynomials, $\mathrm{p.v.}\tfrac{1}{x}$. It is the foundation of PDE via Fourier multipliers. Arbitrary distributions in $\mathcal{D}'$ do NOT have Fourier transforms in general.

q: Prove that $x\cdot\delta_0=0$ as a distribution on $\mathbb{R}$. Pick the correct next step.
text: Prove that $x\cdot\delta_0=0$ as a distribution on $\mathbb{R}$. Pick the correct next step. The function $g(x)=x\phi(x)$ vanishes at $0$: $g(0)=0$. So $\langle x\delta_0,\phi\rangle=0$ for every $\phi$, which is the definition of the zero distribution. This identity $x\delta_0=0$ is the distributional version of the algebraic fact — and it is why $\delta_0$ is a genuine new object, not a function: no function $f$ satisfies $xf=0$ a.e. yet $\int f=1$.

title: 1. From a space to its algebra of functions
16. The Grothendieck parallel — Spec of a ring
C*-algebras and examples
blurb: C*-algebras abstract norm-closed *-algebras of operators and keep spectral control.

title: Spectrum in Banach algebras
blurb: For $a$ in a unital Banach algebra, $\sigma(a)$ is a non-empty compact subset of $\overline{B(0,\|a\|)}\subset\mathbb{C}$, and the spectral radius formula $r(a)=\lim_n\|a^n\|^{1/n}$ recovers a complex-analytic invariant from the norm.

title: Continuous functional calculus
blurb: For a normal element $a$ of a unital $C^*$-algebra, $C(\sigma(a))\cong C^*(a,1)$ via $f\mapsto f(a)$, upgrading Riesz–Dunford to an isometric $*$-isomorphism and powering the spectral theorem.

title: Commutative duality and spectra
blurb: Commutative C*-algebras correspond to compact spaces via Gelfand duality.

title: Noncommutative dictionary
blurb: Noncommutative $C^*$-algebras model noncommutative spaces: points become pure states, measures become states, bundles become projective modules. The irrational rotation algebra $A_\theta$ is the canonical tutorial.

title: Von Neumann algebras and the double commutant
blurb: A unital $*$-subalgebra $M\subseteq B(H)$ is a von Neumann algebra iff $M=M''$ (double-commutant theorem); equivalently it is closed in the strong/weak operator topologies.

title: Factors and type classification
blurb: A factor has trivial center $Z(M)=\mathbb{C}\cdot 1$; Murray–von Neumann sort factors into types I$_n$, I$_\infty$, II$_1$, II$_\infty$, and III, detected by traces and minimal projections.

title: Murray–von Neumann equivalence and dimension
blurb: Projections $p\sim q$ in $M$ iff there is $v\in M$ with $v^*v=p$, $vv^*=q$; on a II$_1$ factor this yields a continuous dimension function $d\colon \mathrm{Proj}(M)\to[0,1]$.

title: Positive elements
blurb: Positive elements $a=b^*b$ form a closed cone, detected by $a=a^*$ with $\sigma(a)\subseteq[0,\infty)$, and induce the partial order $a\le b\iff b-a\ge 0$ (pointwise in $C(X)$, Löwner in $B(H)$).

title: States and pure states
blurb: A state is a positive linear functional with $\phi(1)=1$; the state space $S(A)$ is weak-$*$ compact convex, and its extreme points — the pure states — play the role of points (Borel probability measures on $X$ when $A=C(X)$).

title: GNS construction
blurb: From a state $\omega$ on $A$ build $(\pi_\omega, H_\omega, \Omega_\omega)$: take $\langle a,b\rangle=\omega(b^*a)$, quotient the null ideal, complete, and let $A$ act by left multiplication; $\omega(a)=\langle\Omega_\omega,\pi_\omega(a)\Omega_\omega\rangle$.

title: Approximate units and amenability
blurb: Every $C^*$-algebra has an approximate unit $(e_\lambda)$ with $e_\lambda\ge 0$, $\|e_\lambda\|\le 1$, and $e_\lambda a\to a$; nuclearity and amenability refine this by asking for finite-dimensional approximations.

q: The defining $C^*$-identity on a Banach $*$-algebra is:
text: The defining $C^*$-identity on a Banach $*$-algebra is: The $C^*$-identity $\|a^*a\|=\|a\|^2$ is what makes $C^*$-algebras rigid: it forces the norm to be determined by the algebraic structure.

q: Which element of a unital $C^*$-algebra is necessarily normal?
text: Which element of a unital $C^*$-algebra is necessarily normal? Self-adjoints satisfy $a^*a = a^2 = aa^*$ and unitaries satisfy $a^*a=1=aa^*$. Both are normal; normality includes strictly more than just these two classes.

q: In the $C^*$-algebra $M_2(\mathbb{C})$, the operator norm of the self-adjoint matrix $\begin{pmatrix}3&0\\0&-4\end{pmatrix}$ equals:
text: In the $C^*$-algebra $M_2(\mathbb{C})$, the operator norm of the self-adjoint matrix $\begin{pmatrix}3&0\\0&-4\end{pmatrix}$ equals: For a self-adjoint operator the norm equals the spectral radius: $\|a\|=\max|\lambda_i|=\max(|3|,|-4|)=4$.

q: Which statement is a correct counterexample showing the $C^*$-identity FAILS for a general Banach $*$-algebra?
text: Which statement is a correct counterexample showing the $C^*$-identity FAILS for a general Banach $*$-algebra? $M_2(\mathbb{C})$ with the Frobenius norm is a Banach $*$-algebra but not a $C^*$-algebra. For $A=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$: $A^*A=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\1&1\end{pmatrix}$, $\|A^*A\|_F=\sqrt{4}=2$, but $\|A\|_F^2=(\sqrt{2})^2=2$. Checking the operator norm instead: $\|A\|_{op}=\sqrt{2}$, $\|A^*A\|_{op}=2=(\sqrt{2})^2$, so the operator norm DOES satisfy the $C^*$-identity — this is why only the operator norm makes $M_2(\mathbb{C})$ a $C^*$-algebra.

q: In $M_3(\mathbb{C})$, let $A$ be the diagonal matrix $\mathrm{diag}(2,-3,5)$. Using the $C^*$-identity, compute $\|A^*A\|$.
text: In $M_3(\mathbb{C})$, let $A$ be the diagonal matrix $\mathrm{diag}(2,-3,5)$. Using the $C^*$-identity, compute $\|A^*A\|$. $A$ is self-adjoint, so $A^*A=A^2=\mathrm{diag}(4,9,25)$. Its operator norm equals the largest eigenvalue $25$. Alternatively, $\|A^*A\|=\|A\|^2=(\max|\lambda_i|)^2=5^2=25$.

q: Which of the following correctly orders the steps in proving that a $C^*$-algebra has a unique $C^*$-norm (once the underlying Banach $*$-algebra structure is fixed)?
text: Which of the following correctly orders the steps in proving that a $C^*$-algebra has a unique $C^*$-norm (once the underlying Banach $*$-algebra structure is fixed)? For any $C^*$-norm: $\|a\|^2=\|a^*a\|=r(a^*a)$ by the spectral radius formula for normal elements (and $a^*a$ is always self-adjoint, hence normal). Since $r(a^*a)$ depends only on the algebra structure (spectrum is algebraic), the norm is uniquely determined.

q: For $a$ in a unital Banach algebra $A$, the spectrum $\sigma(a)$ is:
text: For $a$ in a unital Banach algebra $A$, the spectrum $\sigma(a)$ is: Compactness: $\sigma(a)$ is closed (complement is open) and bounded by $\|a\|$. Non-emptiness: if $\sigma(a)=\emptyset$, the resolvent $(\lambda-a)^{-1}$ would be a bounded entire $A$-valued function, which by Liouville forces $a$ to be a scalar — but then $\sigma(a)=\{a\}\ne\emptyset$. Reality only holds for self-adjoint in a $C^*$-algebra.

q: The spectral radius formula gives $r(a)=\lim_n\|a^n\|^{1/n}$. For the nilpotent $N=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}\in M_2(\mathbb{C})$ (operator norm), compute $r(N)$.
text: The spectral radius formula gives $r(a)=\lim_n\|a^n\|^{1/n}$. For the nilpotent $N=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}\in M_2(\mathbb{C})$ (operator norm), compute $r(N)$. $N^2=0$, so $\|N^n\|=0$ for $n\ge 2$, giving $r(N)=0$. Note $\|N\|=1\ne 0=r(N)$: the spectral radius strictly undercuts the norm. This is consistent with $\sigma(N)=\{0\}$.

q: The resolvent map $\lambda\mapsto(a-\lambda)^{-1}$ on $\rho(a)=\mathbb{C}\setminus\sigma(a)$ is:
text: The resolvent map $\lambda\mapsto(a-\lambda)^{-1}$ on $\rho(a)=\mathbb{C}\setminus\sigma(a)$ is: The resolvent identity $(a-\lambda)^{-1}-(a-\mu)^{-1}=(\lambda-\mu)(a-\lambda)^{-1}(a-\mu)^{-1}$ shows the quotient $((a-\lambda)^{-1}-(a-\mu)^{-1})/(\lambda-\mu)\to(a-\mu)^{-2}$ as $\lambda\to\mu$, so the resolvent is complex-differentiable with values in $A$.

q: In the disk algebra $A(\mathbb{D})$ (continuous on $\overline{\mathbb{D}}$, holomorphic on $\mathbb{D}$) with the sup norm, take $a(z)=z$. Which statement is correct?
text: In the disk algebra $A(\mathbb{D})$ (continuous on $\overline{\mathbb{D}}$, holomorphic on $\mathbb{D}$) with the sup norm, take $a(z)=z$. Which statement is correct? Every character of $A(\mathbb{D})$ is $\mathrm{ev}_w$ for some $w\in\overline{\mathbb{D}}$, and $\sigma(a)=\{\chi(a):\chi\in\hat A\}=\{w:w\in\overline{\mathbb{D}}\}=\overline{\mathbb{D}}$. Hence $r(a)=\sup|\sigma(a)|=1=\|a\|_\infty$; this is a Banach algebra where $r=\|\cdot\|$ even without the $C^*$-identity. Characters on the disk algebra are evaluations at points of the closed disk.

q: In $\ell^1(\mathbb{Z})$ under convolution, $\delta_1$ satisfies $\|\delta_1\|_1=1$ and $\|\delta_1^n\|_1=1$ for every $n\ge 1$. Compute $r(\delta_1)$.
text: In $\ell^1(\mathbb{Z})$ under convolution, $\delta_1$ satisfies $\|\delta_1\|_1=1$ and $\|\delta_1^n\|_1=1$ for every $n\ge 1$. Compute $r(\delta_1)$. $r(\delta_1)=\lim_n\|\delta_1^n\|_1^{1/n}=\lim_n 1^{1/n}=1$. Under Gelfand $\delta_1\mapsto(z\mapsto z)$ on $\hat{\ell^1(\mathbb{Z})}=\mathbb{T}$, and $\sup|z|=1$ matches. The Gelfand transform identifies $\ell^1(\mathbb{Z})$ with a subalgebra of $C(\mathbb{T})$; $\delta_1$ becomes $z\mapsto z$.

q: Which statement captures the content of Gelfand's spectral radius formula beyond what submultiplicativity $\|a^n\|\le\|a\|^n$ gives?
text: Which statement captures the content of Gelfand's spectral radius formula beyond what submultiplicativity $\|a^n\|\le\|a\|^n$ gives? Submultiplicativity gives $\|a^n\|^{1/n}\le\|a\|$, so $\limsup\|a^n\|^{1/n}\le\|a\|$. The real content is: the resolvent power series $\sum a^n/\lambda^{n+1}$ converges in the disk $|\lambda|>r(a)$ (by holomorphy of the resolvent outside $\sigma(a)$) and diverges inside, giving $\limsup\|a^n\|^{1/n}=r(a)$. The limit exists by Fekete-type submultiplicativity. Think about when a power series $\sum a^n/\lambda^{n+1}$ for the resolvent converges.

q: For an element $a$ in a unital $C^*$-algebra, the spectrum $\sigma(a)$ is:
text: For an element $a$ in a unital $C^*$-algebra, the spectrum $\sigma(a)$ is: Compactness follows from $\sigma(a)\subseteq\overline{B(0,\|a\|)}$ closed; non-emptiness uses holomorphy of the resolvent $(\lambda-a)^{-1}$ combined with Liouville. Reality $\sigma(a)\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ only holds for self-adjoint $a$.

q: The spectral radius formula says $r(a)=\lim_n \|a^n\|^{1/n}$. For a self-adjoint $a$ with $\|a\|=5$, compute $r(a)$.
text: The spectral radius formula says $r(a)=\lim_n \|a^n\|^{1/n}$. For a self-adjoint $a$ with $\|a\|=5$, compute $r(a)$. For self-adjoint (hence normal) $a$ the $C^*$-identity gives $\|a^n\|=\|a\|^n$, so $r(a)=\|a\|=5$. Equivalently, the continuous functional calculus sends $a\mapsto\mathrm{id}$ on $\sigma(a)$, and $\|a\|=\sup_{\sigma(a)}|\lambda|=r(a)$.

q: The continuous functional calculus for a normal element $a$ gives a $*$-isomorphism:
text: The continuous functional calculus for a normal element $a$ gives a $*$-isomorphism: Gelfand–Naimark applied to the commutative $C^*$-algebra $C^*(a,1)$ generated by $a$ and the unit: its spectrum is $\sigma(a)$ and $f(a)$ is well-defined for any continuous $f$ on $\sigma(a)$. This is how one defines square roots, logarithms, spectral projections, etc.

q: The spectrum of an element can depend on the ambient $C^*$-algebra. Which statement is correct?
text: The spectrum of an element can depend on the ambient $C^*$-algebra. Which statement is correct? A deep theorem of $C^*$-algebra theory: if $B$ is a $C^*$-subalgebra of $A$ (sharing the same unit or using approximate units appropriately), then $\sigma_B(b)=\sigma_A(b)$. This is spectral permanence, and it fails for general Banach algebras. It relies on the fact that invertibility of $b^*b$ or $1-b^*b$ passes between $B$ and $A$.

q: Let $u$ be a unitary in a $C^*$-algebra ($u^*u=uu^*=1$). The spectral radius $r(u)$ equals:
text: Let $u$ be a unitary in a $C^*$-algebra ($u^*u=uu^*=1$). The spectral radius $r(u)$ equals: $\|u\|^2=\|u^*u\|=\|1\|=1$, so $\|u\|=1$. Since $u$ is normal, $r(u)=\|u\|=1$.

q: Via the continuous functional calculus, if $a$ is self-adjoint with $\sigma(a)\subseteq[-1,1]$, one can form $b=\sqrt{1-a^2}$. Which properties does $b$ satisfy?
text: Via the continuous functional calculus, if $a$ is self-adjoint with $\sigma(a)\subseteq[-1,1]$, one can form $b=\sqrt{1-a^2}$. Which properties does $b$ satisfy? The function $t\mapsto\sqrt{1-t^2}$ is real-valued and non-negative on $[-1,1]$, so $b=\sqrt{1-a^2}$ is self-adjoint ($b^*=b$) and positive ($\sigma(b)\subseteq[0,1]$). The functional calculus is a $*$-homomorphism, so $b^2=1-a^2$, i.e., $a^2+b^2=1$. Also $ab=ba$ since both are functions of $a$.

q: An element $a$ of a unital $C^*$-algebra is positive iff:
text: An element $a$ of a unital $C^*$-algebra is positive iff: Equivalent characterizations: $a=b^*b$ for some $b$, or $a=a^*$ with non-negative spectrum. The set of positive elements $A_+$ is a closed convex cone generating the self-adjoint part.

q: The partial order induced by positivity, $a\le b\iff b-a\ge 0$, restricted to $C(X)$ is:
text: The partial order induced by positivity, $a\le b\iff b-a\ge 0$, restricted to $C(X)$ is: For $f\in C(X)$: $\sigma(f)=f(X)$, so $f\ge 0$ iff $f(x)\ge 0$ for all $x$. This is the classical pointwise cone; positivity in $C^*$-algebras is its noncommutative generalization (Löwner order in $B(H)$).

q: In $M_2(\mathbb{C})$ consider $A=\begin{pmatrix}2&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}$. How many of its eigenvalues lie in $[0,\infty)$? (needed to check whether $A$ is positive)
text: In $M_2(\mathbb{C})$ consider $A=\begin{pmatrix}2&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}$. How many of its eigenvalues lie in $[0,\infty)$? (needed to check whether $A$ is positive) Eigenvalues are $2$ and $-1$; only $2\ge 0$, so one eigenvalue lies in $[0,\infty)$. Since not all eigenvalues are non-negative, $A$ is self-adjoint but NOT positive.

q: Which is a valid counterexample showing a self-adjoint element need NOT be positive?
text: Which is a valid counterexample showing a self-adjoint element need NOT be positive? $\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}$ is self-adjoint (diagonal, real entries) but has eigenvalue $-1$, so $\sigma(a)\not\subseteq[0,\infty)$ and $a$ is not positive. Positivity requires $a=a^*$ AND $\sigma(a)\subseteq[0,\infty)$ — both conditions are necessary. Find a self-adjoint element whose spectrum contains a negative number.

q: Any self-adjoint $a$ can be decomposed as $a=a_+-a_-$ with $a_\pm\ge 0$ and $a_+a_-=0$ via the functional calculus. For $a=\mathrm{diag}(3,-4)$ in $M_2$, compute $\|a_-\|$.
text: Any self-adjoint $a$ can be decomposed as $a=a_+-a_-$ with $a_\pm\ge 0$ and $a_+a_-=0$ via the functional calculus. For $a=\mathrm{diag}(3,-4)$ in $M_2$, compute $\|a_-\|$. Apply $t\mapsto t_-=\max(-t,0)$: $a_-=\mathrm{diag}(0,4)$, so $\|a_-\|=4$. Similarly $a_+=\mathrm{diag}(3,0)$, and $a_+a_-=0$ with $a=a_+-a_-$.

q: Which of the following is NOT equivalent to $a\ge 0$ in a unital $C^*$-algebra?
text: Which of the following is NOT equivalent to $a\ge 0$ in a unital $C^*$-algebra? The first three are equivalent characterizations of positivity. The fourth fails: $a=-\tfrac12\cdot 1$ is self-adjoint with $\|a\|=\tfrac12\le 1$, but $\sigma(a)=\{-\tfrac12\}$ is negative, so $a$ is not positive. The last option would make $-\tfrac12\cdot 1$ positive.

q: Gelfand duality identifies the category of commutative unital $C^*$-algebras with:
text: Gelfand duality identifies the category of commutative unital $C^*$-algebras with: Unital commutative $C^*$-algebras $A$ correspond contravariantly to compact Hausdorff spaces via $A\mapsto \widehat{A}$ (characters) with inverse $X\mapsto C(X)$.

q: For a character $\chi\colon A\to\mathbb{C}$ on a unital commutative $C^*$-algebra and $a\in A$, $\chi(a)$ always lies in:
text: For a character $\chi\colon A\to\mathbb{C}$ on a unital commutative $C^*$-algebra and $a\in A$, $\chi(a)$ always lies in: If $\chi(a)=\lambda$ then $\chi(a-\lambda)=0$, so $a-\lambda$ is not invertible (it lies in $\ker\chi$, a maximal ideal). Hence $\lambda\in\sigma(a)$.

q: Let $A=C(\{p_1,p_2,p_3\})\cong\mathbb{C}^3$. How many characters $\chi\colon A\to\mathbb{C}$ are there?
text: Let $A=C(\{p_1,p_2,p_3\})\cong\mathbb{C}^3$. How many characters $\chi\colon A\to\mathbb{C}$ are there? Each point $p_i$ gives an evaluation character $\mathrm{ev}_{p_i}$, and Gelfand duality says these exhaust the character space. So $|\widehat{A}|=|X|=3$.

q: The Gelfand transform $\Gamma:A\to C(\widehat{A})$ sends $a\mapsto\hat{a}$ where $\hat{a}(\chi)=\chi(a)$. For $A=C([0,1])$, which statement about $\Gamma$ is correct?
text: The Gelfand transform $\Gamma:A\to C(\widehat{A})$ sends $a\mapsto\hat{a}$ where $\hat{a}(\chi)=\chi(a)$. For $A=C([0,1])$, which statement about $\Gamma$ is correct? For $A=C(X)$ with $X$ compact Hausdorff, the evaluation maps $\mathrm{ev}_x(f)=f(x)$ identify $\widehat{A}$ homeomorphically with $X$ (by Urysohn's lemma applied to separate points). Under this identification $\hat{f}=f$, so $\Gamma$ is the identity — a $*$-isometric isomorphism. Gelfand duality is an equivalence of categories, not just an injection.

q: A commutative unital $C^*$-algebra $A$ is isomorphic to $C(X)$ for a unique (up to homeomorphism) compact Hausdorff space $X$. What goes wrong if we drop the requirement that $A$ is unital?
text: A commutative unital $C^*$-algebra $A$ is isomorphic to $C(X)$ for a unique (up to homeomorphism) compact Hausdorff space $X$. What goes wrong if we drop the requirement that $A$ is unital? Without a unit, the Gelfand spectrum is locally compact but not necessarily compact. The non-unital version of Gelfand duality gives $A\cong C_0(X)$ for a locally compact Hausdorff space $X$. Compactness of $X$ is equivalent to $A$ being unital.

q: Let $A=C([0,1])$ and $a(t)=t$. The Gelfand transform gives $\hat{a}=\mathrm{id}$ on $[0,1]\cong\widehat{A}$. Compute $\|\hat{a}\|_{\sup}$.
text: Let $A=C([0,1])$ and $a(t)=t$. The Gelfand transform gives $\hat{a}=\mathrm{id}$ on $[0,1]\cong\widehat{A}$. Compute $\|\hat{a}\|_{\sup}$. $\|\hat{a}\|_{\sup}=\sup_{t\in[0,1]}|t|=1$. The Gelfand transform is isometric, so this equals $\|a\|=\sup_{t}|t|=1$, consistent with spectral radius $r(a)=\|a\|=1$ for a normal element.

q: In the noncommutative dictionary, a point of a classical space $X$ corresponds to:
text: In the noncommutative dictionary, a point of a classical space $X$ corresponds to: For $A=C(X)$, every character is $\mathrm{ev}_x$ and pure states are evaluations at points. For noncommutative $A$, pure states — the extreme points of $S(A)$ — generalize points even when $\hat A=\emptyset$.

q: For an irrational $\theta\notin\mathbb{Q}$, the noncommutative torus $A_\theta$ (generated by unitaries $u,v$ with $vu=e^{2\pi i\theta}uv$) has how many characters $A_\theta\to\mathbb{C}$?
text: For an irrational $\theta\notin\mathbb{Q}$, the noncommutative torus $A_\theta$ (generated by unitaries $u,v$ with $vu=e^{2\pi i\theta}uv$) has how many characters $A_\theta\to\mathbb{C}$? A character kills all commutators, but $[u,v]=(e^{2\pi i\theta}-1)uv\neq 0$ for irrational $\theta$. So $A_\theta$ has no characters — its classical spectrum is empty, though $A_\theta$ is a perfectly good 'noncommutative space'.

q: In $A_\theta$ the relation $vu=e^{2\pi i\theta}uv$ uses the phase $e^{2\pi i\theta}$. Evaluate this phase at $\theta=1/4$ as $a+bi$.
text: In $A_\theta$ the relation $vu=e^{2\pi i\theta}uv$ uses the phase $e^{2\pi i\theta}$. Evaluate this phase at $\theta=1/4$ as $a+bi$. $e^{2\pi i\cdot 1/4}=e^{i\pi/2}=\cos(\pi/2)+i\sin(\pi/2)=0+1\cdot i = i$.

q: The noncommutative torus $A_\theta$ (irrational $\theta$) is a simple $C^*$-algebra. Which argument is the core of the proof?
text: The noncommutative torus $A_\theta$ (irrational $\theta$) is a simple $C^*$-algebra. Which argument is the core of the proof? The standard proof: $A_\theta$ has a unique tracial state $\tau$. If $I$ is a nonzero closed ideal, one shows $\tau(p)>0$ for any nonzero positive $a\in I$ (using the Fourier expansion in $u,v$ and uniqueness of $\tau$). From this $1\in I$ by a projection argument, so $I=A_\theta$. Use the unique tracial state and positivity of $\tau$ on nonzero positive elements of $I$.

q: For the noncommutative torus $A_{1/3}$, the relation is $vu=e^{2\pi i/3}uv$. Compute the real part of the phase $e^{2\pi i/3}$.
text: For the noncommutative torus $A_{1/3}$, the relation is $vu=e^{2\pi i/3}uv$. Compute the real part of the phase $e^{2\pi i/3}$. $e^{2\pi i/3}=\cos(2\pi/3)+i\sin(2\pi/3)=-\tfrac12+\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i$. The real part is $-\tfrac12=-0.5$.

q: Which of the following is NOT part of the noncommutative geometry dictionary (Connes)?
text: Which of the following is NOT part of the noncommutative geometry dictionary (Connes)? Points correspond to characters (commutative case) or pure states (noncommutative case), not two-sided ideals. The dictionary is: bundles $\leftrightarrow$ projective modules (Serre–Swan); measures $\leftrightarrow$ states (Riesz–Markov generalized); maps $\leftrightarrow$ $*$-homs (Gelfand duality). Points correspond to characters / pure states, not ideals.

q: A von Neumann algebra $M\subseteq B(H)$ is characterized (von Neumann's double commutant theorem) as:
text: A von Neumann algebra $M\subseteq B(H)$ is characterized (von Neumann's double commutant theorem) as: Von Neumann's double commutant theorem: a unital $*$-subalgebra $M\subseteq B(H)$ is closed in the strong (equivalently weak) operator topology iff $M=M''$. Norm closure alone gives a $C^*$-algebra, which is strictly weaker.

q: For $S\subseteq B(H)$, the commutant $S'$ is defined as:
text: For $S\subseteq B(H)$, the commutant $S'$ is defined as: $S' = \{x\in B(H):[x,s]=0\ \forall s\in S\}$ is the set of operators commuting with everything in $S$. It is automatically a unital $*$-subalgebra closed in the weak operator topology — hence a von Neumann algebra.

q: In $B(\mathbb{C}^2)=M_2(\mathbb{C})$, let $S=\{A\}$ with $A=\mathrm{diag}(1,2)$. The commutant $S'$ consists of all matrices commuting with $A$ — the diagonal matrices. What is $\dim_\mathbb{C} S'$?
text: In $B(\mathbb{C}^2)=M_2(\mathbb{C})$, let $S=\{A\}$ with $A=\mathrm{diag}(1,2)$. The commutant $S'$ consists of all matrices commuting with $A$ — the diagonal matrices.
