text: The optional-stopping theorem (Doob) says $\mathbb{E}[M_\tau]=\mathbb{E}[M_0]$ for a martingale $M$ and stopping time $\tau$, provided: At least one of these conditions is required; otherwise optional-stopping fails (e.g.\ simple random walk stopped at $+1$ has $\mathbb{E}[M_\tau]=1\ne 0=\mathbb{E}[M_0]$).

q: Consider a simple symmetric random walk $S_n$ and the stopping time $\tau=\inf\{n:S_n=1\}$. Why does the naive optional-stopping fail here?
text: Consider a simple symmetric random walk $S_n$ and the stopping time $\tau=\inf\{n:S_n=1\}$. Why does the naive optional-stopping fail here? $\tau<\infty$ a.s.\ (recurrence of 1D walk) but $\mathbb{E}[\tau]=\infty$. The stopped process is not UI because $\sup_n\mathbb{E}|S_{n\wedge\tau}|=\infty$. The conclusion $\mathbb{E}[S_\tau]=1\ne 0$ shows the hypothesis matters. Check UI: is $\sup_n\mathbb{E}|S_{n\wedge\tau}|<\infty$?

q: Doob's martingale convergence theorem says: if $(M_n)$ is a martingale with $\sup_n\mathbb{E}|M_n|<\infty$, then $M_n$ converges:
text: Doob's martingale convergence theorem says: if $(M_n)$ is a martingale with $\sup_n\mathbb{E}|M_n|<\infty$, then $M_n$ converges: $L^1$-boundedness gives a.s.\ convergence to an integrable limit. $L^1$ convergence additionally requires uniform integrability. Classic counterexample: $(M_n)$ with $\mathbb{E}|M_n|=1$ but $M_n\to 0$ a.s. and $\mathbb{E}[M_n]=1\ne 0=\mathbb{E}[M_\infty]$. $L^1$-bounded is weaker than uniform integrability.

q: Doob's $L^p$ maximal inequality says: for a non-negative sub-martingale $M$ and $p>1$,
text: Doob's $L^p$ maximal inequality says: for a non-negative sub-martingale $M$ and $p>1$, Doob's $L^p$ inequality: $\|\max_{k\le n}M_k\|_p\le(p/(p-1))\|M_n\|_p$ for sub-martingales, $p>1$. This is the key tool for a.s.\ convergence proofs and random-time estimates.

q: An $L^1$-bounded martingale need not converge in $L^1$. The classical counterexample is:
text: An $L^1$-bounded martingale need not converge in $L^1$. The classical counterexample is: $M_n$ is a product martingale with $\mathbb{E}[M_n]=1$. But $M_n=0$ as soon as any $X_i=0$, which happens a.s. So $M_n\to 0$ a.s.\ but $\mathbb{E}[M_n]=1$; convergence fails in $L^1$. UI would rule this out. Product martingale: when does it hit $0$?

q: Standard Brownian motion $(W_t)_{t\ge 0}$ has:
text: Standard Brownian motion $(W_t)_{t\ge 0}$ has: Brownian motion is the Gaussian process with these three properties; its paths are continuous but nowhere differentiable and of unbounded variation on every interval.

q: Compute $\mathbb{P}(W_1>1.96)$ where $W_1\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$. Report to 3 decimals.
text: Compute $\mathbb{P}(W_1>1.96)$ where $W_1\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$. Report to 3 decimals. Standard Gaussian tail: $\mathbb{P}(Z>1.96)\approx 0.025$ (the two-tail $5\%$ critical value).

q: The quadratic variation of Brownian motion on $[0,t]$ is:
text: The quadratic variation of Brownian motion on $[0,t]$ is: $\sum_k(W_{t_{k+1}}-W_{t_k})^2\to t$ in probability (and a.s.\ along dyadic refinements) as the mesh shrinks. Infinite variation but finite quadratic variation is the signature of Brownian motion.

q: Donsker's invariance principle says: for i.i.d.\ $X_i$ with $\mathbb{E}[X_i]=0$ and $\text{Var}(X_i)=1$, the scaled random walk $W^{(N)}_t=S_{\lfloor Nt\rfloor}/\sqrt{N}$ converges to Brownian motion:
text: Donsker's invariance principle says: for i.i.d.\ $X_i$ with $\mathbb{E}[X_i]=0$ and $\text{Var}(X_i)=1$, the scaled random walk $W^{(N)}_t=S_{\lfloor Nt\rfloor}/\sqrt{N}$ converges to Brownian motion: Donsker's theorem: the path-valued random element converges in distribution in $C([0,1])$ to standard Brownian motion. This extends the CLT (a finite-dimensional statement at $t=1$) to a functional statement about whole paths. Donsker lifts CLT from a single time to the whole path.

q: For a scaled random walk approximation: $N=10^4$ steps of $\pm 1$ per unit time. What is the order of magnitude (exponent $e$ in $N^{e}$) of the step size needed so the process converges to Brownian motion on $[0,1]$?
text: For a scaled random walk approximation: $N=10^4$ steps of $\pm 1$ per unit time. What is the order of magnitude (exponent $e$ in $N^{e}$) of the step size needed so the process converges to Brownian motion on $[0,1]$? Diffusive scaling: to match variance $t$ in time $t$ with $Nt$ steps of variance $\sigma^2$ each, we need $\sigma^2\cdot N=1$, so $\sigma\sim N^{-1/2}$. Exponent $e=-1/2$. Diffusive scaling: step size $\sim 1/\sqrt{N}$.

q: Brownian motion is a martingale. Which of the following is ALSO a martingale?
text: Brownian motion is a martingale. Which of the following is ALSO a martingale? $\mathbb{E}[W_t^2\mid\mathcal{F}_s]=W_s^2+(t-s)$, so $W_t^2-t$ is a martingale. Analogously, $e^{W_t-t/2}$ (not $e^{W_t}$) is the exponential martingale. $|W_t|$ is a sub-martingale, not a martingale. Compute $\mathbb{E}[W_t^2\mid\mathcal{F}_s]$ for $s<t$.

q: Itô's formula for $f(W_t)$ with $f\in C^2$ is:
text: Itô's formula for $f(W_t)$ with $f\in C^2$ is: Itô's formula has the extra $\tfrac{1}{2}f''(W_t)\,dt$ term coming from $(dW)^2=dt$. This is the fundamental calculus of Brownian motion and stochastic analysis. Brownian motion has non-vanishing quadratic variation; the chain rule picks up a second-order term.

title: Metric spaces
blurb: Distance axioms, open balls, ε-continuity, Cauchy sequences and completeness — the ε–δ archetype before topology gets abstracted.

title: Topological spaces
blurb: A topology as a family of open sets closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections; bases, subspace and product topologies, with closure and interior as duals.

title: Continuity & homeomorphism
blurb: A map is continuous iff preimages of open sets are open — the coordinate-free definition. Homeomorphisms as the isomorphisms of Top.

title: Compactness
blurb: Every open cover has a finite subcover; preserved under continuous images; Heine–Borel, sequential compactness, and why compact Hausdorff is the right target category.

title: Connectedness
blurb: No separation into disjoint nonempty opens; path-connected implies connected; components partition the space.

title: Separation & countability
blurb: T1/Hausdorff/regular/normal, first- and second-countable; Urysohn's lemma and metrization as the payoff.

q: Which of the following is NOT required for $d\colon X\times X\to[0,\infty)$ to be a metric?
text: Which of the following is NOT required for $d\colon X\times X\to[0,\infty)$ to be a metric? Metrics need not be bounded. The three required axioms are identity of indiscernibles, symmetry, and triangle inequality. Boundedness is a separate property (e.g. discrete metrics are bounded by $1$, Euclidean metrics are not).

q: In $(\mathbb{R}^2, d_\infty)$ with $d_\infty(x,y)=\max(|x_1-y_1|,|x_2-y_2|)$, what is the area of the open ball $B(0,1)$ (as a subset of the plane)?
text: In $(\mathbb{R}^2, d_\infty)$ with $d_\infty(x,y)=\max(|x_1-y_1|,|x_2-y_2|)$, what is the area of the open ball $B(0,1)$ (as a subset of the plane)? The sup-ball of radius $1$ around the origin is the open square $(-1,1)\times(-1,1)$, with area $2\cdot 2 = 4$. By contrast the Euclidean disk has area $\pi\approx 3.14$ and the taxicab diamond has area $2$ — same topology, different shapes.

q: Which space is NOT complete with respect to the indicated metric?
text: Which space is NOT complete with respect to the indicated metric? $(0,1)$ is not complete: the sequence $x_n=1/n$ is Cauchy but its limit $0$ lies outside the space. Completeness is a metric property, not a topological one — $(0,1)$ is homeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}$, which is complete.

q: Define $d(x,y)=|x-y|/(1+|x-y|)$ on $\mathbb{R}$. Which statement is correct?
text: Define $d(x,y)=|x-y|/(1+|x-y|)$ on $\mathbb{R}$. Which statement is correct? The function $t\mapsto t/(1+t)$ is subadditive and increasing on $[0,\infty)$, so $d$ satisfies the triangle inequality and is a valid metric. It generates the standard topology because the identity maps are uniformly continuous in both directions locally. However $d(x,y)<1$ for all $x,y$, so $\mathbb{R}$ becomes bounded — but NOT complete under $d$: a sequence diverging to $\infty$ is $d$-Cauchy yet has no limit.

q: In the taxicab metric on $\mathbb{R}^2$ ($d_1(x,y)=|x_1-y_1|+|x_2-y_2|$), what is the area of the open ball $B(0,1)$?
text: In the taxicab metric on $\mathbb{R}^2$ ($d_1(x,y)=|x_1-y_1|+|x_2-y_2|$), what is the area of the open ball $B(0,1)$? The taxicab unit ball is the open square rotated $45°$: vertices $\pm e_1$ and $\pm e_2$. It is a square with diagonal $2$, so side length $\sqrt{2}$ and area $2$.

q: Which is the correct order of steps to prove the Baire Category Theorem for complete metric spaces?
text: Which is the correct order of steps to prove the Baire Category Theorem for complete metric spaces? The standard proof builds a nested sequence of closed balls avoiding each $F_n$, with radii shrinking to $0$. Completeness supplies a point in $\bigcap \overline{B}_n$, which lies in $U_0$ but in none of the $F_n$, contradicting $X=\bigcup F_n$. Nest shrinking closed balls dodging each $F_n$; completeness hands you the contradiction.

q: Let $X=\{a,b,c\}$. Which of the following is a topology on $X$?
text: Let $X=\{a,b,c\}$. Which of the following is a topology on $X$? Option (b) contains $\emptyset$ and $X$, and the union / intersection of any listed opens remains in the family. Option (a) fails because $\{a\}\cup\{b\}=\{a,b\}$ is missing; (c) fails because $\{a,b\}\cap\{b,c\}=\{b\}$ is missing; (d) fails because $\emptyset$ is missing.

q: Why are topologies required to be closed under FINITE intersections rather than arbitrary intersections?
text: Why are topologies required to be closed under FINITE intersections rather than arbitrary intersections? Countable intersections of opens can collapse to non-open sets: $\bigcap_{n\ge 1}(-1/n,1/n)=\{0\}$ is not open in $\mathbb{R}$. Only finite intersections are safe.

q: How many distinct topologies are there on a 2-element set $X=\{a,b\}$?
text: How many distinct topologies are there on a 2-element set $X=\{a,b\}$? The four topologies are: (i) indiscrete $\{\emptyset,X\}$; (ii) discrete $\{\emptyset,\{a\},\{b\},X\}$; (iii) Sierpiński $\{\emptyset,\{a\},X\}$; (iv) the mirror $\{\emptyset,\{b\},X\}$.

q: How many distinct topologies are there on a 3-element set?
text: How many distinct topologies are there on a 3-element set? By exhaustive enumeration (up to labeling) there are 9 non-homeomorphic topologies, but counting labeled topologies on $\{a,b,c\}$ gives 29. This is a classical combinatorial result.

q: The indiscrete topology $\{\emptyset, X\}$ on a set $X$ with $|X|\ge 2$ is:
text: The indiscrete topology $\{\emptyset, X\}$ on a set $X$ with $|X|\ge 2$ is: In the indiscrete topology no singleton is closed (every nonempty open set is all of $X$), so distinct points cannot be topologically distinguished — the space fails $T_0$. It cannot be metrizable since distinct points in any metric space have disjoint open neighborhoods.

q: Which of the following correctly characterizes the closure of a set $A$ in a topological space?
text: Which of the following correctly characterizes the closure of a set $A$ in a topological space? The closure $\overline{A}$ is the intersection of all closed sets containing $A$ — hence the smallest such closed set. Equivalently it is $A\cup A'$ where $A'$ is the derived set of limit points. The boundary $\partial A=\overline{A}\setminus\mathrm{int}(A)$ need not be open.

q: The topological definition of continuity says $f\colon X\to Y$ is continuous iff:
text: The topological definition of continuity says $f\colon X\to Y$ is continuous iff: $f$ is continuous iff $f^{-1}(V)$ is open in $X$ whenever $V$ is open in $Y$. Images of opens generally need not be open — consider $x\mapsto x^2$ on $\mathbb{R}$, which sends the open interval $(-1,1)$ to the non-open $[0,1)$.

q: Which of these pairs of spaces is NOT a pair of homeomorphic spaces?
text: Which of these pairs of spaces is NOT a pair of homeomorphic spaces? $[0,1]$ is compact and $(0,1)$ is not — continuous images of compact sets are compact, so no continuous bijection between them can be a homeomorphism. Meanwhile $(0,1)\cong \mathbb{R}$ via $t\mapsto \tan(\pi(t-1/2))$, and both (c) and (d) are homeomorphic pairs.

q: Why are the open interval $(0,1)$ and the circle $S^1$ not homeomorphic?
text: Why are the open interval $(0,1)$ and the circle $S^1$ not homeomorphic? $(0,1)$ has the cut-point property: removing any interior point disconnects it. $S^1\setminus\{p\}$ is a connected arc. Since homeomorphisms preserve connectedness and bijections, no homeomorphism can match cut points to non-cut points.

q: A bijective continuous map $f:X\to Y$ is NOT necessarily a homeomorphism. Which example witnesses this?
text: A bijective continuous map $f:X\to Y$ is NOT necessarily a homeomorphism. Which example witnesses this? $f:[0,1)\to S^1$ given by $t\mapsto e^{2\pi it}$ is a continuous bijection. The inverse sends $1$ to $0$ but the preimage of $[0,\varepsilon)$ under $f^{-1}$ is not open in $S^1$, so $f^{-1}$ is discontinuous. This shows compactness of the domain is essential for a continuous bijection to be a homeomorphism.

q: Which is the correct order of steps to prove $f:X\to Y$ continuous iff $f^{-1}(C)$ is closed for every closed $C\subseteq Y$?
text: Which is the correct order of steps to prove $f:X\to Y$ continuous iff $f^{-1}(C)$ is closed for every closed $C\subseteq Y$? Complements interchange open and closed sets and commute with preimages: $f^{-1}(Y\setminus C)=X\setminus f^{-1}(C)$. So the open-set criterion and the closed-set criterion are exact logical duals of each other via complementation.

q: The map $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, $f(x)=x^2$, sends the open set $(-2,2)$ to the set $[0,4)$. What is the minimum number of points one must remove from $(-2,2)$ so that the image of the remaining open set is open?
text: The map $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, $f(x)=x^2$, sends the open set $(-2,2)$ to the set $[0,4)$. What is the minimum number of points one must remove from $(-2,2)$ so that the image of the remaining open set is open? Removing the single point $0$ from $(-2,2)$ gives $(-2,0)\cup(0,2)$, whose image under $x^2$ is $(0,4)$, which is open. The failure at $0$ was the only obstruction.

q: Heine–Borel: in $\mathbb{R}^n$ with the Euclidean topology, a subset is compact iff it is:
text: Heine–Borel: in $\mathbb{R}^n$ with the Euclidean topology, a subset is compact iff it is: Heine–Borel: compact in $\mathbb{R}^n$ $\iff$ closed and bounded. Warning — this equivalence is specific to $\mathbb{R}^n$. In infinite-dimensional Banach spaces the closed unit ball is bounded and closed but not compact.

q: Which of the following subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is compact?
text: Which of the following subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is compact? $\{0\}\cup\{1/n\}$ is closed (contains its limit point $0$) and bounded, hence compact. $(0,1)$ is not closed, $[0,\infty)$ is not bounded, $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]$ is not closed in $\mathbb{R}$.

q: Let $K$ be compact, $f\colon K\to\mathbb{R}$ continuous. Which statement is FALSE?
text: Let $K$ be compact, $f\colon K\to\mathbb{R}$ continuous. Which statement is FALSE? The constant function is continuous on any $K$ and is far from injective. The other three are standard consequences of compactness: extreme value theorem, continuous image of compact is compact, Heine–Cantor.

q: The closed unit ball $\overline{B}$ in an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space $H$ is closed and bounded but not compact. Which sequence witnesses the failure of sequential compactness?
text: The closed unit ball $\overline{B}$ in an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space $H$ is closed and bounded but not compact. Which sequence witnesses the failure of sequential compactness? An orthonormal set satisfies $\|e_n\|=1$ (in the ball) and $\|e_n-e_m\|=\sqrt{2}$ for $n\ne m$ by the Pythagorean theorem. No subsequence can be Cauchy, so none converges. This is the standard counterexample showing Heine–Borel fails in infinite dimensions.

q: Which is the correct sequence of implications for a metric space $X$?
text: Which is the correct sequence of implications for a metric space $X$? For metric spaces the three notions — compactness (every open cover has a finite subcover), sequential compactness (every sequence has a convergent subsequence), and limit-point compactness (every infinite subset has a limit point) — are all equivalent. This equivalence fails for general topological spaces.

q: The Cantor set $C\subset[0,1]$ is compact. It is obtained by removing open middle thirds. After $n$ stages, $2^n$ closed intervals remain, each of length $3^{-n}$. What is the total length of $C$ (i.e., its Lebesgue measure)?
text: The Cantor set $C\subset[0,1]$ is compact. It is obtained by removing open middle thirds. After $n$ stages, $2^n$ closed intervals remain, each of length $3^{-n}$. What is the total length of $C$ (i.e., its Lebesgue measure)? After $n$ steps the total length remaining is $(2/3)^n\to 0$. The Cantor set has Lebesgue measure $0$ yet is uncountable and compact — a classic example of a nowhere-dense perfect set.

q: Which of the following spaces is NOT connected?
text: Which of the following spaces is NOT connected? $[0,1]\cup[2,3]$ is disconnected: take $U=(-1,1.5)$, $V=(1.5,4)$; both hit the subspace in disjoint nonempty opens. The other three are connected — intervals and convex sets are path-connected, and path-connected implies connected.

q: The topologist's sine curve $T = \{(x,\sin(1/x)):x\in(0,1]\}\cup(\{0\}\times[-1,1])$ is:
text: The topologist's sine curve $T = \{(x,\sin(1/x)):x\in(0,1]\}\cup(\{0\}\times[-1,1])$ is: $T$ is the closure of a connected set (the graph), so it is connected. But any continuous path from the limit segment to the graph would have to traverse infinitely many oscillations in finite time, which fails to exist. Hence connected but not path-connected.

q: If $X$ is connected and $f\colon X\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous, then $f(X)$ must be:
text: If $X$ is connected and $f\colon X\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous, then $f(X)$ must be: Continuous images of connected sets are connected; the connected subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ are exactly the intervals (possibly unbounded, possibly degenerate). This is the abstract form of the intermediate value theorem.

q: Path-connectedness implies connectedness, but the converse fails. Which is the correct chain of implications (strongest to weakest)?
text: Path-connectedness implies connectedness, but the converse fails. Which is the correct chain of implications (strongest to weakest)? Path-connected always implies connected. The converse needs local path-connectedness as a hypothesis: a connected, locally path-connected space is path-connected. Manifolds are locally Euclidean (hence locally path-connected), so for manifolds connected $\iff$ path-connected — but this is a consequence of the chain, not a coincidence.

q: $\mathbb{R}^n\setminus\{0\}$ is connected for $n\ge 2$ but disconnected for $n=1$. Which topological property explains the $n=1$ case?
text: $\mathbb{R}^n\setminus\{0\}$ is connected for $n\ge 2$ but disconnected for $n=1$. Which topological property explains the $n=1$ case? Removing any point $p$ from $\mathbb{R}$ yields two disjoint open rays, disconnecting the line — $p$ is a cut point. In $\mathbb{R}^n$ for $n\ge 2$, any two points in $\mathbb{R}^n\setminus\{0\}$ can be joined by a path avoiding the origin (route around it), so removal of a single point leaves the space path-connected, hence connected.

q: The rational numbers $\mathbb{Q}$ with the subspace topology from $\mathbb{R}$ are totally disconnected: the only connected subsets are singletons. How many connected components does the subspace $\{1/n : n\ge 1\}\subset\mathbb{R}$ have?
text: The rational numbers $\mathbb{Q}$ with the subspace topology from $\mathbb{R}$ are totally disconnected: the only connected subsets are singletons. How many connected components does the subspace $\{1/n : n\ge 1\}\subset\mathbb{R}$ have? Each point $\{1/n\}$ is isolated in the subspace (the sets $\{1/n\}=\{x:1/(n+1)<x<1/(n-1)\}\cap S$ are open in $S$), so every singleton is a connected component. The subspace has countably infinitely many connected components — but the question asks for the count of components that are NOT singletons, which is $0$.

q: A space is Hausdorff ($T_2$) iff:
text: A space is Hausdorff ($T_2$) iff: $T_2$ = Hausdorff = distinct points can be separated by disjoint opens. Option (a) is $T_1$, option (c) is normality ($T_4$), option (d) is compactness.

q: Which of these is $T_1$ but NOT Hausdorff?
text: Which of these is $T_1$ but NOT Hausdorff? The cofinite topology on $\mathbb{R}$: every singleton is closed (hence $T_1$), but any two nonempty opens have finite complements, so they must intersect — failing Hausdorff. Sierpiński is $T_0$ but not even $T_1$.

q: Urysohn's metrization theorem says a topological space is metrizable if it is:
text: Urysohn's metrization theorem says a topological space is metrizable if it is: Urysohn metrization: a regular, Hausdorff, second-countable space is metrizable. The converse (metric $\Rightarrow$ regular Hausdorff) holds but metric spaces need not be second-countable in general — second-countability is the bite here.

q: Urysohn's Lemma states: if $X$ is normal ($T_4$) and $A,B$ are disjoint closed sets, then there exists a continuous $f:X\to[0,1]$ with $f|_A=0$ and $f|_B=1$. Which step is the key inductive construction in its proof?
text: Urysohn's Lemma states: if $X$ is normal ($T_4$) and $A,B$ are disjoint closed sets, then there exists a continuous $f:X\to[0,1]$ with $f|_A=0$ and $f|_B=1$. Which step is the key inductive construction in its proof? The proof constructs a nested family of open sets $\{U_r\}$ indexed by dyadic rationals via repeated application of normality (which guarantees open sets separating disjoint closed sets). The function $f(x)=\inf\{r:x\in U_r\}$ is then continuous by construction.

q: The Sorgenfrey line ($\mathbb{R}$ with the lower-limit topology, basis $\{[a,b):a<b\}$) is $T_{3.5}$ (Tychonoff) but its square $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$ is NOT normal. Which property does $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$ fail that witnesses non-normality?
text: The Sorgenfrey line ($\mathbb{R}$ with the lower-limit topology, basis $\{[a,b):a<b\}$) is $T_{3.5}$ (Tychonoff) but its square $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$ is NOT normal. Which property does $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$ fail that witnesses non-normality? Jones's lemma: a normal separable space has no uncountable closed discrete subspace. The anti-diagonal $D=\{(x,-x)\}$ is closed and discrete (each $[x,x+1)\times[-x,-x+1)$ meets $D$ only at $(x,-x)$), and $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$ is separable ($\mathbb{Q}^2$ is dense). This contradicts normality.

q: A second-countable space is separable, but a separable space need not be second-countable. Which example shows this?
text: A second-countable space is separable, but a separable space need not be second-countable. Which example shows this? $\mathbb{R}_\ell$ is separable because $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense. But a second-countable space satisfies the countable chain condition (no uncountable disjoint family of opens). The family $\{[x,x+1):x\in\mathbb{R}\setminus\mathbb{Q}\}$ is an uncountable pairwise-disjoint open family, so $\mathbb{R}_\ell$ is not second-countable.

title: 7. Euler characteristic and a taste of cohomology
Paths and homotopy
blurb: Continuous paths I→X, homotopy rel endpoints, and the concatenation that makes loop classes a group.

title: Simply connected domains
blurb: Path-connected space with trivial fundamental group — the domain hypothesis behind existence theorems in complex analysis.

title: Fundamental group
blurb: π₁(X,x₀): loop classes under concatenation. π₁(S¹)=ℤ, basepoint-change, functoriality.

title: Universal cover of S¹
blurb: The exponential map $p: \mathbb{R} \to S^1$, $t \mapsto e^{2\pi i t}$, is a covering with discrete fibre $\mathbb{Z}$. Lifting loops to paths in $\mathbb{R}$ converts the topology of $S^1$ into integer arithmetic — every loop has a winding number, and that's the iso $\pi_1(S^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}$.

title: Covering spaces
blurb: Locally trivial surjections p:E→B with discrete fibers; the Galois correspondence between covers of B and subgroups of π₁(B).

title: Singular homology
blurb: Chains, boundaries, Hₙ(X) as a homotopy invariant; Mayer–Vietoris and the Euler characteristic.

q: Two paths $\gamma_0,\gamma_1:[0,1]\to X$ with the same endpoints are homotopic rel endpoints if:
text: Two paths $\gamma_0,\gamma_1:[0,1]\to X$ with the same endpoints are homotopic rel endpoints if: Homotopy rel endpoints is exactly a continuous deformation $H$ through paths with endpoints fixed for all deformation times.

q: How many boundary points does the interval $[0,1]$ have?
text: How many boundary points does the interval $[0,1]$ have? As a manifold with boundary, $[0,1]$ has boundary $\{0,1\}$, so there are $2$ boundary points.

q: On the loop $\gamma(t)=e^{2\pi i t}$, compute $\gamma(1/4)$ as $a+bi$.
text: On the loop $\gamma(t)=e^{2\pi i t}$, compute $\gamma(1/4)$ as $a+bi$. $\gamma(1/4)=e^{i\pi/2}=\cos(\pi/2)+i\sin(\pi/2)=i$.

q: Path concatenation $\alpha*\beta$ is associative up to homotopy rel endpoints. Which statement is a correct counterexample showing it fails on the nose (not just up to homotopy)?
text: Path concatenation $\alpha*\beta$ is associative up to homotopy rel endpoints. Which statement is a correct counterexample showing it fails on the nose (not just up to homotopy)? $(\alpha*\beta)*\gamma$ spends the first quarter of $[0,1]$ on $\alpha$, the second on $\beta$, and the last half on $\gamma$, whereas $\alpha*(\beta*\gamma)$ spends the first half on $\alpha$. The functions are genuinely different even though they are homotopic rel endpoints via a linear reparametrisation homotopy.

q: Let $\gamma:[0,1]\to\mathbb{C}^*$ be a loop with $\gamma(t)=e^{4\pi i t}$. The homotopy class of $\gamma$ in $\pi_1(\mathbb{C}^*,1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ equals what integer?
text: Let $\gamma:[0,1]\to\mathbb{C}^*$ be a loop with $\gamma(t)=e^{4\pi i t}$. The homotopy class of $\gamma$ in $\pi_1(\mathbb{C}^*,1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ equals what integer? $\gamma$ winds around the origin $4\pi/2\pi=2$ full times, so its winding number is $2$.

q: Which of the following is the correct order of steps in proving that the path-homotopy relation $\simeq$ is an equivalence relation on paths from $x_0$ to $x_1$?
text: Which of the following is the correct order of steps in proving that the path-homotopy relation $\simeq$ is an equivalence relation on paths from $x_0$ to $x_1$? Standard proof order: first the constant homotopy $H(t,s)=\gamma(t)$ gives reflexivity; reversing the deformation parameter gives symmetry; stacking $H_1(t,s)$ on $[0,1/2]$ and $H_2(t,2s-1)$ on $[1/2,1]$ in $s$ gives transitivity.

q: Which space is simply connected?
text: Which space is simply connected? $\mathbb{R}^2$ has trivial fundamental group. The other listed spaces have nontrivial loops that cannot be contracted.

q: What is the rank of $\pi_1(\mathbb{R}^2)$ as an abelian group?
text: What is the rank of $\pi_1(\mathbb{R}^2)$ as an abelian group? $\pi_1(\mathbb{R}^2)=0$, the trivial group, so its rank is $0$.

q: Compute $e^{2\pi i}$.
text: Compute $e^{2\pi i}$. A full turn on the unit circle lands back at $1$: $e^{2\pi i}=1+0i$.

q: $S^2$ is simply connected. Which argument correctly shows every loop $\gamma:S^1\to S^2$ is null-homotopic?
text: $S^2$ is simply connected. Which argument correctly shows every loop $\gamma:S^1\to S^2$ is null-homotopic? A space-filling loop would require $\gamma$ to be surjective; by a small transversality/smooth perturbation argument every smooth loop misses a point. Removing that point gives $\mathbb{R}^2$, and any loop in $\mathbb{R}^2$ contracts to a point via the straight-line homotopy, so $\gamma$ was null-homotopic in $S^2$. Reduce to a plane by using that loops miss at least one point.

q: Which of these spaces is NOT simply connected?
text: Which of these spaces is NOT simply connected? $\mathbb{R}^3$ minus a line is homotopy equivalent to $S^1$ (retract radially onto a circle linking the line), so $\pi_1\cong\mathbb{Z}\neq 0$. The other three spaces are all simply connected.

q: The van Kampen theorem applied to $S^2 = U \cup V$ (each a contractible open hemisphere with $U\cap V\simeq S^1$) gives $\pi_1(S^2)\cong$ the trivial group. How many generators does $\pi_1(U)*_{\pi_1(U\cap V)}\pi_1(V)$ have after the amalgamation kills all generators?
text: The van Kampen theorem applied to $S^2 = U \cup V$ (each a contractible open hemisphere with $U\cap V\simeq S^1$) gives $\pi_1(S^2)\cong$ the trivial group. How many generators does $\pi_1(U)*_{\pi_1(U\cap V)}\pi_1(V)$ have after the amalgamation kills all generators? $\pi_1(U)=\pi_1(V)=0$, so the amalgamated free product $0*_{\mathbb{Z}}0$ forces the single generator of $\pi_1(U\cap V)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ to map to $0$ in both factors, yielding the trivial group with $0$ generators.

q: Which is true about $\pi_1(S^1)$?
text: Which is true about $\pi_1(S^1)$? Loops on $S^1$ are classified by winding number, giving an isomorphism with $\mathbb{Z}$.

q: If a loop winds $-3$ times around $S^1$, what integer does it represent in $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$?
text: If a loop winds $-3$ times around $S^1$, what integer does it represent in $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$? The winding number itself is the class in $\mathbb{Z}$.

q: Compute $(e^{i\pi/3})^3$ as $a+bi$.
By van Kampen's theorem, $\pi_1(T^2)\cong\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}$. Which decomposition is used?
text: By van Kampen's theorem, $\pi_1(T^2)\cong\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}$. Which decomposition is used? $\pi_1$ preserves products: $\pi_1(S^1\times S^1)\cong\pi_1(S^1)\times\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}$. The two generators correspond to the meridian and longitude loops.

q: The Klein bottle $K$ has $\pi_1(K)=\langle a,b\mid abab^{-1}=1\rangle$. This group is non-abelian. Which element witnesses non-commutativity?
text: The Klein bottle $K$ has $\pi_1(K)=\langle a,b\mid abab^{-1}=1\rangle$. This group is non-abelian. Which element witnesses non-commutativity? Rewrite the relation as $ab=ba^{-1}$. This means $a$ and $b$ do NOT commute (unless $a^2=1$), so the group is non-abelian; it is an HNN extension of $\mathbb{Z}$ by $\mathbb{Z}$.

q: Let $X=\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{p_1,p_2\}$ (the plane with two punctures). The rank of $\pi_1(X)$ as a free group equals how many?
text: Let $X=\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{p_1,p_2\}$ (the plane with two punctures). The rank of $\pi_1(X)$ as a free group equals how many? $\mathbb{R}^2$ minus $n$ points deformation-retracts onto a wedge of $n$ circles, so $\pi_1\cong F_n$. For $n=2$ the free group $F_2$ has rank $2$.

q: Under the exponential covering $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$, $p(t)=e^{2\pi i t}$, what is the fibre $p^{-1}(*)$ over the basepoint $*=1$?
text: Under the exponential covering $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$, $p(t)=e^{2\pi i t}$, what is the fibre $p^{-1}(*)$ over the basepoint $*=1$? $e^{2\pi i t}=1$ exactly when $t\in\mathbb{Z}$, so the fibre over the basepoint is the integer lattice $\mathbb{Z}\subset\mathbb{R}$ — the discrete set whose elements label the sheets of the cover.

q: Lift the loop $\gamma(t)=e^{4\pi i t}$ to a path in $\mathbb{R}$ starting at $0$. What integer winding number $\tilde\gamma(1)$ do you get?
text: Lift the loop $\gamma(t)=e^{4\pi i t}$ to a path in $\mathbb{R}$ starting at $0$. What integer winding number $\tilde\gamma(1)$ do you get? The unique lift through $0$ is $\tilde\gamma(t)=2t$, since $p(2t)=e^{2\pi i (2t)}=e^{4\pi i t}=\gamma(t)$. Its endpoint is $\tilde\gamma(1)=2$, so the loop winds twice.

q: Which property of $\mathbb{R}$ makes $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$ the *universal* cover of $S^1$ (rather than just *some* cover)?
text: Which property of $\mathbb{R}$ makes $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$ the *universal* cover of $S^1$ (rather than just *some* cover)? A connected cover is universal precisely when its total space is simply connected ($\pi_1=0$). $\mathbb{R}$ is contractible, hence simply connected, so $\mathbb{R}\to S^1$ dominates every other connected cover via a unique factoring map. "Universal" means initial among connected covers — what does that require of the total space's $\pi_1$?

q: For the covering map $p:\mathbb{R}\to S^1$, $p(t)=e^{2\pi i t}$, the fiber over $1\in S^1$ is:
text: For the covering map $p:\mathbb{R}\to S^1$, $p(t)=e^{2\pi i t}$, the fiber over $1\in S^1$ is: $e^{2\pi i t}=1$ exactly when $t\in\mathbb{Z}$, so $p^{-1}(1)=\mathbb{Z}$.

q: For $p_n:S^1\to S^1$, $p_n(z)=z^n$, how many points lie over a generic point of the target circle when $n=5$?
text: For $p_n:S^1\to S^1$, $p_n(z)=z^n$, how many points lie over a generic point of the target circle when $n=5$? The map $z\mapsto z^n$ is an $n$-sheeted cover, so generic fibers have $n$ points.

q: Under $p_2(z)=z^2$, one preimage of $-1$ is $i$. Give the other preimage as $a+bi$.
text: Under $p_2(z)=z^2$, one preimage of $-1$ is $i$. Give the other preimage as $a+bi$. Solutions to $z^2=-1$ are $\pm i$. If one is $i$, the other is $-i$.

q: The covering space correspondence says connected covers of $X$ (up to isomorphism) correspond to conjugacy classes of subgroups of $\pi_1(X,x_0)$. For $X=S^1$ with $\pi_1\cong\mathbb{Z}$, which cover corresponds to the subgroup $3\mathbb{Z}\le\mathbb{Z}$?
text: The covering space correspondence says connected covers of $X$ (up to isomorphism) correspond to conjugacy classes of subgroups of $\pi_1(X,x_0)$. For $X=S^1$ with $\pi_1\cong\mathbb{Z}$, which cover corresponds to the subgroup $3\mathbb{Z}\le\mathbb{Z}$? The subgroup $n\mathbb{Z}\le\mathbb{Z}$ has index $n$ and corresponds to the $n$-sheeted cyclic cover $z\mapsto z^n$. For $n=3$ this is $p_3$, whose deck transformation group is $\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}$.

q: The universal cover of $T^2=S^1\times S^1$ is $\mathbb{R}^2$. The deck transformation group acts by integer translations. How many deck transformations map the origin $(0,0)$ to the point $(2,3)$?
text: The universal cover of $T^2=S^1\times S^1$ is $\mathbb{R}^2$. The deck transformation group acts by integer translations. How many deck transformations map the origin $(0,0)$ to the point $(2,3)$? Deck transformations of a regular cover act freely and transitively on each fiber. There is exactly $1$ deck transformation sending any chosen fiber point to any other given fiber point.

q: Path lifting: given a covering $p:\tilde{X}\to X$, a path $\gamma:[0,1]\to X$, and a lift $\tilde{\gamma}(0)=\tilde{x}_0\in p^{-1}(\gamma(0))$, how many lifts $\tilde{\gamma}$ exist?
text: Path lifting: given a covering $p:\tilde{X}\to X$, a path $\gamma:[0,1]\to X$, and a lift $\tilde{\gamma}(0)=\tilde{x}_0\in p^{-1}(\gamma(0))$, how many lifts $\tilde{\gamma}$ exist? The unique path-lifting property of covering spaces guarantees exactly one lift $\tilde{\gamma}:[0,1]\to\tilde{X}$ with $\tilde{\gamma}(0)=\tilde{x}_0$ and $p\circ\tilde{\gamma}=\gamma$.

q: Which equality encodes the chain-complex condition?
text: Which equality encodes the chain-complex condition? Boundaries have no boundary: $\partial_{n-1}\circ\partial_n=0$. This ensures $\operatorname{im}\partial_n\subseteq\ker\partial_{n-1}$.

q: Using reduced homology ranks for a torus $T^2$ ($\beta_0,\beta_1,\beta_2)=(1,2,1)$, compute $\chi(T^2)=\beta_0-\beta_1+\beta_2$.
text: Using reduced homology ranks for a torus $T^2$ ($\beta_0,\beta_1,\beta_2)=(1,2,1)$, compute $\chi(T^2)=\beta_0-\beta_1+\beta_2$. $\chi(T^2)=1-2+1=0$.

q: Compute $\dfrac{1+i}{1-i}$ as $a+bi$.
text: Compute $\dfrac{1+i}{1-i}$ as $a+bi$. Multiply numerator/denominator by $1+i$: $\frac{(1+i)^2}{1+1}=\frac{1+2i-1}{2}=i$.

q: A continuous map $f:X\to Y$ induces $f_*:H_n(X)\to H_n(Y)$. If $f$ is homotopic to a constant map, which is true for all $n\ge 1$?
text: A continuous map $f:X\to Y$ induces $f_*:H_n(X)\to H_n(Y)$. If $f$ is homotopic to a constant map, which is true for all $n\ge 1$? Homotopy invariance: homotopic maps induce the same map on $H_n$. A constant map factors through a point, whose reduced homology vanishes in degree $\ge 1$, so $f_*=0$ there.

q: Use Mayer–Vietoris on $S^2=U\cup V$ with $U,V$ contractible open sets and $U\cap V\simeq S^1$ to compute $\chi(S^2)$ from $\chi(U)+\chi(V)-\chi(U\cap V)$. Recall $\chi(\mathrm{pt})=1$ and $\chi(S^1)=0$.
text: Use Mayer–Vietoris on $S^2=U\cup V$ with $U,V$ contractible open sets and $U\cap V\simeq S^1$ to compute $\chi(S^2)$ from $\chi(U)+\chi(V)-\chi(U\cap V)$. Recall $\chi(\mathrm{pt})=1$ and $\chi(S^1)=0$. Inclusion–exclusion for Euler characteristic: $\chi(S^2)=\chi(U)+\chi(V)-\chi(U\cap V)=1+1-0=2$, matching $\beta_0-\beta_1+\beta_2=1-0+1$.

title: 11. Connections to the rest of the notebook
Smooth manifolds and atlases
blurb: Smooth manifolds glue Euclidean charts by smooth transition maps.

title: Examples: spheres, projective spaces, matrix groups
blurb: S^n, RP^n, tori, Grassmannians, and classical matrix groups are the canonical manifolds to have in hand before doing anything intrinsic.

title: Smooth maps and diffeomorphisms
blurb: A map is smooth when every chart representative is C^∞; diffeomorphisms are the isomorphisms of the smooth category.

title: Tangent spaces
blurb: T_pM has three equivalent definitions — equivalence classes of curves, chart velocities, and derivations of C^∞(M) — all naturally isomorphic.

title: Tangent bundle and vector fields
blurb: TM assembles all tangent spaces into a rank-n vector bundle; smooth sections are vector fields and act as derivations on C^∞(M).

title: Lie bracket of vector fields
blurb: [X,Y] = XY−YX is a vector field; it measures the failure of flows to commute and gives 𝔛(M) a Lie algebra structure.

title: Immersions, submersions, and submanifolds
blurb: Rank conditions on df control local models: immersions inject tangentially, submersions project, embeddings land as submanifolds; Whitney says every n-manifold embeds in R^{2n}.

title: Integral curves and flows
blurb: A vector field X integrates to a flow Phi^X_t: the fundamental theorem gives a unique maximal integral curve through each point, complete when M is compact.

title: Partitions of unity
blurb: Subordinate partitions of unity exist on any smooth manifold and are the standard device for gluing local constructions into global ones.

title: Orientation of manifolds
blurb: An orientation is a coherent choice of sign on the top exterior power of every tangent space; equivalently, an atlas whose transitions have positive Jacobian.

q: A smooth atlas on $M$ is a collection of charts $\{(U_\alpha,\varphi_\alpha)\}$ covering $M$ such that:
text: A smooth atlas on $M$ is a collection of charts $\{(U_\alpha,\varphi_\alpha)\}$ covering $M$ such that: Smoothness of transition maps between Euclidean patches is exactly what lifts calculus from $\mathbb{R}^n$ to $M$. No disjointness, compactness, or linearity is required.

q: On $S^2$ with the two-chart stereographic atlas from $N$ and $S$, the overlap $U_N\cap U_S$ is $S^2$ minus how many points?
text: On $S^2$ with the two-chart stereographic atlas from $N$ and $S$, the overlap $U_N\cap U_S$ is $S^2$ minus how many points? The north chart misses $N$ and the south chart misses $S$, so the overlap is $S^2\setminus\{N,S\}$ — two missing points.

q: The transition map between the two stereographic charts on $S^2$ is:
text: The transition map between the two stereographic charts on $S^2$ is: Composing the two stereographic projections gives $(u,v)\mapsto(u,v)/(u^2+v^2)$, a $C^\infty$ diffeomorphism of $\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$.

q: The circle $S^1$ admits a smooth atlas with two charts. Which statement is a correct counterexample showing that a single chart cannot cover $S^1$?
text: The circle $S^1$ admits a smooth atlas with two charts. Which statement is a correct counterexample showing that a single chart cannot cover $S^1$? A single chart would require a homeomorphism from all of $S^1$ onto an open subset of $\mathbb{R}$. But $S^1$ is compact and a continuous bijection from a compact space to a Hausdorff space is a homeomorphism onto its image; no open subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is compact, so no such homeomorphism exists.

q: The torus $T^2 = S^1\times S^1$ carries a smooth atlas derived from the product of two two-chart atlases on $S^1$. What is the minimum number of charts in such a product atlas?
text: The torus $T^2 = S^1\times S^1$ carries a smooth atlas derived from the product of two two-chart atlases on $S^1$. What is the minimum number of charts in such a product atlas? Each factor $S^1$ needs at least $2$ charts, so the product atlas has $2\times 2 = 4$ charts of the form $U_i\times V_j$.

q: Which of the following is the correct order of steps in verifying that two charts $(U,\varphi)$ and $(V,\psi)$ are smoothly compatible?
text: Which of the following is the correct order of steps in verifying that two charts $(U,\varphi)$ and $(V,\psi)$ are smoothly compatible? Two charts are smoothly compatible when their overlap is open (possibly empty) and both transition maps $\psi\circ\varphi^{-1}$ and $\varphi\circ\psi^{-1}$ are $C^\infty$. If the overlap is empty they are trivially compatible, skipping the smoothness check.

q: What is the dimension of the matrix Lie group $\mathrm{SO}(n)$?
text: What is the dimension of the matrix Lie group $\mathrm{SO}(n)$? $\mathrm{SO}(n)$ has dimension $n(n-1)/2$; for $n=3$ this is $3(3-1)/2=3$ — reflecting the three Euler angles.

q: Real projective space $\mathbb{RP}^n$ can be described as:
text: Real projective space $\mathbb{RP}^n$ can be described as: $\mathbb{RP}^n = S^n/\{\pm 1\}$ identifies antipodal points; the resulting space is a compact smooth $n$-manifold, with standard affine charts $\{x_i\ne 0\}$.

q: The Grassmannian $\mathrm{Gr}(k,n)$ of $k$-planes in $\mathbb{R}^n$ has dimension $k(n-k)$. What is $\dim\mathrm{Gr}(2,5)$?
text: The Grassmannian $\mathrm{Gr}(k,n)$ of $k$-planes in $\mathbb{R}^n$ has dimension $k(n-k)$. What is $\dim\mathrm{Gr}(2,5)$? $\dim\mathrm{Gr}(2,5)=2\cdot(5-2)=6$. The charts are graphs of linear maps $W_0\to W_0^\perp$, giving $k(n-k)$ parameters.

q: The unitary group $\mathrm{U}(n)$ is cut out of $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ by $A^*A=I$. What is its real dimension for $n=2$?
text: The unitary group $\mathrm{U}(n)$ is cut out of $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ by $A^*A=I$. What is its real dimension for $n=2$? $\mathrm{U}(n)$ has real dimension $n^2$. For $n=2$ this gives $4$. (Concretely: $\mathrm{U}(2)\cong \mathrm{U}(1)\times\mathrm{SU}(2)/\mathbb{Z}_2$, with $\dim\mathrm{U}(1)=1$ and $\dim\mathrm{SU}(2)=3$.)

q: Why is the cone $C=\{(x,y,z):z^2=x^2+y^2\}\subset\mathbb{R}^3$ NOT a smooth manifold near the apex $(0,0,0)$?
text: Why is the cone $C=\{(x,y,z):z^2=x^2+y^2\}\subset\mathbb{R}^3$ NOT a smooth manifold near the apex $(0,0,0)$? Take any small neighbourhood $U$ of the apex in $C$. Then $U\setminus\{0\}$ has two connected components (the two half-cones). But for any point in a smooth $2$-manifold, removing it leaves a connected punctured disk. So no such neighbourhood is homeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}^2$, and $C$ fails even to be a topological manifold at the apex — smoothness is not the issue, the topology is.

q: Which of the following is a smooth manifold structure on the complex projective line $\mathbb{CP}^1$?
text: Which of the following is a smooth manifold structure on the complex projective line $\mathbb{CP}^1$? $\mathbb{CP}^1$ has two affine charts $U_0=\{[z_0:z_1]:z_0\ne 0\}$ with coordinate $z=z_1/z_0$ and $U_1=\{z_1\ne 0\}$ with coordinate $w=z_0/z_1$; on the overlap $w=1/z$, smooth (in fact holomorphic). Topologically the result is $S^2$ — the Riemann sphere — and one can check the smooth structures match the standard stereographic atlas.

q: A map $f\colon M\to N$ between smooth manifolds is smooth iff:
text: A map $f\colon M\to N$ between smooth manifolds is smooth iff: Smoothness of $f$ is smoothness of every coordinate representative $\hat f = \psi\circ f\circ\varphi^{-1}$, which is a map between open subsets of Euclidean space. Chart compatibility makes this property independent of chart choice.

q: A diffeomorphism $f\colon M\to N$ is:
text: A diffeomorphism $f\colon M\to N$ is: Diffeomorphisms are the isomorphisms of the smooth category: smooth, bijective, with smooth inverse. A smooth bijection need not be a diffeomorphism — consider $x\mapsto x^3$ on $\mathbb{R}$, whose inverse is not smooth at $0$.

q: Let $f\colon\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $f(x,y)=(x+y,\,xy)$. At $p=(1,2)$ the Jacobian is $\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\ y&x\end{pmatrix}_{(1,2)} = \begin{pmatrix}1&1\\2&1\end{pmatrix}$. What is the rank of $df_p$?
text: Let $f\colon\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $f(x,y)=(x+y,\,xy)$. At $p=(1,2)$ the Jacobian is $\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\ y&x\end{pmatrix}_{(1,2)} = \begin{pmatrix}1&1\\2&1\end{pmatrix}$. What is the rank of $df_p$? $\det\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\2&1\end{pmatrix} = 1-2 = -1\ne 0$, so the Jacobian is invertible and $\operatorname{rank}(df_p)=2$.

q: The map $f\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, $f(x)=x^3$, is a smooth bijection. Why is it NOT a diffeomorphism?
text: The map $f\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, $f(x)=x^3$, is a smooth bijection. Why is it NOT a diffeomorphism? $f$ is a smooth bijection, but $df_0 = 3x^2|_{x=0} = 0$ is non-invertible, and $f^{-1}(y)=y^{1/3}$ has $(f^{-1})'(y)=\tfrac{1}{3}y^{-2/3}$, which diverges as $y\to 0$. So $f^{-1}$ fails to be smooth (in fact even $C^1$) at $0$, and $f$ is not a diffeomorphism. The inverse function theorem obstruction: $df_p$ must be an isomorphism at every $p$.

q: Which condition on a smooth map $f\colon M\to N$ is necessary AND sufficient for $f$ to be a local diffeomorphism at $p\in M$?
text: Which condition on a smooth map $f\colon M\to N$ is necessary AND sufficient for $f$ to be a local diffeomorphism at $p\in M$? The inverse function theorem: $f$ is a local diffeomorphism at $p$ iff $df_p$ is a linear isomorphism, which forces $\dim M=\dim N$. Injectivity near $p$ is implied but not sufficient (think $x\mapsto x^3$). Every smooth map pulls back smooth functions.

q: Let $f\colon\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $f(x,y)=(e^x\cos y,\,e^x\sin y)$ (complex exponential). What is $|\det(df_p)|$ at the point $p=(0,0)$?
text: Let $f\colon\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $f(x,y)=(e^x\cos y,\,e^x\sin y)$ (complex exponential). What is $|\det(df_p)|$ at the point $p=(0,0)$? $df_p = \begin{pmatrix} e^x\cos y & -e^x\sin y \\ e^x\sin y & e^x\cos y\end{pmatrix}$, with determinant $e^{2x}$. At $(0,0)$ this is $1$. So $f$ is a local diffeomorphism everywhere, though not globally injective — $f$ is $2\pi$-periodic in $y$.

q: For a smooth $n$-manifold $M$ and $p\in M$, the tangent space $T_pM$ is:
text: For a smooth $n$-manifold $M$ and $p\in M$, the tangent space $T_pM$ is: In any chart the coordinate vectors $\partial/\partial x^1,\dots,\partial/\partial x^n$ form a basis, so $T_pM$ is an $n$-dimensional real vector space.

q: What is the dimension of the tangent bundle $TM$ of a smooth $n$-manifold $M$?
text: What is the dimension of the tangent bundle $TM$ of a smooth $n$-manifold $M$? $TM$ has dimension $2n$; for $n=3$ this is $6$.

q: Under a chart change $y=y(x)$, a tangent vector with components $X^i$ becomes $\tilde X^j = (\partial y^j/\partial x^i)X^i$. If $X=(2,0)$ in coordinates $x$ and the Jacobian at the point is $\begin{pmatrix}3&1\\0&2\end{pmatrix}$, what is $\tilde X^1$?
text: Under a chart change $y=y(x)$, a tangent vector with components $X^i$ becomes $\tilde X^j = (\partial y^j/\partial x^i)X^i$. If $X=(2,0)$ in coordinates $x$ and the Jacobian at the point is $\begin{pmatrix}3&1\\0&2\end{pmatrix}$, what is $\tilde X^1$? $\tilde X^1 = 3\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 0 = 6$.

q: A smooth curve $\gamma:(-\varepsilon,\varepsilon)\to M$ defines a tangent vector $\gamma'(0)\in T_pM$ as a derivation on smooth functions. Which statement correctly orders the steps in showing this derivation satisfies the Leibniz rule?
text: A smooth curve $\gamma:(-\varepsilon,\varepsilon)\to M$ defines a tangent vector $\gamma'(0)\in T_pM$ as a derivation on smooth functions. Which statement correctly orders the steps in showing this derivation satisfies the Leibniz rule? $\gamma'(0)(fg)=\frac{d}{dt}\big|_{t=0}(fg\circ\gamma)=f(p)\,g'(0)+g(p)\,f'(0)=f(p)\,\gamma'(0)(g)+g(p)\,\gamma'(0)(f)$ by the real product rule, confirming Leibniz directly without passing to a chart.

q: Let $f:M\to N$ be a smooth map between manifolds of dimensions $3$ and $5$. What is the dimension of $\ker(df_p)$ if $df_p$ has rank $2$?
text: Let $f:M\to N$ be a smooth map between manifolds of dimensions $3$ and $5$. What is the dimension of $\ker(df_p)$ if $df_p$ has rank $2$? By the rank-nullity theorem applied to $df_p:T_pM\to T_{f(p)}N$, $\dim\ker(df_p)=\dim T_pM-\operatorname{rank}(df_p)=3-2=1$.

q: Which is a correct counterexample showing that the tangent bundle $TS^2$ is not trivial (i.e., $TS^2\ncong S^2\times\mathbb{R}^2$)?
text: Which is a correct counterexample showing that the tangent bundle $TS^2$ is not trivial (i.e., $TS^2\ncong S^2\times\mathbb{R}^2$)? A trivial rank-$n$ bundle admits $n$ everywhere-linearly-independent global sections, i.e., a global frame. The hairy ball theorem asserts every tangent vector field on $S^2$ vanishes somewhere, so no non-vanishing global section exists, let alone a full frame. Hence $TS^2$ is non-trivial.

q: The tangent bundle $TM$ of a smooth $n$-manifold $M$ is:
text: The tangent bundle $TM$ of a smooth $n$-manifold $M$ is: $TM=\bigsqcup_{p\in M}T_pM$ is a rank-$n$ vector bundle over $M$; each fibre is an $n$-plane, and the total space is a smooth manifold of dimension $2n$. $TM$ is trivial ($\cong M\times\mathbb{R}^n$) iff $M$ is parallelizable — false in general (e.g. $S^2$).

q: A smooth vector field on $M$ is:
text: A smooth vector field on $M$ is: A vector field assigns to each point $p\in M$ a vector $X_p\in T_pM$ smoothly, i.e. a smooth section of the projection $\pi\colon TM\to M$. Equivalently, a derivation on $C^\infty(M)$.

q: On $\mathbb{R}^2$, the vector field $X = x\,\partial_x + y\,\partial_y$ acts on the function $f(x,y)=x^2+y^2$ as $Xf$. What is $(Xf)(1,2)$?
text: On $\mathbb{R}^2$, the vector field $X = x\,\partial_x + y\,\partial_y$ acts on the function $f(x,y)=x^2+y^2$ as $Xf$. What is $(Xf)(1,2)$? $Xf = x\cdot 2x + y\cdot 2y = 2(x^2+y^2)$. At $(1,2)$: $2(1+4) = 10$.

q: A smooth manifold $M$ is called parallelizable if $TM$ is trivial (isomorphic to $M\times\mathbb{R}^n$). Which list is correct?
text: A smooth manifold $M$ is called parallelizable if $TM$ is trivial (isomorphic to $M\times\mathbb{R}^n$). Which list is correct? A classical theorem: $S^n$ is parallelizable iff $n\in\{1,3,7\}$, corresponding to the multiplicative structures on $\mathbb{C},\mathbb{H},\mathbb{O}$. The hairy-ball theorem handles $S^2$; more is needed for higher dimensions.

q: For a smooth $n$-manifold $M$, what is the dimension of the total space $TM$ of its tangent bundle when $n=5$?
text: For a smooth $n$-manifold $M$, what is the dimension of the total space $TM$ of its tangent bundle when $n=5$? $\dim TM = 2n$. For $n=5$ this is $10$: a chart $(U,\varphi)$ on $M$ gives a chart on $TM$ with coordinates $(x^1,\dots,x^n,\dot x^1,\dots,\dot x^n)$.

q: Let $X\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ act on $C^\infty(M)$ as a derivation. Which property is NOT required?
text: Let $X\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ act on $C^\infty(M)$ as a derivation. Which property is NOT required? Derivations are $\mathbb{R}$-linear Leibniz operators, automatically local (locality follows from Leibniz applied to bump functions). Multiplicativity $X(fg)=X(f)X(g)$ is the defining property of a ring homomorphism, not a derivation; derivations detect infinitesimal deformations, homomorphisms detect point evaluations.

q: A smooth map $f\colon M^m\to N^n$ is an immersion at $p$ iff $df_p$:
text: A smooth map $f\colon M^m\to N^n$ is an immersion at $p$ iff $df_p$: Immersion = injective differential, rank $m$. Submersion is the surjective case with rank $n$.

q: By the regular value theorem, if $c$ is a regular value of $g\colon\mathbb{R}^{n+1}\to\mathbb{R}$ then $g^{-1}(c)$ is a smooth manifold of dimension $n$. For $g(x)=\|x\|^2$ on $\mathbb{R}^4$ and $c=1$, what is the dimension of $g^{-1}(1)=S^3$?
text: By the regular value theorem, if $c$ is a regular value of $g\colon\mathbb{R}^{n+1}\to\mathbb{R}$ then $g^{-1}(c)$ is a smooth manifold of dimension $n$. For $g(x)=\|x\|^2$ on $\mathbb{R}^4$ and $c=1$, what is the dimension of $g^{-1}(1)=S^3$? $g\colon\mathbb{R}^4\to\mathbb{R}$, and $1$ is regular, so $g^{-1}(1)$ has dimension $4-1=3$.

q: Whitney's embedding theorem states that every smooth $n$-manifold admits a smooth embedding into:
text: Whitney's embedding theorem states that every smooth $n$-manifold admits a smooth embedding into: Whitney (1944): every smooth $n$-manifold embeds smoothly in $\mathbb{R}^{2n}$, and immerses in $\mathbb{R}^{2n-1}$.

q: The figure-eight $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $f(t)=(\sin 2t,\sin t)$, is an injective immersion but not an embedding. Which property fails?
text: The figure-eight $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $f(t)=(\sin 2t,\sin t)$, is an injective immersion but not an embedding. Which property fails? $f$ is injective and $df_t$ is everywhere non-zero, so it is an injective immersion. However, the image is a figure-eight whose crossing point has a neighbourhood in $f(\mathbb{R})$ that is not homeomorphic to an interval: nearby points in the domain map to four branches meeting at the origin, whereas the subspace topology sees all four. Thus $f$ is not a homeomorphism onto its image and fails to be an embedding.

q: Let $g:\mathbb{R}^3\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $g(x,y,z)=(x^2+y^2+z^2,\, x+y+z)$. At the point $p=(1,0,0)$, what is the rank of $dg_p$?
text: Let $g:\mathbb{R}^3\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $g(x,y,z)=(x^2+y^2+z^2,\, x+y+z)$. At the point $p=(1,0,0)$, what is the rank of $dg_p$? $dg_p = \begin{pmatrix}2&0&0\\1&1&1\end{pmatrix}$. The first row has leading entry $2$ and the second has entry $1$ in the second column; the two rows are linearly independent, so $\operatorname{rank}(dg_p)=2$, making $p$ a regular point.

q: By the constant-rank theorem, if $f:M^m\to N^n$ has constant rank $r$ near $p$, what is the local normal form of $f$?
text: By the constant-rank theorem, if $f:M^m\to N^n$ has constant rank $r$ near $p$, what is the local normal form of $f$? The constant-rank theorem (local form): choosing adapted charts around $p$ and $f(p)$, $f$ looks like the standard projection–injection $(x^1,\dots,x^m)\mapsto(x^1,\dots,x^r,0,\dots,0)\in\mathbb{R}^n$. The first $r$ coordinates are kept; the rest are sent to zero.

q: An integral curve of a smooth vector field $X\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ through $p$ is a smooth curve $\gamma\colon I\to M$ with $\gamma(0)=p$ satisfying:
text: An integral curve of a smooth vector field $X\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ through $p$ is a smooth curve $\gamma\colon I\to M$ with $\gamma(0)=p$ satisfying: An integral curve is a trajectory that follows the vector field: its velocity at every time equals the value of $X$ at the current position, i.e. $\gamma'(t)=X_{\gamma(t)}$.

q: On $\mathbb{R}$, the vector field $X=x\,\partial_x$ has integral curve through $x_0=1$ given by $\gamma(t)=e^t$. What is $\gamma(\ln 3)$?
text: On $\mathbb{R}$, the vector field $X=x\,\partial_x$ has integral curve through $x_0=1$ given by $\gamma(t)=e^t$. What is $\gamma(\ln 3)$? Solving $\dot x=x$ with $x(0)=1$ gives $x(t)=e^t$. Then $\gamma(\ln 3)=e^{\ln 3}=3$.

q: On a compact smooth manifold $M$, every smooth vector field is:
text: On a compact smooth manifold $M$, every smooth vector field is: On a compact manifold integral curves cannot escape to infinity in finite time, so the maximal domain of each integral curve is all of $\mathbb{R}$. Equivalently, the flow $\Phi^X_t$ is defined for every real $t$ — $X$ is complete.

q: Which is a correct counterexample to the claim 'every smooth vector field on a non-compact manifold is complete'?
text: Which is a correct counterexample to the claim 'every smooth vector field on a non-compact manifold is complete'? The ODE $\dot x=x^2$, $x(0)=1$ has solution $x(t)=1/(1-t)$, which escapes to $+\infty$ as $t\to 1^-$. So the maximal interval of existence is $(-\infty,1)$ — strictly less than $\mathbb{R}$ — and $X=x^2\partial_x$ on $\mathbb{R}$ is incomplete. Leaving compact sets in finite time per se is fine (A, D) as long as the curve stays in $M$; only blow-up in finite time breaks completeness. Escape to infinity is fine; incompleteness needs blow-up in finite time.

q: The flow $\Phi^X_t$ of a complete vector field $X$ satisfies which identity?
text: The flow $\Phi^X_t$ of a complete vector field $X$ satisfies which identity? Uniqueness of ODE solutions, together with time-translation invariance, forces $\Phi^X_{s+t}=\Phi^X_s\circ\Phi^X_t$ wherever both sides are defined; when $X$ is complete this holds on all of $M\times\mathbb{R}^2$, making $\{\Phi^X_t\}_{t\in\mathbb{R}}$ a one-parameter group of diffeomorphisms of $M$.

q: Let $X=-y\,\partial_x+x\,\partial_y$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$ (rotation generator). Starting at $(1,0)$, the integral curve is $\gamma(t)=(\cos t,\sin t)$. What is the $y$-coordinate of $\Phi^X_{\pi/2}(1,0)$?
text: Let $X=-y\,\partial_x+x\,\partial_y$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$ (rotation generator). Starting at $(1,0)$, the integral curve is $\gamma(t)=(\cos t,\sin t)$. What is the $y$-coordinate of $\Phi^X_{\pi/2}(1,0)$? $\Phi^X_{\pi/2}(1,0)=(\cos(\pi/2),\sin(\pi/2))=(0,1)$, so the $y$-coordinate is $1$.

q: The Lie bracket of two smooth vector fields $X,Y\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ is defined on functions by:
text: The Lie bracket of two smooth vector fields $X,Y\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ is defined on functions by: $[X,Y] = XY - YX$ as operators on $C^\infty(M)$. The second-order terms cancel and the commutator is again a derivation, hence a vector field.

q: On $\mathbb{R}^2$ with $X=\partial_x$ and $Y=x\,\partial_y$, the Lie bracket is $[X,Y]=\partial_y$. Applying $[X,Y]$ to $f(x,y)=y^2$ at the point $(3,4)$ gives what number?
text: On $\mathbb{R}^2$ with $X=\partial_x$ and $Y=x\,\partial_y$, the Lie bracket is $[X,Y]=\partial_y$. Applying $[X,Y]$ to $f(x,y)=y^2$ at the point $(3,4)$ gives what number? $[X,Y]f = \partial_y(y^2) = 2y$. At $(3,4)$ this evaluates to $2\cdot 4 = 8$.

q: Which identity does the Lie bracket satisfy, making $\mathfrak{X}(M)$ a Lie algebra?
text: Which identity does the Lie bracket satisfy, making $\mathfrak{X}(M)$ a Lie algebra? The bracket is antisymmetric ($[X,Y]=-[Y,X]$, so in particular $[X,X]=0$) and satisfies the Jacobi identity. Those two axioms define a Lie algebra over $\mathbb{R}$.

q: The Lie bracket satisfies the Leibniz-like identity $[X,fY] = f[X,Y] + (Xf)Y$ for $f\in C^\infty(M)$. Which statement follows?
text: The Lie bracket satisfies the Leibniz-like identity $[X,fY] = f[X,Y] + (Xf)Y$ for $f\in C^\infty(M)$. Which statement follows? The formula $[X,fY]=f[X,Y]+(Xf)Y$ shows a derivation-of-scalars correction term. Over $\mathbb{R}$ the bracket is bilinear, but over $C^\infty(M)$ it fails — unlike the pointwise operations of a vector bundle. The bracket is a genuinely differential (not tensorial) operation.

q: Two smooth vector fields $X,Y$ on $M$ have flows that commute, $\Phi^X_s\circ\Phi^Y_t=\Phi^Y_t\circ\Phi^X_s$ for all small $s,t$, iff:
text: Two smooth vector fields $X,Y$ on $M$ have flows that commute, $\Phi^X_s\circ\Phi^Y_t=\Phi^Y_t\circ\Phi^X_s$ for all small $s,t$, iff: This is the flow-commutativity theorem: the flows commute (on overlapping domains) iff the bracket $[X,Y]$ vanishes identically. The infinitesimal obstruction to flow commutativity is exactly the bracket, consistent with $\Phi^{-Y}_t\Phi^{-X}_t\Phi^Y_t\Phi^X_t(p)=p+t^2[X,Y]_p+O(t^3)$.

q: On $\mathbb{R}^2$ with $X = \partial_x$ and $Y = x\,\partial_y + y\,\partial_x$, compute the $\partial_y$-component of $[X,Y]$ evaluated at any point.
text: On $\mathbb{R}^2$ with $X = \partial_x$ and $Y = x\,\partial_y + y\,\partial_x$, compute the $\partial_y$-component of $[X,Y]$ evaluated at any point. For vector fields written as $X=X^i\partial_i$, $Y=Y^j\partial_j$, the bracket components are $[X,Y]^k = X^i\partial_i Y^k - Y^i\partial_i X^k$. Here $X=(1,0)$ and $Y=(y,x)$, with $X$ having constant coefficients. $[X,Y]^y = X^i\partial_i Y^y - Y^i\partial_i X^y = \partial_x(x) - 0 = 1$. So $[X,Y]^y = 1$ is constant (independent of position).

q: A smooth partition of unity subordinate to an open cover $\{U_\alpha\}$ of $M$ is a family $\{\rho_\alpha\colon M\to[0,1]\}$ such that:
text: A smooth partition of unity subordinate to an open cover $\{U_\alpha\}$ of $M$ is a family $\{\rho_\alpha\colon M\to[0,1]\}$ such that: A smooth partition of unity subordinate to $\{U_\alpha\}$ consists of smooth non-negative bumps $\rho_\alpha$ supported in the corresponding $U_\alpha$, with the family locally finite (so the sum makes sense pointwise), and $\sum_\alpha\rho_\alpha\equiv 1$ on $M$. Characteristic functions of open sets are not even continuous, so (D) fails.

q: On which class of smooth manifolds is the existence of smooth partitions of unity guaranteed?
text: On which class of smooth manifolds is the existence of smooth partitions of unity guaranteed? Smooth partitions of unity exist on any paracompact smooth manifold; our definition already assumes second countability + Hausdorff, which imply paracompactness, so every smooth manifold in this notebook admits them. Compactness and orientability are irrelevant.

q: Suppose $\{\rho_1,\rho_2,\rho_3\}$ is a partition of unity on $M$ and at a point $p$, $\rho_1(p)=0.2$, $\rho_2(p)=0.5$. What is $\rho_3(p)$?
text: Suppose $\{\rho_1,\rho_2,\rho_3\}$ is a partition of unity on $M$ and at a point $p$, $\rho_1(p)=0.2$, $\rho_2(p)=0.5$. What is $\rho_3(p)$? By definition $\rho_1(p)+\rho_2(p)+\rho_3(p)=1$, so $\rho_3(p)=1-0.2-0.5=0.3$.

q: Which is the standard use of a partition of unity in the proof that every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric?
text: Which is the standard use of a partition of unity in the proof that every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric? On each chart domain $U_\alpha$ pull back the Euclidean metric to get a local Riemannian metric $g_\alpha$. A convex combination $\sum_\alpha\rho_\alpha g_\alpha$ of positive-definite symmetric forms with non-negative weights summing to $1$ is itself positive-definite wherever the sum is non-empty, which is everywhere because $\sum\rho_\alpha=1$. Smoothness follows from local finiteness.

q: Why does the analogous gluing fail for holomorphic (complex-analytic) structures — i.e. why is there no holomorphic partition of unity on a complex manifold?
text: Why does the analogous gluing fail for holomorphic (complex-analytic) structures — i.e. why is there no holomorphic partition of unity on a complex manifold? Smooth bumps exist because $C^\infty$ is extremely flexible — non-analytic functions like $e^{-1/x^2}$ can vanish on open sets. Holomorphic functions obey the identity theorem / maximum principle, so a non-zero holomorphic function with compact support would have to be zero on an open set and hence identically zero. This rigidity kills holomorphic partitions of unity and is why sheaf cohomology and \v{C}ech methods replace them on complex manifolds.

q: A smooth manifold $M$ is orientable iff:
text: A smooth manifold $M$ is orientable iff: Orientability is equivalent to the existence of an oriented atlas — one for which every transition $\varphi_\beta\circ\varphi_\alpha^{-1}$ has $\det D(\varphi_\beta\circ\varphi_\alpha^{-1})>0$ on its domain. Equivalently, $M$ admits a nowhere-vanishing top-degree form.

q: Which of the following manifolds is NOT orientable?
text: Which of the following manifolds is NOT orientable? The Möbius band has a single side: going once around the core circle and returning flips a local frame's handedness, so no consistent orientation exists. $S^2$, $T^2$, and $\mathbb{RP}^3$ are orientable ($\mathbb{RP}^n$ is orientable iff $n$ is odd).

q: How many orientations does a connected orientable smooth manifold admit?
text: How many orientations does a connected orientable smooth manifold admit? An orientation is a choice of sign on the top exterior power at each point, varying continuously. On a connected manifold there are exactly two global choices, differing by an overall sign flip.

q: Which statement correctly identifies $\mathbb{RP}^n$'s orientability pattern?
text: Which statement correctly identifies $\mathbb{RP}^n$'s orientability pattern? $\mathbb{RP}^n = S^n/\{\pm 1\}$. The antipodal map $a\colon S^n\to S^n$ is orientation-preserving iff its degree $(-1)^{n+1}$ is $+1$, i.e. iff $n$ is odd. So the quotient is orientable precisely when $n$ is odd. In particular $\mathbb{RP}^1\cong S^1$ and $\mathbb{RP}^3\cong\mathrm{SO}(3)$ are orientable; $\mathbb{RP}^2$ and $\mathbb{RP}^4$ are not.

q: On the 2-sphere $S^2$ with the usual orientation, consider the two stereographic charts from $N$ and $S$. On their overlap $S^2\setminus\{N,S\}$, the transition is $\varphi_S\circ\varphi_N^{-1}(u,v)=(u,v)/(u^2+v^2)$. What is the sign of the Jacobian determinant of this transition at $(u,v)=(1,0)$? Enter $+1$ or $-1$.
text: On the 2-sphere $S^2$ with the usual orientation, consider the two stereographic charts from $N$ and $S$. On their overlap $S^2\setminus\{N,S\}$, the transition is $\varphi_S\circ\varphi_N^{-1}(u,v)=(u,v)/(u^2+v^2)$. What is the sign of the Jacobian determinant of this transition at $(u,v)=(1,0)$? Enter $+1$ or $-1$. Write the transition as $(u,v)\mapsto(u/r^2,v/r^2)$ with $r^2=u^2+v^2$. Its Jacobian at $(1,0)$ has determinant $-1/r^4=-1$. So these two stereographic charts are oppositely oriented; to get a positively oriented atlas one composes $\varphi_S$ with a reflection. This is why the two-chart stereographic atlas is a smooth atlas but not an oriented atlas as stated — an adjustment is needed.

q: Which of the following is equivalent to the manifold $M$ being orientable?
text: Which of the following is equivalent to the manifold $M$ being orientable? The standard equivalence: $M$ is orientable iff $\Lambda^n T^*M$ has a nowhere-zero smooth section, i.e. there is a volume form. Every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric (via partitions of unity), so (B) is not equivalent. Trivial $TM$ (parallelizability) is strictly stronger than orientability — e.g. $S^2$ is orientable but not parallelizable.

title: Differential forms and wedge product
blurb: Alternating multilinear forms package oriented area and volume data.

title: Exterior derivative
blurb: The exterior derivative extends gradient/curl/divergence and satisfies d^2=0.

title: Pullback of forms
blurb: A smooth map f:M→N contravariantly transports k-forms back, commuting with d and ∧ — this is why integration of forms is coordinate-invariant.

title: Integration of forms over chains
blurb: A smooth k-chain is a formal sum of smooth simplices; integrating a k-form over a chain is defined via pullback to the standard simplex and extends by linearity.

title: Stokes theorem and de Rham cohomology
blurb: Stokes unifies integration theorems and identifies topology via closed/exact forms.

q: Which identity characterizes the wedge product of two $1$-forms $\alpha,\beta$?
text: Which identity characterizes the wedge product of two $1$-forms $\alpha,\beta$? The wedge of $1$-forms is antisymmetric: $\alpha\wedge\beta = -\beta\wedge\alpha$. In particular $\alpha\wedge\alpha = 0$, which is exactly the alternating/oriented-area property.

q: What is $\dim \Omega^2(\mathbb{R}^4)$ over $C^\infty(\mathbb{R}^4)$? (Equivalently, $\binom{4}{2}$.)
text: What is $\dim \Omega^2(\mathbb{R}^4)$ over $C^\infty(\mathbb{R}^4)$? (Equivalently, $\binom{4}{2}$.) A basis of $\Omega^k(\mathbb{R}^n)$ is $\{dx^{i_1}\wedge\cdots\wedge dx^{i_k}\}$ with strictly increasing indices, so the dimension is $\binom{n}{k}=\binom{4}{2}=6$.

q: Let $v=(2,1)$ and $w=(1,3)$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$. Compute $(dx\wedge dy)(v,w) = v_1 w_2 - v_2 w_1$.
text: Let $v=(2,1)$ and $w=(1,3)$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$. Compute $(dx\wedge dy)(v,w) = v_1 w_2 - v_2 w_1$. $(dx\wedge dy)(v,w)=\det\begin{pmatrix}2&1\\1&3\end{pmatrix}=2\cdot 3-1\cdot 1=5$.

q: Let $\alpha = x\,dy\wedge dz + y\,dz\wedge dx + z\,dx\wedge dy$ on $\mathbb{R}^3$. Compute $(dx\wedge dy\wedge dz)(e_1,e_2,e_3)$ where $e_i$ are standard basis vectors.
text: Let $\alpha = x\,dy\wedge dz + y\,dz\wedge dx + z\,dx\wedge dy$ on $\mathbb{R}^3$. Compute $(dx\wedge dy\wedge dz)(e_1,e_2,e_3)$ where $e_i$ are standard basis vectors. $(dx\wedge dy\wedge dz)(e_1,e_2,e_3)=\det[dx^i(e_j)]=\det I_3=1$. The wedge of all coordinate $1$-forms evaluates to $1$ on the positively oriented standard frame.

q: Which is a counterexample showing that $\alpha\wedge\alpha=0$ does NOT hold for all forms?
text: Which is a counterexample showing that $\alpha\wedge\alpha=0$ does NOT hold for all forms? Self-wedge vanishes for odd-degree forms (since $\alpha\wedge\alpha=(-1)^{k^2}\alpha\wedge\alpha$ forces $0$ when $k$ is odd). For even $k$, e.g. $k=2$ in $\mathbb{R}^4$: $(dx^1\wedge dx^2+dx^3\wedge dx^4)^2=2\,dx^1\wedge dx^2\wedge dx^3\wedge dx^4\ne 0$.

q: Compute $\dim\Omega^k(\mathbb{R}^5)$ for $k=3$.
text: Compute $\dim\Omega^k(\mathbb{R}^5)$ for $k=3$. $\binom{5}{3}=10$. Basis elements are $dx^{i_1}\wedge dx^{i_2}\wedge dx^{i_3}$ with $i_1<i_2<i_3$ from $\{1,2,3,4,5\}$.

q: Which property characterizes the exterior derivative $d$ (in addition to $df$ being the usual differential and $d$ being a graded derivation)?
text: Which property characterizes the exterior derivative $d$ (in addition to $df$ being the usual differential and $d$ being a graded derivation)? The defining axioms are: $df$ is the usual differential on $0$-forms, $d$ is a graded derivation, and $d^2=0$. The last is dual to $\partial\partial=0$ for chains.

q: For $\omega = x\,dy - y\,dx$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$, compute the coefficient of $dx\wedge dy$ in $d\omega$ (i.e. $Q_x - P_y$ where $\omega = P\,dx + Q\,dy$).
text: For $\omega = x\,dy - y\,dx$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$, compute the coefficient of $dx\wedge dy$ in $d\omega$ (i.e. $Q_x - P_y$ where $\omega = P\,dx + Q\,dy$). Here $P=-y$, $Q=x$, so $Q_x-P_y = 1-(-1)=2$. Thus $d\omega = 2\,dx\wedge dy$.

q: On $\mathbb{R}^3$, which classical identity does $d^2=0$ encode when applied to a $1$-form $\omega_X$ coming from a vector field $X$?
text:
On $\mathbb{R}^3$, which classical identity does $d^2=0$ encode when applied to a $1$-form $\omega_X$ coming from a vector field $X$? Via $f\leftrightarrow 0$-form, $X\leftrightarrow 1$-form, and $\star$ to get $2$-forms, $d^2=0$ becomes $
abla	imes(
abla f)=0$ and $
abla\cdot(
abla	imes X)=0$ — two classical identities, one proof.

q: Let $\omega = xyz\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz$ on $\mathbb{R}^3$. What is $d\omega$?
text: Let $\omega = xyz\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz$ on $\mathbb{R}^3$. What is $d\omega$? $\omega$ is already a $3$-form on $\mathbb{R}^3$, so $d\omega\in\Omega^4(\mathbb{R}^3)=0$. The coefficient (thought of as a $4$-form component) is $0$.

q: Compute $d(e^{xy}\,dx)$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$.
text: Compute $d(e^{xy}\,dx)$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$. $d(e^{xy}\,dx)=d(e^{xy})\wedge dx=(ye^{xy}\,dy+xe^{xy}\,dx)\wedge dx=ye^{xy}\,dy\wedge dx=-ye^{xy}\,dx\wedge dy$. Wait — the coefficient of $dx\wedge dy$ is $-(-ye^{xy})$: re-checking, $d(e^{xy}\,dx)=\frac{\partial e^{xy}}{\partial y}dy\wedge dx=xe^{xy}\,dy\wedge dx=-xe^{xy}\,dx\wedge dy$. So choice 1 with coefficient $xe^{xy}$ (negated orientation) matches choice B written as $xe^{xy}\,dx\wedge dy$ if we note $dy\wedge dx=-dx\wedge dy$.

q: Order these steps in deriving $d^2f=0$ for a smooth function $f$ on $\mathbb{R}^n$. Which ordering is correct? (I) Write $df=\sum_i \partial_i f\,dx^i$. (II) Apply $d$ again: $d^2f=\sum_{i,j}\partial_j\partial_i f\,dx^j\wedge dx^i$. (III) Note the sum over $i<j$ telescopes as $(\partial_j\partial_i f - \partial_i\partial_j f)dx^j\wedge dx^i=0$ by Schwarz. (IV) Antisymmetry of $\wedge$ pairs $(j,i)$ with $(i,j)$.
text: Order these steps in deriving $d^2f=0$ for a smooth function $f$ on $\mathbb{R}^n$. Which ordering is correct? (I) Write $df=\sum_i \partial_i f\,dx^i$. (II) Apply $d$ again: $d^2f=\sum_{i,j}\partial_j\partial_i f\,dx^j\wedge dx^i$. (III) Note the sum over $i<j$ telescopes as $(\partial_j\partial_i f - \partial_i\partial_j f)dx^j\wedge dx^i=0$ by Schwarz. (IV) Antisymmetry of $\wedge$ pairs $(j,i)$ with $(i,j)$. Logical order: (I) compute $df$, (II) apply $d$ to get a double sum, (IV) invoke antisymmetry of wedge to pair terms, (III) conclude each paired sum vanishes by Schwarz (equality of mixed partials).

q: Which compatibility does the pullback $f^*$ satisfy for a smooth $f:M\to N$?
text: Which compatibility does the pullback $f^*$ satisfy for a smooth $f:M\to N$? Pullback is an algebra homomorphism that commutes with $d$: $f^*(\alpha\wedge\beta)=f^*\alpha\wedge f^*\beta$ and $f^*\circ d = d\circ f^*$. These two facts are what make differential forms natural.

q: Let $f(u,v) = (u+v,\ uv)$ from $\mathbb{R}^2$ to $\mathbb{R}^2$ with target coordinates $(x,y)$. Compute the coefficient of $du\wedge dv$ in $f^*(dx\wedge dy)$ at $(u,v)=(3,1)$.
text: Let $f(u,v) = (u+v,\ uv)$ from $\mathbb{R}^2$ to $\mathbb{R}^2$ with target coordinates $(x,y)$. Compute the coefficient of $du\wedge dv$ in $f^*(dx\wedge dy)$ at $(u,v)=(3,1)$. $f^*dx = du+dv$ and $f^*dy = v\,du+u\,dv$, so $f^*(dx\wedge dy) = (du+dv)\wedge(v\,du+u\,dv) = (u-v)\,du\wedge dv$. At $(3,1)$ the coefficient is $3-1=2$. (Equivalently, the Jacobian determinant $\det Df = u-v$.)

q: Let $\iota:S^1\hookrightarrow\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$ parametrized by $\theta\mapsto(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)$. Compute $\iota^*\bigl((-y\,dx+x\,dy)/(x^2+y^2)\bigr)$.
text: Let $\iota:S^1\hookrightarrow\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$ parametrized by $\theta\mapsto(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)$. Compute $\iota^*\bigl((-y\,dx+x\,dy)/(x^2+y^2)\bigr)$. On $S^1$, $x=\cos\theta$, $y=\sin\theta$, $x^2+y^2=1$, $dx=-\sin\theta\,d\theta$, $dy=\cos\theta\,d\theta$. Then $-y\,dx+x\,dy=\sin^2\theta\,d\theta+\cos^2\theta\,d\theta=d\theta$. The pullback of the angle form is literally $d\theta$.

q: Let $f(u,v)=(u^2-v^2,\ 2uv)$ from $\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2$. Compute $\det Df$ at $(u,v)=(1,1)$.
text: Let $f(u,v)=(u^2-v^2,\ 2uv)$ from $\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2$. Compute $\det Df$ at $(u,v)=(1,1)$. $Df=\begin{pmatrix}2u & -2v \\ 2v & 2u\end{pmatrix}$, so $\det Df=4u^2+4v^2$. At $(1,1)$: $4+4=8$. Equivalently, $f$ corresponds to $z\mapsto z^2$ and $|f'(z)|^2=4|z|^2=8$ at $z=1+i$.

q: Which is a counterexample showing that $f^*$ does NOT always yield a nonzero form?
text: Which is a counterexample showing that $f^*$ does NOT always yield a nonzero form? When $\dim M < \dim N$, one cannot wedge more than $\dim M$ independent $1$-forms on $M$, so $\Omega^{\dim N}(M)=0$. Thus $f^*$ kills every top form of $N$, giving zero regardless of $f$.

q: Let $g(s,t)=(s\cos t,\ s\sin t)$ (polar coordinates), $s>0$. Compute the coefficient of $ds\wedge dt$ in $g^*(dx\wedge dy)$ at $s=2$.
text: Let $g(s,t)=(s\cos t,\ s\sin t)$ (polar coordinates), $s>0$. Compute the coefficient of $ds\wedge dt$ in $g^*(dx\wedge dy)$ at $s=2$. $g^*dx=\cos t\,ds-s\sin t\,dt$, $g^*dy=\sin t\,ds+s\cos t\,dt$. Wedging: $g^*(dx\wedge dy)=s(\cos^2 t+\sin^2 t)\,ds\wedge dt=s\,ds\wedge dt$. At $s=2$ the coefficient is $2$.

q: For a smooth $k$-simplex $\sigma:\Delta^k\to M$ and $\omega\in\Omega^k(M)$, the integral $\int_\sigma \omega$ is defined as:
text: For a smooth $k$-simplex $\sigma:\Delta^k\to M$ and $\omega\in\Omega^k(M)$, the integral $\int_\sigma \omega$ is defined as: Integration over a smooth simplex is $\int_\sigma\omega := \int_{\Delta^k}\sigma^*\omega$. This is well-defined because $\sigma^*\omega$ is a $k$-form on $\Delta^k\subset\mathbb{R}^k$, which integrates via the standard Lebesgue integral of its $du^1\wedge\cdots\wedge du^k$ coefficient.

q: Why is $\int_\sigma\omega$ independent of the parametrization $\sigma$ (up to orientation-preserving reparametrization)?
text: Why is $\int_\sigma\omega$ independent of the parametrization $\sigma$ (up to orientation-preserving reparametrization)? For an orientation-preserving diffeomorphism $\phi$, change of variables gives $\int\phi^*\eta = \int\eta$. Since forms pull back with a Jacobian determinant, the antisymmetry of $\wedge$ is exactly what makes integration of forms reparametrization-invariant.

q: Let $\sigma:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $\sigma(t)=(t,t^2)$, and $\omega = dy$. Compute $\int_\sigma\omega$.
text: Let $\sigma:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $\sigma(t)=(t,t^2)$, and $\omega = dy$. Compute $\int_\sigma\omega$. $\sigma^*\omega = \sigma^*(dy) = d(t^2) = 2t\,dt$. Then $\int_\sigma\omega = \int_0^1 2t\,dt = 1$. Geometrically, $\int_\sigma dy$ is just the net vertical displacement of $\sigma$, $y(1)-y(0)=1-0=1$.

q: Let $c = \sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ be a $1$-chain where $\sigma_1(t)=(t,0)$ and $\sigma_2(t)=(0,t)$ for $t\in[0,1]$, and let $\omega=x\,dx+y\,dy$. Compute $\int_c\omega$.
text: Let $c = \sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ be a $1$-chain where $\sigma_1(t)=(t,0)$ and $\sigma_2(t)=(0,t)$ for $t\in[0,1]$, and let $\omega=x\,dx+y\,dy$. Compute $\int_c\omega$. $\int_{\sigma_1}\omega=\int_0^1 t\,dt=\frac{1}{2}$. $\int_{\sigma_2}\omega=\int_0^1 t\,dt=\frac{1}{2}$. So $\int_c\omega=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2}=0$. Indeed $\omega=d(\frac{x^2+y^2}{2})$ is exact, so its integral over any cycle (or any chain with equal endpoints) is path-independent.

q: Order the steps for computing $\int_\sigma (x\,dy)$ over $\sigma(t)=(\cos t,\sin t)$, $t\in[0,\pi]$. (I) Pull back: $x\,dy=\cos t\cdot\cos t\,dt=\cos^2 t\,dt$. (II) Integrate: $\int_0^\pi\cos^2 t\,dt=\pi/2$. (III) Identify $x=\cos t$, $dy=\cos t\,dt$. (IV) Parametrize the curve.
text: Order the steps for computing $\int_\sigma (x\,dy)$ over $\sigma(t)=(\cos t,\sin t)$, $t\in[0,\pi]$. (I) Pull back: $x\,dy=\cos t\cdot\cos t\,dt=\cos^2 t\,dt$. (II) Integrate: $\int_0^\pi\cos^2 t\,dt=\pi/2$. (III) Identify $x=\cos t$, $dy=\cos t\,dt$. (IV) Parametrize the curve. Correct order: (IV) lay out the parametrization, (III) express the form's ingredients via the parametrization, (I) form the pullback, (II) integrate over $[0,\pi]$.

q: The boundary of the standard $2$-simplex $\Delta^2$ consists of three $1$-simplices. How many $0$-simplices (vertices) does $\partial\Delta^2$ have (counting with multiplicity from the chain formula $\partial_0\circ\partial_1 c=0$, i.e. how many distinct vertices)?
text: The boundary of the standard $2$-simplex $\Delta^2$ consists of three $1$-simplices. How many $0$-simplices (vertices) does $\partial\Delta^2$ have (counting with multiplicity from the chain formula $\partial_0\circ\partial_1 c=0$, i.e. how many distinct vertices)? $\partial\Delta^2 = [v_1,v_2]-[v_0,v_2]+[v_0,v_1]$. Each edge contributes its two endpoints; the three edges share the three vertices $v_0,v_1,v_2$. All boundary-of-boundary cancellations confirm $\partial^2=0$, and there are $3$ distinct vertices.

q: Stokes' theorem says:
text: Stokes' theorem says: Stokes: $\int_M d\omega = \int_{\partial M}\omega$. It unifies the fundamental theorem of calculus, Green, classical Stokes, and divergence theorems.

q: Let $\omega = (-y\,dx + x\,dy)/(x^2+y^2)$ on $\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$ and let $\gamma$ be a loop winding $n=3$ times counterclockwise around $0$. Compute $\oint_\gamma \omega / \pi$.
text: Let $\omega = (-y\,dx + x\,dy)/(x^2+y^2)$ on $\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$ and let $\gamma$ be a loop winding $n=3$ times counterclockwise around $0$. Compute $\oint_\gamma \omega / \pi$. $\oint_\gamma \omega = 2\pi n = 6\pi$, so $\oint_\gamma\omega/\pi = 6$. Nonzero integral means $\omega$ is closed but not exact.
