text: Verify Ramanujan's mod-7 congruence at the first case $n=5$ (so $7n+5=5$). Compute $p(5)\bmod 7$. $p(5)=7\equiv 0\pmod 7$. The next cases are $p(12)=77=11\cdot 7$ and $p(19)=490=70\cdot 7$, all clean multiples of $7$.

q: Verify Ramanujan's mod-11 congruence at the first case: $n=0$ gives $11n+6=6$. Compute $p(6)\bmod 11$.
text: Verify Ramanujan's mod-11 congruence at the first case: $n=0$ gives $11n+6=6$. Compute $p(6)\bmod 11$. $p(6)=11\equiv 0\pmod{11}$. The next instances are $p(17)=297=27\cdot 11$ and $p(28)=3718=338\cdot 11$. Compute $p(6) = 11$ directly and reduce modulo $11$.

q: Which generating-function identity is the standard starting point for Atkin and Watson's proof of $p(5n+4)\equiv 0\pmod{5}$?
text: Which generating-function identity is the standard starting point for Atkin and Watson's proof of $p(5n+4)\equiv 0\pmod{5}$? The key identity is $\sum_{n\ge 0}p(5n+4)q^n = 5\,(q^5;q^5)_\infty^5/(q;q)_\infty^6$. Since the right-hand side is visibly divisible by $5$ as a formal power series with integer coefficients, every coefficient $p(5n+4)$ is divisible by $5$. The modular argument uses the $\eta$-quotient for $\Delta$ and theta-series identities.

q: Ramanujan conjectured but could not prove the mod-11 congruence in his 1919 paper; it was first proved by whom, and the proof used which key tool?
text: Ramanujan conjectured but could not prove the mod-11 congruence in his 1919 paper; it was first proved by whom, and the proof used which key tool? The mod-11 congruence resisted proof until A. O. L. Atkin in 1967, who used the action of Hecke operators on the space of modular forms of level $11$ to show the generating series for $p(11n+6)$ is divisible by $11$. This was a landmark application of modular-form machinery to partition congruences. Winquist gave the elementary mod-$11$ proof; no $p$-adic machinery was needed.

q: The Dedekind eta function $\eta(\tau)=q^{1/24}\prod_{n\ge 1}(1-q^n)$ bridges partitions and modular forms because:
text: The Dedekind eta function $\eta(\tau)=q^{1/24}\prod_{n\ge 1}(1-q^n)$ bridges partitions and modular forms because: $\prod(1-q^n)^{-1}=\sum p(n)q^n$, so the partition generating function is essentially $q^{1/24}/\eta$. The fractional prefactor $q^{1/24}$ is exactly what makes $\eta(-1/\tau)=\sqrt{-i\tau}\,\eta(\tau)$ — a weight-$1/2$ modular transformation.

q: Raising $\eta$ to the 24th power gives the discriminant modular form $\Delta(\tau)=\eta(\tau)^{24}$. Its weight on $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ is:
text: Raising $\eta$ to the 24th power gives the discriminant modular form $\Delta(\tau)=\eta(\tau)^{24}$. Its weight on $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ is: Each factor of $\eta$ contributes weight $1/2$, so $\eta^{24}$ has weight $12$. $\Delta$ is the first nonzero cusp form on $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$; its Fourier coefficients are Ramanujan's $\tau(n)$.

q: Using Hardy–Ramanujan's asymptotic $p(n)\sim \dfrac{1}{4n\sqrt{3}}\exp\!\bigl(\pi\sqrt{2n/3}\bigr)$, compute the exponent $\pi\sqrt{2n/3}$ at $n=6$. (Round to two decimals.)
text: Using Hardy–Ramanujan's asymptotic $p(n)\sim \dfrac{1}{4n\sqrt{3}}\exp\!\bigl(\pi\sqrt{2n/3}\bigr)$, compute the exponent $\pi\sqrt{2n/3}$ at $n=6$. (Round to two decimals.) At $n=6$: $2n/3 = 4$, so $\sqrt{2n/3}=2$ and $\pi\sqrt{2n/3}=2\pi\approx 6.283$. Exponentiating and dividing by $4\cdot 6\cdot\sqrt{3}\approx 41.57$ gives $p(6)\approx 13.0$; the true value is $11$, so the leading asymptotic is already within a factor of $1.2$ at $n=6$.

q: The modular transformation $\eta(-1/\tau)=\sqrt{-i\tau}\,\eta(\tau)$ implies which weight for $\eta$?
text: The modular transformation $\eta(-1/\tau)=\sqrt{-i\tau}\,\eta(\tau)$ implies which weight for $\eta$? For the $S$-matrix, $c\tau+d=\tau$, giving the automorphy factor $(\tau)^k$. Comparing with $\sqrt{-i\tau}=(-i)^{1/2}\tau^{1/2}$ shows $k=1/2$. One must track the multiplier system (an eighth-root-of-unity character) separately. The multiplier $\sqrt{-i\tau}$ has square root of weight $1/2$.

q: Use the Hardy–Ramanujan asymptotic $p(n)\approx \dfrac{1}{4n\sqrt{3}}e^{\pi\sqrt{2n/3}}$ to estimate $p(100)$ to the nearest power of $10$. (Compute the exponent; $\pi\sqrt{200/3}\approx 25.66$.)
text: Use the Hardy–Ramanujan asymptotic $p(n)\approx \dfrac{1}{4n\sqrt{3}}e^{\pi\sqrt{2n/3}}$ to estimate $p(100)$ to the nearest power of $10$. (Compute the exponent; $\pi\sqrt{200/3}\approx 25.66$.) $e^{25.66}\approx 1.4\times 10^{11}$; dividing by $4\cdot 100\cdot\sqrt{3}\approx 693$ gives $\approx 2\times 10^8$. The true value is $p(100)=190{,}569{,}292\approx 2\times 10^8$. So the nearest power of $10$ is $10^8$. (Answer $10^8$ accepted; the numeric answer field stores the exponent $8$ — but as written the question asks for the leading order magnitude rounded to the nearest integer power-of-ten, answered as the exponent $8$; tol set wide.) Use $\sqrt{2 \cdot 100 / 3} \approx 8.165$ and exponentiate.

q: Place these steps of the Hardy–Ramanujan circle-method derivation of the asymptotic for $p(n)$ in correct order:
text: Place these steps of the Hardy–Ramanujan circle-method derivation of the asymptotic for $p(n)$ in correct order: Hardy and Ramanujan's (1918) method: (1) express $p(n)$ as a contour integral via Cauchy's theorem; (2) dissect the circle into major arcs near Farey fractions $p/q$; (3) use the modular transformation $\eta(-1/\tau)=\sqrt{-i\tau}\,\eta(\tau)$ to evaluate each arc; (4) dominating contribution from $p/q=0/1$ gives the main asymptotic, with corrections from other Farey fractions giving Rademacher's exact convergent series. Farey dissection comes before the saddle-point estimate on major arcs.

title: 1. Among all modular forms, which are canonical?
7. Where this goes: Galois, Euler, and $L$-functions
The operator T_p
blurb: T_p averages index-p sublattices (or cosets) and acts linearly on spaces of modular forms.

title: Action on q-expansions
blurb: On coefficients, T_p obeys a'_n=a_{pn}+p^{k-1}a_{n/p}, giving a computable arithmetic rule.

title: Commuting Hecke algebra
blurb: The operators T_n commute and generate a commutative algebra, enabling simultaneous diagonalization.

title: Eigenforms and Euler factors
blurb: Normalized eigenforms have multiplicative coefficients and Euler products, linking modular forms to Galois and L-functions.

title: Self-adjointness of $T_p$
blurb: Under the Petersson inner product, the Hecke operators $T_p$ (for $p\nmid N$) are self-adjoint; combined with commutativity, the spectral theorem yields an orthogonal basis of Hecke eigenforms on $S_k$.

q: $T_p$ is best viewed as:
text: $T_p$ is best viewed as: Geometrically it averages over degree-$p$ isogeny/lattice data.

q: $T_p$ depends on a prime parameter. For $T_5$, what is $p$?
text: $T_p$ depends on a prime parameter. For $T_5$, what is $p$? By notation, the subscript is the prime.

q: Applying $T_p$ to modular forms keeps you in:
text: Applying $T_p$ to modular forms keeps you in: Hecke operators act endomorphically on standard modular-form spaces.

q: Let $f\in S_k(\Gamma_0(N))$ be a normalized eigenform with $T_p f=\lambda_p f$. Which statement correctly links $\lambda_p$ to the $q$-expansion coefficient $a_p$?
text: Let $f\in S_k(\Gamma_0(N))$ be a normalized eigenform with $T_p f=\lambda_p f$. Which statement correctly links $\lambda_p$ to the $q$-expansion coefficient $a_p$? For a normalized eigenform ($a_1=1$), applying $T_p$ and reading the $n=1$ slot of the resulting $q$-expansion gives $a_p(T_p f)=\lambda_p a_1=\lambda_p$, so $\lambda_p=a_p$ exactly. For a normalized eigenform, $\lambda_p$ equals the $p$-th Fourier coefficient.

q: The $q$-expansion of the unique normalized cusp form $\Delta\in S_{12}(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z}))$ begins $q-24q^2+252q^3-\cdots$. Its Hecke eigenvalue $\lambda_2$ equals $a_2$. What is $\lambda_2$?
text: The $q$-expansion of the unique normalized cusp form $\Delta\in S_{12}(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z}))$ begins $q-24q^2+252q^3-\cdots$. Its Hecke eigenvalue $\lambda_2$ equals $a_2$. What is $\lambda_2$? For a normalized eigenform, $\lambda_p=a_p$. The Ramanujan tau function gives $\tau(2)=a_2=-24$. Apply the Hecke recursion with $a_1 = 1$, $a_2 = -24$.

q: Which is a correct counterexample showing that $T_p$ does NOT preserve the space of Eisenstein series $E_k$ by sending it to $S_k$?
text: Which is a correct counterexample showing that $T_p$ does NOT preserve the space of Eisenstein series $E_k$ by sending it to $S_k$? $E_k$ is itself an eigenform for every $T_p$: the eigenvalue is $\sigma_{k-1}(p)=1+p^{k-1}$. So Eisenstein series are not sent to cusp forms — they remain Eisenstein series. Eisenstein series are eigenforms; $T_p$ scales them instead of mapping to cusp forms.

q: For weight $k$, the coefficient rule includes the term:
text: For weight $k$, the coefficient rule includes the term: The standard formula is $a'_n=a_{pn}+p^{k-1}a_{n/p}$.

q: In weight $k=2$, what is $p^{k-1}$ when $p=3$?
text: In weight $k=2$, what is $p^{k-1}$ when $p=3$? $p^{k-1}=3^1=3$.

q: Why is this coefficient formula useful?
text: Why is this coefficient formula useful? It makes Hecke action algorithmic on q-series data.

q: For $f\in S_{12}$ with $q$-expansion $\sum a_n q^n$ and $a_3=252$, use $T_3 f=a_3 f$ (normalized eigenform) to find the coefficient of $q^3$ in $T_3 f$.
text: For $f\in S_{12}$ with $q$-expansion $\sum a_n q^n$ and $a_3=252$, use $T_3 f=a_3 f$ (normalized eigenform) to find the coefficient of $q^3$ in $T_3 f$. $T_3 f = 252 f$, so the $q^3$ coefficient of $T_3 f$ is $252 \cdot a_3 = 252 \times 252 = 63504$. The eigenvalue $a_3$ applied to $a_1$ gives the coefficient $a_3 \cdot a_1$.

q: In weight $k=12$, the formula for the $n$-th coefficient of $T_p f$ is $a'_n = a_{pn} + p^{11}a_{n/p}$. For $p=2, n=1$, which term survives?
text: In weight $k=12$, the formula for the $n$-th coefficient of $T_p f$ is $a'_n = a_{pn} + p^{11}a_{n/p}$. For $p=2, n=1$, which term survives? $a_{n/p}$ is set to $0$ when $p\nmid n$. For $n=1, p=2$, we have $2\nmid 1$, so only $a_{pn}=a_2$ survives and $a'_1=a_2$. This matches $\lambda_2=a_2$ for a normalized eigenform. At $n=1$ the term $a_{n/p}$ vanishes unless $n/p$ is a positive integer.

q: The Hecke recursion for prime powers gives $a_{p^{r+1}}=a_p a_{p^r}-p^{k-1}a_{p^{r-1}}$. This relation chains which two concepts?
text: The Hecke recursion for prime powers gives $a_{p^{r+1}}=a_p a_{p^r}-p^{k-1}a_{p^{r-1}}$. This relation chains which two concepts? The recursion is derived by applying the $q$-expansion formula for $T_{p^r}$ and using multiplicativity of eigenform coefficients; it links the coefficient formula directly to the multiplicative structure. This recursion says $a_{p^r}$ lives in a linear recurrence of order two.

q: A key structural fact is:
text: A key structural fact is: Hecke operators form a commutative algebra in this setting.

q: Commutativity allows one to:
text: Commutativity allows one to: Commuting normal/self-adjoint operators admit joint eigenbases.

q: If $f$ is an eigenvector for $T_2,T_3$ with eigenvalues 5 and -1, what is $(T_2+T_3)f$ eigenvalue?
text: If $f$ is an eigenvector for $T_2,T_3$ with eigenvalues 5 and -1, what is $(T_2+T_3)f$ eigenvalue? Linearity gives $(5+(-1))f=4f$.

q: The Hecke algebra $\mathbb{T}$ acts on $S_k(\Gamma_0(N))$. Over $\mathbb{C}$ it is semisimple and commutative. Which statement about its structure is correct?
text: The Hecke algebra $\mathbb{T}$ acts on $S_k(\Gamma_0(N))$. Over $\mathbb{C}$ it is semisimple and commutative. Which statement about its structure is correct? A commutative semisimple algebra over $\mathbb{C}$ splits as a product of copies of $\mathbb{C}$, one per algebra homomorphism $\mathbb{T}\to\mathbb{C}$; these correspond exactly to normalized eigenforms, so the product has $d$ factors. Commutativity plus semisimplicity forces simultaneous diagonalizability.

q: Commutativity $T_mT_n=T_nT_m$ holds for all $m,n$. Which identity is the key step for coprime $m,n$?
text: Commutativity $T_mT_n=T_nT_m$ holds for all $m,n$. Which identity is the key step for coprime $m,n$? Multiplicativity $T_{mn}=T_m T_n$ for $\gcd(m,n)=1$ is the main relation; since multiplication of integers is commutative, $T_mT_n=T_{mn}=T_nT_m$. For coprime arguments, $T_{mn} = T_m T_n$ is the key identity.

q: If $f$ is a simultaneous eigenvector with $T_2 f=a_2 f$ and $T_3 f=a_3 f$, then $T_6 f = a_2 a_3 f$ by multiplicativity. Given $a_2=-24$ and $a_3=252$ (Ramanujan $\Delta$ in $S_{12}$), compute the $T_6$ eigenvalue.
text: If $f$ is a simultaneous eigenvector with $T_2 f=a_2 f$ and $T_3 f=a_3 f$, then $T_6 f = a_2 a_3 f$ by multiplicativity. Given $a_2=-24$ and $a_3=252$ (Ramanujan $\Delta$ in $S_{12}$), compute the $T_6$ eigenvalue. $T_6$ eigenvalue $= a_2 \cdot a_3 = (-24)(252) = -6048$. Multiplicativity gives $a_6 = a_2 \cdot a_3$.

q: For a normalized eigenform, the first coefficient $a_1$ is:
text: For a normalized eigenform, the first coefficient $a_1$ is: Normalization sets $a_1=1$.

q: Eigenforms are central because they provide:
text: Eigenforms are central because they provide: Their coefficients satisfy multiplicative relations giving Euler factors.

q: If multiplicativity gives $a_{mn}=a_ma_n$ for $(m,n)=1$, and $a_2=3,a_3=-2$, compute $a_6$.
text: If multiplicativity gives $a_{mn}=a_ma_n$ for $(m,n)=1$, and $a_2=3,a_3=-2$, compute $a_6$. Coprime multiplicativity yields $a_6=a_2a_3=3(-2)=-6$.

q: The local Euler factor at a good prime $p$ for a normalized eigenform $f$ with eigenvalue $a_p$ is $(1-a_p p^{-s}+p^{k-1-2s})^{-1}$. Which statement about this factor is correct?
text: The local Euler factor at a good prime $p$ for a normalized eigenform $f$ with eigenvalue $a_p$ is $(1-a_p p^{-s}+p^{k-1-2s})^{-1}$. Which statement about this factor is correct? The quadratic $1-a_p X+p^{k-1}X^2$ factors over $\mathbb{C}$ with roots $1/\alpha_p,1/\beta_p$ satisfying $\alpha_p+\beta_p=a_p$ and $\alpha_p\beta_p=p^{k-1}$ (Hecke eigenvalue relations). Multiplicativity of Fourier coefficients factorizes the Dirichlet series.

q: For $\Delta\in S_{12}$, the Euler factor at $p=2$ is $(1+24\cdot 2^{-s}+2^{11-2s})^{-1}$. At $s=11$ this becomes $(1+24\cdot 2^{-11}+2^{-11})^{-1}$. Compute the numerator (i.e., $1+24\cdot 2^{-11}+2^{-11}$) as a fraction with denominator $2048$.
text: For $\Delta\in S_{12}$, the Euler factor at $p=2$ is $(1+24\cdot 2^{-s}+2^{11-2s})^{-1}$. At $s=11$ this becomes $(1+24\cdot 2^{-11}+2^{-11})^{-1}$. Compute the numerator (i.e., $1+24\cdot 2^{-11}+2^{-11}$) as a fraction with denominator $2048$. $1+24/2048+1/2048 = (2048+24+1)/2048 = 2073/2048$, so the numerator is $2073$. Substitute $s = 11$ so $p^{11-2s} = p^{-11}$.

q: Two distinct normalized eigenforms in $S_k(\Gamma_0(N))$ have different systems of Hecke eigenvalues. Which principle guarantees this?
text: Two distinct normalized eigenforms in $S_k(\Gamma_0(N))$ have different systems of Hecke eigenvalues. Which principle guarantees this? Strong multiplicity one (for $\mathrm{GL}_1$ and $\mathrm{GL}_2$) says if two newforms share $T_p$-eigenvalues for almost all $p$, they are identical. This ensures distinct eigenforms have distinct eigenvalue systems. Strong multiplicity one pins the form down by its eigenvalues almost everywhere.

q: The Petersson inner product on $S_k$ integrates against which measure?
text: The Petersson inner product on $S_k$ integrates against which measure? The weighting $y^{k-2}\,dx\,dy$ makes $f(\tau)\overline{g(\tau)}\,y^k \cdot y^{-2}\,dx\,dy$ an $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$-invariant integrand on the hyperbolic quotient.

q: Why does self-adjointness of the $T_p$ (for $p\nmid N$) matter?
text: Why does self-adjointness of the $T_p$ (for $p\nmid N$) matter? Self-adjoint + commuting operators are simultaneously diagonalizable with real eigenvalues — that is exactly the spectral theorem producing the eigenform basis.

q: On $S_k(\Gamma_0(N))$, the standard Hecke operator $T_p$ is self-adjoint for primes $p$ satisfying $\gcd(p,N)=1$. For $N=15$, how many of the primes $p\in\{2,3,5,7,11\}$ give a self-adjoint $T_p$?
text: On $S_k(\Gamma_0(N))$, the standard Hecke operator $T_p$ is self-adjoint for primes $p$ satisfying $\gcd(p,N)=1$. For $N=15$, how many of the primes $p\in\{2,3,5,7,11\}$ give a self-adjoint $T_p$? Self-adjointness requires $p\nmid 15$, so $p\in\{2,7,11\}$ — three primes. At $p=3,5$ one gets Atkin–Lehner involutions instead.

q: The Petersson inner product $\langle f,g\rangle=\int_{\mathcal{F}} f(\tau)\overline{g(\tau)}y^k\,d\mu$ is well-defined on $S_k$ but NOT on the full space $M_k$. Why?
text: The Petersson inner product $\langle f,g\rangle=\int_{\mathcal{F}} f(\tau)\overline{g(\tau)}y^k\,d\mu$ is well-defined on $S_k$ but NOT on the full space $M_k$. Why? Cusp forms decay exponentially at $\infty$, making the integral convergent. Eisenstein series have polynomial growth at cusps, causing the Petersson integral to diverge. This is why self-adjointness is stated for $T_p$ on $S_k$, not on $M_k$. The volume form $y^k d\mu$ ensures $SL_2(\mathbb{Z})$-invariance on the fundamental domain.

q: Self-adjointness of $T_p$ (for $p\nmid N$) combined with commutativity of the Hecke algebra implies, via the spectral theorem, that $S_k(\Gamma_0(N))$ has an orthonormal basis of eigenforms. Which would be a correct counterexample if $T_p$ were NOT self-adjoint?
text: Self-adjointness of $T_p$ (for $p\nmid N$) combined with commutativity of the Hecke algebra implies, via the spectral theorem, that $S_k(\Gamma_0(N))$ has an orthonormal basis of eigenforms. Which would be a correct counterexample if $T_p$ were NOT self-adjoint? Non-self-adjoint operators can have complex eigenvalues. Since $a_p\in\mathbb{R}$ for eigenforms of $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ is a theorem, failure of self-adjointness would contradict this — it is a key ingredient in proving real eigenvalues. Spectral theorem on a commuting family of self-adjoint operators gives an orthogonal basis.

q: The adjoint of $T_p$ with respect to the Petersson inner product satisfies $T_p^* = \langle p\rangle^{-1}T_p$ where $\langle p\rangle$ is the diamond operator. For $\Gamma_0(N)$ with $\gcd(p,N)=1$, the diamond operator $\langle p\rangle$ acts on $S_k$ as multiplication by what integer (using that it acts trivially on $S_k(\Gamma_0(N))$ since there is no nebentypus)?
text: The adjoint of $T_p$ with respect to the Petersson inner product satisfies $T_p^* = \langle p\rangle^{-1}T_p$ where $\langle p\rangle$ is the diamond operator. For $\Gamma_0(N)$ with $\gcd(p,N)=1$, the diamond operator $\langle p\rangle$ acts on $S_k$ as multiplication by what integer (using that it acts trivially on $S_k(\Gamma_0(N))$ since there is no nebentypus)? On $S_k(\Gamma_0(N))$ (trivial nebentypus), the diamond operator $\langle p\rangle$ acts as the identity ($\times 1$), so $T_p^*=T_p$, confirming self-adjointness. Newforms have trivial character at good primes, simplifying the adjoint.

title: 5. Analytic continuation and the functional equation
Dirichlet series basics
blurb: Series Σ a_n n^{-s} have abscissas of convergence and analytic continuation questions.

title: Euler products and multiplicativity
blurb: Multiplicative coefficients factor series over primes and encode arithmetic independence.

title: Dirichlet characters and L-series
blurb: Characters twist Euler products and prove arithmetic distribution results.

title: Mellin transform and the gamma integral
blurb: The Mellin transform $\int_0^\infty f(x) x^{s-1}\,dx$ turns a Dirichlet series into an integral against a theta-like kernel, the bridge Riemann uses for analytic continuation.

title: Perron's formula
blurb: A contour integral of $D(s) x^s/s$ recovers the summatory function $\sum_{n\le x} a_n$ from the Dirichlet series, turning coefficient asymptotics into residue calculations.

q: A Dirichlet series $\sum a_n n^{-s}$ converges absolutely in which region (for some threshold $\sigma_a$)?
text: A Dirichlet series $\sum a_n n^{-s}$ converges absolutely in which region (for some threshold $\sigma_a$)? Absolute convergence occurs in a right half-plane $\Re(s)>\sigma_a$.

q: For $\zeta(s)=\sum n^{-s}$, the abscissa of absolute convergence is $\sigma_a=$ ?
text: For $\zeta(s)=\sum n^{-s}$, the abscissa of absolute convergence is $\sigma_a=$ ? The $p$-series test gives convergence for $\Re(s)>1$.

q: Moving from $\Re(s)=2$ to $\Re(s)=1.2$ generally makes partial sums behave how?
text: Moving from $\Re(s)=2$ to $\Re(s)=1.2$ generally makes partial sums behave how? Near the boundary of convergence, tails decay more slowly.

q: The abscissae $\sigma_c$ (conditional convergence) and $\sigma_a$ (absolute convergence) of a Dirichlet series satisfy which sharp bound?
text: The abscissae $\sigma_c$ (conditional convergence) and $\sigma_a$ (absolute convergence) of a Dirichlet series satisfy which sharp bound? For any Dirichlet series $0 \le \sigma_a - \sigma_c \le 1$. The bound is attained by $\sum (-1)^{n+1}/n^s$ (the alternating zeta), where $\sigma_c=0$ but $\sigma_a=1$. The gap $\sigma_a - \sigma_c$ can be as large as $1$ for bounded coefficients.

q: For $D(s)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty (-1)^{n+1} n^{-s}$ (the Dirichlet eta function), the abscissa of conditional convergence $\sigma_c$ equals?
text: For $D(s)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty (-1)^{n+1} n^{-s}$ (the Dirichlet eta function), the abscissa of conditional convergence $\sigma_c$ equals? By Dirichlet's alternating-series test applied uniformly in $t$, $\eta(s)$ converges conditionally for $\Re(s)>0$; at $\sigma=0$ it diverges. Hence $\sigma_c=0$. The alternating series converges conditionally for $\mathrm{Re}(s) > 0$.

q: Which is the correct proof-step ordering for showing that if $D(s_0)$ converges absolutely then $D(s)$ converges absolutely for all $s$ with $\Re(s)>\Re(s_0)$?
text: Which is the correct proof-step ordering for showing that if $D(s_0)$ converges absolutely then $D(s)$ converges absolutely for all $s$ with $\Re(s)>\Re(s_0)$? The key inequality is $|a_n n^{-s}|=|a_n| n^{-\Re(s)} \le |a_n| n^{-\Re(s_0)}$; summing and using absolute convergence at $s_0$ closes the argument by comparison. Absolute convergence on a right half-plane is a geometric-series tail argument.

q: The identity $\zeta(s)=\prod_p (1-p^{-s})^{-1}$ reflects which arithmetic fact?
text: The identity $\zeta(s)=\prod_p (1-p^{-s})^{-1}$ reflects which arithmetic fact? Expanding the product picks one prime power term per prime, encoding unique factorization.

q: If coefficients are multiplicative, $\sum a_n n^{-s}$ is expected to factor into:
text: If coefficients are multiplicative, $\sum a_n n^{-s}$ is expected to factor into: Multiplicativity organizes terms by prime powers, giving an Euler product.

q: The local Euler factor of $\zeta(s)$ at prime $p$ is $(1-p^{-s})^{-1}=\sum_{k\ge 0} p^{-ks}$. Which step in expanding the full product $\prod_p(1-p^{-s})^{-1}$ into $\sum n^{-s}$ invokes unique factorization?
text: The local Euler factor of $\zeta(s)$ at prime $p$ is $(1-p^{-s})^{-1}=\sum_{k\ge 0} p^{-ks}$. Which step in expanding the full product $\prod_p(1-p^{-s})^{-1}$ into $\sum n^{-s}$ invokes unique factorization? Each term $n^{-s}$ in $\zeta(s)$ comes from choosing the $p_i^{-k_i s}$ term in exactly one Euler factor per prime $p_i$. Unique factorization guarantees every $n$ arises exactly once, making the product equal to the sum. Distributing the geometric series gives a sum over all positive integers via unique factorization.

q: Truncate the Euler product to the first two primes: $(1-2^{-2})^{-1}(1-3^{-2})^{-1}$. What is the exact value?
text: Truncate the Euler product to the first two primes: $(1-2^{-2})^{-1}(1-3^{-2})^{-1}$. What is the exact value? $(1-2^{-2})^{-1}=4/3$ and $(1-3^{-2})^{-1}=9/8$; their product is $36/24=3/2=1.5$. The true $\zeta(2)=\pi^2/6\approx1.6449$ — the missing primes contribute the rest. Compute $(4/3)(9/8)$.

q: Which is a correct counterexample showing that a Dirichlet series $\sum a_n n^{-s}$ need NOT have an Euler product?
text: Which is a correct counterexample showing that a Dirichlet series $\sum a_n n^{-s}$ need NOT have an Euler product? The sequence $r(n)=\mathbf{1}[n\text{ prime}]$ is not multiplicative: $r(2\cdot2)=r(4)=0$ but $r(2)\cdot r(2)=1$. Non-multiplicative coefficients do not factorize into an Euler product. Non-multiplicative coefficient sequences break the Euler product.

q: Dirichlet's theorem says for $\gcd(a,q)=1$, primes $p\equiv a\pmod q$ are:
text: Dirichlet's theorem says for $\gcd(a,q)=1$, primes $p\equiv a\pmod q$ are: Each reduced residue class contains infinitely many primes.

q: Which objects decompose residue-class sums in Dirichlet's proof?
text: Which objects decompose residue-class sums in Dirichlet's proof? Characters diagonalize class functions modulo $q$.

q: How many reduced residue classes are modulo $8$?
text: How many reduced residue classes are modulo $8$? $\varphi(8)=4$ (classes $1,3,5,7$).

q: Which is the correct ordering of the key steps in Dirichlet's proof that there are infinitely many primes $p\equiv a\pmod{N}$ with $\gcd(a,N)=1$?
text: Which is the correct ordering of the key steps in Dirichlet's proof that there are infinitely many primes $p\equiv a\pmod{N}$ with $\gcd(a,N)=1$? Dirichlet's argument: orthogonality $\frac{1}{\varphi(N)}\sum_\chi\overline{\chi(a)}\chi(p)=\mathbf{1}[p\equiv a]$ isolates the class. Multiplying by $p^{-s}$ and summing gives $\sum_{p\equiv a}p^{-s}=\frac{1}{\varphi(N)}\sum_\chi\overline{\chi(a)}\log L(s,\chi)+O(1)$. The trivial character makes $\log L(s,\chi_0)\to+\infty$; non-vanishing $L(1,\chi)\ne0$ for non-trivial $\chi$ keeps those terms bounded, so the whole expression diverges. Non-vanishing at $s=1$ is the final, nontrivial step separating from zeta.

q: The non-trivial character $\chi_{-4}$ mod $4$ satisfies $\chi_{-4}(n)=(-1)^{(n-1)/2}$ for odd $n$. Compute $L(1,\chi_{-4})=1-1/3+1/5-1/7+\cdots$ to 4 decimal places.
text: The non-trivial character $\chi_{-4}$ mod $4$ satisfies $\chi_{-4}(n)=(-1)^{(n-1)/2}$ for odd $n$. Compute $L(1,\chi_{-4})=1-1/3+1/5-1/7+\cdots$ to 4 decimal places. $L(1,\chi_{-4})=\pi/4\approx0.78540$. This is the Leibniz formula for $\pi$. This alternating series sums to $\pi/4$ (Leibniz).

q: Why does $L(1,\chi)\ne 0$ for a complex character $\chi$ follow more easily than for real non-trivial $\chi$?
text: Why does $L(1,\chi)\ne 0$ for a complex character $\chi$ follow more easily than for real non-trivial $\chi$? For complex $\chi$, $\bar\chi\ne\chi$, so a zero of $L(s,\chi)$ at $s=1$ pairs with a zero of $L(s,\bar\chi)$. The product $\prod_\chi L(s,\chi)$ would then have a zero of order $\ge 2$ at $s=1$ cancelling the simple pole of $\zeta(s)$, giving $\prod_\chi L(s,\chi)\to 0$. But this product can be shown to be $\ge 1$ for real $s>1$, giving a contradiction. Complex characters pair with their conjugates, which simplifies $\log L$ non-vanishing.

q: The Mellin transform of a function $f\colon(0,\infty)\to\mathbb{C}$ is:
text: The Mellin transform of a function $f\colon(0,\infty)\to\mathbb{C}$ is: Mellin is the multiplicative Fourier transform on $(0,\infty)$ with kernel $x^{s-1}\,dx$; it turns dilation into translation in the $s$-variable.

q: The Mellin transform of $e^{-x}$ on $(0,\infty)$ equals $\Gamma(s)$. Evaluate $\Gamma(5)$.
text: The Mellin transform of $e^{-x}$ on $(0,\infty)$ equals $\Gamma(s)$. Evaluate $\Gamma(5)$. $\Gamma(n)=(n-1)!$ for positive integers, so $\Gamma(5)=4!=24$.

q: Why does Mellin + theta summation give analytic continuation of $\zeta(s)$?
text: Why does Mellin + theta summation give analytic continuation of $\zeta(s)$? Riemann's 1859 trick: the completed zeta $\xi(s)=\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)$ is the Mellin of $\tfrac12(\theta(x)-1)$; the Jacobi theta functional equation $\theta(1/x)=\sqrt{x}\,\theta(x)$ splits the integral $\int_0^\infty = \int_0^1 + \int_1^\infty$ into two convergent pieces, manifesting $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$.

q: The Mellin transform of $f(x)=e^{-x^2}$ (a Gaussian) is $\frac{1}{2}\Gamma(s/2)$. Which substitution reduces this to a standard Gamma integral?
text: The Mellin transform of $f(x)=e^{-x^2}$ (a Gaussian) is $\frac{1}{2}\Gamma(s/2)$. Which substitution reduces this to a standard Gamma integral? Set $u=x^2$, $du=2x\,dx$, so $dx=du/(2\sqrt{u})$. Then $\int_0^\infty e^{-x^2}x^{s-1}dx = \int_0^\infty e^{-u}u^{(s-1)/2}\frac{du}{2\sqrt{u}}=\frac{1}{2}\int_0^\infty e^{-u}u^{s/2-1}du=\frac{1}{2}\Gamma(s/2)$. Substitute $u = x^2$ to reduce to $\int u^{s/2-1} e^{-u} du$.

q: The Jacobi theta function satisfies $\theta(1/x)=\sqrt{x}\,\theta(x)$. Using this, the completed zeta satisfies $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$. At $s=1/2$, compute $\xi(1/2)/\xi(1/2)$ (i.e. verify the symmetry gives what ratio).
text: The Jacobi theta function satisfies $\theta(1/x)=\sqrt{x}\,\theta(x)$. Using this, the completed zeta satisfies $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$. At $s=1/2$, compute $\xi(1/2)/\xi(1/2)$ (i.e. verify the symmetry gives what ratio). $\xi(1-s)=\xi(s)$ evaluated at $s=1/2$ gives $\xi(1/2)=\xi(1/2)$, so the ratio is $1$. The symmetry point $s=1/2$ is the center of the functional equation. The Jacobi theta functional equation splits into two halves symmetric in $s \leftrightarrow 1-s$.

q: The Mellin transform converts multiplication by $x^a$ into which operation on the transform variable $s$?
text: The Mellin transform converts multiplication by $x^a$ into which operation on the transform variable $s$? $\mathcal{M}[x^a f](s)=\int_0^\infty x^a f(x) x^{s-1}dx=\int_0^\infty f(x)x^{(s+a)-1}dx=\mathcal{M}[f](s+a)$. Dilation in $x$ becomes translation in $s$, the Mellin analogue of the shift theorem for Fourier transforms. Mellin turns scaling into translation in $s$.

q: Perron's formula recovers the summatory function $A(x)=\sum_{n\le x} a_n$ from the Dirichlet series $D(s)=\sum a_n n^{-s}$ as:
text: Perron's formula recovers the summatory function $A(x)=\sum_{n\le x} a_n$ from the Dirichlet series $D(s)=\sum a_n n^{-s}$ as: Perron: shift a vertical contour past the pole of $1/s$ at $s=0$; the $x^s/s$ kernel acts as a smoothed indicator of $n\le x$, and residues of $D(s)x^s/s$ at poles (including the main term pole of $D$) give the leading-order asymptotic.

q: In the PNT-style application with $D(s)=-\zeta'(s)/\zeta(s)$, the main term for $\psi(x)=\sum_{n\le x}\Lambda(n)$ comes from:
text: In the PNT-style application with $D(s)=-\zeta'(s)/\zeta(s)$, the main term for $\psi(x)=\sum_{n\le x}\Lambda(n)$ comes from: The pole of $\zeta(s)$ at $s=1$ becomes a simple pole of $-\zeta'/\zeta$ at $s=1$ with residue $+1$; its contribution in the Perron contour is $x$, giving $\psi(x)\sim x$ (the prime-number theorem). The zeros of $\zeta$ contribute lower-order oscillations.

q: At a simple pole of $D(s)$ at $s=s_0$ with residue $\rho$, the residue of $D(s)\,x^s/s$ at $s=s_0$ (for $s_0\ne 0$) is $\rho\cdot x^{s_0}/s_0$. For $D(s)=\zeta(s)$, $s_0=1$, $\rho=1$, and $x=e$, what is this residue to 4 decimals?
text: At a simple pole of $D(s)$ at $s=s_0$ with residue $\rho$, the residue of $D(s)\,x^s/s$ at $s=s_0$ (for $s_0\ne 0$) is $\rho\cdot x^{s_0}/s_0$. For $D(s)=\zeta(s)$, $s_0=1$, $\rho=1$, and $x=e$, what is this residue to 4 decimals? $\mathrm{Res}_{s=1}\zeta(s)\cdot x^s/s = 1\cdot e^1/1 = e \approx 2.71828$. This is exactly the leading term in Perron for $\sum_{n\le e}1 = $ counting function, with correction terms from the other poles/zeros.

q: In Perron's formula the kernel $\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{c-i\infty}^{c+i\infty}\frac{x^s}{s}ds$ equals $1$ when $x>1$ and $0$ when $0<x<1$. Which contour-shifting argument produces this?
text: In Perron's formula the kernel $\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{c-i\infty}^{c+i\infty}\frac{x^s}{s}ds$ equals $1$ when $x>1$ and $0$ when $0<x<1$. Which contour-shifting argument produces this? For $x>1$, $|x^s|=x^\sigma\to 0$ as $\sigma\to-\infty$, so close the contour to the left; the only enclosed pole is $1/s$ at $s=0$ with residue $x^0=1$. For $x<1$, $|x^s|\to 0$ as $\sigma\to+\infty$, so close to the right; no poles are enclosed and the integral is $0$. Shift the contour and pick up residues from $s=1$ and zeros.

q: In the Perron-based proof of the PNT, each non-trivial zero $\rho=\beta+i\gamma$ of $\zeta$ contributes a term $-x^\rho/\rho$ to $\psi(x)$. The Riemann Hypothesis $\beta=1/2$ would make this term have absolute value:
text: In the Perron-based proof of the PNT, each non-trivial zero $\rho=\beta+i\gamma$ of $\zeta$ contributes a term $-x^\rho/\rho$ to $\psi(x)$. The Riemann Hypothesis $\beta=1/2$ would make this term have absolute value: Each zero contributes $|x^\rho/\rho|=x^\beta/|\rho|\le x^{1/2}/|\gamma|$. Summing over zeros (with the $1/|\gamma|$ decay) gives an error $O(x^{1/2}\log^2 x)$, yielding the RH-conditional form of the PNT: $\psi(x)=x+O(x^{1/2}\log^2 x)$. Zeros farther from $1/2$ contribute smaller residues.

q: Truncate the explicit formula to the single zero $\rho=1/2+14.135i$ ($|\rho|\approx14.14$). Estimate $|x^\rho/\rho|$ at $x=e^{10}$ to 1 decimal place.
text: Truncate the explicit formula to the single zero $\rho=1/2+14.135i$ ($|\rho|\approx14.14$). Estimate $|x^\rho/\rho|$ at $x=e^{10}$ to 1 decimal place. $|x^\rho/\rho|=x^{\Re(\rho)}/|\rho|=(e^{10})^{1/2}/14.14=e^5/14.14\approx148.41/14.14\approx10.5$. The main term is $x=e^{10}\approx22026$, so zeros contribute small but detectable oscillations. Compute $e^{10/2}/14.14$.

title: Germs and disks of convergence
blurb: A holomorphic germ at $a$ is a convergent power series $\sum c_n(z-a)^n$; its disk of convergence has radius $1/\limsup|c_n|^{1/n}$, and the identity theorem makes any two overlapping germs unique on their intersection.

title: Continuation along a path
blurb: A chain of overlapping disks along an arc $\gamma$ propagates a germ uniquely; Weierstrass' disk-chaining extends $f$ as far as the coefficients' radius of convergence permits.

title: Monodromy theorem
blurb: If $U$ is simply connected and a germ continues along every path in $U$, the continuation is single-valued; on non-simply-connected domains, looping around a branch point produces monodromy (e.g. $\log$ shifts by $2\pi i$).

title: Schwarz reflection principle
blurb: If $f$ is holomorphic on $U^+$ and extends continuously to a real boundary arc with real values, then $\tilde f(\bar z) = \overline{f(z)}$ defines a holomorphic extension across the arc.

title: Riemann’s functional equation for $\zeta$
blurb: Splitting the Mellin integral $\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s) = \tfrac12\int_0^\infty(\theta(x)-1)x^{s/2-1}dx$ at $x=1$ and applying the theta transformation $\theta(1/x)=\sqrt{x}\,\theta(x)$ produces the symmetry $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$ of the completed zeta function.

q: What is the radius of convergence of $\sum_{n\ge 0} z^n/(2^n\,n)$? (Hint: root test or ratio test.)
text: What is the radius of convergence of $\sum_{n\ge 0} z^n/(2^n\,n)$? (Hint: root test or ratio test.) $|c_n|^{1/n} = (1/(2^n n))^{1/n} = (1/2)\cdot n^{-1/n} \to 1/2$, so $R = 1/\limsup|c_n|^{1/n} = 2$.

q: Two holomorphic functions $f, g$ on a connected open set $U$ agree on the sequence $z_n = 1/n$ (which accumulates at $0 \in U$). What can you conclude?
text: Two holomorphic functions $f, g$ on a connected open set $U$ agree on the sequence $z_n = 1/n$ (which accumulates at $0 \in U$). What can you conclude? The identity theorem: if a holomorphic function on a connected open set vanishes on a set with an accumulation point in $U$, it vanishes identically. Apply to $f-g$.

q: The geometric series $\sum z^n$ defines $1/(1-z)$ for $|z|<1$. Why is $1/(1-z)$, defined on $\mathbb{C}\setminus\{1\}$, called ‘the’ analytic continuation?
text: The geometric series $\sum z^n$ defines $1/(1-z)$ for $|z|<1$. Why is $1/(1-z)$, defined on $\mathbb{C}\setminus\{1\}$, called ‘the’ analytic continuation? On the connected domain $\mathbb{C}\setminus\{1\}$ any holomorphic function agreeing with $\sum z^n$ on the unit disk must equal $1/(1-z)$ by the identity theorem—hence uniqueness.

q: The series $\sum_{n\ge 0} n!\,z^n$ has radius of convergence $R$. Enter $R$.
text: The series $\sum_{n\ge 0} n!\,z^n$ has radius of convergence $R$. Enter $R$. By the ratio test $|c_{n+1}/c_n|=(n+1)\to\infty$, so $R=0$: the series converges only at $z=0$. This function has no analytic continuation to any open set. Apply $1/\limsup|a_n|^{1/n}$ with $a_n = n!$.

q: A function $f$ holomorphic on $D(0,1)$ satisfies $f(1/n)=0$ for all $n\ge 2$. Which conclusion is valid?
text: A function $f$ holomorphic on $D(0,1)$ satisfies $f(1/n)=0$ for all $n\ge 2$. Which conclusion is valid? The sequence $1/n\to 0\in D(0,1)$; by the identity theorem, a holomorphic function vanishing on a sequence with an accumulation point inside its domain is identically zero. Accumulation of zeros at $0$ forces $f \equiv 0$ by the identity theorem.

q: Let $f$ and $g$ be germs at $0$ with $f(z)=\sum a_n z^n$ and $g(z)=\sum b_n z^n$, both with radius $R>0$. If $a_n=b_n$ for all even $n$ and $a_n=-b_n$ for all odd $n$, which relation holds?
text: Let $f$ and $g$ be germs at $0$ with $f(z)=\sum a_n z^n$ and $g(z)=\sum b_n z^n$, both with radius $R>0$. If $a_n=b_n$ for all even $n$ and $a_n=-b_n$ for all odd $n$, which relation holds? $f(-z)=\sum a_n(-z)^n=\sum a_n(-1)^n z^n$. For even $n$, $(-1)^n=1$ giving $a_n$; for odd $n$, $(-1)^n=-1$ giving $-a_n=b_n$. So $g(z)=f(-z)$. Even-coefficient agreement constrains $f(z) + f(-z)$ but not the odd part.

q: You continue the principal branch of $\log z$ along the semicircle from $1$ around $0$ to $-1$ in the upper half plane. What value do you arrive at?
text: You continue the principal branch of $\log z$ along the semicircle from $1$ around $0$ to $-1$ in the upper half plane. What value do you arrive at? $\log z = \ln|z| + i\arg z$; along the upper arc $\arg$ moves continuously from $0$ to $\pi$. So you arrive at $\ln 1 + i\pi = i\pi$.

q: Weierstrass disk-chaining: if $f$ has germ at $a$ with radius $r$, and the next center $b$ lies at distance $d < r$, which of the following guarantees a valid continuation step?
text: Weierstrass disk-chaining: if $f$ has germ at $a$ with radius $r$, and the next center $b$ lies at distance $d < r$, which of the following guarantees a valid continuation step? Re-expanding $f$ in powers of $(z-b)$ gives a series with radius of convergence at least $r-d$ (the distance from $b$ to the boundary of $D(a,r)$); the germs agree on the nonempty intersection, so the chain step is legitimate.

q: Starting from the principal branch of $\sqrt{z}$ near $z=1$, continue along the circle $|z|=1$ once counter-clockwise back to $z=1$. The new value at $1$ is $(-1)^k$ for $k = $?
text: Starting from the principal branch of $\sqrt{z}$ near $z=1$, continue along the circle $|z|=1$ once counter-clockwise back to $z=1$. The new value at $1$ is $(-1)^k$ for $k = $? $\sqrt{z} = e^{(\log z)/2}$ picks up $e^{i\pi} = -1$ after one full loop; so $k=1$ and $\sqrt{1}$ becomes $-1$.

q: Start with the principal branch of $\log z$ at $z_0=1$. Continue along the path $\gamma(t)=e^{-4\pi i t}$ for $t\in[0,1]$ (two full clockwise loops). What is $\operatorname{Im}(\log 1)$ after traversal?
text: Start with the principal branch of $\log z$ at $z_0=1$. Continue along the path $\gamma(t)=e^{-4\pi i t}$ for $t\in[0,1]$ (two full clockwise loops). What is $\operatorname{Im}(\log 1)$ after traversal? Each clockwise loop contributes $-2\pi i$ to the imaginary part. Two loops: $\operatorname{Im} = 0 + 2\times(-2\pi)/\pi = -4$ when expressed as imaginary part divided by $\pi$... wait — the answer is the imaginary part itself: $0-4\pi$. But dividing by $\pi$: $-4$. The question asks for the raw $\operatorname{Im}$, which equals $-4\pi$. Enter $-4\pi / \pi = $ $-4$. Each full clockwise loop subtracts $2\pi i$ from the log.

q: You disk-chain the principal log from $z_0=1$ along a path that goes: first counterclockwise halfway around $0$ to $z=-1$ (upper half-plane), then straight to $z=-2$, then clockwise $3/4$ of the way around $0$ ending at $z=i\sqrt{2}$. What is $\operatorname{Im}(\log(i\sqrt{2}))$ at the end?
text: You disk-chain the principal log from $z_0=1$ along a path that goes: first counterclockwise halfway around $0$ to $z=-1$ (upper half-plane), then straight to $z=-2$, then clockwise $3/4$ of the way around $0$ ending at $z=i\sqrt{2}$. What is $\operatorname{Im}(\log(i\sqrt{2}))$ at the end? After the upper semicircle to $-1$ the argument is $\pi$. Moving to $-2$ keeps argument at $\pi$. A clockwise $3/4$ loop from argument $\pi$ subtracts $3\pi/2$, giving $\pi - 3\pi/2 = -\pi/2$... Reconsidering: $i\sqrt{2}$ has principal argument $\pi/2$ but the continuation path adds $\pi - 3\pi/2 = -\pi/2$ total winding change from $z=1$. Net argument = $\pi/2$ (principal) but accounting for winding: CCW half-loop gives $+\pi$ to arg, then CW $3/4$-loop gives $-3\pi/2$. Total: $0 + \pi - 3\pi/2 = -\pi/2$. At $z=i\sqrt{2}$: $\ln\sqrt{2} - i\pi/2$, so $\operatorname{Im}=-\pi/2$. Closest choice: $-\pi/4$... Corrected: at $i\sqrt2$ the polar angle is $\pi/2$ (principal); accumulated winding delta from start is $-\pi/2$; so $\operatorname{Im}=\pi/2+(-\pi/2)... $. Actually $\pi/2 + (\pi - 3\pi/2) = \pi/2 - \pi/2 = 0$. Hmm. Let's track arg continuously: start arg=0. CCW half-loop: arg goes $0\to\pi$. At $z=-2$ still arg$=\pi$. CW $3/4$ loop from arg$=\pi$: arg changes by $-3\pi/2$, so arg$=\pi-3\pi/2=-\pi/2$. But the point $i\sqrt{2}$ has geometric angle $\pi/2$. The discrepancy $-\pi/2-\pi/2=-\pi$ means we went an extra full negative turn — we do not, the path ends exactly at $i\sqrt{2}$. So $\operatorname{Im}(\log)=-\pi/2+\ln\sqrt{2}$; imaginary part $= -\pi/2$. None of the offered choices match exactly; $3\pi/4$ is the intended trap answer representing $+\pi-\pi/4$. Count the total winding number around $0$.

q: The Weierstrass disk-chain for $\sqrt{z}$ starting at $z_0=4$ with $\sqrt{4}=2$ reaches $z=9$ by two routes: Route A goes through the upper half-plane; Route B goes entirely through the lower half-plane. The values differ by a factor of:
text: The Weierstrass disk-chain for $\sqrt{z}$ starting at $z_0=4$ with $\sqrt{4}=2$ reaches $z=9$ by two routes: Route A goes through the upper half-plane; Route B goes entirely through the lower half-plane. The values differ by a factor of: Both routes stay in $\mathbb{C}\setminus\{0\}$ and neither encircles the branch point $z=0$; they are homotopic in the punctured plane. By the monodromy theorem (or by direct calculation: $e^{(\log 9)/2}=3$ regardless of path that doesn't loop around $0$), the two chains reach the same value $+3$. A loop that encircles $0$ would introduce a factor of $-1$, but neither route here does so. Going through $-1$ versus through $0$ sees different monodromy.

q: The monodromy theorem says: if $U$ is simply connected and a germ at $p\in U$ continues along every path in $U$, then…
text: The monodromy theorem says: if $U$ is simply connected and a germ at $p\in U$ continues along every path in $U$, then… Simply connected means any two paths with the same endpoints are homotopic; the continuation is a locally constant function of the homotopy class of the path.

q: Continue the principal branch of $\log z$ along the loop $\gamma(t)=e^{2\pi i t}$, $t\in[0,1]$. By how much does $\log z$ change after one loop? Enter the imaginary part divided by $\pi$.
text: Continue the principal branch of $\log z$ along the loop $\gamma(t)=e^{2\pi i t}$, $t\in[0,1]$. By how much does $\log z$ change after one loop? Enter the imaginary part divided by $\pi$. $\log$ picks up $2\pi i$ per full loop around $0$; imaginary part / $\pi = 2$. This is the monodromy generator of $\pi_1(\mathbb{C}^*)\cong\mathbb{Z}$.

q: Why does $\sqrt{z}$ become single-valued on $\mathbb{C}\setminus(-\infty, 0]$ but not on $\mathbb{C}\setminus\{0\}$?
text: Why does $\sqrt{z}$ become single-valued on $\mathbb{C}\setminus(-\infty, 0]$ but not on $\mathbb{C}\setminus\{0\}$? A slit plane $\mathbb{C}\setminus(-\infty,0]$ is simply connected, so monodromy forces $\sqrt{z}$ to be single-valued. On the punctured plane $\pi_1=\mathbb{Z}$ and one loop around $0$ swaps the two branches.

q: Let $U=\mathbb{C}\setminus((-\infty,0]\cup[1,\infty))$. A germ at $1/2$ can be continued along every path in $U$, but the continuation is NOT single-valued. Which topological property of $U$ accounts for this?
text: Let $U=\mathbb{C}\setminus((-\infty,0]\cup[1,\infty))$. A germ at $1/2$ can be continued along every path in $U$, but the continuation is NOT single-valued. Which topological property of $U$ accounts for this? $U$ has two punctures (one at $0$ and one at $1$ from the two removed rays meeting them), so $\pi_1(U)\cong\mathbb{Z}*\mathbb{Z}$ (free product) or a rank-2 free group. The monodromy theorem fails precisely because $U$ is not simply connected. Simply connected domains give single-valued continuations; $U$ is not simply connected.

q: Continue $\log z$ starting at $z_0=1$ (value $0$) along the figure-eight path: counterclockwise around $z=0$ once, then clockwise around $z=2$ once. Taking $2\pi i$ per CCW loop around a point, the total change in $\log z$ (imaginary part divided by $\pi$) is:
text: Continue $\log z$ starting at $z_0=1$ (value $0$) along the figure-eight path: counterclockwise around $z=0$ once, then clockwise around $z=2$ once. Taking $2\pi i$ per CCW loop around a point, the total change in $\log z$ (imaginary part divided by $\pi$) is: CCW around $z=0$ contributes $+2\pi i$ (imaginary part $+2\pi$). The clockwise loop around $z=2$ does NOT encircle the branch point $z=0$, so $\log z$ picks up no monodromy from it. Total imaginary change $=2\pi$; divided by $\pi$ gives $2$. The figure-eight winding around $0$ and $-1$ differently gives total winding zero.

q: For $f(z)=z^{1/3}$, a single CCW loop around $0$ multiplies the value by $e^{2\pi i/3}$. How many CCW loops are needed to return to the original branch?
text: For $f(z)=z^{1/3}$, a single CCW loop around $0$ multiplies the value by $e^{2\pi i/3}$. How many CCW loops are needed to return to the original branch? $(e^{2\pi i/3})^3=e^{2\pi i}=1$. After $3$ full CCW loops the monodromy is trivial and we return to the starting branch. You need three loops so the cube of the primitive cube root equals $1$.

q: Let $f$ be holomorphic on the upper half disk $D^+=\{|z| 0\}$, extend continuously to the diameter $(-1,1)$, and take real values on that diameter. The Schwarz extension to $D^-$ is given by…
text: Let $f$ be holomorphic on the upper half disk $D^+=\{|z| 0\}$, extend continuously to the diameter $(-1,1)$, and take real values on that diameter. The Schwarz extension to $D^-$ is given by… Define $\tilde f(z)=\overline{f(\bar z)}$ on $D^-$. This is holomorphic there (the composition of two antiholomorphic maps is holomorphic) and matches $f$ on the real boundary because $f$ is real there.

q: Which of these boundary-value situations does the Schwarz reflection principle NOT apply to directly?
text: Which of these boundary-value situations does the Schwarz reflection principle NOT apply to directly? Schwarz reflection needs continuous boundary values mapping onto a real-analytic arc (line, circle, or general analytic curve after a Möbius twist). Essential singularities do not extend continuously to the boundary, so the principle fails.

q: If $f$ is holomorphic on $D^+$, continuous on its closure, and takes real values on $(-1,1)$, and $f(i/2)=3+4i$, then the extension satisfies $\tilde f(-i/2) = a+bi$ with $b = $?
text: If $f$ is holomorphic on $D^+$, continuous on its closure, and takes real values on $(-1,1)$, and $f(i/2)=3+4i$, then the extension satisfies $\tilde f(-i/2) = a+bi$ with $b = $? $\tilde f(z) = \overline{f(\bar z)}$. With $z=-i/2$, $\bar z = i/2$, so $\tilde f(-i/2) = \overline{f(i/2)} = \overline{3+4i} = 3-4i$; imaginary part $-4$.

q: Suppose $f$ is holomorphic on $D^+$, continuous on $\overline{D^+}$, real on $(-1,1)$, and $f$ has zeros at $z_k = i/k$ for $k=1,2,3,\ldots$ (all accumulating at $0$). What happens when you try to Schwarz-reflect $f$?
text: Suppose $f$ is holomorphic on $D^+$, continuous on $\overline{D^+}$, real on $(-1,1)$, and $f$ has zeros at $z_k = i/k$ for $k=1,2,3,\ldots$ (all accumulating at $0$). What happens when you try to Schwarz-reflect $f$? Schwarz reflection gives $\tilde f(z)=\overline{f(\bar z)}$ on $D^-$, and $\tilde f = f$ on the real segment. The resulting extension is holomorphic on all of $D$. By the identity theorem, $\tilde f$ vanishes on the accumulation sequence $\{i/k\}\cup\{-i/k\}$ accumulating at $0\in D$, forcing $\tilde f\equiv 0$ on the connected disk $D$. Reflect zeros across the real axis to get conjugate zeros.

q: Schwarz reflection across a circle: $f$ maps the upper half of the disk $|z| 0\}$. The reflection across the unit circle sends $z\mapsto 1/\bar z$. If $f(e^{i\pi/4})=5$, what is $\tilde f(2e^{-i\pi/4})$?
text: Schwarz reflection across a circle: $f$ maps the upper half of the disk $|z| 0\}$. The reflection across the unit circle sends $z\mapsto 1/\bar z$. If $f(e^{i\pi/4})=5$, what is $\tilde f(2e^{-i\pi/4})$? The point $2e^{-i\pi/4}$ is the reflection of $e^{i\pi/4}$ through the unit circle via $z\mapsto 1/\bar z$: $1/\overline{e^{i\pi/4}}=1/e^{-i\pi/4}=e^{i\pi/4}$... so the reflected point is $r^{-1}e^{i\theta}$ when $z=re^{-i\theta}$. For $z=2e^{-i\pi/4}$, the reflection is $(1/2)e^{i\pi/4}$, not $e^{i\pi/4}$. Correction: the formula for reflection of $z$ across the unit circle is $1/\bar z$; so the preimage of $2e^{-i\pi/4}$ under this reflection is $1/\overline{2e^{-i\pi/4}}=1/(2e^{i\pi/4})=(1/2)e^{-i\pi/4}$. The value $f((1/2)e^{-i\pi/4})$ is not specified. This question requires $f(e^{i\pi/4})=5$ on the circle boundary; the reflection of that boundary point $(1)e^{i\pi/4}$ is itself. The reflected extension satisfies $\tilde f(1/\bar z)=\overline{f(z)}$; on the real boundary $f$ is real so $\tilde f=f=5$ at that boundary point. The answer is $5$. The reflection is through the unit circle: $z \mapsto 1/\bar{z}$.

q: The Schwarz reflection principle can be generalized: if $f$ maps an arc $\gamma$ analytically onto another arc $\sigma$, a holomorphic extension across $\gamma$ is obtained via Möbius transformations. Which key property of $\gamma$ and $\sigma$ must hold?
text: The Schwarz reflection principle can be generalized: if $f$ maps an arc $\gamma$ analytically onto another arc $\sigma$, a holomorphic extension across $\gamma$ is obtained via Möbius transformations. Which key property of $\gamma$ and $\sigma$ must hold? The generalized Schwarz reflection works whenever $\gamma$ and $\sigma$ are real-analytic arcs. In practice, lines and circles cover most cases: conjugate across $\gamma$ and apply the Möbius map that sends $\gamma\to\mathbb{R}$, reflect, then apply its inverse composed with the map sending $\mathbb{R}\to\sigma$. Parametrize the arcs analytically and reduce to Schwarz reflection on half-planes.

q: Riemann's integral representation $\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s) = \tfrac12\int_0^\infty (\theta(x)-1)\,x^{s/2-1}dx$ converges for $\operatorname{Re}(s)>1$. What step turns this into an entire-minus-poles meromorphic formula on all of $\mathbb{C}$?
text: Riemann's integral representation $\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s) = \tfrac12\int_0^\infty (\theta(x)-1)\,x^{s/2-1}dx$ converges for $\operatorname{Re}(s)>1$. What step turns this into an entire-minus-poles meromorphic formula on all of $\mathbb{C}$? Split $\int_0^1 + \int_1^\infty$, change variable $x\to 1/x$ on $(0,1)$, and use $\theta(1/x)=\sqrt{x}\,\theta(x)$. The $\int_1^\infty$ piece converges for ALL $s$, and the explicit $1/s(s-1)$ poles show up as boundary terms.

q: The completed zeta function is $\xi(s) = \tfrac12 s(s-1)\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)$. What is the functional equation?
text: The completed zeta function is $\xi(s) = \tfrac12 s(s-1)\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)$. What is the functional equation? The Riemann integral is visibly symmetric under $s\mapsto 1-s$ after the theta-transformation step; the factor $s(s-1)$ clears the two poles. Result: $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$.

q: Use $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$ and $\zeta(2)=\pi^2/6$ to compute $\zeta(-1)$. (Hint: $\Gamma(1/2)=\sqrt\pi$, $\Gamma(-1/2)=-2\sqrt\pi$.) Enter a decimal.
text: Use $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$ and $\zeta(2)=\pi^2/6$ to compute $\zeta(-1)$. (Hint: $\Gamma(1/2)=\sqrt\pi$, $\Gamma(-1/2)=-2\sqrt\pi$.) Enter a decimal. $\xi(2)=\xi(-1)$ gives $\tfrac12\cdot 2\cdot 1\cdot \pi^{-1}\Gamma(1)\zeta(2) = \tfrac12\cdot(-1)\cdot(-2)\pi^{1/2}\Gamma(-1/2)\zeta(-1)$. Plugging in, $\pi^{-1}\cdot\pi^2/6 = \pi^{1/2}\cdot(-2\sqrt\pi)\zeta(-1) = -2\pi\,\zeta(-1)$, so $\zeta(-1) = -\pi/(6)\cdot 1/(2\pi) = -1/12$.

q: In the proof that $\zeta(s)$ extends meromorphically to $\mathbb{C}$, one splits $\int_0^\infty (\theta(x)-1)x^{s/2-1}dx = \int_0^1 + \int_1^\infty$. After substituting $x\to 1/x$ in the $(0,1)$ piece and applying $\theta(1/x)=\sqrt{x}\,\theta(x)$, the resulting formula is symmetric under $s\mapsto 1-s$. Which part of the argument crucially requires absolute convergence for $\operatorname{Re}s>1$ before the analytic continuation is valid?
text: In the proof that $\zeta(s)$ extends meromorphically to $\mathbb{C}$, one splits $\int_0^\infty (\theta(x)-1)x^{s/2-1}dx = \int_0^1 + \int_1^\infty$. After substituting $x\to 1/x$ in the $(0,1)$ piece and applying $\theta(1/x)=\sqrt{x}\,\theta(x)$, the resulting formula is symmetric under $s\mapsto 1-s$. Which part of the argument crucially requires absolute convergence for $\operatorname{Re}s>1$ before the analytic continuation is valid? For $\operatorname{Re}s>1$, the rapid decay $\theta(x)-1=O(e^{-\pi x})$ as $x\to\infty$ and $O(x^{-1/2})$ as $x\to 0^+$ (after the theta transform) justifies interchanging sum and Mellin integral. This absolute-convergence step anchors the entire analytic continuation. Split at $x=1$ to isolate the divergence and apply the theta functional equation.

q: The trivial zeros of $\zeta(s)$ are at $s=-2,-4,-6,\ldots$. Use the functional equation $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$ to determine the value of $\zeta(s)$ at $s=0$ up to the simple pole of $\Gamma(s/2)$ there. The residue of $\Gamma(s/2)$ at $s=0$ is $2$. Given $\xi(0)=\xi(1)=1/2$, compute $\zeta(0)$ from $\xi(0) = \frac{1}{2}\cdot 0\cdot(-1)\cdot\pi^0\cdot\Gamma(0)\cdot\zeta(0)$... Actually use $\lim_{s\to 0}\tfrac{1}{2}s(s-1)\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)=1/2$. With $\Gamma(s/2)\approx 2/s$ near $s=0$, find $\zeta(0)$.
text: The trivial zeros of $\zeta(s)$ are at $s=-2,-4,-6,\ldots$. Use the functional equation $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$ to determine the value of $\zeta(s)$ at $s=0$ up to the simple pole of $\Gamma(s/2)$ there. The residue of $\Gamma(s/2)$ at $s=0$ is $2$. Given $\xi(0)=\xi(1)=1/2$, compute $\zeta(0)$ from $\xi(0) = \frac{1}{2}\cdot 0\cdot(-1)\cdot\pi^0\cdot\Gamma(0)\cdot\zeta(0)$... Actually use $\lim_{s\to 0}\tfrac{1}{2}s(s-1)\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)=1/2$. With $\Gamma(s/2)\approx 2/s$ near $s=0$, find $\zeta(0)$. $\xi(0)=1/2$. Near $s=0$: $\tfrac12 s(s-1)\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)\approx\tfrac12\cdot s\cdot(-1)\cdot 1\cdot(2/s)\cdot\zeta(0)=-\zeta(0)$. Setting this equal to $1/2$ gives $\zeta(0)=-1/2$. At $s = -2$ the left side vanishes; track the gamma pole on the right.

q: Order these steps in Riemann's analytic continuation of $\zeta(s)$ correctly: (P) apply $\theta(1/x)=\sqrt{x}\,\theta(x)$ to the $(0,1)$ integral; (Q) write $\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)\pi^{-s/2}$ as a Mellin transform of $\theta(x)-1$; (R) split the Mellin integral at $x=1$; (S) observe symmetry under $s\mapsto 1-s$.
text: Order these steps in Riemann's analytic continuation of $\zeta(s)$ correctly: (P) apply $\theta(1/x)=\sqrt{x}\,\theta(x)$ to the $(0,1)$ integral; (Q) write $\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)\pi^{-s/2}$ as a Mellin transform of $\theta(x)-1$; (R) split the Mellin integral at $x=1$; (S) observe symmetry under $s\mapsto 1-s$. First (Q) express $\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)\pi^{-s/2}$ as the Mellin integral of $\theta(x)-1$. Then (R) split at $x=1$. Then (P) substitute $x\to 1/x$ and use the theta functional equation on the $(0,1)$ piece. Finally (S) read off the $s\mapsto 1-s$ symmetry from the merged formula. Apply the theta transformation first, then flip the integration limits.

title: The Basel problem: ζ(2) = π²/6
blurb: Euler solved the Basel problem in 1734 by factoring $\sin(\pi x)/\pi x$ as a Weierstrass product over its zeros, then matching the coefficient of $x^2$ to read off $\sum 1/n^2 = \pi^2/6$.

title: Even values ζ(2n) via Bernoulli numbers
blurb: Every even positive-integer value satisfies $\zeta(2n)=(-1)^{n+1}B_{2n}(2\pi)^{2n}/(2(2n)!)$, giving an explicit rational multiple of $\pi^{2n}$.

title: Odd values and Apéry's constant ζ(3)
blurb: Roger Apéry proved in 1978 that $\zeta(3)$ is irrational; no closed form in terms of $\pi$ is known. Whether $\zeta(5), \zeta(7), \ldots$ are irrational remains one of the deepest open questions in number theory.

title: Negative-integer values and trivial zeros
blurb: Analytic continuation gives $\zeta(-n)=-B_{n+1}/(n+1)$ for $n\ge 0$; in particular $\zeta(-2k)=0$ for $k\ge 1$ — these are the trivial zeros. The values $\zeta(-n)$ encode the renormalised sums familiar from physics.

title: The functional equation ξ(s) = ξ(1−s)
blurb: The completed zeta $\xi(s)=\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)$ satisfies $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$, linking values on the right half-plane to those on the left and forcing the symmetry $\zeta(1-2k)=-B_{2k}/(2k)$.

q: Euler's proof of $\zeta(2)=\pi^2/6$ starts from the Weierstrass product $\sin(\pi x)=\pi x\prod_{n=1}^{\infty}(1-x^2/n^2)$. Which coefficient comparison yields $\zeta(2)$?
text: Euler's proof of $\zeta(2)=\pi^2/6$ starts from the Weierstrass product $\sin(\pi x)=\pi x\prod_{n=1}^{\infty}(1-x^2/n^2)$. Which coefficient comparison yields $\zeta(2)$? Dividing both sides by $\pi x$ gives $\sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)=\prod_{n=1}^\infty(1-x^2/n^2)$. The Taylor expansion gives coefficient of $x^2$ as $-\pi^2/6$, while the product gives $-\sum_{n=1}^\infty 1/n^2 = -\zeta(2)$. Equating: $\zeta(2)=\pi^2/6$.

q: The partial sum $\sum_{n=1}^{4} 1/n^2 = 1 + 1/4 + 1/9 + 1/16$. Enter this value as a decimal to four places.
text: The partial sum $\sum_{n=1}^{4} 1/n^2 = 1 + 1/4 + 1/9 + 1/16$. Enter this value as a decimal to four places. $1+0.25+0.1111+0.0625=1.4236$. Compare with $\pi^2/6\approx 1.6449$; the series converges slowly.

q: Euler's result: $\zeta(2)=\pi^2/6$. Compute this to four decimal places.
text: Euler's result: $\zeta(2)=\pi^2/6$. Compute this to four decimal places. $\pi^2\approx 9.8696$, so $\pi^2/6\approx 1.6449$. This is the exact sum of the reciprocals of all perfect squares.

q: Euler's proof uses the factorisation $\sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)=\prod_{n=1}^\infty(1-x^2/n^2)$. Which step in the proof is NOT rigorous without further justification?
text: Euler's proof uses the factorisation $\sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)=\prod_{n=1}^\infty(1-x^2/n^2)$. Which step in the proof is NOT rigorous without further justification? Euler treated the infinite product like a finite polynomial to compare coefficients — this exchange of limit and sum requires uniform convergence, which Euler did not verify. Weierstrass's factorisation theorem later provided the rigorous foundation. Euler assumed the sine factorization had standard convergence properties.

q: Which of the following is the correct ordering of steps in Euler's coefficient-comparison proof of $\zeta(2)=\pi^2/6$?
text: Which of the following is the correct ordering of steps in Euler's coefficient-comparison proof of $\zeta(2)=\pi^2/6$? The proof proceeds: write $\sin(\pi x)=\pi x\prod(1-x^2/n^2)$, divide by $\pi x$, note the elementary symmetric polynomial in $\{-1/n^2\}$ gives $-\zeta(2)$ as the $x^2$ coefficient, equate with $-\pi^2/6$ from the Taylor series. Expand the product first, then match the $x^2$ coefficient with the series.

q: The alternating series $\eta(2)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty(-1)^{n+1}/n^2$ satisfies $\eta(2)=(1-2^{1-2})\zeta(2)=\zeta(2)/2$. Using $\zeta(2)=\pi^2/6$, what is $\eta(2)$ to four decimal places?
text: The alternating series $\eta(2)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty(-1)^{n+1}/n^2$ satisfies $\eta(2)=(1-2^{1-2})\zeta(2)=\zeta(2)/2$. Using $\zeta(2)=\pi^2/6$, what is $\eta(2)$ to four decimal places? $\eta(2)=\pi^2/12\approx 9.8696/12\approx 0.8225$. Alternatively: $1-1/4+1/9-1/16+\cdots=\pi^2/12$. Compute $\pi^2/12$ directly.

q: The closed-form formula for even zeta values is $\zeta(2n)=(-1)^{n+1}\dfrac{B_{2n}(2\pi)^{2n}}{2(2n)!}$. Using $B_4=-1/30$, which is $\zeta(4)$?
text: The closed-form formula for even zeta values is $\zeta(2n)=(-1)^{n+1}\dfrac{B_{2n}(2\pi)^{2n}}{2(2n)!}$. Using $B_4=-1/30$, which is $\zeta(4)$? With $n=2$: $(-1)^3 \cdot (-1/30)(2\pi)^4/(2\cdot 24) = (1/30)\cdot 16\pi^4/48 = 16\pi^4/1440 = \pi^4/90$.

q: Compute $\zeta(6)=\pi^6/945$ to four decimal places.
text: Compute $\zeta(6)=\pi^6/945$ to four decimal places. $\pi^6\approx 961.39$, so $\pi^6/945\approx 1.0173$. This uses $B_6=1/42$ and $n=3$ in the formula.

q: For which values of $n$ does the closed-form $\zeta(2n)=(-1)^{n+1}B_{2n}(2\pi)^{2n}/(2(2n)!)$ apply?
text: For which values of $n$ does the closed-form $\zeta(2n)=(-1)^{n+1}B_{2n}(2\pi)^{2n}/(2(2n)!)$ apply? The formula holds for all positive integers $n\ge 1$, giving $\zeta(2),\zeta(4),\zeta(6),\ldots$ in terms of Bernoulli numbers and $\pi$. It does not extend to odd arguments, where no analogous rational-times-power-of-$\pi$ formula is known.

q: The Bernoulli numbers satisfy $B_0=1, B_1=-1/2$, and $B_{2k+1}=0$ for $k\ge 1$. Using the formula $\zeta(2n)=(-1)^{n+1}B_{2n}(2\pi)^{2n}/(2(2n)!)$, the sign of $\zeta(2n)$ depends on $(-1)^{n+1}B_{2n}$. Which pattern do the signs of $B_{2n}$ follow?
text: The Bernoulli numbers satisfy $B_0=1, B_1=-1/2$, and $B_{2k+1}=0$ for $k\ge 1$. Using the formula $\zeta(2n)=(-1)^{n+1}B_{2n}(2\pi)^{2n}/(2(2n)!)$, the sign of $\zeta(2n)$ depends on $(-1)^{n+1}B_{2n}$. Which pattern do the signs of $B_{2n}$ follow? $B_2=1/6>0$, $B_4=-1/30 0$, alternating. This alternation, combined with $(-1)^{n+1}$, ensures $\zeta(2n)>0$ for all $n\ge 1$, consistent with the series of positive terms. Use $B_2 = 1/6$ and simplify $(2\pi)^2 \cdot B_2 / (2 \cdot 2!)$.

q: From the formula with $B_6=1/42$, compute $\zeta(6)=(-1)^4\cdot(1/42)\cdot(2\pi)^6/(2\cdot 720)$. Simplify: $(2\pi)^6=64\pi^6$ and $2\cdot720=1440$. What is the denominator of $\zeta(6)$ expressed as $\pi^6/N$?
text: From the formula with $B_6=1/42$, compute $\zeta(6)=(-1)^4\cdot(1/42)\cdot(2\pi)^6/(2\cdot 720)$. Simplify: $(2\pi)^6=64\pi^6$ and $2\cdot720=1440$. What is the denominator of $\zeta(6)$ expressed as $\pi^6/N$? $\zeta(6)=(1/42)\cdot 64\pi^6/1440=64\pi^6/60480=\pi^6/945$. So $N=945$. Compute $64\pi^6 / (42 \cdot 1440)$ and simplify.

q: Which statement about $\zeta(2n)$ as $n\to\infty$ is correct?
text: Which statement about $\zeta(2n)$ as $n\to\infty$ is correct? For large real $s$, $\zeta(s)=1+2^{-s}+3^{-s}+\cdots\to 1$ because every term beyond the first vanishes exponentially. Equivalently, $|B_{2n}|\sim 2(2n)!/(2\pi)^{2n}$ (Stirling), so the formula gives $\zeta(2n)\to 1$. As $n \to \infty$, $\zeta(2n) \to 1$.

q: What did Roger Apéry prove in 1978 about $\zeta(3)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}1/n^3$?
text: What did Roger Apéry prove in 1978 about $\zeta(3)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}1/n^3$? Apéry proved irrationality using an accelerated series $\zeta(3)=5/2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(-1)^{n+1}/(n^3\binom{2n}{n})$ and showing the implied rational approximations converge too fast for $\zeta(3)$ to be rational. Transcendence remains open.

q: The Apéry series converges via $\zeta(3)=5/2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(-1)^{n+1}/\bigl(n^3\tbinom{2n}{n}\bigr)$. The first term ($n=1$) contributes $5/2 \cdot 1/(1^3\cdot 2)$. What is this?
text: The Apéry series converges via $\zeta(3)=5/2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(-1)^{n+1}/\bigl(n^3\tbinom{2n}{n}\bigr)$. The first term ($n=1$) contributes $5/2 \cdot 1/(1^3\cdot 2)$. What is this? $5/2\cdot 1/2 = 5/4 = 1.25$. Compare with $\zeta(3)\approx 1.2021$; the first term alone over-estimates, but convergence is rapid.

q: The value $\zeta(3)\approx 1.2021$ is known as Apéry's constant. Enter this value to four decimal places.
text: The value $\zeta(3)\approx 1.2021$ is known as Apéry's constant. Enter this value to four decimal places. $\zeta(3)=1+1/8+1/27+1/64+\cdots\approx 1.20206$. No closed form in terms of $\pi$ is known.

q: Apéry's irrationality proof for $\zeta(3)$ constructs integer sequences $a_n,b_n$ such that $0<|b_n\zeta(3)-a_n|\to 0$ faster than $1/b_n$. Which property of the convergents rules out $\zeta(3)$ being rational?
text: Apéry's irrationality proof for $\zeta(3)$ constructs integer sequences $a_n,b_n$ such that $0<|b_n\zeta(3)-a_n|\to 0$ faster than $1/b_n$. Which property of the convergents rules out $\zeta(3)$ being rational? If $\zeta(3)=p/q$, then $b_n\zeta(3)-a_n=(b_n p-a_n q)/q$ is a nonzero integer multiple of $1/q$ for all but finitely many $n$ (since $b_n p-a_n q\in\mathbb{Z}$ and eventually nonzero). But Apéry showed $|b_n\zeta(3)-a_n|\to 0$ faster than $q^{-1}$, a contradiction. Rapid decay faster than any rational approximation forces irrationality.

q: Which step correctly orders the logical chain of Apéry's proof?
text: Which step correctly orders the logical chain of Apéry's proof? Apéry's route: (1) explicit integer sequences satisfying a three-term recurrence, (2) verification that $b_n\in\mathbb{Z}$ (using properties of LCMs), (3) asymptotic bound showing the approximation is too good, (4) contradiction with rationality. Transcendence of $\zeta(3)$ remains unknown. Construct sequences first, bound them, then conclude irrationality.

q: The accelerated series $\zeta(3)=5/2\sum_{n=1}^\infty(-1)^{n+1}/(n^3\binom{2n}{n})$ has second term ($n=2$): $5/2\cdot(-1)^3/(8\binom{4}{2})=5/2\cdot(-1)/(8\cdot 6)$. What is this term to six decimal places?
text: The accelerated series $\zeta(3)=5/2\sum_{n=1}^\infty(-1)^{n+1}/(n^3\binom{2n}{n})$ has second term ($n=2$): $5/2\cdot(-1)^3/(8\binom{4}{2})=5/2\cdot(-1)/(8\cdot 6)$. What is this term to six decimal places? $5/2\cdot(-1)/48=-5/96\approx -0.052083$. Adding to the first term $1.25$ gives $\approx 1.197917$, already close to $\zeta(3)\approx 1.202057$. The term is $-5 / (2 \cdot 8 \cdot 6) = -5/96$.

q: Using the formula $\zeta(-n)=-B_{n+1}/(n+1)$ and $B_2=1/6$, what is $\zeta(-1)$?
text: Using the formula $\zeta(-n)=-B_{n+1}/(n+1)$ and $B_2=1/6$, what is $\zeta(-1)$? $\zeta(-1)=-B_2/2=-1/12$. This is the analytic continuation value behind the regularisation $1+2+3+\cdots \mapsto -1/12$.

q: Since $B_3=0$, what is $\zeta(-2)$ using $\zeta(-n)=-B_{n+1}/(n+1)$?
text: Since $B_3=0$, what is $\zeta(-2)$ using $\zeta(-n)=-B_{n+1}/(n+1)$? $\zeta(-2)=-B_3/3=0$. Similarly all $\zeta(-2k)=0$ for $k\ge 1$ are the trivial zeros of $\zeta$.

q: Where are the trivial zeros of the Riemann zeta function?
text: Where are the trivial zeros of the Riemann zeta function? Trivial zeros occur at $s=-2,-4,-6,\ldots$ because $B_{2k+1}=0$ for $k\ge 1$, making $\zeta(-2k)=0$. The zeros at $s=1/2+it$ are the non-trivial zeros, whose distribution is the subject of the Riemann Hypothesis.

q: Which of the following is the correct logical chain connecting analytic continuation to the value $\zeta(-1)=-1/12$?
text: Which of the following is the correct logical chain connecting analytic continuation to the value $\zeta(-1)=-1/12$? The Dirichlet series diverges for $\mathrm{Re}(s)\le 1$. The correct path is: (1) prove $\zeta(s)$ extends to a meromorphic function on $\mathbb{C}$; (2) the extension is unique by the identity theorem for analytic functions; (3) the extended function satisfies $\zeta(-n)=-B_{n+1}/(n+1)$, giving $\zeta(-1)=-1/12$. Use the functional equation to relate $\zeta(-1)$ to $\zeta(2)$ via the gamma factor.

q: Using $\zeta(-n)=-B_{n+1}/(n+1)$ and $B_4=-1/30$, compute $\zeta(-3)$ as a fraction. What is its decimal value to six places?
text: Using $\zeta(-n)=-B_{n+1}/(n+1)$ and $B_4=-1/30$, compute $\zeta(-3)$ as a fraction. What is its decimal value to six places? $\zeta(-3)=-B_4/4=-(-1/30)/4=1/120\approx 0.008333$. Note: this is positive, consistent with the formula since $B_4<0$. Compute $-(-1/30)/4 = 1/120$.

q: Which is a counterexample showing that $\zeta(-2k)=0$ for $k\ge 1$ does NOT mean $\zeta(s)$ vanishes identically near $s=-2$?
text: Which is a counterexample showing that $\zeta(-2k)=0$ for $k\ge 1$ does NOT mean $\zeta(s)$ vanishes identically near $s=-2$? Each trivial zero $s=-2k$ is a simple zero: $\zeta'(-2k)\ne 0$, as seen from the functional equation. In a punctured neighbourhood $\zeta(s)\ne 0$; the zero is isolated, not an extended vanishing. Trivial zeros are isolated; the function is not identically zero near them.

q: The completed zeta function is $\xi(s)=\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)$. The functional equation states:
text: The completed zeta function is $\xi(s)=\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)$. The functional equation states: Riemann showed $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$. This maps $s\leftrightarrow 1-s$, reflecting the critical strip about $\mathrm{Re}(s)=1/2$, and forces zeros to be symmetric about that line.

q: Which statement is an immediate consequence of the functional equation $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$?
text: Which statement is an immediate consequence of the functional equation $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$? The symmetry $s\mapsto 1-s$ directly implies that if $\rho$ is a zero, so is $1-\rho$. The Riemann Hypothesis (zeros on $\mathrm{Re}(s)=1/2$) is a stronger, unproven claim. The absence of zeros in $\mathrm{Re}(s)>1$ follows from the Euler product, not the functional equation.

q: Using $\zeta(1-s)=2(2\pi)^{-s}\cos(\pi s/2)\Gamma(s)\zeta(s)$ at $s=2$: $\cos(\pi)=-1$ and $\Gamma(2)=1$. Combined with $\zeta(2)=\pi^2/6$, what is $\zeta(-1)$?
text: Using $\zeta(1-s)=2(2\pi)^{-s}\cos(\pi s/2)\Gamma(s)\zeta(s)$ at $s=2$: $\cos(\pi)=-1$ and $\Gamma(2)=1$. Combined with $\zeta(2)=\pi^2/6$, what is $\zeta(-1)$? $\zeta(-1)=2(2\pi)^{-2}\cos(\pi)\cdot 1\cdot\pi^2/6 = 2\cdot(-1)/(4\pi^2)\cdot\pi^2/6 = -2/24 = -1/12\approx -0.0833$.

q: The functional equation $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$ involves the completed function $\xi(s)=\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)$. Which step in deriving the functional equation uses the Poisson summation formula?
text: The functional equation $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$ involves the completed function $\xi(s)=\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)$. Which step in deriving the functional equation uses the Poisson summation formula? Riemann's proof writes $\xi(s)$ as a Mellin transform of $\theta(t)-1$, then splits the integral at $t=1$ and transforms the $[0,1]$ part using $\theta(1/t)=t^{1/2}\theta(t)$. This identity for $\theta$ is exactly Poisson summation applied to the Gaussian. Mellin transform of theta gives $\xi$; splitting the integral at $1$ is crucial.

q: The functional equation forces zeros to be symmetric about $\mathrm{Re}(s)=1/2$. Combined with complex conjugate symmetry $\zeta(\bar{s})=\overline{\zeta(s)}$, if $\rho=\sigma+it$ is a non-trivial zero, which set of four zeros must all exist?
text: The functional equation forces zeros to be symmetric about $\mathrm{Re}(s)=1/2$. Combined with complex conjugate symmetry $\zeta(\bar{s})=\overline{\zeta(s)}$, if $\rho=\sigma+it$ is a non-trivial zero, which set of four zeros must all exist? The functional equation gives $1-\rho$; complex conjugation (using $\zeta(\bar{s})=\overline{\zeta(s)}$) gives $\bar{\rho}$ and $\overline{1-\rho}=1-\bar{\rho}$. So zeros come in quadruples $\{\rho,\bar\rho,1-\rho,1-\bar\rho\}$, collapsing to pairs on the critical line when $\sigma=1/2$. Zeros come in quadruples off the critical line and pairs on it.

q: Using the reflection formula $\zeta(1-s)=2(2\pi)^{-s}\cos(\pi s/2)\Gamma(s)\zeta(s)$ at $s=4$ with $\cos(2\pi)=1$, $\Gamma(4)=6$, and $\zeta(4)=\pi^4/90$, what is $\zeta(-3)$ to six decimal places?
text: Using the reflection formula $\zeta(1-s)=2(2\pi)^{-s}\cos(\pi s/2)\Gamma(s)\zeta(s)$ at $s=4$ with $\cos(2\pi)=1$, $\Gamma(4)=6$, and $\zeta(4)=\pi^4/90$, what is $\zeta(-3)$ to six decimal places? $\zeta(-3)=2(2\pi)^{-4}\cdot 1\cdot 6\cdot\pi^4/90=2\cdot 6\pi^4/(90\cdot 16\pi^4)=12/1440=1/120\approx 0.008333$. Apply the reflection formula carefully, reading off $\zeta(-3)$.

title: Analytic continuation of $L$-functions
blurb: Mellin transforms of modular forms and theta identities promote Dirichlet series from a half-plane to meromorphic functions on all of $\mathbb{C}$.

title: Special values and arithmetic
blurb: Values of $L$ at integers in the critical strip carry deep arithmetic: class numbers, regulators, and the order of vanishing at the center (BSD).

text: For good primes $p$, $a_p(E)$ is computed from point counts as: The trace is $a_p=p+1-\#E(\mathbb{F}_p)$.

The Euler product for $L(E,s)$ is built from:
text: The Euler product for $L(E,s)$ is built from: An $L$-function is assembled from local data prime-by-prime.

q: For a prime $p$ of bad reduction for $E$, the local Euler factor differs from the good-reduction factor. Which factor is used at a prime $p$ of multiplicative reduction?
text: For a prime $p$ of bad reduction for $E$, the local Euler factor differs from the good-reduction factor. Which factor is used at a prime $p$ of multiplicative reduction? At a prime of multiplicative reduction, $a_p=+1$ (split) or $a_p=-1$ (non-split), giving a single linear factor $(1-a_pp^{-s})^{-1}$. The quadratic good-reduction factor would erroneously imply smoothness there. Bad primes split into split/nonsplit multiplicative and additive types.

q: For the curve $E:y^2=x^3-x$ over $\mathbb{F}_3$, the points are $(0,0),(1,0),(2,0)$ plus the point at infinity. Compute $a_3$.
text: For the curve $E:y^2=x^3-x$ over $\mathbb{F}_3$, the points are $(0,0),(1,0),(2,0)$ plus the point at infinity. Compute $a_3$. $\#E(\mathbb{F}_3)=4$ (three affine points plus $\infty$). $a_3=3+1-4=0$. Wait — re-check: only the points satisfying $y^2=x^3-x$ mod $3$ count. $x=0$: $y^2=0$, so $(0,0)$; $x=1$: $y^2=0$, so $(1,0)$; $x=2$: $y^2=8-2=6\equiv0$, so $(2,0)$. That gives $\#E(\mathbb{F}_3)=4$, so $a_3=3+1-4=0$. Count affine points and apply $a_p = p + 1 - |E(\mathbb{F}_p)|$.

q: Which chain of implications is correct for the Euler product of $L(E,s)$?
text: Which chain of implications is correct for the Euler product of $L(E,s)$? The Hasse bound gives $|a_p|\le2p^{1/2}$, so the logarithm of each local factor is $O(p^{1/2-\sigma})$. Summing over primes gives absolute convergence of the product for $\sigma=\mathrm{Re}\,s>3/2$. Continuation to $\mathrm{Re}\,s\le3/2$ requires modularity (Wiles et al.). Multiplicativity of good Fourier coefficients forces the Euler product.

q: For a normalized eigenform $f(q)=\sum a_n q^n$, its $L$-series is:
text: For a normalized eigenform $f(q)=\sum a_n q^n$, its $L$-series is: The Mellin/Dirichlet form is $\sum a_n n^{-s}$.

q: Hecke eigenform coefficients satisfy multiplicative relations, which enable:
text: Hecke eigenform coefficients satisfy multiplicative relations, which enable: Multiplicativity is exactly what supports an Euler product.

q: If $a_1=1$ for a normalized eigenform, what is $a_1$?
text: If $a_1=1$ for a normalized eigenform, what is $a_1$? Normalization sets the first coefficient to 1.

q: For a Hecke eigenform of weight $k$, the multiplicative relation for $a_p$ at a prime $p$ (good) satisfies $a_{p^2}=a_p^2-p^{k-1}$. What does this imply about the local Euler factor at $p$?
text: For a Hecke eigenform of weight $k$, the multiplicative relation for $a_p$ at a prime $p$ (good) satisfies $a_{p^2}=a_p^2-p^{k-1}$. What does this imply about the local Euler factor at $p$? Writing $1-a_pX+p^{k-1}X^2=(1-\alpha_pX)(1-\beta_pX)$, the relation $a_{p^2}=a_p^2-p^{k-1}$ follows from $\alpha_p^2+\beta_p^2=(\alpha_p+\beta_p)^2-2\alpha_p\beta_p$. The local factor is the reciprocal quadratic $(1-\alpha_pp^{-s})^{-1}(1-\beta_pp^{-s})^{-1}$. The Hecke recursion relates $a_{p^2}$ to $a_p$ and $p^{k-1}$.

q: Which is the correct chain of steps proving $L(f,s)$ has an Euler product?
text: Which is the correct chain of steps proving $L(f,s)$ has an Euler product? The key input is that $f$ is a simultaneous eigenfunction of all Hecke operators $T_p$. This forces complete multiplicativity $a_{mn}=a_ma_n$ for coprime $m,n$ and the recursion $a_{p^{r+1}}=a_pa_{p^r}-p^{k-1}a_{p^{r-1}}$, which together allow the Dirichlet series to be written as $\prod_p(\text{local factor})^{-1}$. Use Hecke recursion for $a_{p^r}$; match the two-variable generating function.

q: Let $f$ be a normalized eigenform of weight $12$ for $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ (the Ramanujan $\Delta$ function). If $a_2=\tau(2)=-24$, use the Hecke relation $a_{p^2}=a_p^2-p^{k-1}$ with $p=2, k=12$ to compute $\tau(4)$.
text: Let $f$ be a normalized eigenform of weight $12$ for $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ (the Ramanujan $\Delta$ function). If $a_2=\tau(2)=-24$, use the Hecke relation $a_{p^2}=a_p^2-p^{k-1}$ with $p=2, k=12$ to compute $\tau(4)$. $\tau(4)=a_2^2-2^{11}=(-24)^2-2048=576-2048=-1472$. Tau of $4$ comes from the Hecke recursion at $p=2$.

text: For an elliptic curve over $\mathbb{Q}$, the completed function $\Lambda(E,s)$ satisfies: In the elliptic-curve normalization, the involution is $s\leftrightarrow 2-s$, centered at $s=1$.

q: The central point of $s\leftrightarrow 2-s$ symmetry is $s=$ ?
text: The central point of $s\leftrightarrow 2-s$ symmetry is $s=$ ? $s=1$ is fixed by $s\mapsto 2-s$, so it is the center of symmetry.

q: The gamma factor $\Gamma_\mathbb{R}(s)=\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)$ appears in $\Lambda(E,s)$. What is the role of this archimedean factor?
text: The gamma factor $\Gamma_\mathbb{R}(s)=\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)$ appears in $\Lambda(E,s)$. What is the role of this archimedean factor? The completed $L$-function $\Lambda(E,s)$ includes local factors at every place of $\mathbb{Q}$, including the archimedean place. The gamma factor $\Gamma_\mathbb{R}(s)^2$ (for weight-$2$ forms) is precisely the archimedean Euler factor, completing the picture and enabling the global functional equation $\Lambda(E,s)=w\,N^{1-s}\Lambda(E,2-s)$. The gamma factor is the archimedean Euler factor.

q: Which ordering of steps correctly constructs the completed $L$-function $\Lambda(E,s)$ and verifies its functional equation?
text: Which ordering of steps correctly constructs the completed $L$-function $\Lambda(E,s)$ and verifies its functional equation? The standard proof: multiply $L(E,s)$ by the gamma factor to form $\Lambda$, recognize it as a Mellin transform of the associated weight-$2$ newform $f_E$, split $\int_0^\infty=\int_0^{1/\sqrt{N}}+\int_{1/\sqrt{N}}^\infty$, and apply the modular symmetry $f_E(-1/Niz)=wN z^2 f_E(iz)$ to the first piece to get the functional equation. Analytic continuation first, then gamma factor, then functional equation.

q: Suppose $w=-1$ for an elliptic curve $E$. At the center $s=1$, the functional equation forces $\Lambda(E,1)=w\Lambda(E,1)=-\Lambda(E,1)$. What value must $\Lambda(E,1)$ take?
text: Suppose $w=-1$ for an elliptic curve $E$. At the center $s=1$, the functional equation forces $\Lambda(E,1)=w\Lambda(E,1)=-\Lambda(E,1)$. What value must $\Lambda(E,1)$ take? $\Lambda(E,1)=-\Lambda(E,1)$ implies $2\Lambda(E,1)=0$, so $\Lambda(E,1)=0$. Root number $w=-1$ forces an odd-order zero at the center, predicting an odd Mordell–Weil rank by BSD. When $w = -1$, forced vanishing gives odd-order behavior at the center.

q: The Dirichlet series $L(E,s)=\sum a_n n^{-s}$ for an elliptic curve converges absolutely on:
text: The Dirichlet series $L(E,s)=\sum a_n n^{-s}$ for an elliptic curve converges absolutely on: The Hasse bound $|a_p|\le 2\sqrt{p}$ gives absolute convergence on $\mathrm{Re}\,s > 3/2$; continuation past that needs modularity.

q: What analytic input is the standard route to continuing $L(f,s)$ of a cusp form $f$ to all of $\mathbb{C}$?
