text: For the distinguished open $D(f)=\{\mathfrak p : f\notin \mathfrak p\}\subset \operatorname{Spec}(R)$, the ring of sections is: $\mathcal O(D(f))=R_f$: inverting $f$ allows $1/f$ as a regular function exactly on the locus where $f$ does not vanish.

q: The residue field $\kappa(\mathfrak p)$ at the generic point $\mathfrak p=(0)$ of $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$ is $\mathbb Q$. What is its characteristic?
text: The residue field $\kappa(\mathfrak p)$ at the generic point $\mathfrak p=(0)$ of $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$ is $\mathbb Q$. What is its characteristic? $\mathbb Q$ has characteristic $0$. By contrast $\kappa((p))=\mathbb F_p$ at a closed point has characteristic $p$ — different points can have residue fields of different characteristics.

q: For $R=k[x]/(x^2)$ (the dual numbers), $\operatorname{Spec}(R)$ has a single point $\mathfrak m=(x)$. The stalk $\mathcal{O}_{\mathfrak m}$ equals $R_{\mathfrak m}$. Which statement about sections on the unique non-empty open set is correct?
text: For $R=k[x]/(x^2)$ (the dual numbers), $\operatorname{Spec}(R)$ has a single point $\mathfrak m=(x)$. The stalk $\mathcal{O}_{\mathfrak m}$ equals $R_{\mathfrak m}$. Which statement about sections on the unique non-empty open set is correct? Global sections of $\mathcal{O}$ on $\operatorname{Spec}(R)$ equal $R$ itself. The nilpotent $x$ is a legitimate global section; scheme theory allows non-reduced rings and their nilpotents encode infinitesimal thickening.

q: Let $f:\operatorname{Spec}(B)\to\operatorname{Spec}(A)$ be the morphism induced by a ring map $\phi:A\to B$. The image of a prime $\mathfrak q\subset B$ under $f$ is:
text: Let $f:\operatorname{Spec}(B)\to\operatorname{Spec}(A)$ be the morphism induced by a ring map $\phi:A\to B$. The image of a prime $\mathfrak q\subset B$ under $f$ is: The continuous map on spectra sends $\mathfrak q$ to $f(\mathfrak q)=\phi^{-1}(\mathfrak q)=\{a\in A:\phi(a)\in\mathfrak q\}$, which is prime in $A$ because $\phi$ is a ring homomorphism.

q: Let $R=\mathbb{Z}[i]=\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)$. The prime $(3)\subset\mathbb{Z}$ splits in $R$ as $(3)=(3,i-?)(3,i+?)$ because $x^2+1=(x-1)(x+1)\pmod{3}$ is incorrect; in fact $x^2+1$ is irreducible mod $3$. How many primes of $R$ lie over $(3)$?
text: Let $R=\mathbb{Z}[i]=\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)$. The prime $(3)\subset\mathbb{Z}$ splits in $R$ as $(3)=(3,i-?)(3,i+?)$ because $x^2+1=(x-1)(x+1)\pmod{3}$ is incorrect; in fact $x^2+1$ is irreducible mod $3$. How many primes of $R$ lie over $(3)$? $x^2+1\pmod{3}$: checking $x=0,1,2$ gives $1,2,2$ — no root, so $x^2+1$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{F}_3$. Thus $(3)$ remains prime in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ (it is inert), and there is exactly $1$ prime of $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ above $3$.

q: The projective line $\mathbb P^1_k$ is built by gluing two copies of $\mathbb A^1_k=\operatorname{Spec}k[x]$ and $\operatorname{Spec}k[y]$ along the overlap $\{x\ne 0\}\cong\{y\ne 0\}$ via:
text: The projective line $\mathbb P^1_k$ is built by gluing two copies of $\mathbb A^1_k=\operatorname{Spec}k[x]$ and $\operatorname{Spec}k[y]$ along the overlap $\{x\ne 0\}\cong\{y\ne 0\}$ via: The transition is $y=1/x$: on the overlap $\operatorname{Spec}k[x,x^{-1}]=\operatorname{Spec}k[y,y^{-1}]$ with $y$ inverse to $x$. The two origins become $0$ and $\infty$.

q: How many closed points does $\mathbb P^1_{\mathbb F_q}$ have over a finite field $\mathbb F_q$? Give the answer for $q=5$.
text: How many closed points does $\mathbb P^1_{\mathbb F_q}$ have over a finite field $\mathbb F_q$? Give the answer for $q=5$. $|\mathbb P^1(\mathbb F_q)|=q+1$. For $q=5$ that is $5+1=6$ closed points: the $5$ values of $x\in\mathbb F_5$ plus the point at infinity.

q: Which statement about $\mathbb P^1_k$ is TRUE?
text: Which statement about $\mathbb P^1_k$ is TRUE? $\mathbb P^1_k$ is the classic non-affine scheme. Global sections of $\mathcal O$ are just $k$ (too few for an affine of positive dimension), yet it is covered by two affine charts $\mathbb A^1_k$.

q: To glue two affine schemes $X_1=\operatorname{Spec}(A_1)$ and $X_2=\operatorname{Spec}(A_2)$ along open subsets $U_{12}\subset X_1$ and $U_{21}\subset X_2$ via an isomorphism $\varphi:U_{12}\xrightarrow{\sim}U_{21}$, which cocycle condition must hold when gluing three affines $X_1,X_2,X_3$?
text: To glue two affine schemes $X_1=\operatorname{Spec}(A_1)$ and $X_2=\operatorname{Spec}(A_2)$ along open subsets $U_{12}\subset X_1$ and $U_{21}\subset X_2$ via an isomorphism $\varphi:U_{12}\xrightarrow{\sim}U_{21}$, which cocycle condition must hold when gluing three affines $X_1,X_2,X_3$? The cocycle condition $\varphi_{ki}=\varphi_{kj}\circ\varphi_{ji}$ on triple overlaps ensures the local isomorphisms are consistent; without it the resulting space may fail to be a scheme (or may not even be well-defined as a ringed space).

q: The projective space $\mathbb{P}^n_k$ is covered by $n+1$ standard affine charts $D_+(x_i)\cong\mathbb{A}^n_k$. For $n=3$, how many affine charts are in the standard cover?
text: The projective space $\mathbb{P}^n_k$ is covered by $n+1$ standard affine charts $D_+(x_i)\cong\mathbb{A}^n_k$. For $n=3$, how many affine charts are in the standard cover? $\mathbb{P}^3_k$ is covered by $D_+(x_0),D_+(x_1),D_+(x_2),D_+(x_3)$, giving $n+1=4$ affine charts.

q: Which is a correct counterexample showing that a scheme obtained by gluing affines need not be separated?
text: Which is a correct counterexample showing that a scheme obtained by gluing affines need not be separated? The line with doubled origin is the standard non-separated scheme: two copies of $\mathbb{A}^1_k$ glued on the complement of the origin. The two origins cannot be separated by open sets, and the diagonal is not closed in the product, violating separatedness. Think of a classic Hausdorff-failure analogue in point-set topology.

q: Closed subschemes of $\operatorname{Spec} R$ correspond bijectively to:
text: Closed subschemes of $\operatorname{Spec} R$ correspond bijectively to: Each ideal $I\subseteq R$ gives a closed subscheme $\operatorname{Spec}(R/I)\hookrightarrow\operatorname{Spec}R$, and distinct ideals give distinct subschemes (even when their radicals — hence underlying sets — agree). Radical ideals pick out only the reduced closed subschemes. Different ideals with the same $\sqrt{I}$ can still define different subschemes.

q: The ring $R=k[x,y]/(xy)$ has how many irreducible components when viewed as $\operatorname{Spec}R$?
text: The ring $R=k[x,y]/(xy)$ has how many irreducible components when viewed as $\operatorname{Spec}R$? The minimal primes of $k[x,y]/(xy)$ are $(x)$ and $(y)$, corresponding to the two coordinate axes. So $\operatorname{Spec}R$ is the union of two irreducible components meeting at the origin.

q: The ideal $I=(x^2,xy,y^2)\subset k[x,y]$ has radical $\sqrt I = (x,y)$. As a $k$-vector space the quotient $k[x,y]/I$ has what dimension (the length of the fat point)?
text: The ideal $I=(x^2,xy,y^2)\subset k[x,y]$ has radical $\sqrt I = (x,y)$. As a $k$-vector space the quotient $k[x,y]/I$ has what dimension (the length of the fat point)? A $k$-basis of $k[x,y]/(x^2,xy,y^2)$ is $\{1,x,y\}$, so the quotient has $k$-dimension $3$. The reduced point has length $1$, so this is a non-reduced length-$3$ scheme concentrated at the origin.

q: Let $R = k[x,y]/(x^2-y^3)$ (cuspidal cubic). Which statement is TRUE?
text: Let $R = k[x,y]/(x^2-y^3)$ (cuspidal cubic). Which statement is TRUE? $x^2-y^3$ is irreducible in $k[x,y]$ (it is a prime, since $k[x,y]$ is a UFD and the polynomial is irreducible), so $R=k[x,y]/(x^2-y^3)$ is an integral domain — reduced, and $\operatorname{Spec}R$ is irreducible with generic point $(0)$. The cusp at the origin is a singular point, but singular $\ne$ non-reduced.

q: The reduced subscheme underlying the closed subscheme $V(I)\subset\operatorname{Spec}R$ is:
text: The reduced subscheme underlying the closed subscheme $V(I)\subset\operatorname{Spec}R$ is: Passing to the radical $\sqrt I$ kills all nilpotents in the quotient. The reduced subscheme with the same underlying set $V(I)$ is $\operatorname{Spec}(R/\sqrt I)$; the inclusion $\sqrt I\supseteq I$ induces a natural closed immersion $\operatorname{Spec}(R/\sqrt I)\hookrightarrow\operatorname{Spec}(R/I)$.

q: How many minimal primes does $R=\mathbb Z[x]/(6x) = \mathbb Z[x]/(2\cdot 3\cdot x)$ have? (Equivalently, how many irreducible components does $\operatorname{Spec}R$ have?)
text: How many minimal primes does $R=\mathbb Z[x]/(6x) = \mathbb Z[x]/(2\cdot 3\cdot x)$ have? (Equivalently, how many irreducible components does $\operatorname{Spec}R$ have?) In $\mathbb Z[x]$, the prime $(6x)=(2)\cap(3)\cap(x)$ factors as a meet of three minimal primes over $(6x)$: $(2)$, $(3)$, and $(x)$. In $\mathbb Z[x]/(6x)$ these become the three minimal primes, so $\operatorname{Spec}R$ has $3$ irreducible components.

q: What is the Krull dimension of $\operatorname{Spec} k[x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4]$?
text: What is the Krull dimension of $\operatorname{Spec} k[x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4]$? $\dim k[x_1,\dots,x_n]=n$, realised by the chain $(0)\subsetneq(x_1)\subsetneq(x_1,x_2)\subsetneq(x_1,x_2,x_3)\subsetneq(x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4)$. For $n=4$ this gives dimension $4$.

q: What is $\dim\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z$?
text: What is $\dim\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z$? The longest chain of primes in $\mathbb Z$ is $(0)\subsetneq(p)$ for any prime $p$ — length $1$. So $\dim\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z=1$; geometrically it is a 'curve' indexed by primes plus a generic point.

q: For a finite-type scheme $X$ over a field $k$, the dimension of $X$ at a closed point $x\in X$ is:
text: For a finite-type scheme $X$ over a field $k$, the dimension of $X$ at a closed point $x\in X$ is: Dimension is a local invariant: $\dim_x X = \dim \mathcal O_{X,x}$, the Krull dimension of the local ring at $x$. The global $\dim X$ is the supremum over closed points (on a finite-type scheme over a field).

q: What is $\dim\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[x_1,x_2]$?
text: What is $\dim\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[x_1,x_2]$? For a Noetherian ring $A$, $\dim A[x]=\dim A + 1$. Starting from $\dim\mathbb Z = 1$: $\dim\mathbb Z[x_1]=2$, $\dim\mathbb Z[x_1,x_2]=3$. A maximal chain is $(0)\subsetneq(p)\subsetneq(p,x_1)\subsetneq(p,x_1,x_2)$ for any prime $p$.

q: Let $X=\operatorname{Spec}(k[x,y]/(xy))$ be the union of two coordinate axes. Which statement is TRUE?
text: Let $X=\operatorname{Spec}(k[x,y]/(xy))$ be the union of two coordinate axes. Which statement is TRUE? The two irreducible components are the axes $V(x)$ and $V(y)$, each a copy of $\mathbb A^1_k$ of dimension $1$. So $\dim X=\max=1$, and since both components have the same dimension, $X$ is equidimensional.

q: For the projective space $\mathbb P^n_k$, which assertion is correct?
text: For the projective space $\mathbb P^n_k$, which assertion is correct? Dimension is a local notion. $\mathbb P^n_k$ is covered by $n+1$ open affine charts each isomorphic to $\mathbb A^n_k$, so $\dim\mathbb P^n_k=n$. The fact that global sections are just $k$ is irrelevant to dimension (and is what distinguishes projective from affine).

q: In $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[x]$, the closure of the prime $(x)$ equals:
text: In $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[x]$, the closure of the prime $(x)$ equals: The closure of a prime $\mathfrak p$ is $V(\mathfrak p)=\{\mathfrak q\supseteq\mathfrak p\}$. For $\mathfrak p=(x)$, these are all primes containing $x$, which are exactly $(x)$ itself together with all $(x,p)$ for $p$ prime — the entire 'vertical axis' of $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[x]$.

q: A generic point of an irreducible closed subset $Z\subseteq\operatorname{Spec}R$ is:
text: A generic point of an irreducible closed subset $Z\subseteq\operatorname{Spec}R$ is: Every irreducible closed subset $Z$ of a scheme has a unique generic point $\eta$ with $\overline{\{\eta\}}=Z$. For $Z=V(\mathfrak p)$ with $\mathfrak p$ prime minimal with that property, the generic point is exactly $\mathfrak p$ itself.

q: How many generic points does the scheme $\operatorname{Spec}(k[x,y]/(xy))$ have? (Count minimal primes.)
text: How many generic points does the scheme $\operatorname{Spec}(k[x,y]/(xy))$ have? (Count minimal primes.) The minimal primes of $k[x,y]/(xy)$ are $(x)$ and $(y)$, one per irreducible component (the two coordinate axes). Each is the generic point of its component, so the scheme has $2$ generic points.

q: Let $\mathfrak p\leadsto\mathfrak q$ denote specialization (i.e. $\mathfrak p\subseteq\mathfrak q$). Which statement about specialization in $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[x]$ is correct?
text: Let $\mathfrak p\leadsto\mathfrak q$ denote specialization (i.e. $\mathfrak p\subseteq\mathfrak q$). Which statement about specialization in $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[x]$ is correct? Specialization flows from smaller (more generic) to larger (more special): $(0)\subsetneq(p)\subsetneq(p,x-a)$ gives a chain of specializations from the generic point of the whole spectrum, through the height-$1$ prime $(p)$, down to a closed point. Specialization is antisymmetric, not symmetric; closed points are terminal.

q: In $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[x]$, what is the maximum length of a specialization chain $\mathfrak p_0\subsetneq\mathfrak p_1\subsetneq\cdots\subsetneq\mathfrak p_n$? (Give $n$, the number of strict inclusions.)
text: In $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[x]$, what is the maximum length of a specialization chain $\mathfrak p_0\subsetneq\mathfrak p_1\subsetneq\cdots\subsetneq\mathfrak p_n$? (Give $n$, the number of strict inclusions.) $\dim\mathbb Z[x]=2$, achieved by e.g. $(0)\subsetneq(p)\subsetneq(p,x-a)$: three primes, two strict inclusions. So the longest specialization chain has length $n=2$.

q: Which claim about generic points and open dense subsets is TRUE in a Noetherian irreducible scheme $X$ with generic point $\eta$?
text: Which claim about generic points and open dense subsets is TRUE in a Noetherian irreducible scheme $X$ with generic point $\eta$? Since $\overline{\{\eta\}}=X$, a closed set $Z\subsetneq X$ cannot contain $\eta$. Equivalently, an open $U$ is dense (its complement is a proper closed set) iff $\eta\in U$. This is why 'generic' properties — those that hold on a dense open — are exactly the ones that hold at $\eta$.

q: The residue field $\kappa(\mathfrak p)$ at a prime $\mathfrak p\subset R$ is defined as:
text: The residue field $\kappa(\mathfrak p)$ at a prime $\mathfrak p\subset R$ is defined as: $\kappa(\mathfrak p)=\operatorname{Frac}(R/\mathfrak p)$, or equivalently the quotient of the local ring $R_{\mathfrak p}$ by its maximal ideal. For a maximal $\mathfrak p$, $R/\mathfrak p$ is already a field, but at non-maximal primes you need the fraction field.

q: At the generic point $(0)\in\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[x]$, the residue field is:
text: At the generic point $(0)\in\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[x]$, the residue field is: $\kappa((0))=\operatorname{Frac}(\mathbb Z[x]/(0))=\operatorname{Frac}(\mathbb Z[x])=\mathbb Q(x)$, the field of rational functions in one variable over $\mathbb Q$.

q: At the maximal ideal $\mathfrak m=(2,x^2+x+1)\subset\mathbb Z[x]$, the residue field is $\mathbb Z[x]/(2,x^2+x+1)\cong\mathbb F_2[x]/(x^2+x+1)$. How many elements does $\kappa(\mathfrak m)$ have?
text: At the maximal ideal $\mathfrak m=(2,x^2+x+1)\subset\mathbb Z[x]$, the residue field is $\mathbb Z[x]/(2,x^2+x+1)\cong\mathbb F_2[x]/(x^2+x+1)$. How many elements does $\kappa(\mathfrak m)$ have? $x^2+x+1$ has no root in $\mathbb F_2$ (check $x=0,1$ both give $1$), so it is irreducible. Hence $\kappa(\mathfrak m)=\mathbb F_2[x]/(x^2+x+1)=\mathbb F_4$, a field of $4$ elements.

q: Consider $f=x^2+1\in\mathbb Z[x]$, regarded as a 'function' on $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[x]$. At which of the following points does $f$ vanish, in the sense $f\in\mathfrak p$?
text:
Consider $f=x^2+1\in\mathbb Z[x]$, regarded as a 'function' on $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[x]$. At which of the following points does $f$ vanish, in the sense $f\in\mathfrak p$? '$f$ vanishes at $\mathfrak p$' means $f\in\mathfrak p$, equivalently $f\equiv 0$ in $\kappa(\mathfrak p)$. At $\mathfrak m=(5,x-2)$: $\kappa(\mathfrak m)=\mathbb F_5$ and $f\mapsto 2^2+1=5\equiv 0$, so yes. At $(3,x-1)$: $f\mapsto 2
ot\equiv 0$. At $(0)$: $\kappa((0))=\mathbb Q(x)$ and $f=x^2+1
e 0$, so $f
otin(0)$.

q: A $k$-point of a $k$-scheme $X$ — a morphism $\operatorname{Spec}k\to X$ — corresponds to:
text: A $k$-point of a $k$-scheme $X$ — a morphism $\operatorname{Spec}k\to X$ — corresponds to: A morphism $\operatorname{Spec}k\to X$ picks out a point $x\in X$ and, since its image lies in the residue field, a $k$-algebra map $\kappa(x)\to k$, i.e. an embedding $\kappa(x)\hookrightarrow k$. When $k$ is algebraically closed and $\kappa(x)=k$ this recovers 'classical points', but in general the extra choice of embedding matters (Galois conjugate points).

q: In $\mathbb Z[x]$, consider the maximal ideal $\mathfrak m=(p)$ of the height-$1$ prime $(p)\subset\mathbb Z[x]$ — i.e. the prime $(p)$ itself, height $1$, not a closed point. The residue field $\kappa((p))=\mathbb F_p(x)$. What is its transcendence degree over $\mathbb F_p$?
text: In $\mathbb Z[x]$, consider the maximal ideal $\mathfrak m=(p)$ of the height-$1$ prime $(p)\subset\mathbb Z[x]$ — i.e. the prime $(p)$ itself, height $1$, not a closed point. The residue field $\kappa((p))=\mathbb F_p(x)$. What is its transcendence degree over $\mathbb F_p$? $\kappa((p))=\operatorname{Frac}(\mathbb Z[x]/(p))=\operatorname{Frac}(\mathbb F_p[x])=\mathbb F_p(x)$. The transcendence degree of $\mathbb F_p(x)$ over $\mathbb F_p$ is $1$ — one transcendental generator $x$. (This matches the intuition that $(p)$ is a 'curve' in $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[x]$.)

q: A locally ringed space is a pair $(X,\mathcal O_X)$ where $\mathcal O_X$ is a sheaf of rings such that:
text: A locally ringed space is a pair $(X,\mathcal O_X)$ where $\mathcal O_X$ is a sheaf of rings such that: By definition, 'locally ringed' means every stalk is a local ring (a commutative ring with a unique maximal ideal). Stalks being fields is the much stronger condition that defines a 'sheaf of fields', not the right notion for schemes.

q: A morphism of locally ringed spaces $(f, f^\sharp)\colon(X,\mathcal O_X)\to(Y,\mathcal O_Y)$ must satisfy, in addition to being a morphism of ringed spaces, that for each $x\in X$ the induced stalk map:
text: A morphism of locally ringed spaces $(f, f^\sharp)\colon(X,\mathcal O_X)\to(Y,\mathcal O_Y)$ must satisfy, in addition to being a morphism of ringed spaces, that for each $x\in X$ the induced stalk map: The extra condition is that each induced stalk map is a local ring homomorphism: $f^\sharp_x(\mathfrak m_{f(x)})\subseteq\mathfrak m_x$. Without this, maps between $\operatorname{Spec}$s would not correspond to ring homomorphisms in the right direction.

q: A scheme is a locally ringed space that is:
text: A scheme is a locally ringed space that is: A scheme $X$ is a locally ringed space such that every point has an open neighborhood $U$ with $(U,\mathcal O_X|_U)\cong(\operatorname{Spec}R,\mathcal O_{\operatorname{Spec}R})$ for some ring $R$ (depending on $U$). Globally isomorphic to a single $\operatorname{Spec}R$ is the stronger 'affine' condition.

q: Consider the ring map $\iota\colon k\hookrightarrow k[x]_{(x)}$ (inclusion of $k$ into the localization at $(x)$). The induced map on spectra sends the unique point of $\operatorname{Spec}k[x]_{(x)}\cong\{(0),(x)\}$ to a point of $\operatorname{Spec}k=\{*\}$. Which stalk map fails to be local if we mistakenly use a non-local ring homomorphism here?
text: Consider the ring map $\iota\colon k\hookrightarrow k[x]_{(x)}$ (inclusion of $k$ into the localization at $(x)$). The induced map on spectra sends the unique point of $\operatorname{Spec}k[x]_{(x)}\cong\{(0),(x)\}$ to a point of $\operatorname{Spec}k=\{*\}$. Which stalk map fails to be local if we mistakenly use a non-local ring homomorphism here? For a ring map $\phi\colon A\to B$ with $A$ local (maximal ideal $\mathfrak m_A$) and $B$ local (maximal ideal $\mathfrak m_B$), $\phi$ is local iff $\phi(\mathfrak m_A)\subseteq\mathfrak m_B$. When $A=k$ is a field, $\mathfrak m_A=(0)$ and $\phi(0)=0\in\mathfrak m_B$ automatically — locality is free. The subtlety only appears when $A$ has a non-zero maximal ideal that could map to a unit.

q: Let $X$ be the topological space $\{\eta,s\}$ with $\{s\}$ closed (so $\eta$ is generic). Equip it with a sheaf $\mathcal O$ such that $\mathcal O(X)=R$, $\mathcal O(\{\eta\})=K$ where $R$ is a DVR with fraction field $K$, and restriction is the localization. Is $(X,\mathcal O)$ a scheme?
text: Let $X$ be the topological space $\{\eta,s\}$ with $\{s\}$ closed (so $\eta$ is generic). Equip it with a sheaf $\mathcal O$ such that $\mathcal O(X)=R$, $\mathcal O(\{\eta\})=K$ where $R$ is a DVR with fraction field $K$, and restriction is the localization. Is $(X,\mathcal O)$ a scheme? A DVR $R$ has exactly two primes: $(0)$ and the unique maximal $\mathfrak m$. So $\operatorname{Spec}R=\{(0),\mathfrak m\}$ with $\{\mathfrak m\}$ closed and $\{(0)\}$ generic, and $\mathcal O(X)=R$, $\mathcal O(\{(0)\})=\operatorname{Frac}R=K$. The described $(X,\mathcal O)$ matches $\operatorname{Spec}R$ exactly, and the stalks are $R_{\mathfrak m}=R$ and $R_{(0)}=K$, both local.

q: Which of the following is an example of a ringed space that is NOT a locally ringed space?
text: Which of the following is an example of a ringed space that is NOT a locally ringed space? $\mathbb Z\times\mathbb Z$ is a product ring with two maximal ideals $\mathbb Z\times 0$ and $0\times\mathbb Z$ — it is not local. So the ringed space $(\{*\},\mathbb Z\times\mathbb Z)$ fails to be locally ringed. All the other examples are schemes, hence locally ringed.

q: For a graded ring $S=\bigoplus_{d\ge 0}S_d$, the underlying set of $\operatorname{Proj}S$ consists of:
text: For a graded ring $S=\bigoplus_{d\ge 0}S_d$, the underlying set of $\operatorname{Proj}S$ consists of: $\operatorname{Proj}S=\{\mathfrak p\subset S\text{ homogeneous prime}:\mathfrak p\not\supseteq S_+\}$. Excluding those containing $S_+$ kills the 'irrelevant' locus that would otherwise give a cone point.

q: For $S=k[x_0,x_1,\dots,x_n]$, the basic open $D_+(x_i)\subset\operatorname{Proj}S$ is canonically isomorphic to:
text: For $S=k[x_0,x_1,\dots,x_n]$, the basic open $D_+(x_i)\subset\operatorname{Proj}S$ is canonically isomorphic to: On $D_+(x_i)$ we may 'dehomogenize' by setting $x_i=1$. Formally $D_+(x_i)=\operatorname{Spec}((S_{x_i})_0)$ and the degree-$0$ part of $S_{x_i}$ is generated by $x_j/x_i$ for $j\ne i$, giving $\mathbb A^n_k$.

q: How many standard affine charts cover $\mathbb P^5_k=\operatorname{Proj}k[x_0,\dots,x_5]$?
text: How many standard affine charts cover $\mathbb P^5_k=\operatorname{Proj}k[x_0,\dots,x_5]$? $\mathbb P^n_k$ is covered by $n+1$ charts $D_+(x_i)$ for $i=0,\dots,n$. For $n=5$, that is $6$ charts.

q: Consider the twisted cubic $C=\operatorname{Proj}k[s^3,s^2t,st^2,t^3]\subset\mathbb P^3_k$, the image of $\mathbb P^1_k\hookrightarrow\mathbb P^3_k$ via $[s:t]\mapsto[s^3:s^2t:st^2:t^3]$. Which description is correct?
text: Consider the twisted cubic $C=\operatorname{Proj}k[s^3,s^2t,st^2,t^3]\subset\mathbb P^3_k$, the image of $\mathbb P^1_k\hookrightarrow\mathbb P^3_k$ via $[s:t]\mapsto[s^3:s^2t:st^2:t^3]$. Which description is correct? The graded ring $k[s^3,s^2t,st^2,t^3]\subset k[s,t]$ is the Veronese subalgebra. Its Proj is the image of $\mathbb P^1_k\to\mathbb P^3_k$, which is an embedded copy of $\mathbb P^1_k$ — the twisted cubic. So $\dim C=1$.

q: Let $S=k[x_0,x_1,x_2]$ and $I=(x_0^2+x_1^2-x_2^2)$. Then $\operatorname{Proj}(S/I)$ is:
text: Let $S=k[x_0,x_1,x_2]$ and $I=(x_0^2+x_1^2-x_2^2)$. Then $\operatorname{Proj}(S/I)$ is: $x_0^2+x_1^2-x_2^2$ is a non-degenerate homogeneous quadratic form; over algebraically closed $k$ with $\operatorname{char}k\ne 2$ it defines a smooth conic in $\mathbb P^2_k$. All smooth plane conics are isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_k$ (e.g. via stereographic projection from any rational point).

q: The global sections $H^0(\mathbb P^n_k,\mathcal O(d))$ for $d\ge 0$ is the degree-$d$ part of $k[x_0,\dots,x_n]$. What is $\dim_k H^0(\mathbb P^2_k,\mathcal O(3))$? (Number of degree-$3$ monomials in $3$ variables.)
text: The global sections $H^0(\mathbb P^n_k,\mathcal O(d))$ for $d\ge 0$ is the degree-$d$ part of $k[x_0,\dots,x_n]$. What is $\dim_k H^0(\mathbb P^2_k,\mathcal O(3))$? (Number of degree-$3$ monomials in $3$ variables.) The number of degree-$d$ monomials in $n+1$ variables is $\binom{n+d}{d}$. For $n=2$, $d=3$: $\binom{5}{3}=10$. The ten monomials are $x_0^3,x_0^2x_1,x_0^2x_2,x_0x_1^2,x_0x_1x_2,x_0x_2^2,x_1^3,x_1^2x_2,x_1x_2^2,x_2^3$.

q: For a morphism $f\colon X\to Y$ and $y\in Y$, the scheme-theoretic fiber $X_y$ is defined as:
text: For a morphism $f\colon X\to Y$ and $y\in Y$, the scheme-theoretic fiber $X_y$ is defined as: The fiber over $y$ is $X_y = X\times_Y \operatorname{Spec}\kappa(y)$, the base change of $X$ along the inclusion of the residue field at $y$. Its underlying set is $f^{-1}(y)$, but the fiber-product definition records scheme structure, multiplicities, and residue-field extensions.

q: Consider the structural morphism $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[x]\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z$. The fiber over the closed point $(5)\in\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z$ is:
text: Consider the structural morphism $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[x]\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z$. The fiber over the closed point $(5)\in\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z$ is: The fiber over $(p)$ is $\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb Z[x]\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb F_p)=\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb F_p[x]=\mathbb A^1_{\mathbb F_p}$. For $p=5$ this is the affine line over $\mathbb F_5$. Tensor $\mathbb Z[x]$ with the residue field $\kappa((5))=\mathbb F_5$.

q: The generic fiber of a morphism $X\to Y$ (with $Y$ integral) is the fiber over:
text: The generic fiber of a morphism $X\to Y$ (with $Y$ integral) is the fiber over: The generic fiber is $X_\eta = X\times_Y \operatorname{Spec}K(Y)$, the fiber over the generic point of $Y$. It carries all properties that hold on a dense open subset of $Y$; 'special fibers' are the fibers over closed points.

q: Consider $f\colon\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[i]\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z$. How many closed points does the fiber over $(5)\in\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z$ have?
text: Consider $f\colon\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[i]\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z$. How many closed points does the fiber over $(5)\in\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z$ have? Fiber over $(5)$ is $\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb Z[i]\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb F_5)=\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb F_5[x]/(x^2+1)$. Over $\mathbb F_5$, $x^2+1=(x-2)(x+2)$ (since $2^2=4\equiv-1$), so the fiber is $\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb F_5\times\mathbb F_5)$: two closed points. ($5$ splits in $\mathbb Z[i]$.)

q: For the family $X=\operatorname{Spec}k[t,x]/(x^2-t)\to Y=\operatorname{Spec}k[t]$ (with $k$ algebraically closed of characteristic $\ne 2$), which statement correctly compares the fibers?
text: For the family $X=\operatorname{Spec}k[t,x]/(x^2-t)\to Y=\operatorname{Spec}k[t]$ (with $k$ algebraically closed of characteristic $\ne 2$), which statement correctly compares the fibers? Over $t=a\ne 0$: $k[x]/(x^2-a)\cong k\times k$, giving two reduced closed points. Over $t=0$: $k[x]/(x^2)$, a single non-reduced length-$2$ fat point. This is the standard example of a family with a degenerate special fiber — the double cover of $\mathbb A^1$ ramified at $0$. What happens to the two roots $\pm\sqrt{t}$ as $t\to 0$?

q: Let $f\colon\mathbb P^1_{\mathbb Z}\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z$. Which statement about the fibers is TRUE?
text: Let $f\colon\mathbb P^1_{\mathbb Z}\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z$. Which statement about the fibers is TRUE? Base change commutes with Proj: $\mathbb P^1_{\mathbb Z}\times_{\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z}\operatorname{Spec}\kappa((p))=\mathbb P^1_{\mathbb F_p}$, and similarly the generic fiber is $\mathbb P^1_{\mathbb Q}$. The fibers are genuine $\mathbb P^1$'s over each residue field — the 'arithmetic surface' $\mathbb P^1_{\mathbb Z}$ is a family of projective lines parametrized by $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z$.

title: Presheaves on Open(X)
blurb: A presheaf is a contravariant functor from the poset of open sets to Set, packaging restriction maps into the functor's action on inclusions.

title: Presheaves and sheaf condition
blurb: Sheaf axioms encode local-to-global compatibility for sections over open covers: locality pins sections by their restrictions, gluing assembles compatible families.

title: Stalks and germs
blurb: Stalks are filtered colimits over neighborhoods of a point; germs are their elements, and stalk-wise checks detect local properties of sheaves.

title: Morphisms of sheaves and stalk criteria
blurb: Sheaf morphisms are natural transformations; injectivity and isomorphism are checkable on stalks, while stalkwise surjectivity is strictly weaker than global surjectivity — the seed of sheaf cohomology.

title: Sheafification as a left adjoint
blurb: Sheafification is the left adjoint to the inclusion Sh(X) into PSh(X); it preserves stalks and turns every presheaf into its universal sheaf approximation.

title: Ringed spaces and $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
blurb: An $\mathcal{O}_X$-module is a sheaf of abelian groups with compatible $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module structures on sections; locally free ones are vector bundles in disguise.

title: Quasi-coherent sheaves on affine schemes
blurb: The tilde functor $M \mapsto \widetilde{M}$ is an equivalence between $R$-modules and quasi-coherent sheaves on Spec R, globalizing module theory into algebraic geometry.

q: A presheaf of sets on $X$ is, by definition:
text: A presheaf of sets on $X$ is, by definition: Restriction $F(U) \to F(V)$ for $V \subseteq U$ reverses the inclusion arrow $V \hookrightarrow U$, which is exactly contravariance. Equivalently, a presheaf is a functor out of $\mathrm{Open}(X)^{\mathrm{op}}$.

q: For a presheaf $F$ and opens $W \subseteq V \subseteq U$, functoriality demands:
text: For a presheaf $F$ and opens $W \subseteq V \subseteq U$, functoriality demands: Restricting to $W$ in one shot must equal: restrict to $V$ first, then to $W$. This is the composition law of the contravariant functor, $\rho^U_W = \rho^V_W \circ \rho^U_V$.

q: How many morphisms are there in $\mathrm{Open}(X)$ from $V$ to $U$ when $V \subseteq U$ and $V \ne U$?
text: How many morphisms are there in $\mathrm{Open}(X)$ from $V$ to $U$ when $V \subseteq U$ and $V \ne U$? $\mathrm{Open}(X)$ is a poset viewed as a category: there is exactly one morphism $V \to U$ when $V \subseteq U$, and none otherwise.

q: Morphisms of presheaves $\varphi\colon F \to G$ are exactly:
text: Morphisms of presheaves $\varphi\colon F \to G$ are exactly: Presheaves are functors, so their morphisms are natural transformations: one map per open, with squares $F(U) \to G(U) \to G(V)$ equal to $F(U) \to F(V) \to G(V)$. Stalk maps are induced, not defining. Natural transformations commute with every restriction map.

q: Which of the following is NOT a presheaf on $\mathbb{R}$ (with its usual topology)?
text: Which of the following is NOT a presheaf on $\mathbb{R}$ (with its usual topology)? An inclusion $V \hookrightarrow U$ induces a map $\pi_0(V) \to \pi_0(U)$ (send a component of $V$ to the component of $U$ containing it), going the WRONG way for a presheaf. So $U \mapsto \pi_0(U)$ is naturally a covariant functor on $\mathrm{Open}(X)$, not a presheaf. The other three are presheaves (the bounded one fails to be a sheaf, but is still a presheaf). Look for an assignment not functorial under restriction.

q: Let $X$ be a space with $|\mathrm{Open}(X)| = 3$ (e.g. $X = \{a,b\}$ with topology $\{\emptyset, \{a\}, X\}$). A presheaf of sets picks a set $F(U)$ for each open and a restriction for each inclusion. How many non-identity inclusions $V \subsetneq U$ are there in this $\mathrm{Open}(X)$? (These are the non-trivial restrictions you must specify.)
text: Let $X$ be a space with $|\mathrm{Open}(X)| = 3$ (e.g. $X = \{a,b\}$ with topology $\{\emptyset, \{a\}, X\}$). A presheaf of sets picks a set $F(U)$ for each open and a restriction for each inclusion. How many non-identity inclusions $V \subsetneq U$ are there in this $\mathrm{Open}(X)$? (These are the non-trivial restrictions you must specify.) The three opens are $\emptyset \subsetneq \{a\} \subsetneq X$. Strict inclusions: $\emptyset \subsetneq \{a\}$, $\emptyset \subsetneq X$, and $\{a\} \subsetneq X$ — three in total. A presheaf of sets is just a functor into Set.

q: A presheaf $F$ on $X$ is a sheaf iff, for every open $U \subseteq X$ and every open cover $U = \bigcup_i U_i$:
text: A presheaf $F$ on $X$ is a sheaf iff, for every open $U \subseteq X$ and every open cover $U = \bigcup_i U_i$: The sheaf axiom packages locality (injectivity on $F(U)\to\prod F(U_i)$) and gluing (compatible families lift) as the equalizer condition.

q: Let $B$ be the presheaf on $\mathbb{R}$ with $B(U) = \{$bounded continuous $f\colon U\to\mathbb{R}\}$. Which sheaf axiom does $B$ fail?
text: Let $B$ be the presheaf on $\mathbb{R}$ with $B(U) = \{$bounded continuous $f\colon U\to\mathbb{R}\}$. Which sheaf axiom does $B$ fail? Bounded functions glue to an unbounded function: on $\mathbb{R} = \bigcup_n (-n,n)$ the compatible family $f_n(x)=x$ has no bounded global lift, so gluing fails.

q: For a sheaf of abelian groups $F$, what is $|F(\emptyset)|$? (Enter the size of the underlying set.)
text: For a sheaf of abelian groups $F$, what is $|F(\emptyset)|$? (Enter the size of the underlying set.) The empty cover of $\emptyset$ forces $F(\emptyset)$ to be the terminal object of the target category — for abelian groups, the zero group $\{0\}$, which has $1$ element.

q: Consider the constant presheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}^{\mathrm{pre}}$ on a disconnected space $X = U_1 \sqcup U_2$ assigning $\mathbb{Z}$ to every non-empty open. Its sheafification $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$ satisfies $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}(X) \cong$ :
text: Consider the constant presheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}^{\mathrm{pre}}$ on a disconnected space $X = U_1 \sqcup U_2$ assigning $\mathbb{Z}$ to every non-empty open. Its sheafification $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$ satisfies $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}(X) \cong$ : On $X = U_1 \sqcup U_2$ the sheaf condition forces $F(X) \cong F(U_1)\times F(U_2)$ because $\{U_1,U_2\}$ is a cover with $U_1\cap U_2=\emptyset$. The constant presheaf assigned $\mathbb{Z}$ to $X$, but its sheafification assigns $\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}$, showing constant presheaf $\neq$ constant sheaf on disconnected spaces. Disconnected opens fail gluing for the constant presheaf.

q: Which is the correct order of steps in verifying the equalizer condition for the sheaf $\mathcal{O}_X$ of holomorphic functions on a Riemann surface?
text: Which is the correct order of steps in verifying the equalizer condition for the sheaf $\mathcal{O}_X$ of holomorphic functions on a Riemann surface? The standard verification separates into two parts matching the two parts of the equalizer: first prove $e:F(U)\to\prod F(U_i)$ is injective (locality/separation), then prove every element of the equalizer is in the image (gluing/descent). Verify equalizer: identity of sections on overlaps, then glue.

q: Let $F$ be the skyscraper sheaf at $x\in X$ with stalk $\mathbb{Z}/5\mathbb{Z}$. For any open $U$ not containing $x$, $|F(U)|$ equals what?
text: Let $F$ be the skyscraper sheaf at $x\in X$ with stalk $\mathbb{Z}/5\mathbb{Z}$. For any open $U$ not containing $x$, $|F(U)|$ equals what? A skyscraper sheaf assigns the trivial group (of size $1$) to every open set not containing the base point $x$, and the stalk group $\mathbb{Z}/5\mathbb{Z}$ to every open containing $x$. Skyscraper sheaf assigns the singleton to any open missing $x$.

q: The stalk $F_x$ of a sheaf at $x\in X$ is defined as:
text: The stalk $F_x$ of a sheaf at $x\in X$ is defined as: A germ at $x$ is an equivalence class $[(U,s)]$ of a section on some open neighborhood, modulo agreement on smaller neighborhoods — exactly the filtered colimit $\varinjlim_{x\in U} F(U)$.

q: For a sheaf $F$ and sections $s,t\in F(U)$ with $s_x = t_x$ for every $x\in U$, which conclusion holds?
text: For a sheaf $F$ and sections $s,t\in F(U)$ with $s_x = t_x$ for every $x\in U$, which conclusion holds? Agreement on all stalks produces an open cover on which $s$ and $t$ coincide; the sheaf's locality axiom then forces $s = t$ on $U$.

q: On a scheme $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,R$, the stalk of $\mathcal{O}_X$ at the prime $\mathfrak{p}$ is $R_{\mathfrak{p}}$. For $R = \mathbb{Z}$ and $\mathfrak{p} = (5)$, what is the residue field $R_{\mathfrak{p}}/\mathfrak{p}R_{\mathfrak{p}}$'s characteristic?
text: On a scheme $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,R$, the stalk of $\mathcal{O}_X$ at the prime $\mathfrak{p}$ is $R_{\mathfrak{p}}$. For $R = \mathbb{Z}$ and $\mathfrak{p} = (5)$, what is the residue field $R_{\mathfrak{p}}/\mathfrak{p}R_{\mathfrak{p}}$'s characteristic? $\mathbb{Z}_{(5)}/5\mathbb{Z}_{(5)} = \mathbb{F}_5$, the residue field at the prime $(5)$, which has characteristic $5$.

q: A morphism $\varphi:F\to G$ of sheaves on $X$ is an isomorphism if and only if:
text: A morphism $\varphi:F\to G$ of sheaves on $X$ is an isomorphism if and only if: For sheaves, a morphism is an isomorphism iff it is an isomorphism on all stalks. This fails for presheaves — the key point is that sheafification preserves stalks, so stalk-wise data is the correct local invariant. Stalk-wise isomorphism is equivalent to being a sheaf iso.

q: For $\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{C},0}$ the stalk of holomorphic functions at $0\in\mathbb{C}$, the function $f(z)=z^3+2z$ has germ $[(U,f)]$ at $0$. What is the value of this germ evaluated at $z=0$, i.e., $f(0)$?
text: For $\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{C},0}$ the stalk of holomorphic functions at $0\in\mathbb{C}$, the function $f(z)=z^3+2z$ has germ $[(U,f)]$ at $0$. What is the value of this germ evaluated at $z=0$, i.e., $f(0)$? $f(0) = 0^3 + 2\cdot 0 = 0$. The germ encodes all local data of $f$ near $0$, including $f(0)=0$. The germ of $z^3 + 2z$ at $0$ has value $0$.

q: The étalé space $\acute{E}t(F)$ of a sheaf $F$ is constructed so that sections of $F$ over $U$ correspond bijectively to:
text: The étalé space $\acute{E}t(F)$ of a sheaf $F$ is constructed so that sections of $F$ over $U$ correspond bijectively to: The étalé (or étale) space is $\acute{E}t(F)=\bigsqcup_{x\in X}F_x$ topologized so that $F(U)\cong\{$continuous sections of $\pi$ over $U\}$. This is the sheaf-of-sections construction and underpins the equivalence between sheaves and étalé bundles. Etale space sections are continuous local sections over $U$.

q: For a morphism $\varphi\colon F \to G$ of sheaves on $X$, which stalk criterion is correct?
text: For a morphism $\varphi\colon F \to G$ of sheaves on $X$, which stalk criterion is correct? Monomorphisms in $\mathsf{Sh}(X)$ coincide with sectionwise injective, equivalently stalkwise injective. Epimorphisms are stalkwise surjective but NOT sectionwise surjective in general — that asymmetry is the seed of sheaf cohomology.

q: On $X = \mathbb{C}^\times$, consider $\exp\colon \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X^\times$ sending $f \mapsto e^f$. This map is:
text: On $X = \mathbb{C}^\times$, consider $\exp\colon \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X^\times$ sending $f \mapsto e^f$. This map is: Locally on a small disc a holomorphic logarithm exists, so $\exp_x$ is surjective on every stalk. But no global holomorphic logarithm of $z$ exists on $\mathbb{C}^\times$ (the branch cut obstructs it), so $z \not\in \exp(\mathcal{O}(\mathbb{C}^\times))$.

q: A short exact sequence $0 \to F' \to F \to F'' \to 0$ of sheaves of abelian groups on $X$ yields a long exact sequence in sheaf cohomology. The cohomology degree where the first new obstruction to global surjectivity appears is $H^i(X, F')$ for $i = ?$
text: A short exact sequence $0 \to F' \to F \to F'' \to 0$ of sheaves of abelian groups on $X$ yields a long exact sequence in sheaf cohomology. The cohomology degree where the first new obstruction to global surjectivity appears is $H^i(X, F')$ for $i = ?$ Applying $\Gamma(X,-)$ gives $0 \to \Gamma F' \to \Gamma F \to \Gamma F'' \to H^1(X, F') \to \cdots$. The connecting map lands in $H^1$, which measures exactly the failure of $\Gamma F \to \Gamma F''$ to be surjective.

q: For a morphism $\varphi\colon F \to G$ of sheaves of abelian groups, how is the image sheaf $\mathrm{im}\,\varphi$ defined, and why?
text: For a morphism $\varphi\colon F \to G$ of sheaves of abelian groups, how is the image sheaf $\mathrm{im}\,\varphi$ defined, and why? The presheaf image $U \mapsto \varphi_U F(U)$ captures 'sections of $G$ actually coming from $F$ on $U$', but a section of $G$ that is locally of this form need not lift to a global preimage — exactly the exponential example. Sheafifying fixes this, and on stalks one still has $(\mathrm{im}\,\varphi)_x = \mathrm{im}(\varphi_x)$. Sheafify the naive presheaf image to define the image sheaf.

q: Let $\varphi\colon F \to G$ be a morphism of sheaves of sets with $\varphi_x\colon F_x \to G_x$ a bijection for every $x \in X$. Then:
text: Let $\varphi\colon F \to G$ be a morphism of sheaves of sets with $\varphi_x\colon F_x \to G_x$ a bijection for every $x \in X$. Then: For sheaves, stalkwise iso $\iff$ iso. Sectionwise bijectivity is actually a consequence: stalkwise injective gives sectionwise injective (using locality), and stalkwise surjective + locality + gluing of $F$ gives sectionwise surjective. Together: $\varphi_U$ is a bijection for every $U$, hence $\varphi$ is an iso in $\mathsf{Sh}(X)$. Stalkwise bijection may not force global bijection without exactness.

q: Let $X = \mathbb{C}^\times$. The cokernel sheaf of $\exp\colon \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X^\times$ (stalkwise surjective) is the zero sheaf, yet the presheaf cokernel $U \mapsto \mathcal{O}^\times(U)/\exp \mathcal{O}(U)$ evaluated at $U = X$ is a nontrivial group isomorphic to $H^1(\mathbb{C}^\times, \underline{2\pi i\mathbb{Z}})$. What is the rank of this $\mathbb{Z}$-module?
text: Let $X = \mathbb{C}^\times$. The cokernel sheaf of $\exp\colon \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X^\times$ (stalkwise surjective) is the zero sheaf, yet the presheaf cokernel $U \mapsto \mathcal{O}^\times(U)/\exp \mathcal{O}(U)$ evaluated at $U = X$ is a nontrivial group isomorphic to $H^1(\mathbb{C}^\times, \underline{2\pi i\mathbb{Z}})$. What is the rank of this $\mathbb{Z}$-module? $H^1(\mathbb{C}^\times, \underline{\mathbb{Z}}) \cong \mathbb{Z}$ (generated by the winding-number cocycle), so the $\mathbb{Z}$-rank is $1$. This is why the presheaf cokernel disagrees with the sheaf cokernel: sheafification wipes out the obstruction, which reappears as $H^1$. Cokernel on $\mathbb{C}^\times$ is trivial as a sheaf but non-trivial as a presheaf.

q: The sheafification functor $(-)^\#\colon \mathsf{PSh}(X) \to \mathsf{Sh}(X)$ is:
text: The sheafification functor $(-)^\#\colon \mathsf{PSh}(X) \to \mathsf{Sh}(X)$ is: Sheafification is the left adjoint to $\iota$. The universal property: any presheaf map $F \to \iota G$ into a sheaf factors uniquely through the unit $\eta\colon F \to F^\#$.

q: Which invariant of a presheaf $F$ is preserved by sheafification?
text: Which invariant of a presheaf $F$ is preserved by sheafification? The unit $\eta\colon F \to F^\#$ induces an isomorphism $F_x \xrightarrow{\sim} (F^\#)_x$ on every stalk. Global and sectional data CAN change — e.g. the constant presheaf on a disconnected space has $F(X) = S$ but $F^\#(X) = S^{\#(\mathrm{components})}$.

q: Let $X = U_1 \sqcup U_2 \sqcup U_3$ be a disjoint union of three nonempty opens, and let $F = \underline{\mathbb{Z}/2}^{\mathrm{pre}}$ be the constant presheaf with value $\mathbb{Z}/2$. How many elements does the sheafified global-sections set $F^\#(X)$ have?
text: Let $X = U_1 \sqcup U_2 \sqcup U_3$ be a disjoint union of three nonempty opens, and let $F = \underline{\mathbb{Z}/2}^{\mathrm{pre}}$ be the constant presheaf with value $\mathbb{Z}/2$. How many elements does the sheafified global-sections set $F^\#(X)$ have? The constant sheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}/2}$ assigns to $X$ the locally-constant functions $X \to \mathbb{Z}/2$. With three components, this is $(\mathbb{Z}/2)^3$, which has $2^3 = 8$ elements. The unsheafified presheaf $F(X)$ has only $2$ elements, so sheafification has multiplied the global sections from $2$ to $8$.

q: A reflective subcategory $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{D}$ is one where the inclusion has a left adjoint $L\colon \mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{C}$. Why is $\mathsf{Sh}(X) \subseteq \mathsf{PSh}(X)$ with reflector $(-)^\#$ a good thing for computing in sheaves?
text: A reflective subcategory $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{D}$ is one where the inclusion has a left adjoint $L\colon \mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{C}$. Why is $\mathsf{Sh}(X) \subseteq \mathsf{PSh}(X)$ with reflector $(-)^\#$ a good thing for computing in sheaves? Left adjoints preserve colimits. So colimits in $\mathsf{Sh}(X)$ are the reflection of presheaf colimits: compute pointwise, then sheafify. Limits, by contrast, are computed pointwise directly and do not need sheafification — the inclusion $\iota$ preserves limits as a right adjoint. The reflection unit provides the universal sheafification map.

q: Sheafification of abelian presheaves $(-)^\#\colon \mathsf{PSh}(X, \mathsf{Ab}) \to \mathsf{Sh}(X, \mathsf{Ab})$ is exact. Which combination of facts is essential to this exactness?
text: Sheafification of abelian presheaves $(-)^\#\colon \mathsf{PSh}(X, \mathsf{Ab}) \to \mathsf{Sh}(X, \mathsf{Ab})$ is exact. Which combination of facts is essential to this exactness? Two ingredients: (1) sheafification preserves stalks (it's the universal gluing fix that leaves germs alone), (2) exactness of sheaves of abelian groups is stalk-local. Combined: $(F^\#)_x = F_x$, so stalk-exact in presheaves $\implies$ stalk-exact in sheaves $\iff$ exact in $\mathsf{Sh}(X, \mathsf{Ab})$. Exactness comes from stalks being preserved.

q: Let $F$ be any presheaf on a space $X$. The 'plus construction' $F^+$ applied once yields a separated presheaf; applied twice yields a sheaf. How many applications of $(-)^+$ are required in general to land in $\mathsf{Sh}(X)$?
text: Let $F$ be any presheaf on a space $X$. The 'plus construction' $F^+$ applied once yields a separated presheaf; applied twice yields a sheaf. How many applications of $(-)^+$ are required in general to land in $\mathsf{Sh}(X)$? Grothendieck's theorem: $F^{++} = F^\#$ is always a sheaf, so two applications suffice. One application is not enough: $F^+$ is separated (locality holds) but may still fail gluing. First plus-construction separates; second makes gluing work.

q: A ringed space is a pair $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ where:
text: A ringed space is a pair $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ where: A ringed space packages 'geometry' ($X$, a topological space) with 'functions' ($\mathcal{O}_X$, a sheaf of rings). Examples: $(M, C^\infty_M)$ for a smooth manifold; $(\mathrm{Spec}\,R, \widetilde{R})$ for an affine scheme.

q: A ringed space $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ is called LOCALLY ringed when:
text: A ringed space $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ is called LOCALLY ringed when: Local ringedness is the stalk-level condition: every $\mathcal{O}_{X,x}$ is a local ring. The maximal ideal is 'functions vanishing at $x$', and the residue field $\kappa(x) = \mathcal{O}_{X,x}/\mathfrak{m}_x$ is the field of values at $x$. Smooth manifolds, complex manifolds, and schemes are all locally ringed; general ringed spaces need not be.

q: For a smooth manifold $M$, the sheaf of sections of the trivial rank-$5$ bundle $M \times \mathbb{R}^5 \to M$ is a locally free $C^\infty_M$-module of rank equal to what integer?
text: For a smooth manifold $M$, the sheaf of sections of the trivial rank-$5$ bundle $M \times \mathbb{R}^5 \to M$ is a locally free $C^\infty_M$-module of rank equal to what integer? The locally-free-sheaf / vector-bundle dictionary: rank of the bundle = rank of the module. A rank-$n$ bundle corresponds to a locally free $\mathcal{O}_M$-module of rank $n$; the trivial rank-$5$ bundle gives $(C^\infty_M)^5$, a free (hence locally free) module of rank $5$.

q: A morphism of locally ringed spaces $(f, f^\#)\colon (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ includes a data piece beyond a continuous map + sheaf-of-rings map. What is the extra condition?
text: A morphism of locally ringed spaces $(f, f^\#)\colon (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ includes a data piece beyond a continuous map + sheaf-of-rings map. What is the extra condition? Without the local-ring condition on stalks, one gets 'ring-valued' morphisms that don't correspond to anything geometric — e.g. between schemes one could otherwise construct a map $\mathrm{Spec}\,k(t) \to \mathrm{Spec}\,k[t]_{(t)}$ that inverts the inclusion, which is not a scheme morphism. Locality of the stalk map is exactly what pins down 'the point goes to the point it should.' Local ring maps respect maximal ideals.

q: For an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$ on a ringed space, which statement about tensor product is correct?
text: For an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$ on a ringed space, which statement about tensor product is correct? The presheaf $U \mapsto \mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$ generally fails the gluing axiom, so tensor product of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules is defined by sheafifying. By contrast, $\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})$ is already a sheaf (hom satisfies gluing because morphisms glue). Tensor with $\mathcal{O}_X$ sheafifies locally.

q: On $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}$ with structure sheaf $\mathcal{O}_X$, the stalk at the prime $(0)$ is $\mathbb{Q}$. What is the residue field $\kappa((0)) = \mathcal{O}_{X,(0)}/\mathfrak{m}_{(0)}$? (Enter $0$ if it is zero, or the characteristic of the residue field otherwise.)
text: On $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}$ with structure sheaf $\mathcal{O}_X$, the stalk at the prime $(0)$ is $\mathbb{Q}$. What is the residue field $\kappa((0)) = \mathcal{O}_{X,(0)}/\mathfrak{m}_{(0)}$? (Enter $0$ if it is zero, or the characteristic of the residue field otherwise.) $\mathbb{Q}$ is already a field, so its maximal ideal is $(0)$ and the residue field is $\mathbb{Q}/(0) = \mathbb{Q}$, which has characteristic $0$. At closed points $(p)$ the residue field is $\mathbb{F}_p$; at the generic point $(0)$ the residue field is $\mathbb{Q}$. At the generic point the structure sheaf localizes to the field of fractions.

q: The sheafification $F^+$ of a presheaf $F$ is characterized by:
text: The sheafification $F^+$ of a presheaf $F$ is characterized by: Sheafification is left-adjoint to the inclusion $\mathsf{Sh}(X)\hookrightarrow\mathsf{PSh}(X)$: it enforces gluing while preserving stalks $(F^+)_x = F_x$.

q: On $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,R$, the functor $M\mapsto \widetilde{M}$ taking an $R$-module to its associated quasi-coherent sheaf:
text: On $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,R$, the functor $M\mapsto \widetilde{M}$ taking an $R$-module to its associated quasi-coherent sheaf: The key theorem: $M\mapsto\widetilde{M}$ is an equivalence of categories with inverse $\mathcal{F}\mapsto\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})$. This reduces quasi-coherent sheaf theory on an affine scheme to module theory.

q: For $R=\mathbb{Z}$, $M = \mathbb{Z}/6$, and $f = 2$, the section $\widetilde{M}(D(2)) = M_2 = M[\tfrac{1}{2}]$ has how many elements?
text: For $R=\mathbb{Z}$, $M = \mathbb{Z}/6$, and $f = 2$, the section $\widetilde{M}(D(2)) = M_2 = M[\tfrac{1}{2}]$ has how many elements? Inverting $2$ in $\mathbb{Z}/6$ kills the $2$-part: $(\mathbb{Z}/6)[1/2] = \mathbb{Z}/3$, which has $3$ elements.

q: Which is the correct sequence of steps in the two-step construction of sheafification $F\mapsto F^+$?
text: Which is the correct sequence of steps in the two-step construction of sheafification $F\mapsto F^+$? The plus-construction $F^+$ yields a separated presheaf from any presheaf. Applying it a second time to the separated presheaf yields the sheaf $(F^+)^+$. Grothendieck showed two applications always suffice. Start with the plus-construction twice.

q: Let $R = k[x,y]/(xy)$ and $M = R/(x)\cong k[y]$. The localization $M_x = M[1/x]$ has how many elements when $k = \mathbb{F}_2$ and we count elements of $M_x$ that are annihilated by $x$? (Hint: $xM=0$.)
text: Let $R = k[x,y]/(xy)$ and $M = R/(x)\cong k[y]$. The localization $M_x = M[1/x]$ has how many elements when $k = \mathbb{F}_2$ and we count elements of $M_x$ that are annihilated by $x$? (Hint: $xM=0$.) Since $x$ annihilates every element of $M = k[y]$ (as $x\cdot k[y]=0$ in $R/(x)$), inverting $x$ forces $M_x = 0$. So every element of $M_x$ is $0$, and $0$ elements are annihilated by $x$ in a trivial group sense — $M_x$ itself has size $1$ (the zero module), so there are $0$ non-zero elements annihilated by $x$. Since $xy = 0$ forces $x$ invertible to kill $y$, the localization collapses.

q: A coherent sheaf on a Noetherian scheme $X$ is a quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ that is additionally:
text: A coherent sheaf on a Noetherian scheme $X$ is a quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ that is additionally: Coherence requires local finite generation (and, for non-Noetherian rings, also that the kernel of any map from a free finite-rank sheaf is finitely generated). On a Noetherian scheme, local finite generation suffices. Coherent means finitely generated with finitely presented kernel on opens.

title: 2. The affine case: morphisms are ring maps (backwards)
Morphisms of schemes
blurb: Scheme maps are local ring maps glued globally and tested on affines.

title: Open, closed, and locally closed immersions
blurb: Immersions are the subobject inclusions of $\mathsf{Sch}$: $\operatorname{Spec}(A_f)\hookrightarrow\operatorname{Spec} A$ (open) and $\operatorname{Spec}(A/I)\hookrightarrow\operatorname{Spec} A$ (closed).

title: Fiber products and base change
blurb: Fiber products represent pullback problems and define base-change geometry.

title: Geometric fibers and smoothness intuition
blurb: Fibers over points reveal dimension, singularities, and arithmetic variation in families.

title: Separated and proper morphisms
blurb: Separated means the diagonal $\Delta_f\colon X\to X\times_S X$ is a closed immersion (Hausdorff-like); proper means separated, of finite type, and universally closed — the valuative criterion makes this the scheme version of compact.

q: A morphism of schemes $f\colon X\to Y$ is a continuous map $|X|\to|Y|$ together with a map of sheaves of rings $f^{\#}\colon \mathcal{O}_Y\to f_{*}\mathcal{O}_X$ satisfying which extra condition?
text: A morphism of schemes $f\colon X\to Y$ is a continuous map $|X|\to|Y|$ together with a map of sheaves of rings $f^{\#}\colon \mathcal{O}_Y\to f_{*}\mathcal{O}_X$ satisfying which extra condition? A morphism of schemes is a morphism of locally ringed spaces: the stalk maps $f^{\#}_x$ must be local, i.e. send the maximal ideal $\mathfrak{m}_{f(x)}$ into $\mathfrak{m}_x$. Without this, arbitrary ringed-space maps between spectra don't correspond to ring maps.

q: For affine schemes, what is the anti-equivalence between rings and affine schemes?
text: For affine schemes, what is the anti-equivalence between rings and affine schemes? $\operatorname{Spec}$ is a contravariant functor: a ring map $\varphi\colon A\to B$ induces $\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\colon \operatorname{Spec} B\to\operatorname{Spec} A$ by pulling back prime ideals via $\mathfrak{q}\mapsto \varphi^{-1}(\mathfrak{q})$.

q: How many morphisms $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_5\to \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-1)$ over $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$ are there? (Equivalently, how many ring maps $\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-1)\to \mathbb{F}_5$ fixing $\mathbb{Z}$?)
text: How many morphisms $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_5\to \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-1)$ over $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$ are there? (Equivalently, how many ring maps $\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-1)\to \mathbb{F}_5$ fixing $\mathbb{Z}$?) Such a ring map is determined by the image of $x$, which must satisfy $x^2=1$ in $\mathbb{F}_5$. The solutions are $x=\pm 1$, so there are $2$ morphisms.

q: Consider the ringed-space map $f\colon \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}$ defined on the underlying space as the identity but with $f^{\#}\colon \mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{C}$ being complex conjugation. Why is this NOT a morphism of schemes over $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}$?
text: Consider the ringed-space map $f\colon \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}$ defined on the underlying space as the identity but with $f^{\#}\colon \mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{C}$ being complex conjugation. Why is this NOT a morphism of schemes over $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}$? Complex conjugation $\sigma\colon\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{C}$ is a $\mathbb{Q}$-algebra automorphism (it fixes $\mathbb{Q}\subset\mathbb{R}$) and sends the unique maximal ideal $(0)$ to $(0)$, so the stalk map is local. It defines a perfectly valid non-trivial automorphism of $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}$ over $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}$. Morphisms of affine schemes correspond to opposite ring maps.

q: How many ring maps $\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)\to\mathbb{F}_3$ are there? (Hint: how many solutions does $x^2\equiv -1\pmod 3$ have?)
text: How many ring maps $\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)\to\mathbb{F}_3$ are there? (Hint: how many solutions does $x^2\equiv -1\pmod 3$ have?) We need $x^2\equiv -1\equiv 2\pmod 3$. Checking: $0^2=0$, $1^2=1$, $2^2=4\equiv 1$. None equals $2$, so $x^2+1$ has no roots in $\mathbb{F}_3$, and there are $0$ ring maps, meaning no morphisms $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_3\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[i]$. Solve $x^2 \equiv -1 \pmod 3$: check $x = 0, 1, 2$.

q: Which of the following is the correct chain of implications for a morphism of locally ringed spaces $f\colon(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\to(Y,\mathcal{O}_Y)$?
text: Which of the following is the correct chain of implications for a morphism of locally ringed spaces $f\colon(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\to(Y,\mathcal{O}_Y)$? A morphism of ringed spaces is a continuous map plus a map $f^{\#}\colon\mathcal{O}_Y\to f_*\mathcal{O}_X$. It is a morphism of locally ringed spaces iff additionally each stalk map $f^{\#}_x$ is local. When source and target are schemes this is exactly the correct notion of scheme morphism. Local ring maps send maximal ideals into maximal ideals.

q: A morphism $i\colon Z\to X$ is a closed immersion iff:
text: A morphism $i\colon Z\to X$ is a closed immersion iff: Closed immersion = topological closed inclusion + surjective map of structure sheaves. The kernel of $i^{\#}$ is the ideal sheaf cutting out $Z$.

q: Which ring map corresponds to the open immersion $D(f)\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} A$?
text: Which ring map corresponds to the open immersion $D(f)\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} A$? Open immersions onto a basic open come from localization: $A\to A_f = A[1/f]$ inverts $f$, and primes of $A_f$ correspond exactly to primes of $A$ not containing $f$.

q: The closed subscheme $V((x^2))\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} k[x]$ has the same underlying topological space as $V((x))$ (just the origin), but a different structure sheaf. What is $\dim_k \Gamma(V((x^2)),\mathcal{O}) = \dim_k k[x]/(x^2)$?
text: The closed subscheme $V((x^2))\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} k[x]$ has the same underlying topological space as $V((x))$ (just the origin), but a different structure sheaf. What is $\dim_k \Gamma(V((x^2)),\mathcal{O}) = \dim_k k[x]/(x^2)$? $k[x]/(x^2)$ has $k$-basis $\{1,x\}$, so dimension $2$. This is the 'fat point' / tangent direction attached to the origin — a non-reduced closed subscheme.

q: Let $Z\hookrightarrow X$ be a closed immersion with ideal sheaf $\mathcal{I}$, and let $U\hookrightarrow X$ be the complementary open immersion onto $X\setminus Z$. Which sequence of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules is exact?
text: Let $Z\hookrightarrow X$ be a closed immersion with ideal sheaf $\mathcal{I}$, and let $U\hookrightarrow X$ be the complementary open immersion onto $X\setminus Z$. Which sequence of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules is exact? The ideal sheaf $\mathcal{I}\subset\mathcal{O}_X$ is the kernel of $i^{\#}\colon\mathcal{O}_X\twoheadrightarrow i_*\mathcal{O}_Z$, giving the short exact sequence $0\to\mathcal{I}\to\mathcal{O}_X\to i_*\mathcal{O}_Z\to 0$. This is the fundamental way closed subschemes encode their 'thickness' via ideal sheaves. The open complement has structure sheaf equal to $\mathcal{O}_X$ restricted.

q: Which is a valid counterexample showing that open immersions need NOT be affine?
text: Which is a valid counterexample showing that open immersions need NOT be affine? $\mathbb{A}^2_k\setminus\{(0,0)\}$ is an open subscheme of $\mathbb{A}^2_k$ but is not affine: its global sections are still $k[x,y]$ (by Hartogs), yet it is not isomorphic to $\operatorname{Spec} k[x,y]$ because it is not affine (the structure sheaf has the right global sections but the space is not affine by Serre's criterion). The line $\mathbb{A}^2 \setminus \{0\}$ is not affine.

q: The $n$-th infinitesimal neighborhood of the origin in $\mathbb{A}^1_k$ is $\operatorname{Spec} k[x]/(x^n)$. What is $\dim_k k[x]/(x^3)$?
text: The $n$-th infinitesimal neighborhood of the origin in $\mathbb{A}^1_k$ is $\operatorname{Spec} k[x]/(x^n)$. What is $\dim_k k[x]/(x^3)$? $k[x]/(x^3)$ has $k$-basis $\{1,x,x^2\}$, dimension $3$. Generally the $n$-th infinitesimal neighborhood has $\dim_k = n$. Compute dimension as $n$ using the basis $1, x, \ldots, x^{n-1}$.

q: In the category of affine schemes, the fiber product $\operatorname{Spec} A \times_{\operatorname{Spec} R} \operatorname{Spec} B$ is represented by which ring?
text: In the category of affine schemes, the fiber product $\operatorname{Spec} A \times_{\operatorname{Spec} R} \operatorname{Spec} B$ is represented by which ring? Fiber product is the categorical pullback; on affines it's dual to the pushout in rings, which is the tensor product: $\operatorname{Spec} A\times_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\operatorname{Spec} B = \operatorname{Spec}(A\otimes_R B)$.

q: Which property is preserved under arbitrary base change?
text: Which property is preserved under arbitrary base change? Closed immersions are preserved by base change: if $Z\hookrightarrow X$ is closed and $X'\to X$ any morphism, then $Z\times_X X'\hookrightarrow X'$ is again a closed immersion. Topological bijectivity and point-count in fibers are not stable under base change.

q: As a $\mathbb{Q}$-vector space, what is the dimension of $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})\otimes_{\mathbb{Q}} \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$?
text: As a $\mathbb{Q}$-vector space, what is the dimension of $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})\otimes_{\mathbb{Q}} \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$? $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt 2)$ has $\mathbb{Q}$-dimension $2$, so the tensor product has dimension $2\cdot 2 = 4$. Geometrically, $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt 2)\times_{\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}}\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt 2)$ is $4$-dimensional over $\mathbb{Q}$ and splits as two points over $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt 2)$.

q: Compute $\mathbb{F}_p\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{F}_p$ as a ring. Which option is correct?
text: Compute $\mathbb{F}_p\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{F}_p$ as a ring. Which option is correct? $\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\cong\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}$ because $\mathbb{Z}/p\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Z}/p\cong\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(p,p)\mathbb{Z}=\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}=\mathbb{F}_p$. Geometrically, $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_p\times_{\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}}\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_p=\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_p$: both copies lie over the same closed point $(p)\in\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$. The tensor product over $\mathbb{Z}$ of $\mathbb{F}_p$ with itself is $\mathbb{F}_p$.

q: The fiber product $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}\times_{\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{R}}\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}$ corresponds to $\mathbb{C}\otimes_{\mathbb{R}}\mathbb{C}$. As a $\mathbb{C}$-algebra this splits as $\mathbb{C}\times\mathbb{C}$ (two points). What is $\dim_{\mathbb{R}}(\mathbb{C}\otimes_{\mathbb{R}}\mathbb{C})$?
text: The fiber product $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}\times_{\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{R}}\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}$ corresponds to $\mathbb{C}\otimes_{\mathbb{R}}\mathbb{C}$. As a $\mathbb{C}$-algebra this splits as $\mathbb{C}\times\mathbb{C}$ (two points). What is $\dim_{\mathbb{R}}(\mathbb{C}\otimes_{\mathbb{R}}\mathbb{C})$? $\mathbb{C}\cong\mathbb{R}[x]/(x^2+1)$, so $\mathbb{C}\otimes_{\mathbb{R}}\mathbb{C}\cong\mathbb{C}[x]/(x^2+1)\cong\mathbb{C}[x]/(x-i)(x+i)\cong\mathbb{C}\times\mathbb{C}$, which has $\mathbb{R}$-dimension $4$. The base-change from $\mathbb{R}$ to $\mathbb{C}$ splits the single $\mathbb{R}$-point of $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}$ into two $\mathbb{C}$-conjugate points. Compute $\mathbb{C} \otimes_\mathbb{R} \mathbb{C}$ as $\mathbb{C}[x]/(x^2+1)$.

q: The universal property of the fiber product $X\times_S Y$ says: morphisms $T\to X\times_S Y$ correspond bijectively to pairs $(f\colon T\to X,\ g\colon T\to Y)$ satisfying which condition?
text: The universal property of the fiber product $X\times_S Y$ says: morphisms $T\to X\times_S Y$ correspond bijectively to pairs $(f\colon T\to X,\ g\colon T\to Y)$ satisfying which condition? The fiber product is a categorical pullback: $T$-points are pairs $(f,g)$ such that $p_X\circ f = p_Y\circ g\colon T\to S$ (both map to the same point over $S$). This is the defining commutativity of the pullback square. Yoneda applied to the fiber product gives the universal property.

q: For a morphism $f\colon X\to Y$ and a point $y\in Y$ with residue field $\kappa(y)$, the scheme-theoretic fiber $X_y$ is defined as:
text: For a morphism $f\colon X\to Y$ and a point $y\in Y$ with residue field $\kappa(y)$, the scheme-theoretic fiber $X_y$ is defined as: The scheme-theoretic fiber is the base change $X\times_Y \operatorname{Spec}\kappa(y)$. Its underlying topological space is $f^{-1}(y)$, but the scheme structure records multiplicities and nilpotents.

q: Consider $f\colon \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-5)\to \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$. How many (geometric, i.e. over $\overline{\mathbb{F}_p}$) points does the fiber above the closed point $(5)\in\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$ have, counted without multiplicity?
text: Consider $f\colon \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-5)\to \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$. How many (geometric, i.e. over $\overline{\mathbb{F}_p}$) points does the fiber above the closed point $(5)\in\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$ have, counted without multiplicity? The fiber over $(5)$ is $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_5[x]/(x^2)$, a single point with a nilpotent — ramified. Topologically the fiber has $1$ point; scheme-theoretically its length is $2$.

q: For the same family $f\colon \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-5)\to \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$, how many geometric points does the fiber over $(11)$ have? (Hint: is $5$ a square mod $11$? Check $4^2=16\equiv 5$.)
text: For the same family $f\colon \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-5)\to \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$, how many geometric points does the fiber over $(11)$ have? (Hint: is $5$ a square mod $11$? Check $4^2=16\equiv 5$.) Since $5\equiv 4^2\pmod{11}$, the polynomial $x^2-5$ factors as $(x-4)(x+4)$ over $\mathbb{F}_{11}$, so the fiber is $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_{11}[x]/(x-4)(x+4)$, which has $2$ reduced points. The prime $11$ splits in $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt 5]$.

q: For $f\colon\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-5)\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$, the fiber over the generic point $(0)$ is $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{5})$. What is the correct sequence of fiber types as the prime $p$ varies?
text: For $f\colon\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-5)\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$, the fiber over the generic point $(0)$ is $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{5})$. What is the correct sequence of fiber types as the prime $p$ varies? The three behaviors of a prime in a quadratic extension $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{5}]$: ramified ($p=5$, fiber is a fat point of length $2$), inert (the Legendre symbol $(5/p)=-1$, fiber is a single point with residue field $\mathbb{F}_{p^2}$), or split ($(5/p)=1$, fiber is two reduced $\mathbb{F}_p$-points). At the generic point, tensor with $\mathbb{Q}$; it splits if $5$ is a square.

q: The fiber of $f\colon\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$ over the inert prime $(3)$ (since $-1$ is not a square mod $3$) is $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_9$. What is $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_3}\mathbb{F}_9$?
text: The fiber of $f\colon\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$ over the inert prime $(3)$ (since $-1$ is not a square mod $3$) is $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_9$. What is $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_3}\mathbb{F}_9$? $\mathbb{F}_9=\mathbb{F}_{3^2}$ is a degree-$2$ extension of $\mathbb{F}_3$, so $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_3}\mathbb{F}_9=2$. Inert primes give irreducible fibers whose residue field is a degree-$2$ extension of $\mathbb{F}_p$. At $(3)$: $\mathbb{F}_3[x]/(x^2+1)$ is the field $\mathbb{F}_9$.

q: A morphism $f\colon X\to Y$ has geometrically connected fibers if $X_y\times_{\kappa(y)}\overline{\kappa(y)}$ is connected for every $y\in Y$. Which is a counterexample where fibers are connected but NOT geometrically connected?
text: A morphism $f\colon X\to Y$ has geometrically connected fibers if $X_y\times_{\kappa(y)}\overline{\kappa(y)}$ is connected for every $y\in Y$. Which is a counterexample where fibers are connected but NOT geometrically connected? $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt 2)\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}$ has a single-point fiber (connected) but $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt 2)\otimes_{\mathbb{Q}}\overline{\mathbb{Q}}\cong\overline{\mathbb{Q}}\times\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$, which has two connected components. Thus the fiber is connected but not geometrically connected. Base change to the algebraic closure to test geometric connectedness.

q: A morphism $f\colon X\to S$ is separated iff the diagonal $\Delta_f\colon X\to X\times_S X$ is:
text: A morphism $f\colon X\to S$ is separated iff the diagonal $\Delta_f\colon X\to X\times_S X$ is: Separatedness = closed diagonal. This is the scheme-theoretic analog of Hausdorff, where in topology the diagonal is closed iff limits are unique.

q: Which of the following schemes over $\operatorname{Spec} k$ fails to be separated?
text: Which of the following schemes over $\operatorname{Spec} k$ fails to be separated? The line with doubled origin is the canonical non-separated scheme: both origins are limits of the same punctured line, so the diagonal of the structure map is not closed. All other options are separated.

q: Which of these morphisms is proper?
text: Which of these morphisms is proper? Projective space is the prototype proper morphism over a field. Affine space fails the valuative criterion (a $K$-point like $1/t\in\mathbb{A}^1_K$ does not extend to the DVR), as does $\mathbb{G}_m$.

q: The valuative criterion for properness states: $f\colon X\to S$ is proper (separated + finite type + universally closed) iff for every DVR $R$ with fraction field $K$, every commutative square $\operatorname{Spec} K\to X$, $\operatorname{Spec} R\to S$ has a unique filling $\operatorname{Spec} R\to X$. Which example demonstrates that $\mathbb{A}^1_k\to\operatorname{Spec} k$ fails this criterion?
text: The valuative criterion for properness states: $f\colon X\to S$ is proper (separated + finite type + universally closed) iff for every DVR $R$ with fraction field $K$, every commutative square $\operatorname{Spec} K\to X$, $\operatorname{Spec} R\to S$ has a unique filling $\operatorname{Spec} R\to X$. Which example demonstrates that $\mathbb{A}^1_k\to\operatorname{Spec} k$ fails this criterion? The $K$-rational point $x=1/t\in K=k((t))$ gives a map $\operatorname{Spec} K\to\mathbb{A}^1_k$. To extend to $R=k[[t]]$ we would need $1/t\in k[[t]]$, which is false since $1/t$ has a pole. So no filling exists, and $\mathbb{A}^1_k\to\operatorname{Spec} k$ is not proper. Existence first, then uniqueness of the extension.

q: Which is the correct order of logical implications in the proof that a composition of proper morphisms is proper?
text: Which is the correct order of logical implications in the proof that a composition of proper morphisms is proper? Each defining condition of properness (finite type, separated, universally closed) is stable under composition. Finite type: $\mathcal{O}$-algebras of finite type compose. Separated: the diagonal of the composition factors through a closed immersion. Universally closed: if $f$ and $g$ are universally closed then so is $g\circ f$ (closed sets map to closed sets through each step). Composition of closed immersions with proper base changes stays proper.

q: The diagonal $\Delta\colon\mathbb{P}^1_k\to\mathbb{P}^1_k\times_k\mathbb{P}^1_k$ is a closed immersion. The image of $\Delta$ is the diagonal divisor in the surface $\mathbb{P}^1\times\mathbb{P}^1$. What is the self-intersection number $\Delta\cdot\Delta$ on $\mathbb{P}^1\times\mathbb{P}^1$? (Hint: use $\Delta^2=\deg(T_{\mathbb{P}^1})=\chi(\mathbb{P}^1)$.)
text: The diagonal $\Delta\colon\mathbb{P}^1_k\to\mathbb{P}^1_k\times_k\mathbb{P}^1_k$ is a closed immersion. The image of $\Delta$ is the diagonal divisor in the surface $\mathbb{P}^1\times\mathbb{P}^1$. What is the self-intersection number $\Delta\cdot\Delta$ on $\mathbb{P}^1\times\mathbb{P}^1$? (Hint: use $\Delta^2=\deg(T_{\mathbb{P}^1})=\chi(\mathbb{P}^1)$.) The self-intersection of the diagonal equals the degree of the tangent bundle $T_{\mathbb{P}^1}$, which equals $\chi(\mathbb{P}^1)=2$ (by Euler characteristic, $\chi(\mathbb{P}^1)=2$ since $\mathbb{P}^1\cong S^2$). Alternatively, $T_{\mathbb{P}^1}\cong\mathcal{O}(2)$ has degree $2$. Diagonal image is the anti-diagonal-complement inside $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$.

title: Functor-of-points viewpoint
blurb: A space is studied through all maps into it, turning geometry into a representable functor problem.

title: Representability and moduli functors
blurb: Representable functors correspond to genuine geometric objects.

title: When functors require stacks
blurb: Automorphisms obstruct representability by schemes and motivate stack language.

title: The Yoneda embedding for schemes
blurb: The assignment $X \mapsto h_X$ is a fully faithful embedding of schemes into presheaves on rings, so a scheme is determined by — and can be constructed from — its functor of points.

title: Base change as pullback of functors
blurb: Reading $X_S(T) = X(T) \times_{\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}(T)} S(T)$ turns base change into a pointwise fiber product of sets and makes Weil restriction transparent.

q: In the functor-of-points picture, a scheme $X$ is encoded by which assignment?
text: In the functor-of-points picture, a scheme $X$ is encoded by which assignment? Yoneda says $X$ is determined by $h_X$. Restricting along $\mathrm{Spec}\dashv\Gamma$ gives the functor $R\mapsto X(R)=\mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm{Spec}\,R,X)$ on commutative rings, which captures $X$ up to unique isomorphism.

q: For $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(y^2-x^3-1)$, what is $X(R)$?
text: For $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(y^2-x^3-1)$, what is $X(R)$? For an affine $X=\mathrm{Spec}\,A$, $X(R)=\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{CRing}}(A,R)$. A ring hom out of $\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(y^2-x^3-1)$ is exactly a choice of $(a,b)\in R^2$ satisfying the relation.

q: For $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(y^2-x^3-1)$, count $|X(\mathbb{F}_2)|$ by brute force over $a,b\in\{0,1\}$.
text: For $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(y^2-x^3-1)$, count $|X(\mathbb{F}_2)|$ by brute force over $a,b\in\{0,1\}$. Over $\mathbb{F}_2$, check all four pairs: $(0,0): 0\ne 1$; $(0,1): 1=1$ ✓; $(1,0): 0 = 1^3+1 = 2 \equiv 0$ ✓; $(1,1): 1 \ne 0$. So $|X(\mathbb{F}_2)|=2$.

q: Let $A = \mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(y^2 - x^3 - 1)$ and $B = \mathbb{Z}[t,t^{-1}]$. A ring map $f: A \to B$ is determined by images $f(x) = u, f(y) = v$ in $B$ satisfying $v^2 = u^3 + 1$. Which of the following is a valid pair $(u,v)$ in $B = \mathbb{Z}[t,t^{-1}]$?
text: Let $A = \mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(y^2 - x^3 - 1)$ and $B = \mathbb{Z}[t,t^{-1}]$. A ring map $f: A \to B$ is determined by images $f(x) = u, f(y) = v$ in $B$ satisfying $v^2 = u^3 + 1$. Which of the following is a valid pair $(u,v)$ in $B = \mathbb{Z}[t,t^{-1}]$? We need $v^2 = u^3 + 1$ in $\mathbb{Z}[t,t^{-1}]$. Choice (b): $u=0, v=1$ gives $1 = 0 + 1$ ✓. Choice (a): $0 = t^3 + 1$ is false in a polynomial ring. Choices (c) and (d) similarly fail numerically. A ring map is determined by images of generators subject to relations.

q: Count $|X(\mathbb{F}_3)|$ for $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(y^2 - x^3 - 1)$ by checking all pairs $(a,b) \in \mathbb{F}_3^2$.
The correct order of reasoning that proves $h_X \cong h_Y \Rightarrow X \cong Y$ (Yoneda uniqueness) is:
text: The correct order of reasoning that proves $h_X \cong h_Y \Rightarrow X \cong Y$ (Yoneda uniqueness) is: Yoneda gives $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X,h_Y)\cong\mathrm{Hom}(X,Y)$ and vice versa. A natural isomorphism $\eta:h_X\xrightarrow{\sim}h_Y$ corresponds to $f:X\to Y$ and $\eta^{-1}$ to $g:Y\to X$; since $\eta^{-1}\circ\eta=\mathrm{id}_{h_X}$ and Yoneda is an isomorphism of sets, $g\circ f=\mathrm{id}_X$ and $f\circ g=\mathrm{id}_Y$. Yoneda gives a map by evaluating at $h_X(X) = \mathrm{id}_X$.

q: A functor $F:\mathrm{CRing}\to\mathsf{Set}$ is representable by an affine scheme $\mathrm{Spec}\,A$ iff:
text: A functor $F:\mathrm{CRing}\to\mathsf{Set}$ is representable by an affine scheme $\mathrm{Spec}\,A$ iff: Representability by $\mathrm{Spec}\,A$ means exactly that $F\cong h_{\mathrm{Spec}\,A}$, i.e. $F(R)\cong\mathrm{Hom}(A,R)$ naturally in $R$. The universal element is the image of $\mathrm{id}_A$.

q: Which ring represents the multiplicative group functor $\mathbb{G}_m$ with $\mathbb{G}_m(R)=R^\times$?
text: Which ring represents the multiplicative group functor $\mathbb{G}_m$ with $\mathbb{G}_m(R)=R^\times$? A ring hom $\mathbb{Z}[t,t^{-1}]\to R$ is exactly a choice of unit of $R$ (the image of $t$ must be invertible). So $\mathbb{G}_m=\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[t,t^{-1}]$.

q: The functor $\mathrm{GL}_n$ is represented by $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x_{ij}, t]/(t\det(X)-1)$. How many generators (counting $t$) does this ring have for $n=2$?
text: The functor $\mathrm{GL}_n$ is represented by $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x_{ij}, t]/(t\det(X)-1)$. How many generators (counting $t$) does this ring have for $n=2$? There are $n^2$ matrix entries $x_{ij}$ and one extra variable $t$ forcing $\det$ invertible. For $n=2$: $4+1=5$ generators.

q: The functor $F(R) = \{f \in R[x] : f \text{ is monic of degree } 2\}$ (monic quadratics over $R$) is representable. Which ring $A$ represents it?
text: The functor $F(R) = \{f \in R[x] : f \text{ is monic of degree } 2\}$ (monic quadratics over $R$) is representable. Which ring $A$ represents it? A monic quadratic over $R$ is $x^2 + ax + b$ with $a,b \in R$, determined by the pair $(a,b)$ freely. A ring map $\mathbb{Z}[a,b] \to R$ is exactly such a pair, so $F \cong h_{\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[a,b]} = \mathbb{A}^2$. No relation is needed since there is no constraint on $(a,b)$. Monic quadratics have two free coefficients: $A = \mathbb{Z}[a_0, a_1]$.

q: Which of the following functors $\mathrm{CRing} \to \mathsf{Set}$ is provably NOT representable by any scheme?
text: Which of the following functors $\mathrm{CRing} \to \mathsf{Set}$ is provably NOT representable by any scheme?
