The functor in (c) is the functor of rank-1 projective modules; taking isomorphism classes rather than objects destroys the sheaf/descent condition (exactly as with $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$), so it is not representable by a scheme. The others: (a) $= \mathbb{A}^1$, (b) $= \mathbb{A}^1$ again, (d) $= \mathbb{G}_m$. Nonrepresentable functors usually fail descent or Zariski-sheafhood.

q: The additive group scheme $\mathbb{G}_a$ represents $R \mapsto (R,+)$ and is $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[t]$. Compute $|\mathbb{G}_a(\mathbb{F}_p)|$ for $p = 7$.
text: The additive group scheme $\mathbb{G}_a$ represents $R \mapsto (R,+)$ and is $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[t]$. Compute $|\mathbb{G}_a(\mathbb{F}_p)|$ for $p = 7$. $\mathbb{G}_a(\mathbb{F}_p) = (\mathbb{F}_p, +)$, which has $p$ elements. For $p=7$: $|\mathbb{G}_a(\mathbb{F}_7)| = 7$. Count $|\mathbb{F}_4, +| = 4$.

q: The moduli functor $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}:R\mapsto\{\text{elliptic curves over }R\}/\cong$ fails to be representable primarily because:
text: The moduli functor $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}:R\mapsto\{\text{elliptic curves over }R\}/\cong$ fails to be representable primarily because: The automorphism $[-1]$ (and extra ones at $j=0,1728$) lets you build nontrivial families that are pointwise isomorphic to the trivial family but globally distinct. Passing to isomorphism classes forgets this gluing data, breaking the sheaf condition.

q: The fix promoted by stacks is to replace the target category $\mathsf{Set}$ by:
text: The fix promoted by stacks is to replace the target category $\mathsf{Set}$ by: An algebraic stack is (essentially) a sheaf of groupoids $\mathrm{CRing}\to\mathsf{Grpd}$. Remembering automorphisms as 2-arrows restores descent, which taking isomorphism classes destroyed.

q: For an elliptic curve $E$ over a field of characteristic $0$ with generic $j$-invariant, what is $|\mathrm{Aut}(E)|$?
text: For an elliptic curve $E$ over a field of characteristic $0$ with generic $j$-invariant, what is $|\mathrm{Aut}(E)|$? In characteristic $0$ a generic elliptic curve has automorphism group $\{\pm1\}$, of order $2$ (only $j=0$ and $j=1728$ give larger automorphism groups).

q: Let $S = \{p_0, p_1\}$ be a two-point base scheme and $E/k$ an elliptic curve. The product family $A = E \times S$ and the twisted family $B$ (glued via $[-1]$ over one point) both have $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}(\{p_i\}) = [E]$ for each $i$. Which statement correctly explains why this contradicts representability?
text: Let $S = \{p_0, p_1\}$ be a two-point base scheme and $E/k$ an elliptic curve. The product family $A = E \times S$ and the twisted family $B$ (glued via $[-1]$ over one point) both have $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}(\{p_i\}) = [E]$ for each $i$. Which statement correctly explains why this contradicts representability? A fine moduli space $M$ with $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}\cong h_M$ would give a bijection $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}(S)\cong\mathrm{Hom}(S,M)$. Both $A$ and $B$ define the same element of $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}(S)$ (same isomorphism class at each point), hence the same map $S\to M$, hence $A\cong B$ by the universal property — but $A \not\cong B$ as families. Contradiction. Twisting by $[-1] \in \mathrm{Aut}(E)$ gives a torsor that is not trivial globally.

q: Elliptic curves with full level-$N$ structure (a choice of basis for $E[N]$) are representable by the modular curve $Y(N)$ for $N\ge 3$. What is the key reason adding level structure restores representability?
text: Elliptic curves with full level-$N$ structure (a choice of basis for $E[N]$) are representable by the modular curve $Y(N)$ for $N\ge 3$. What is the key reason adding level structure restores representability? An automorphism of $(E,\alpha_N)$ must fix the level-$N$ basis. For $N\ge 3$ the only automorphism of $E$ that fixes $E[N]$ pointwise is the identity (since $[-1]$ acts as $-1\ne 1$ on $N$-torsion when $N>2$). With trivial automorphisms, the twisted-family obstruction vanishes and the moduli functor is representable. Level-$N$ kills the automorphism group for $N \ge 3$.

q: An elliptic curve with $j = 1728$ over $\mathbb{Q}(i)$ has $\mathrm{Aut}(E) \cong \mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}$. What is $|\mathrm{Aut}(E)|$?
text: An elliptic curve with $j = 1728$ over $\mathbb{Q}(i)$ has $\mathrm{Aut}(E) \cong \mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}$. What is $|\mathrm{Aut}(E)|$? The curve $y^2 = x^3 - x$ (j=1728) has the extra automorphism $(x,y)\mapsto(-x,iy)$ of order 4 over a field containing $i$. So $|\mathrm{Aut}(E)| = 4$. Automorphisms of $E$ at $j = 1728$ form $\mathbb{Z}/4$; base change to $\mathbb{Q}(i)$ sees them all.

q: The Yoneda embedding $h_{(-)}:\mathsf{Sch} \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Fun}(\mathsf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}},\mathsf{Set})$ is:
text: The Yoneda embedding $h_{(-)}:\mathsf{Sch} \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Fun}(\mathsf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}},\mathsf{Set})$ is: The Yoneda lemma gives $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X, h_Y) \cong \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{Sch}}(X,Y)$ naturally, so $X \mapsto h_X$ is fully faithful: every natural transformation between representables comes from a unique scheme map.

q: Under Yoneda, the universal element of $h_X(X) = \mathrm{Hom}(X,X)$ that witnesses representability is:
text: Under Yoneda, the universal element of $h_X(X) = \mathrm{Hom}(X,X)$ that witnesses representability is: The Yoneda lemma identifies $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X,F) \cong F(X)$ by evaluating on $\mathrm{id}_X$. So the identity is the universal $X$-point, and every natural transformation is recovered by pushing $\mathrm{id}_X$ forward.

q: Let $F(R) = R \times R$ as a set-valued functor on $\mathrm{CRing}$. Which scheme represents $F$? Enter the integer $n$ such that $F \cong h_{\mathbb{A}^n}$.
text: Let $F(R) = R \times R$ as a set-valued functor on $\mathrm{CRing}$. Which scheme represents $F$? Enter the integer $n$ such that $F \cong h_{\mathbb{A}^n}$. $\mathbb{A}^n(R) = \mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb{Z}[x_1,\dots,x_n], R) = R^n$. To get $R \times R = R^2$ we take $n=2$, so $F$ is represented by the affine plane $\mathbb{A}^2$.

q: Suppose $\eta: h_X \Rightarrow h_Y$ is a natural transformation. Unraveling full faithfulness, the correct chain of steps to produce a scheme map $f: X \to Y$ from $\eta$ is:
text: Suppose $\eta: h_X \Rightarrow h_Y$ is a natural transformation. Unraveling full faithfulness, the correct chain of steps to produce a scheme map $f: X \to Y$ from $\eta$ is: Full faithfulness goes: $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X,h_Y)\xrightarrow{\sim}h_Y(X)=\mathrm{Hom}(X,Y)$ by $\eta\mapsto\eta_X(\mathrm{id}_X)$. The inverse sends $f$ to the natural transformation whose $R$-component is $h_Y(-)\circ f_* = (-\circ f)$. Naturality is exactly this formula; no choices or colimits are needed. Evaluate the natural transformation at the identity element.

q: A presheaf $F:\mathsf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$ lies in the essential image of the Yoneda embedding (i.e., is representable) iff it satisfies which conditions?
text: A presheaf $F:\mathsf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$ lies in the essential image of the Yoneda embedding (i.e., is representable) iff it satisfies which conditions? By Grothendieck's representability theorem (in the affine case: Zariski sheaf + covering by representable opens), a presheaf is a scheme iff it satisfies Zariski descent and is locally isomorphic to affine schemes. Preserving limits is not sufficient: the structure sheaf condition is the key extra datum. Representability needs the Zariski-sheaf property and local representability.

q: Via Yoneda, $\mathrm{Nat}(h_{\mathbb{A}^1}, h_{\mathbb{A}^2}) \cong \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{Sch}}(\mathbb{A}^1, \mathbb{A}^2) \cong \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{CRing}}(\mathbb{Z}[x,y], \mathbb{Z}[t])$. A ring map is determined by images $x\mapsto f(t),\, y\mapsto g(t)$. How many such natural transformations correspond to linear maps $x\mapsto t,\, y\mapsto t^2 - 1$? Enter $1$ if this is a valid natural transformation, $0$ otherwise.
text: Via Yoneda, $\mathrm{Nat}(h_{\mathbb{A}^1}, h_{\mathbb{A}^2}) \cong \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{Sch}}(\mathbb{A}^1, \mathbb{A}^2) \cong \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{CRing}}(\mathbb{Z}[x,y], \mathbb{Z}[t])$. A ring map is determined by images $x\mapsto f(t),\, y\mapsto g(t)$. How many such natural transformations correspond to linear maps $x\mapsto t,\, y\mapsto t^2 - 1$? Enter $1$ if this is a valid natural transformation, $0$ otherwise. A ring map $\mathbb{Z}[x,y]\to\mathbb{Z}[t]$ is determined freely by any images of $x$ and $y$ (no relations in $\mathbb{Z}[x,y]$). So $x\mapsto t,\, y\mapsto t^2-1$ is a valid ring map, hence a valid natural transformation via Yoneda. The answer is $1$. Natural transformations correspond to elements of $h_Y(\mathbb{A}^1)$.

q: If $X \to \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}$ is a scheme and $S = \mathrm{Spec}\,k$, then for a $k$-algebra $R$ the base-changed scheme $X_k$ satisfies:
text: If $X \to \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}$ is a scheme and $S = \mathrm{Spec}\,k$, then for a $k$-algebra $R$ the base-changed scheme $X_k$ satisfies: Base change to $k$ remembers the same functor $X$ but restricted to the slice category of $k$-algebras. Equivalently $X_k(R) = X(R)$ where $R$ is regarded with its given $k$-algebra structure — pulling back along $\mathrm{Spec}\,R \to \mathrm{Spec}\,k \to \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}$.

q: The functor-of-points identity $(X \times_S Y)(T) = X(T) \times_{S(T)} Y(T)$ says that the fiber product of schemes:
text: The functor-of-points identity $(X \times_S Y)(T) = X(T) \times_{S(T)} Y(T)$ says that the fiber product of schemes: Representable functors preserve limits. So the representable functor of $X \times_S Y$ is the pointwise fiber product of the representable functors of $X, S, Y$ — this is exactly what lets you compute base changes by solving equations over each test ring.

q: Let $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)$. How many elements does $X(\mathbb{F}_5) = X \times_{\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_5$ have at the level of $\mathbb{F}_5$-points?
text: Let $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)$. How many elements does $X(\mathbb{F}_5) = X \times_{\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_5$ have at the level of $\mathbb{F}_5$-points? $X(\mathbb{F}_5) = \{a \in \mathbb{F}_5 : a^2 = -1\}$. Since $2^2 = 4 \equiv -1 \pmod 5$ and $3^2 = 9 \equiv -1 \pmod 5$, there are exactly $2$ solutions.

q: Let $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2 - p)$ for a prime $p$. At the special fiber $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_p$, the base-changed scheme $X_{\mathbb{F}_p}$ is $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^2)$. What kind of scheme is this?
text: Let $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2 - p)$ for a prime $p$. At the special fiber $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_p$, the base-changed scheme $X_{\mathbb{F}_p}$ is $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^2)$. What kind of scheme is this? $\mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^2)$ is the dual numbers over $\mathbb{F}_p$: it has a unique maximal ideal $(x)$, so a single closed point, but $x$ is nonzero and nilpotent. This is a non-reduced fat point encoding the ramification of $p$ in $X$. The two generic roots $\pm\sqrt{p}$ collide modulo $p$. Base change to $\mathbb{F}_p$: $x^2 \equiv 0 \pmod p$ has the special fiber.

q: The Weil restriction $\mathrm{Res}_{\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R}}\,\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}$ represents the functor $R \mapsto \mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}(R \otimes_{\mathbb{R}} \mathbb{C}) = R\otimes_\mathbb{R}\mathbb{C}$ on $\mathbb{R}$-algebras. Which real scheme represents this functor?
text: The Weil restriction $\mathrm{Res}_{\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R}}\,\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}$ represents the functor $R \mapsto \mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}(R \otimes_{\mathbb{R}} \mathbb{C}) = R\otimes_\mathbb{R}\mathbb{C}$ on $\mathbb{R}$-algebras. Which real scheme represents this functor? As an $\mathbb{R}$-module, $\mathbb{C} \cong \mathbb{R}^2$ (via $a+bi\mapsto(a,b)$), so $R\otimes_\mathbb{R}\mathbb{C}\cong R^2$. The functor $R\mapsto R^2$ is $\mathbb{A}^2_{\mathbb{R}}$. This is the classical fact that $\mathrm{Res}_{\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R}}\,\mathbb{A}^1$ is $\mathbb{A}^2_{\mathbb{R}}$, explaining why a complex variable has two real degrees of freedom. Weil restriction doubles the dimension over $\mathbb{R}$.

q: Let $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)$ and $Y = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[y]/(y^2+1)$. Using the fiber-product formula $(X\times_{\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}} Y)(\mathbb{F}_5) = X(\mathbb{F}_5) \times Y(\mathbb{F}_5)$, compute $|(X \times Y)(\mathbb{F}_5)|$.
text: Let $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)$ and $Y = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[y]/(y^2+1)$. Using the fiber-product formula $(X\times_{\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}} Y)(\mathbb{F}_5) = X(\mathbb{F}_5) \times Y(\mathbb{F}_5)$, compute $|(X \times Y)(\mathbb{F}_5)|$. $X(\mathbb{F}_5) = \{2,3\}$ (solutions to $a^2\equiv -1\pmod 5$), so $|X(\mathbb{F}_5)|=2$. Similarly $|Y(\mathbb{F}_5)|=2$. The fiber-product formula gives $|X(\mathbb{F}_5)|\times|Y(\mathbb{F}_5)| = 2\times 2 = 4$. Count ring maps to each of the factors.

title: Elliptic curves and Weierstrass models
blurb: A smooth genus-1 curve with basepoint admits Weierstrass equations and rich arithmetic.

title: Group law on cubic curves
blurb: Chord-tangent constructions define an abelian group on rational points.

title: The j-invariant and isomorphism classes
blurb: The j-invariant is a single number that classifies elliptic curves over an algebraically closed field up to isomorphism, and witnesses the coarse moduli space M_{1,1}.

title: Complex uniformization and CM
blurb: Over C every elliptic curve is a torus C/Λ; curves whose endomorphism ring is larger than Z have complex multiplication by an order in an imaginary quadratic field.

title: Mordell–Weil and invariants
blurb: Rational points form a finitely generated group with rank and torsion invariants.

q: For $y^2=x^3+ax+b$, nonsingularity is equivalent to:
text: For $y^2=x^3+ax+b$, nonsingularity is equivalent to: A nonzero discriminant means no cusp or node.

q: A singular cubic typically has what local defect?
text: A singular cubic typically has what local defect? Singularity appears as node/cusp where derivatives vanish simultaneously.

q: The singular Weierstrass cubic $y^2=x^3$ (a cusp at the origin) has smooth locus isomorphic (as a group) to:
text: The singular Weierstrass cubic $y^2=x^3$ (a cusp at the origin) has smooth locus isomorphic (as a group) to: A cuspidal cubic has smooth locus $\cong\mathbb{G}_a$ (the additive group), parametrised by $t\mapsto(t^2,t^3)$. A nodal cubic has smooth locus $\cong\mathbb{G}_m$.

q: For $E:y^2=x^3+x^2$ (a node at the origin, $a=0,b=0$ in Weierstrass form after the change $x\mapsto x-1/3$ is NOT yet done), compute $4a^3+27b^2$ directly from $a=0,b=0$.
text: For $E:y^2=x^3+x^2$ (a node at the origin, $a=0,b=0$ in Weierstrass form after the change $x\mapsto x-1/3$ is NOT yet done), compute $4a^3+27b^2$ directly from $a=0,b=0$. $4(0)^3+27(0)^2=0$, confirming $\Delta=0$ and a singular point.

q: Which is the correct order of steps to verify $y^2=x^3-3x+2$ is singular?
text: Which is the correct order of steps to verify $y^2=x^3-3x+2$ is singular? $f(1)=1-3+2=0$ and $f'(1)=3-3=0$, so $x=1$ is a double root, giving a node at $(1,0)$. One then confirms $\Delta=-16(4(-3)^3+27(2)^2)=-16(-108+108)=0$.

q: On an elliptic curve, $P+Q+R=\mathcal O$ when:
text: On an elliptic curve, $P+Q+R=\mathcal O$ when: The secant line meets the cubic in three points summing to zero.

q: The identity element for the elliptic-curve group law is:
text: The identity element for the elliptic-curve group law is: Projectively, the neutral element is the point at infinity.

q: If a point has order $5$, the smallest positive $n$ with $nP=\mathcal O$ is:
text: If a point has order $5$, the smallest positive $n$ with $nP=\mathcal O$ is: That is the definition of order.

q: The slick proof that the chord-and-tangent group law is associative uses:
text: The slick proof that the chord-and-tangent group law is associative uses: One identifies $E$ with $\operatorname{Pic}^0(E)$ via $P\mapsto[P]-[O]$; since $\operatorname{Pic}^0$ is a group by construction, associativity is automatic and one avoids all coordinate computation. Transport the group structure from a place where associativity is obvious.

q: On $E: y^2=x^3-x$ over $\mathbb{Q}$, the $2$-torsion points satisfy $y=0$. How many affine $2$-torsion points does $E$ have?
text: On $E: y^2=x^3-x$ over $\mathbb{Q}$, the $2$-torsion points satisfy $y=0$. How many affine $2$-torsion points does $E$ have? $y=0$ gives $x^3-x=x(x-1)(x+1)=0$, so $x\in\{0,1,-1\}$: three affine points $(0,0),(1,0),(-1,0)$. Together with $\mathcal O$, the full $E[2]\cong(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$ has $4$ elements.

q: The degree of the multiplication-by-$n$ map $[n]:E\to E$ is:
text: The degree of the multiplication-by-$n$ map $[n]:E\to E$ is: $\deg[n]=n^2$, so the kernel $E[n]$ has $n^2$ geometric points over $\bar k$ (when $\operatorname{char}(k)\nmid n$), isomorphic to $(\mathbb{Z}/n)^2$.

q: Two short Weierstrass curves over $\bar k$ are isomorphic iff:
text: Two short Weierstrass curves over $\bar k$ are isomorphic iff: $j(E)=1728\cdot 4a^3/(4a^3+27b^2)$ is a complete isomorphism invariant over $\bar k$.

q: Compute $j(E)$ for $E: y^2=x^3-x$ (i.e. $a=-1, b=0$).
text: Compute $j(E)$ for $E: y^2=x^3-x$ (i.e. $a=-1, b=0$). With $b=0$, $j=1728\cdot 4a^3/(4a^3)=1728$. This is the CM-by-$\mathbb{Z}[i]$ curve.

q: Which $j$-values correspond to curves with $|\operatorname{Aut}(E)|>2$?
text: Which $j$-values correspond to curves with $|\operatorname{Aut}(E)|>2$? At $j=0$ the curve has an order-$6$ automorphism (via $\omega$); at $j=1728$ it has an order-$4$ automorphism (via $i$). Generic $j$ gives $\operatorname{Aut}(E)=\{\pm1\}$.

q: Two curves $E: y^2=x^3+x$ and $E': y^2=x^3+4x$ have the same $j$-invariant $1728$. Over $\mathbb{Q}$, they are:
text: Two curves $E: y^2=x^3+x$ and $E': y^2=x^3+4x$ have the same $j$-invariant $1728$. Over $\mathbb{Q}$, they are: Same $j$ over $\bar k$ means isomorphic over $\bar k$, but the twist parameter $u^4 a'=a$ requires $u^4=4$, so $u=\sqrt{2}\notin\mathbb{Q}$. They are $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$-twists of each other, not isomorphic over $\mathbb{Q}$. Find the scalar $u$ needed to match coefficients, then check if it lives in $\mathbb{Q}$.

q: Compute $j(E)$ for $E: y^2=x^3+1$ (i.e. $a=0, b=1$). Recall $j=1728\cdot 4a^3/(4a^3+27b^2)$.
text: Compute $j(E)$ for $E: y^2=x^3+1$ (i.e. $a=0, b=1$). Recall $j=1728\cdot 4a^3/(4a^3+27b^2)$. $4a^3=0$, so $j=1728\cdot 0/(0+27)=0$. This is the CM-by-$\mathbb{Z}[\omega]$ curve.

q: The isomorphism $(a,b)\mapsto(u^4 a, u^6 b)$ for $u\in\bar k^\times$ induces a $\bar k^\times$-action of weight $(4,6)$. The $j$-invariant $j=1728\cdot 4a^3/(4a^3+27b^2)$ is invariant because:
text: The isomorphism $(a,b)\mapsto(u^4 a, u^6 b)$ for $u\in\bar k^\times$ induces a $\bar k^\times$-action of weight $(4,6)$. The $j$-invariant $j=1728\cdot 4a^3/(4a^3+27b^2)$ is invariant because: Under $(a,b)\mapsto(u^4 a,u^6 b)$: numerator $4a^3\mapsto 4u^{12}a^3$ and denominator $4a^3+27b^2\mapsto u^{12}(4a^3+27b^2)$. The $u^{12}$ cancels, leaving $j$ unchanged.

q: Over $\mathbb{C}$, every elliptic curve is analytically isomorphic to:
text: Over $\mathbb{C}$, every elliptic curve is analytically isomorphic to: The Weierstrass $\wp$-function embeds $\mathbb{C}/\Lambda$ as a cubic $y^2=4x^3-g_2 x-g_3$ in $\mathbb{CP}^2$.

q: An elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{C}$ has complex multiplication when:
text: An elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{C}$ has complex multiplication when: CM means $\operatorname{End}(E)$ is an order in an imaginary quadratic field $K$; generically $\operatorname{End}(E)=\mathbb{Z}$.

q: For the CM lattice $\Lambda=\mathbb{Z}[i]$, the endomorphism 'multiplication by $i$' has degree (i.e. number of sheets as an isogeny) equal to $|i|^2=$?
text: For the CM lattice $\Lambda=\mathbb{Z}[i]$, the endomorphism 'multiplication by $i$' has degree (i.e. number of sheets as an isogeny) equal to $|i|^2=$? $|i|^2=1$, so multiplication by $i$ is an automorphism (degree $1$), consistent with $j=1728$ having $\operatorname{Aut}(E)\supset\{\pm1,\pm i\}$.

q: Two lattices $\Lambda$ and $\Lambda'$ give isomorphic elliptic curves over $\mathbb{C}$ iff:
text: Two lattices $\Lambda$ and $\Lambda'$ give isomorphic elliptic curves over $\mathbb{C}$ iff: An analytic isomorphism $\mathbb{C}/\Lambda\to\mathbb{C}/\Lambda'$ must lift to a $\mathbb{C}$-linear map $z\mapsto\alpha z$, which maps $\Lambda$ onto $\Lambda'$. So isomorphism $\Leftrightarrow$ homothety $\Lambda'=\alpha\Lambda$.

q: The endomorphism 'multiplication by $1+i$' on $\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{Z}[i]$ is an isogeny of degree $|1+i|^2=$?
text: The endomorphism 'multiplication by $1+i$' on $\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{Z}[i]$ is an isogeny of degree $|1+i|^2=$? $|1+i|^2=1^2+1^2=2$. The kernel is $\frac{1}{1+i}\mathbb{Z}[i]/\mathbb{Z}[i]\cong\mathbb{Z}/2$, so the isogeny has degree $2$.

q: Which of the following correctly describes the significance of $j((1+\sqrt{-163})/2)=-640320^3$?
text: Which of the following correctly describes the significance of $j((1+\sqrt{-163})/2)=-640320^3$? $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-163})$ has class number $1$, so the Hilbert class field equals $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-163})$ and its singular modulus $j$ must lie in $\mathbb{Q}$. Since $j=-640320^3$, we get $e^{\pi\sqrt{163}}=j+744+O(e^{-2\pi\sqrt{163}})\approx 640320^3+744$.

q: Mordell–Weil states $E(\mathbb{Q})$ is:
text: Mordell–Weil states $E(\mathbb{Q})$ is: $E(\mathbb{Q})\cong E(\mathbb{Q})_{\text{tors}}\oplus\mathbb{Z}^r$.

q: If $E(\mathbb{Q})\cong (\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})\oplus\mathbb{Z}^3$, the rank is:
text: If $E(\mathbb{Q})\cong (\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})\oplus\mathbb{Z}^3$, the rank is: Rank is the exponent of the free part.

q: The torsion subgroup in Mordell–Weil is:
text: The torsion subgroup in Mordell–Weil is: Over $\mathbb{Q}$, torsion is finite (Mazur classifies possibilities).

q: Which of the following is NOT on Mazur's list of possible torsion subgroups of $E(\mathbb{Q})$?
text: Which of the following is NOT on Mazur's list of possible torsion subgroups of $E(\mathbb{Q})$? Mazur's theorem (1977) restricts $E(\mathbb{Q})_{\text{tors}}$ to $\mathbb{Z}/n$ for $n\le 10$ or $n=12$, or $\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2n$ for $n\le 4$. $\mathbb{Z}/11$ is not on the list; the allowed cyclic groups go up to $n=10$ and then jump to $12$.

q: The BSD conjecture predicts $\operatorname{ord}_{s=1}L(E,s)=r$. For which ranks is this unconditionally known?
text: The BSD conjecture predicts $\operatorname{ord}_{s=1}L(E,s)=r$. For which ranks is this unconditionally known? Gross–Zagier (1986) links the derivative $L'(E,1)$ to the height of a Heegner point, and Kolyvagin's Euler system bounds the Selmer group. Together these prove BSD for analytic rank $\le 1$.

q: In the proof of the weak Mordell–Weil theorem, which exact sequence is central?
text: In the proof of the weak Mordell–Weil theorem, which exact sequence is central? Applying Galois cohomology to $0\to E[n]\to E\xrightarrow{[n]}E\to 0$ gives the Kummer map $E(K)/nE(K)\hookrightarrow H^1(G_K,E[n])$. Since $E[n]$ is defined over a finite extension and the cocycles satisfy local conditions, the image (the Selmer group) is finite, proving $E(K)/nE(K)$ is finite. You need a sequence that turns $E(K)/nE(K)$ into something cohomological.

title: 3. The group law on the smooth part
5. Good reduction vs. bad reduction, numerically
Singular cubic types
blurb: Nodal and cuspidal degenerations control failures of smooth group laws.

title: Reduction modulo p
blurb: Reducing Weierstrass models modulo p tracks good and bad fibers.

title: Bad reduction taxonomy
blurb: Local reduction types forecast conductor exponents and arithmetic complexity.

title: Point counting and the Hasse bound
blurb: At good primes $|a_p| \le 2\sqrt p$ (Hasse); at multiplicative primes $a_p = \pm 1$; at additive primes $a_p = 0$. The four-row table assembles the local Euler factors.

title: Minimal Weierstrass model and conductor
blurb: A global minimal model has the smallest possible discriminant. Its bad primes, weighted by Ogg's formula, produce the conductor $N(E)$ — the number that appears in every $L$-function statement.

q: How many distinct tangent lines does the nodal cubic $y^2 = x^3 + x^2$ have at its singular point $(0,0)$?
text: How many distinct tangent lines does the nodal cubic $y^2 = x^3 + x^2$ have at its singular point $(0,0)$? The degree-$2$ part at the origin is $y^2 - x^2 = (y-x)(y+x)$, which factors into two distinct linear forms. So the tangent cone is two lines $y = \pm x$, exactly the node picture.

q: At the cusp of $y^2 = x^3$, the degree-$2$ part of the defining polynomial is:
text: At the cusp of $y^2 = x^3$, the degree-$2$ part of the defining polynomial is: The lowest-order terms of $y^2 - x^3$ at the origin are just $y^2$, a single repeated factor. That single tangent direction with multiplicity $2$ is what distinguishes a cusp from a node.

q: For the cuspidal parametrization $(x,y) = (t^2, t^3)$, evaluate $y$ at $t = 3$.
text: For the cuspidal parametrization $(x,y) = (t^2, t^3)$, evaluate $y$ at $t = 3$. $y = t^3 = 3^3 = 27$. The cusp $y^2 = x^3$ is parametrized by $t \mapsto (t^2,t^3)$, with $t=0$ mapping to the singular point.

q: The cuspidal cubic $y^2 = x^3$ and the nodal cubic $y^2 = x^3 + x^2$ are both singular at the origin. Which chain of implications correctly orders their tangent-cone analysis?
text: The cuspidal cubic $y^2 = x^3$ and the nodal cubic $y^2 = x^3 + x^2$ are both singular at the origin. Which chain of implications correctly orders their tangent-cone analysis? The standard procedure: isolate the lowest-degree (degree-$2$) homogeneous part at the singular point, then factor it over $\mathbb{R}$. For $y^2-x^3$ the degree-$2$ part is $y^2$ (one repeated factor) → cusp; for $y^2-x^3-x^2$ it is $y^2-x^2=(y-x)(y+x)$ (two distinct factors) → node. Tangent cone factorization distinguishes nodes (two lines) from cusps (double line).

q: For the nodal cubic $C: y^2 = x^3 + x^2$, the parametrization $(x,y)=(t^2-1,\, t(t^2-1))$ covers the smooth locus. Verify: compute $y^2 - x^3 - x^2$ at $t=2$ and give the answer.
text: For the nodal cubic $C: y^2 = x^3 + x^2$, the parametrization $(x,y)=(t^2-1,\, t(t^2-1))$ covers the smooth locus. Verify: compute $y^2 - x^3 - x^2$ at $t=2$ and give the answer. At $t=2$: $x=4-1=3$, $y=2(4-1)=6$. Then $y^2-x^3-x^2=36-27-9=0$, confirming the point $(3,6)$ lies on the cubic. Substitute the parametrization and simplify to the defining equation.

q: A cubic plane curve over $\mathbb{C}$ with a node is NOT isomorphic (as an algebraic curve) to one with a cusp. Which is the best algebraic reason?
text: A cubic plane curve over $\mathbb{C}$ with a node is NOT isomorphic (as an algebraic curve) to one with a cusp. Which is the best algebraic reason? Both singularities give geometric genus $0$ curves, but the smooth part of the nodal cubic is $\mathbb{G}_m \cong \mathbb{C}^*$ while the smooth part of the cuspidal cubic is $\mathbb{G}_a \cong \mathbb{C}$. These are non-isomorphic algebraic groups, so the curves cannot be isomorphic. Nodes have two distinct tangent directions; cusps only one.

q: Let $E : y^2 = x^3 + ax + b$ with $a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$ and discriminant $\Delta = -16(4a^3 + 27 b^2)$. The reduction $\tilde E_p$ is singular (bad reduction) iff:
text: Let $E : y^2 = x^3 + ax + b$ with $a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$ and discriminant $\Delta = -16(4a^3 + 27 b^2)$. The reduction $\tilde E_p$ is singular (bad reduction) iff: A Weierstrass cubic is smooth over $\mathbb{F}_p$ exactly when its discriminant is nonzero mod $p$. So bad reduction at $p$ is the condition $p \mid \Delta$; good reduction is $p \nmid \Delta$.

For $E : y^2 = x^3 + 1$ (so $a = 0, b = 1$), compute $\Delta = -16(4a^3 + 27 b^2)$. $\Delta = -16(4\cdot 0 + 27\cdot 1) = -16\cdot 27 = -432 = -2^4\cdot 3^3$, so the only primes of bad reduction for this model are $2$ and $3$.

q: For $E : y^2 = x^3 + 1$ with $\Delta = -432 = -2^4\cdot 3^3$, at which prime below is the reduction good?
text: For $E : y^2 = x^3 + 1$ with $\Delta = -432 = -2^4\cdot 3^3$, at which prime below is the reduction good? Bad primes are those dividing $\Delta = -432$, namely $p = 2$ and $p = 3$. Every other prime, including $p = 5$, is a prime of good reduction.

q: Let $E: y^2 = x^3 - x$ with $\Delta = 64$. Which prime $p$ gives bad reduction, and what is the singular type of $\tilde E_p$?
text: Let $E: y^2 = x^3 - x$ with $\Delta = 64$. Which prime $p$ gives bad reduction, and what is the singular type of $\tilde E_p$? $\Delta=64=2^6$, so the only prime of bad reduction is $p=2$. Reducing $y^2=x^3-x$ mod $2$ gives $y^2=x^3+x=x(x^2+1)=x(x+1)^2$ mod $2$; the singular point is $(1,0)$ and the tangent factors split over $\mathbb{F}_2$, indicating a node (multiplicative reduction). The discriminant is $64$, divisible only by $2$.

q: For $E: y^2 = x^3 - 7$ (so $a=0$, $b=-7$), compute $\Delta = -16(4\cdot 0 + 27\cdot 49)$.
text: For $E: y^2 = x^3 - 7$ (so $a=0$, $b=-7$), compute $\Delta = -16(4\cdot 0 + 27\cdot 49)$. $\Delta = -16 \cdot 27 \cdot 49 = -16 \cdot 1323 = -21168$. The prime factorization is $-2^4\cdot 3^3\cdot 7^2$, so bad primes are $2, 3, 7$. Compute $-16 \cdot 27 \cdot 49$.

q: Suppose $E: y^2 = x^3 + px$ for a prime $p$. The discriminant is $\Delta = -64p^3$. As we vary $p$, which statement about the good-reduction primes is correct?
text: Suppose $E: y^2 = x^3 + px$ for a prime $p$. The discriminant is $\Delta = -64p^3$. As we vary $p$, which statement about the good-reduction primes is correct? Bad primes are exactly those dividing $\Delta = -64p^3 = -2^6 p^3$, namely $\ell = 2$ and $\ell = p$. Every other prime $\ell\ne 2,p$ satisfies $\ell\nmid\Delta$ and thus gives good reduction. Good reduction corresponds to primes coprime to the discriminant.

q: In the Kodaira–Néron preview, the smooth locus of a cuspidal reduction $\tilde E_p$ is isomorphic to:
text: In the Kodaira–Néron preview, the smooth locus of a cuspidal reduction $\tilde E_p$ is isomorphic to: Cuspidal reduction is called additive precisely because the smooth locus is the additive group $\mathbb{G}_a(\mathbb{F}_p) = \mathbb{F}_p$, which has order $p$.

q: A node over $\mathbb{F}_p$ is called split multiplicative when:
text: A node over $\mathbb{F}_p$ is called split multiplicative when: Split means the tangent slopes $\pm s$ lie in $\mathbb{F}_p$, so the smooth locus is $\mathbb{G}_m(\mathbb{F}_p) \cong \mathbb{F}_p^\times$ of order $p-1$. Non-split means the slopes live in $\mathbb{F}_{p^2} \setminus \mathbb{F}_p$, giving a non-split torus of order $p+1$.

q: For a non-split multiplicative reduction at $p = 7$, the smooth part of $\tilde E_p$ has order $p+1$. Compute this order.
text: For a non-split multiplicative reduction at $p = 7$, the smooth part of $\tilde E_p$ has order $p+1$. Compute this order. The non-split torus has order $p + 1 = 7 + 1 = 8$. Split multiplicative would instead give $p - 1 = 6$; additive would give $p = 7$.

q: At additive reduction the smooth locus is $\mathbb{G}_a(\mathbb{F}_p)\cong\mathbb{F}_p$. If we naively count all $\mathbb{F}_p$-points of $\tilde E_p$ (including the singular point), what is $|\tilde E_p(\mathbb{F}_p)|$?
text: At additive reduction the smooth locus is $\mathbb{G}_a(\mathbb{F}_p)\cong\mathbb{F}_p$. If we naively count all $\mathbb{F}_p$-points of $\tilde E_p$ (including the singular point), what is $|\tilde E_p(\mathbb{F}_p)|$? The smooth locus has $p$ points (isomorphic to $\mathbb{G}_a$). The singular point is one additional $\mathbb{F}_p$-rational point, giving $p+1$ total $\mathbb{F}_p$-points on $\tilde E_p$. However, the group law applies only on the smooth locus. Count $|E^{\mathrm{ns}}(\mathbb{F}_p)| = p$.

q: A split multiplicative reduction at $p=13$ has smooth locus $\mathbb{G}_m(\mathbb{F}_{13})\cong\mathbb{F}_{13}^\times$. Compute the order of this group.
text: A split multiplicative reduction at $p=13$ has smooth locus $\mathbb{G}_m(\mathbb{F}_{13})\cong\mathbb{F}_{13}^\times$. Compute the order of this group. $|\mathbb{F}_{13}^\times| = 13-1 = 12$. For split multiplicative reduction the smooth locus order is always $p-1$. Compute $13 - 1 = 12$.

q: Which sequence of reduction types correctly orders them by the group-theoretic complexity of their smooth locus, from simplest to most complex?
text: Which sequence of reduction types correctly orders them by the group-theoretic complexity of their smooth locus, from simplest to most complex? $\mathbb{G}_a$ is the simplest (unipotent, no torsion structure), $\mathbb{G}_m$ is rank-$1$ multiplicative, the non-split torus is a rank-$1$ non-trivial form of $\mathbb{G}_m$ with a Galois twist, and an elliptic curve is an abelian variety of dimension $1$. This is the natural complexity ladder. Additive has zero-dimensional growth $\mathbb{G}_a$; multiplicative gives $\mathbb{G}_m$.

q: For an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{F}_p$ with good reduction, Hasse's theorem bounds $a_p := p + 1 - |E(\mathbb{F}_p)|$ by:
text: For an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{F}_p$ with good reduction, Hasse's theorem bounds $a_p := p + 1 - |E(\mathbb{F}_p)|$ by: Hasse's theorem (a Riemann-hypothesis analogue for $E/\mathbb{F}_p$) gives $|a_p| \le 2\sqrt p$. Equivalently $|E(\mathbb{F}_p)|$ lies in the interval $[p + 1 - 2\sqrt p,\; p + 1 + 2\sqrt p]$.

q: For $E\colon y^2 = x^3 - x$ over $\mathbb{F}_5$, direct count gives $|E(\mathbb{F}_5)| = 8$ (including $O$). Compute $a_5$.
text: For $E\colon y^2 = x^3 - x$ over $\mathbb{F}_5$, direct count gives $|E(\mathbb{F}_5)| = 8$ (including $O$). Compute $a_5$. $a_5 = p + 1 - |E(\mathbb{F}_p)| = 5 + 1 - 8 = -2$. This satisfies $|a_5| = 2 \le 2\sqrt 5 \approx 4.47$, so Hasse is happy.

q: At a split multiplicative prime (a node with $\mathbb{F}_p$-rational tangent slopes), the local $a_p$ is:
text: At a split multiplicative prime (a node with $\mathbb{F}_p$-rational tangent slopes), the local $a_p$ is: Split multiplicative reduction contributes $a_p = +1$: the smooth locus is $\mathbb{G}_m(\mathbb{F}_p)$ of order $p-1$, plus the singular point gives $|\tilde E(\mathbb{F}_p)| = p$, so $a_p = p + 1 - p = 1$. Non-split gives $a_p = -1$; additive gives $a_p = 0$.

q: For $E: y^2 = x^3 - x$ over $\mathbb{F}_7$, direct enumeration gives $|E(\mathbb{F}_7)| = 8$ (including $O$). Compute $a_7$.
text: For $E: y^2 = x^3 - x$ over $\mathbb{F}_7$, direct enumeration gives $|E(\mathbb{F}_7)| = 8$ (including $O$). Compute $a_7$. $a_7 = 7 + 1 - 8 = 0$. A curve with $a_p=0$ is called supersingular at $p$; here $E$ has CM by $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ and $7\equiv 3\pmod{4}$ is inert, forcing $a_7=0$. Use $a_7 = 7 + 1 - |E(\mathbb{F}_7)|$.

q: Hasse's bound $|a_p|\le 2\sqrt{p}$ implies $|E(\mathbb{F}_p)|$ lies in an interval. For $p=101$, which interval is correct?
text: Hasse's bound $|a_p|\le 2\sqrt{p}$ implies $|E(\mathbb{F}_p)|$ lies in an interval. For $p=101$, which interval is correct? $2\sqrt{101}\approx 20.1$, so $|E(\mathbb{F}_{101})|\in[101+1-20,\,101+1+20]=[82,122]$. (Rounding down $2\sqrt{101}<20.1$ confirms both endpoints.) Interval is $[102 - 2\sqrt{101},\ 102 + 2\sqrt{101}]$.

q: At a bad prime, is Hasse's theorem ($|a_p|\le 2\sqrt p$) still valid, and why?
text: At a bad prime, is Hasse's theorem ($|a_p|\le 2\sqrt p$) still valid, and why? Hasse's theorem is a statement about smooth elliptic curves over $\mathbb{F}_p$. At a bad prime the reduction is singular (not an elliptic curve), so Hasse does not apply. Instead, $a_p$ takes the special values $+1$ (split mult.), $-1$ (non-split mult.), or $0$ (additive) by the explicit group-order formulas. Bad reduction still gives $|a_p| \le 1$ from the Neron model.

q: Under the substitution $(x,y) \mapsto (u^2 x,\, u^3 y)$ on a Weierstrass equation, the discriminant transforms as:
text: Under the substitution $(x,y) \mapsto (u^2 x,\, u^3 y)$ on a Weierstrass equation, the discriminant transforms as: The short Weierstrass form transforms $a \to u^{-4} a,\, b \to u^{-6} b$, and $\Delta = -16(4a^3 + 27 b^2)$ picks up $u^{-12}$; when we clear denominators the integer model's discriminant scales by $u^{12}$. This is exactly why minimality asks for the smallest achievable $v_p(\Delta)$ at each prime.

q: For an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$ with multiplicative reduction at $p = 11$ and good reduction at every other prime, the conductor exponent at $11$ is $f_{11} = 1$. Compute the conductor $N(E)$.
text: For an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$ with multiplicative reduction at $p = 11$ and good reduction at every other prime, the conductor exponent at $11$ is $f_{11} = 1$. Compute the conductor $N(E)$. $N(E) = \prod_{p\ \text{bad}} p^{f_p} = 11^1 = 11$. This is the conductor of the curve $y^2 + y = x^3 - x^2$ (Cremona 11a1), the smallest-conductor elliptic curve over $\mathbb{Q}$.

q: At a prime $p \ne 2, 3$ of additive reduction, Ogg's formula gives a conductor exponent of:
text: At a prime $p \ne 2, 3$ of additive reduction, Ogg's formula gives a conductor exponent of: For $p \ne 2, 3$ (tame reduction), additive reduction contributes $f_p = 2$ and multiplicative reduction contributes $f_p = 1$. At $p = 2, 3$ wild ramification in the Galois representation can add a non-negative correction.

q: To check minimality of a Weierstrass model at a prime $p\ne 2,3$, which condition must fail for the model to be non-minimal?
text: To check minimality of a Weierstrass model at a prime $p\ne 2,3$, which condition must fail for the model to be non-minimal? For short Weierstrass $y^2=x^3+ax+b$ and $p\ne 2,3$, the model is non-minimal at $p$ iff we can perform $(x,y)\mapsto(p^2 x, p^3 y)$ to reduce $v_p(\Delta)$ by $12$; this requires $p^4\mid a$ and $p^6\mid b$. If either fails, the model is already minimal at $p$. Non-minimal means there is a scaling reducing the discriminant further.

q: For the non-minimal model $y^2 = x^3 + 16x + 64$ (where $a=16=2^4$, $b=64=2^6$), apply $(x,y)\mapsto(4x,8y)$ to get a minimal model $y^2=x^3+Ax+B$. Compute $A$.
text: For the non-minimal model $y^2 = x^3 + 16x + 64$ (where $a=16=2^4$, $b=64=2^6$), apply $(x,y)\mapsto(4x,8y)$ to get a minimal model $y^2=x^3+Ax+B$. Compute $A$. Under $(x,y)=(u^2 X, u^3 Y)$ with $u=2$: $a\mapsto u^{-4}a=a/16=1$ and $b\mapsto u^{-6}b=b/64=1$, giving the minimal model $Y^2=X^3+X+1$ with $A=1$. Scaling by $(u^2, u^3)$ changes coefficients by $u^4$ and $u^6$.

q: The conductor $N(E)=\prod_p p^{f_p}$ for an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$. Which is the correct proof-step ordering to compute $N(E)$ for $E: y^2+y=x^3-x^2$?
text: The conductor $N(E)=\prod_p p^{f_p}$ for an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$. Which is the correct proof-step ordering to compute $N(E)$ for $E: y^2+y=x^3-x^2$? The standard algorithm: (1) put the curve in (minimal) Weierstrass form, (2) compute the discriminant $\Delta$, (3) identify bad primes as prime divisors of $\Delta$, (4) at each bad prime determine $f_p$ via Ogg's formula ($f_p=1$ for multiplicative, $f_p\ge 2$ for additive), and (5) set $N=\prod p^{f_p}$. Ogg formula: $f_p = v_p(\Delta) - m + 1$ where $m$ is the number of components.

title: Moduli problems and parameters
blurb: A moduli problem asks for a geometric space classifying objects in families.

title: Fine versus coarse moduli
blurb: Universal families may fail; coarse moduli still classify isomorphism classes.

title: Automorphisms obstruct fine moduli
blurb: Nontrivial automorphisms let trivial families be twisted into non-isomorphic families, killing representability of $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$.

title: Level structure and compactification ideas
blurb: Rigidifying data often repairs representability and leads to richer moduli stacks/spaces.

title: Higher-genus moduli and stable curves
blurb: For $g\ge 2$ the coarse space $M_g$ has dimension $3g-3$; Deligne–Mumford compactify by adding stable nodal curves.

q: A moduli problem for elliptic curves over $\mathbb{C}$ is encoded by a functor $\mathcal{M}\colon\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$. What does $\mathcal{M}(S)$ contain?
text: A moduli problem for elliptic curves over $\mathbb{C}$ is encoded by a functor $\mathcal{M}\colon\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$. What does $\mathcal{M}(S)$ contain? The moduli functor sends a base $S$ to iso classes of families $E\to S$ of the objects of interest — here, flat proper families of elliptic curves.

q: Over $\mathbb{C}$, the $j$-invariant induces a bijection between iso classes of elliptic curves and $\mathbb{A}^1(\mathbb{C})=\mathbb{C}$. What is the (complex) dimension of this parameter space?
text: Over $\mathbb{C}$, the $j$-invariant induces a bijection between iso classes of elliptic curves and $\mathbb{A}^1(\mathbb{C})=\mathbb{C}$. What is the (complex) dimension of this parameter space? $\mathbb{A}^1(\mathbb{C})=\mathbb{C}$ has complex dimension $1$, matching $\dim M_{1,1}=1$.

q: For the curve $y^2=x^3+1$ (so $a=0$, $b=1$), compute the discriminant $\Delta=-16(4a^3+27b^2)$ as $a+bi$.
text: For the curve $y^2=x^3+1$ (so $a=0$, $b=1$), compute the discriminant $\Delta=-16(4a^3+27b^2)$ as $a+bi$. $\Delta=-16(0+27)=-16\cdot 27=-432$, which is nonzero, confirming the curve is smooth (hence an elliptic curve).

q: The moduli functor $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ is NOT representable. Which chain of reasoning correctly identifies the first obstruction?
text: The moduli functor $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ is NOT representable. Which chain of reasoning correctly identifies the first obstruction? A representable functor $h_M$ cannot distinguish two families with the same classifying map. Twisting a constant family by a nontrivial $\mathbb{Z}/6$-torsor (at $j=0$) gives an isotrivial but non-trivial family, exposing the failure: $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}\not\cong h_M$ for any scheme $M$. Automorphisms create non-isomorphic families with the same $j$-invariant.

q: Let $E: y^2 = x^3 + x$ (so $a=1$, $b=0$). Compute the discriminant $\Delta = -16(4a^3+27b^2)$.
text: Let $E: y^2 = x^3 + x$ (so $a=1$, $b=0$). Compute the discriminant $\Delta = -16(4a^3+27b^2)$. $\Delta = -16(4\cdot1+0) = -16\cdot4 = -64$. Since $\Delta\ne 0$, the curve is smooth. Compute $-16(4 \cdot 1) = -64$.

q: A family of elliptic curves $\mathcal{E}\to S$ induces a morphism $f:S\to\mathbb{A}^1_j$. If $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ were a fine moduli space with universal family $\mathcal{U}$, one would need $\mathcal{E}\cong f^*\mathcal{U}$. Why does this fail for the family $y^2=x^3+t^2$ over $\mathbb{A}^1_t$?
text: A family of elliptic curves $\mathcal{E}\to S$ induces a morphism $f:S\to\mathbb{A}^1_j$. If $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ were a fine moduli space with universal family $\mathcal{U}$, one would need $\mathcal{E}\cong f^*\mathcal{U}$. Why does this fail for the family $y^2=x^3+t^2$ over $\mathbb{A}^1_t$? For $y^2=x^3+t^2$, scaling $x=u^2 x'$, $y=u^3 y'$, $t=u^3 t'$ shows constant $j=1728$, so $f:\mathbb{A}^1_t\to\mathbb{A}^1_j$ is constant. Yet the family is non-trivial (the twist by $t^2$ cannot be removed over $\mathbb{Z}$), contradicting $\mathcal{E}\cong f^*\mathcal{U}$. Fine moduli means every family comes from a unique classifying map.

q: Which statement characterises a fine moduli space $M$ for $\mathcal{M}$?
text: Which statement characterises a fine moduli space $M$ for $\mathcal{M}$? Fine moduli is exactly representability: $\mathcal{M}\cong h_M$. The identity $\mathrm{id}_M\in h_M(M)$ corresponds to the universal family $\mathcal{E}\to M$.

q: The $j$-line $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ is a moduli space for elliptic curves. Which type?
text: The $j$-line $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ is a moduli space for elliptic curves. Which type? The $j$-line is the coarse moduli space of $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$: geometric points match iso classes bijectively, but no universal family exists because of automorphisms.

q: The obstruction to fine moduli for $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ is the presence of nontrivial automorphisms. Every elliptic curve has at least the $[-1]$ involution. What is the order of the automorphism group of a generic elliptic curve (with $j\ne 0,1728$)?
text: The obstruction to fine moduli for $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ is the presence of nontrivial automorphisms. Every elliptic curve has at least the $[-1]$ involution. What is the order of the automorphism group of a generic elliptic curve (with $j\ne 0,1728$)? For generic $j$, the only nontrivial automorphism is $[-1]:(x,y)\mapsto(x,-y)$, so $|\mathrm{Aut}(E)|=2$.

q: Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a moduli functor with a coarse moduli space $M$. Which combination of properties does $M$ satisfy, but a fine moduli space additionally requires?
text: Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a moduli functor with a coarse moduli space $M$. Which combination of properties does $M$ satisfy, but a fine moduli space additionally requires? A coarse moduli space satisfies (i) the bijection on $\bar{k}$-points and (ii) initiality. Fine moduli additionally demands representability: a universal family over $M$ inducing $\mathcal{F}\cong h_M$ as functors. Coarse moduli universalizes on points but not on families.

q: The curve $y^2=x^3-x$ has $j=1728$. Its automorphism group over $\bar{\mathbb{Q}}$ is cyclic of order $4$, generated by $(x,y)\mapsto(-x,iy)$. How many elements does $\mathrm{Aut}(E)$ have?
text: The curve $y^2=x^3-x$ has $j=1728$. Its automorphism group over $\bar{\mathbb{Q}}$ is cyclic of order $4$, generated by $(x,y)\mapsto(-x,iy)$. How many elements does $\mathrm{Aut}(E)$ have? At $j=1728$ the automorphism group is $\mathbb{Z}/4$, strictly larger than the generic $\mathbb{Z}/2$ but smaller than $\mathbb{Z}/6$ at $j=0$. Order-$4$ automorphism comes from $(x, y) \mapsto (-x, iy)$.

q: Which statement is a correct counterexample showing the $j$-line $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ is NOT a fine moduli space?
text: Which statement is a correct counterexample showing the $j$-line $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ is NOT a fine moduli space? Both families send every point to $j=0$ ($j(y^2=x^3+c)=0$ for any $c\ne 0$), yet twisting by $t$ gives a non-trivial $\mathbb{Z}/6$-torsor. If $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ were fine, the two families would be pulled back from the universal one via the same map and hence isomorphic — contradiction. Twist families over $\mathrm{Spec}\,k[t, t^{-1}]$ break fine representability.

q: Why does the existence of nontrivial automorphisms prevent $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ from being representable?
text: Why does the existence of nontrivial automorphisms prevent $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ from being representable? A representable functor $h_M$ can only see families that pull back from a single classifying map. Twisting by a nontrivial cocycle in $H^1(S,\underline{\mathrm{Aut}})$ produces genuinely different families with identical classifying maps, which $h_M$ cannot distinguish.

q: The curve $y^2=x^3+1$ has $j=0$. Its automorphism group over $\bar{\mathbb{Q}}$ is cyclic of what order? (Hint: it is generated by $(x,y)\mapsto(\zeta_3 x, -y)$.)
text: The curve $y^2=x^3+1$ has $j=0$. Its automorphism group over $\bar{\mathbb{Q}}$ is cyclic of what order? (Hint: it is generated by $(x,y)\mapsto(\zeta_3 x, -y)$.) At $j=0$ the automorphism group is $\mathbb{Z}/6$, generated by the order-6 map $(x,y)\mapsto(\zeta_3 x,-y)$ where $\zeta_3$ is a primitive cube root of unity. This is the maximum automorphism group for an elliptic curve in characteristic $\ne 2,3$.

q: The family $E_2: y^2=x^3+t^6$ over $\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{Q}}\setminus\{0\}$ is a nontrivial twist of $E_1: y^2=x^3+1$. The obstruction class lives in:
text: The family $E_2: y^2=x^3+t^6$ over $\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{Q}}\setminus\{0\}$ is a nontrivial twist of $E_1: y^2=x^3+1$. The obstruction class lives in: Twists of a fixed family are classified by first Čech cohomology with coefficients in the automorphism sheaf. The nontriviality of this $H^1$ is exactly the obstruction to representability.

q: Order the steps in showing that $[-1]\in\mathrm{Aut}(E)$ obstructs representability of $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$:
text: Order the steps in showing that $[-1]\in\mathrm{Aut}(E)$ obstructs representability of $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$: The standard three-step argument: (A) construct the twist, (B) identify $[-1]$ as the monodromy of the torsor, (C) apply Yoneda to conclude the representing object would need to distinguish the two families via the same map — impossible. Nontrivial torsors for $[-1]$ give distinct families with the same invariant.

q: Let $E_0: y^2=x^3+1$ (so $j=0$, $|\mathrm{Aut}|=6$) and $E_1: y^2=x^3-1$. The twist $E_1$ is a non-trivial torsor under $\mathbb{Z}/6$. Over $\mathbb{C}$, $E_0\cong E_1$ because $j(E_0)=j(E_1)=0$. How many distinct isomorphism classes of elliptic curves over $\mathbb{C}$ have $j=0$?
text: Let $E_0: y^2=x^3+1$ (so $j=0$, $|\mathrm{Aut}|=6$) and $E_1: y^2=x^3-1$. The twist $E_1$ is a non-trivial torsor under $\mathbb{Z}/6$. Over $\mathbb{C}$, $E_0\cong E_1$ because $j(E_0)=j(E_1)=0$. How many distinct isomorphism classes of elliptic curves over $\mathbb{C}$ have $j=0$? Over $\mathbb{C}$ the $j$-invariant is a complete isomorphism invariant, so $j=0$ corresponds to exactly one iso class. Twists become isomorphic over $\mathbb{C}$ because $\mathbb{C}^*$ is algebraically closed. The twist corresponds to a square root of a non-square element.

q: Consider a $\mathbb{Z}/2$-torsor $P\to S$ twisted by a class in $H^1(S,\mathbb{Z}/2)$. If $S=\mathbb{A}^1_t\setminus\{0\}$ and the torsor is $\operatorname{Spec}\mathcal{O}_S[u]/(u^2-t)$, this class is:
text: Consider a $\mathbb{Z}/2$-torsor $P\to S$ twisted by a class in $H^1(S,\mathbb{Z}/2)$. If $S=\mathbb{A}^1_t\setminus\{0\}$ and the torsor is $\operatorname{Spec}\mathcal{O}_S[u]/(u^2-t)$, this class is: $\mathcal{O}(\mathbb{A}^1\setminus\{0\})=k[t,t^{-1}]$ does not contain $\sqrt{t}$, so the $\mathbb{Z}/2$-cover $u^2=t$ is a genuinely non-trivial torsor, representing a nontrivial class in $H^1(S,\mathbb{Z}/2)$. Monodromy around the puncture detects the nontrivial torsor.

q: Why is a level-$N$ structure required to satisfy $N\ge 3$ to rigidify $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ into a fine moduli space?
text: Why is a level-$N$ structure required to satisfy $N\ge 3$ to rigidify $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ into a fine moduli space? On $E[2]$, multiplication by $-1$ is the identity because $2P=0$ implies $-P=P$. So $[-1]$ fixes any basis of $E[2]$, i.e. level-$2$ is not rigid enough. For $N\ge 3$, $[-1]\ne 1$ on $E[N]$.

q: For smooth projective curves of genus $g\ge 2$, $\dim M_g=3g-3$. Compute $\dim M_4$.
text: For smooth projective curves of genus $g\ge 2$, $\dim M_g=3g-3$. Compute $\dim M_4$. $3(4)-3=9$. This is the deformation-theoretic count $\dim H^1(C,T_C)$.

q: The Deligne–Mumford compactification $\overline{M}_{g,n}$ adds stable curves. Using $\dim M_{g,n}=3g-3+n$ for $g\ge 2$, compute $\dim M_{2,3}$.
text: The Deligne–Mumford compactification $\overline{M}_{g,n}$ adds stable curves. Using $\dim M_{g,n}=3g-3+n$ for $g\ge 2$, compute $\dim M_{2,3}$. $3(2)-3+3=3+3=6$.

q: Why does adding a level-$N$ structure for $N\ge 3$ rigidify $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$, turning the Deligne–Mumford stack into a representable functor (a scheme)?
text:
Why does adding a level-$N$ structure for $N\ge 3$ rigidify $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$, turning the Deligne–Mumford stack into a representable functor (a scheme)? For $N\ge 3$, $[-1]
e\mathrm{id}$ on $E[N]\cong(\mathbb{Z}/N)^2$: it sends every basis vector $v$ to $-v
e v$ (since $N\ge 3$ means $-1
e 1$ in $\mathbb{Z}/N$). Hence $[-1]$ does not preserve any level-$N$ basis, so the automorphism group of a level-$N$-structured elliptic curve is trivial and the functor becomes representable. Level-$N$ with $N \ge 3$ kills the generic automorphism group.

q: Compute $\dim\overline{M}_{3,2}$ using $\dim M_{g,n}=3g-3+n$.
text: Compute $\dim\overline{M}_{3,2}$ using $\dim M_{g,n}=3g-3+n$. $3(3)-3+2=6+2=8$. Apply $\dim = 3 \cdot 3 - 3 + 2$.

q: The boundary $\overline{M}_g\setminus M_g$ parametrises stable nodal curves. Which type of degeneration corresponds to an irreducible component of the boundary divisor $\Delta_0\subset\overline{M}_g$?
text: The boundary $\overline{M}_g\setminus M_g$ parametrises stable nodal curves. Which type of degeneration corresponds to an irreducible component of the boundary divisor $\Delta_0\subset\overline{M}_g$? $\Delta_0$ consists of irreducible nodal curves: a smooth curve of genus $g-1$ with two points identified to create one node. Boundary divisors $\Delta_h$ for $h\ge 1$ consist of two components of genera $h$ and $g-h$ joined at a node. Irreducible nodal curves sit on the boundary of $\overline{M}_g$.

q: Compute $\dim M_g$ for $g=6$ using the Riemann–Roch formula $\dim M_g=3g-3$.
text: Compute $\dim M_g$ for $g=6$ using the Riemann–Roch formula $\dim M_g=3g-3$. $3(6)-3=15$. This matches $\dim H^1(C,T_C)$, the first-order deformation space of a smooth genus-$6$ curve.

q: A stable $n$-pointed curve $(C, p_1,\dots,p_n)$ of genus $g$ is required to have at-worst-nodal singularities, be proper and connected, and satisfy which finiteness condition?
text: A stable $n$-pointed curve $(C, p_1,\dots,p_n)$ of genus $g$ is required to have at-worst-nodal singularities, be proper and connected, and satisfy which finiteness condition? Stability is exactly finiteness of the automorphism group. Equivalent combinatorial conditions: every smooth rational component has $\ge 3$ special points, every genus-$1$ component has $\ge 1$. This is what cuts out a Deligne–Mumford stack.

q: Which of the following is NOT a stable curve of genus $2$?
text: Which of the following is NOT a stable curve of genus $2$? A smooth $\mathbb{P}^1$ alone has arithmetic genus $0$, not $2$, and also has infinite automorphism group $\mathrm{PGL}_2$ — it fails stability on every count. The other options are standard boundary strata of $\overline{M}_2$ and $\overline{M}_{2,3}$.

q: Order the steps in proving that $\overline{M}_{g,n}$ is a proper Deligne–Mumford stack for $2g-2+n>0$:
text: Order the steps in proving that $\overline{M}_{g,n}$ is a proper Deligne–Mumford stack for $2g-2+n>0$: Deligne–Mumford's argument: first introduce the stable-curve notion (finiteness of $\mathrm{Aut}$), then verify the valuative criterion (semistable reduction theorem provides the extension), and deduce properness from the algebraic stack structure. Geometric invariant theory first, then properness via stable reduction.

q: A stable rational ($g=0$) curve with $n$ marked points exists only for $n\ge 3$ (stability condition $2\cdot0-2+n>0$ and finite $\mathrm{Aut}$). What is the minimum $n$ for which $\overline{M}_{0,n}$ is a non-empty proper scheme?
text: A stable rational ($g=0$) curve with $n$ marked points exists only for $n\ge 3$ (stability condition $2\cdot0-2+n>0$ and finite $\mathrm{Aut}$). What is the minimum $n$ for which $\overline{M}_{0,n}$ is a non-empty proper scheme? For $g=0$, stability requires $\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{P}^1,p_1,\dots,p_n)$ finite, which forces $n\ge 3$. $\overline{M}_{0,3}$ is a single point (three points on $\mathbb{P}^1$ are rigidified by $\mathrm{PGL}_2$), so $n=3$ is the minimum. Automorphism groups become finite with three or more marked points.

q: Which of the following is a stable genus-$1$ curve with $1$ marked point (an element of $\overline{M}_{1,1}$)?
text: Which of the following is a stable genus-$1$ curve with $1$ marked point (an element of $\overline{M}_{1,1}$)? A rational nodal cubic has arithmetic genus $1$ and one node. With a smooth marked point $p$, the automorphism group is finite (it equals $\mathbb{Z}/2$ in many cases), satisfying the stability condition. A $\mathbb{P}^1$ with one point has $\mathrm{Aut}=\mathbb{C}^*$ — infinite, hence not stable. A stable genus-$1$ curve with one marked point is a nodal cubic with a smooth point.

title: Čech cocycles and cohomology
blurb: Cover-based cocycles detect obstruction classes for global patching.

title: Leray acyclic covers and affine vanishing
blurb: When every finite intersection is $\mathcal{F}$-acyclic, Čech cohomology on the cover equals the derived-functor cohomology. Affine covers of schemes are Leray for quasi-coherent sheaves by Serre's affine vanishing theorem.

title: Long exact sequence in cohomology
blurb: A short exact sequence of sheaves induces a long exact sequence of cohomology groups, with a connecting homomorphism $\delta$ that measures obstructions to lifting sections.

title: Derived functor sheaf cohomology
blurb: Right derived functors of global sections define H^i(X,F) abstractly.

title: Picard groups and Serre duality signals
blurb: Line bundles, extensions, and duality theorems are naturally cohomological.

q: Given a cover $\mathcal{U}=\{U_\alpha\}$ of $X$ and a sheaf $\mathcal{F}$, a $1$-cocycle is a family $\{t_{\alpha\beta}\in\mathcal{F}(U_{\alpha\beta})\}$ satisfying which identity on $U_{\alpha\beta\gamma}$?
text: Given a cover $\mathcal{U}=\{U_\alpha\}$ of $X$ and a sheaf $\mathcal{F}$, a $1$-cocycle is a family $\{t_{\alpha\beta}\in\mathcal{F}(U_{\alpha\beta})\}$ satisfying which identity on $U_{\alpha\beta\gamma}$? The alternating Čech differential gives $(d t)_{\alpha\beta\gamma}=t_{\beta\gamma}-t_{\alpha\gamma}+t_{\alpha\beta}$; setting it to zero is the cocycle condition on triple overlaps.

q: Why is $\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U},\mathcal{F})=\mathcal{F}(X)$?
text: Why is $\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U},\mathcal{F})=\mathcal{F}(X)$? A $0$-cochain is $\{s_\alpha\in\mathcal{F}(U_\alpha)\}$; $(ds)_{\alpha\beta}=s_\beta-s_\alpha=0$ forces the $s_\alpha$ to glue by the sheaf axiom, yielding exactly a global section.

q: For the circle $S^1$ with the constant sheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$ and a standard good cover by two overlapping arcs whose intersection has two connected components, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} (\check{H}^1(S^1,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})\otimes\mathbb{Q})$.
text: For the circle $S^1$ with the constant sheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$ and a standard good cover by two overlapping arcs whose intersection has two connected components, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} (\check{H}^1(S^1,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})\otimes\mathbb{Q})$. $\check{H}^1(S^1,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})\cong\mathbb{Z}$ (the winding-number class), so rationally it is $1$-dimensional.

q: For the torus $T^2$ with the constant sheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$, which statement about a cover by two open sets is correct?
text: For the torus $T^2$ with the constant sheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$, which statement about a cover by two open sets is correct? Two contractible open sets $U,V$ covering $T^2$ must have $U\cap V\simeq S^1\sqcup S^1$ (not contractible), so the cover fails the Leray acyclicity condition on intersections. This is precisely why a good cover (all intersections contractible) is required for Čech to recover sheaf cohomology. A two-set cover cannot capture $H^2(T^2) = \mathbb{Z}$ but can catch $H^1$.

q: For the cover of $S^2$ by two contractible open hemispheres $U,V$ with $U\cap V\simeq S^1$, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}}\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U},\underline{\mathbb{Q}})$ directly from the Čech complex $\check{C}^0\to\check{C}^1$.
text: For the cover of $S^2$ by two contractible open hemispheres $U,V$ with $U\cap V\simeq S^1$, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}}\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U},\underline{\mathbb{Q}})$ directly from the Čech complex $\check{C}^0\to\check{C}^1$. $\check{C}^0=\underline{\mathbb{Q}}(U)\oplus\underline{\mathbb{Q}}(V)\cong\mathbb{Q}^2$ and $\check{C}^1=\underline{\mathbb{Q}}(U\cap V)$. Since $U\cap V\simeq S^1$, $H^0(U\cap V,\underline{\mathbb{Q}})\cong\mathbb{Q}$. The coboundary $d\colon\mathbb{Q}^2\to\mathbb{Q}$, $(a,b)\mapsto b-a$, is surjective with kernel $\mathbb{Q}$, giving $\ker/\mathrm{im}=0$. So $\check{H}^1=0$, consistent with $H^1(S^2)=0$. Use $H^1(S^1) = \mathbb{Q}$ for the overlap.

q: Which is the correct order of steps to verify that a Čech cocycle $\{t_{\alpha\beta}\}$ is a coboundary?
text: Which is the correct order of steps to verify that a Čech cocycle $\{t_{\alpha\beta}\}$ is a coboundary? A $1$-cochain $\{t_{\alpha\beta}\}$ is a coboundary iff there exist local sections $s_\alpha\in\mathcal{F}(U_\alpha)$ with $t_{\alpha\beta}=s_\beta|_{U_{\alpha\beta}}-s_\alpha|_{U_{\alpha\beta}}$. Exhibiting explicit $s_\alpha$ is the direct verification; the cocycle condition is necessary but not sufficient. Trivialize on a refinement to exhibit the coboundary.

q: A cover $\mathcal{U} = \{U_\alpha\}$ of $X$ is Leray for a sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ when:
text: A cover $\mathcal{U} = \{U_\alpha\}$ of $X$ is Leray for a sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ when: The Leray condition asks that $\mathcal{F}$ be acyclic on every finite intersection of the cover. When satisfied, the canonical map $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U},\mathcal{F}) \to H^p(X,\mathcal{F})$ is an isomorphism for every $p$.

q: Which of the following guarantees that an affine open cover of a scheme is Leray for any quasi-coherent sheaf?
text: Which of the following guarantees that an affine open cover of a scheme is Leray for any quasi-coherent sheaf? Serre proved that affine schemes have no higher quasi-coherent cohomology, so every finite intersection of affine opens (also affine when the scheme is separated) is $\mathcal{F}$-acyclic. Hence an affine open cover is Leray.

q: The standard affine cover of $\mathbb{P}^2$ has three charts $U_0,U_1,U_2$. How many terms does the resulting Čech complex $\check C^0 \to \check C^1 \to \check C^2$ have in $\check C^1$?
text: The standard affine cover of $\mathbb{P}^2$ has three charts $U_0,U_1,U_2$. How many terms does the resulting Čech complex $\check C^0 \to \check C^1 \to \check C^2$ have in $\check C^1$? $\check C^1$ indexes strictly-increasing pairs $(\alpha_0,\alpha_1)$ from $\{0,1,2\}$, so its rank is $\binom{3}{2} = 3$. (Triple intersections contribute $\binom{3}{3}=1$ term in $\check C^2$.)

q: Let $X=\mathbb{P}^1_k$ with affine cover $U_0=\{x_0\ne 0\}$, $U_1=\{x_1\ne 0\}$, and $\mathcal{F}=\mathcal{O}(-3)$. Which statement correctly explains why this cover is Leray for $\mathcal{O}(-3)$?
text: Let $X=\mathbb{P}^1_k$ with affine cover $U_0=\{x_0\ne 0\}$, $U_1=\{x_1\ne 0\}$, and $\mathcal{F}=\mathcal{O}(-3)$. Which statement correctly explains why this cover is Leray for $\mathcal{O}(-3)$? Each chart and their intersection are affine schemes. Serre's vanishing theorem for quasi-coherent sheaves on affine schemes ensures $H^i>0$ vanishes on each piece, making the cover Leray and the Čech computation exact. On affine opens, quasi-coherent cohomology vanishes in positive degree.

q: Using the Leray cover $\{U_0,U_1\}$ of $\mathbb{P}^2$ (standard affine cover by three charts), the Čech complex for a quasi-coherent sheaf has terms in degrees $0,1,2$. How many terms does $\check{C}^2$ have?
text: Using the Leray cover $\{U_0,U_1\}$ of $\mathbb{P}^2$ (standard affine cover by three charts), the Čech complex for a quasi-coherent sheaf has terms in degrees $0,1,2$. How many terms does $\check{C}^2$ have? $\check{C}^2$ indexes strictly-increasing triples from $\{0,1,2\}$: only $(0,1,2)$, giving $\binom{3}{3}=1$ term. Check that triple intersections are affine.

q: If $\mathcal{U}$ is a Leray cover for $\mathcal{F}$ but NOT for $\mathcal{G}$, and there is a map $\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{G}$, which conclusion is valid?
text: If $\mathcal{U}$ is a Leray cover for $\mathcal{F}$ but NOT for $\mathcal{G}$, and there is a map $\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{G}$, which conclusion is valid? The Leray isomorphism $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U},\mathcal{F})\cong H^p(X,\mathcal{F})$ applies sheaf-by-sheaf; $\mathcal{F}$ satisfies the hypothesis but $\mathcal{G}$ does not, so the Čech-to-derived comparison holds for $\mathcal{F}$ and may fail for $\mathcal{G}$. Spectral sequence mismatch breaks Leray calculation for $\mathcal{G}$.

q: A short exact sequence of sheaves $0\to\mathcal{F}'\to\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{F}''\to 0$ produces a long exact sequence whose connecting homomorphism $\delta$ goes in which direction?
text: A short exact sequence of sheaves $0\to\mathcal{F}'\to\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{F}''\to 0$ produces a long exact sequence whose connecting homomorphism $\delta$ goes in which direction? The connecting map raises degree by one and jumps from the quotient sheaf back to the subsheaf: $\delta\colon H^p(\mathcal{F}'') \to H^{p+1}(\mathcal{F}')$. In Čech language it lifts a local section of $\mathcal{F}''$ to one of $\mathcal{F}$; the failure of the lifts to glue is a class in $H^{p+1}(\mathcal{F}')$.

q: In the exponential sequence $0 \to \underline{\mathbb{Z}} \to \mathcal{O} \to \mathcal{O}^\times \to 0$ on $\mathbb{C}^\times$, what does $\delta\colon H^0(\mathcal{O}^\times) \to H^1(\underline{\mathbb{Z}})$ send the global section $z$ to?
text: In the exponential sequence $0 \to \underline{\mathbb{Z}} \to \mathcal{O} \to \mathcal{O}^\times \to 0$ on $\mathbb{C}^\times$, what does $\delta\colon H^0(\mathcal{O}^\times) \to H^1(\underline{\mathbb{Z}})$ send the global section $z$ to? On $\mathbb{C}^\times$ the global section $z$ has no single-valued logarithm, so its image under $\delta$ is non-trivial. Concretely the class is $1\in H^1(\mathbb{C}^\times,\underline{\mathbb{Z}}) = \mathbb{Z}$, the winding-number generator.

q: From $0\to\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}\to\mathcal{O}_p\to 0$ (skyscraper at a point) and the vanishing $H^i(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}(-1))=0$ for all $i$, deduce $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}_p)$.
text: From $0\to\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}\to\mathcal{O}_p\to 0$ (skyscraper at a point) and the vanishing $H^i(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}(-1))=0$ for all $i$, deduce $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}_p)$. The sandwiching $H^i(\mathcal{O}(-1)) = 0$ forces $H^0(\mathcal{O}) \xrightarrow{\sim} H^0(\mathcal{O}_p)$. Since $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}) = 1$, we get $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}_p) = 1$ — the stalk at $p$.

q: From the exponential sequence $0\to\underline{\mathbb{Z}}\to\mathcal{O}_X\to\mathcal{O}_X^\times\to 0$ on a compact Riemann surface $X$, the connecting map $\delta\colon H^1(X,\mathcal{O}_X^\times)\to H^2(X,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})$ sends a line bundle $L$ to which invariant?
text: From the exponential sequence $0\to\underline{\mathbb{Z}}\to\mathcal{O}_X\to\mathcal{O}_X^\times\to 0$ on a compact Riemann surface $X$, the connecting map $\delta\colon H^1(X,\mathcal{O}_X^\times)\to H^2(X,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})$ sends a line bundle $L$ to which invariant? The connecting homomorphism $\delta$ in the long exact sequence of the exponential sequence sends a holomorphic line bundle (class in $H^1(\mathcal{O}^\times)=\mathrm{Pic}(X)$) to its first Chern class in $H^2(X,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})\cong\mathbb{Z}$, which equals the degree of the line bundle. Extract $H^1(X, \mathbb{Z}) \to H^1(X, \mathcal{O}) \to H^1(X, \mathcal{O}^\times) \to H^2(X, \mathbb{Z})$.

q: On $\mathbb{P}^1$, apply the long exact sequence of $0\to\mathcal{O}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}^{\oplus 2}\to\mathcal{O}(1)\to 0$ (Euler sequence twisted). Given $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O})=1$, $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, $\dim H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, compute $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(1))$.
text: On $\mathbb{P}^1$, apply the long exact sequence of $0\to\mathcal{O}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}^{\oplus 2}\to\mathcal{O}(1)\to 0$ (Euler sequence twisted). Given $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O})=1$, $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, $\dim H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, compute $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(1))$. From the long exact sequence: $0\to H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))\to H^0(\mathcal{O})^2\to H^0(\mathcal{O}(1))\to H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))\to\cdots$. Since the flanking terms vanish, the middle map is an isomorphism followed by a surjection: $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(1))=2\cdot 1=2$. Apply $H^0$ and use the Euler exact sequence.

q: Which is the correct order of steps to construct the connecting homomorphism $\delta\colon H^p(X,\mathcal{F}'')\to H^{p+1}(X,\mathcal{F}')$ from a SES $0\to\mathcal{F}'\to\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{F}''\to 0$?
text: Which is the correct order of steps to construct the connecting homomorphism $\delta\colon H^p(X,\mathcal{F}'')\to H^{p+1}(X,\mathcal{F}')$ from a SES $0\to\mathcal{F}'\to\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{F}''\to 0$? The standard construction: embed each sheaf in an injective sheaf, extend to a SES of injective resolutions (which splits in each degree by injectivity of $I'^p$), apply $\Gamma$, and apply the snake lemma to the resulting SES of cochain complexes. The resulting boundary map on cohomology is $\delta$. Zig-zag in the snake lemma gives $\delta$.

q: Which property of the global-sections functor $\Gamma(X,-)$ is measured by the derived functors $R^i\Gamma$?
text: Which property of the global-sections functor $\Gamma(X,-)$ is measured by the derived functors $R^i\Gamma$? $\Gamma(X,-)$ is left exact but not right exact; $R^i\Gamma$ measures exactly the right-exactness defect via a long exact sequence.

q: Which class of sheaves is NOT automatically $\Gamma$-acyclic?
text: Which class of sheaves is NOT automatically $\Gamma$-acyclic? Injective, flabby, and soft (on paracompact Hausdorff) sheaves are all $\Gamma$-acyclic. A locally constant sheaf like $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$ on $S^1$ has nonzero $H^1$, so it is not acyclic in general.

q: On $\mathbb{P}^1_k$ the line bundle $\mathcal{O}(-5)$ has $h^0=0$. Using $\dim_k H^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(n))=\max(-n-1,0)$, compute $\dim_k H^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(-5))$.
text: On $\mathbb{P}^1_k$ the line bundle $\mathcal{O}(-5)$ has $h^0=0$. Using $\dim_k H^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(n))=\max(-n-1,0)$, compute $\dim_k H^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(-5))$. $\max(-(-5)-1,0)=\max(4,0)=4$.

q: Which of these sheaves on $S^1$ is NOT $\Gamma$-acyclic, and why?
text: Which of these sheaves on $S^1$ is NOT $\Gamma$-acyclic, and why? The constant sheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$ on $S^1$ has $H^1(S^1,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})\cong\mathbb{Z}$, witnessing the winding-number class. Flabby and injective sheaves are acyclic by construction; skyscraper sheaves at a point have $H^i=0$ for $i>0$ since points are acyclic. Constant sheaves on $S^1$ have nontrivial $H^1$ detecting the loop.

q: For $\mathbb{P}^2_k$, the formula $\dim H^1(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O}(n))=0$ for all $n$. Using this and the exact sequence $0\to\mathcal{O}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}\to\mathcal{O}_H\to 0$ (restriction to a hyperplane $H\cong\mathbb{P}^1$), compute $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O})$ given $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O})=1$.
text: For $\mathbb{P}^2_k$, the formula $\dim H^1(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O}(n))=0$ for all $n$. Using this and the exact sequence $0\to\mathcal{O}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}\to\mathcal{O}_H\to 0$ (restriction to a hyperplane $H\cong\mathbb{P}^1$), compute $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O})$ given $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O})=1$. The long exact sequence gives $0\to H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))\to H^0(\mathcal{O})\to H^0(\mathcal{O}_H)\to H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))\to\cdots$. Since $H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$ and $H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, we get $H^0(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O})\cong H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O})=1$. Combine short exact sequence with vanishing of $H^1(\mathcal{O}(n))$.

q: Why does the global-sections functor $\Gamma(X,-)$ fail to be right exact on $\mathbf{Ab}(X)$ (abelian sheaves on $X$)?
text: Why does the global-sections functor $\Gamma(X,-)$ fail to be right exact on $\mathbf{Ab}(X)$ (abelian sheaves on $X$)? A morphism of sheaves is surjective iff it is surjective on stalks. Global sections need not be surjective: the exponential $\mathcal{O}\to\mathcal{O}^\times$ is locally surjective (every holomorphic function has local logarithms), but $z\in H^0(\mathbb{C}^\times,\mathcal{O}^\times)$ has no global logarithm, so $H^0(\mathcal{O})\to H^0(\mathcal{O}^\times)$ is not surjective. Sections functor is only left exact without injective resolutions.

q: The Picard group of a ringed space $(X,\mathcal{O}_X)$ is canonically isomorphic to which Čech cohomology group?
text: The Picard group of a ringed space $(X,\mathcal{O}_X)$ is canonically isomorphic to which Čech cohomology group? Line bundles are classified by their transition cocycles in $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ modulo coboundaries: $\mathrm{Pic}(X)\cong\check{H}^1(X,\mathcal{O}_X^\times)$.

q: On $\mathbb{P}^1$ with $\omega_{\mathbb{P}^1}=\mathcal{O}(-2)$, Serre duality gives $\dim H^1(\mathcal{O}(-7))=\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(7-2))$. Compute this common dimension.
text: On $\mathbb{P}^1$ with $\omega_{\mathbb{P}^1}=\mathcal{O}(-2)$, Serre duality gives $\dim H^1(\mathcal{O}(-7))=\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(7-2))$. Compute this common dimension. $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(5))=5+1=6$, and $\max(-(-7)-1,0)=6$, so the duality check holds.

q: Use Riemann–Roch on a smooth projective curve of genus $g=4$ together with Serre duality to compute $\dim H^0(C,L)-\dim H^0(C,\omega_C\otimes L^{-1})$ for a line bundle $L$ of degree $d=10$.
text: Use Riemann–Roch on a smooth projective curve of genus $g=4$ together with Serre duality to compute $\dim H^0(C,L)-\dim H^0(C,\omega_C\otimes L^{-1})$ for a line bundle $L$ of degree $d=10$. Riemann–Roch: $h^0(L)-h^1(L)=d-g+1=10-4+1=7$, and Serre duality identifies $h^1(L)=h^0(\omega_C\otimes L^{-1})$.

q: On an elliptic curve $E$ (genus $1$, $\omega_E\cong\mathcal{O}_E$), Serre duality gives $H^1(E,L)\cong H^0(E,L^{-1})^\vee$. For a degree-$3$ line bundle $L$, which statement is correct?
text: On an elliptic curve $E$ (genus $1$, $\omega_E\cong\mathcal{O}_E$), Serre duality gives $H^1(E,L)\cong H^0(E,L^{-1})^\vee$. For a degree-$3$ line bundle $L$, which statement is correct? Serre duality: $H^1(E,L)\cong H^0(E,\omega_E\otimes L^{-1})^\vee = H^0(E,L^{-1})^\vee$. Since $L^{-1}$ has degree $-3<0$, $H^0(E,L^{-1})=0$, so $\dim H^1(E,L)=0$. Then Riemann–Roch gives $\dim H^0(E,L)=\deg L -g+1=3$. Serre duality gives $H^1 \cong H^0$-dual for self-dual canonical.

q: For the projective line $\mathbb{P}^1$ and the line bundle $\mathcal{O}(n)$, Serre duality says $h^1(\mathcal{O}(n))=h^0(\mathcal{O}(-n-2))$. Compute $h^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(-4))$ using this duality.
text: For the projective line $\mathbb{P}^1$ and the line bundle $\mathcal{O}(n)$, Serre duality says $h^1(\mathcal{O}(n))=h^0(\mathcal{O}(-n-2))$. Compute $h^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(-4))$ using this duality. $h^1(\mathcal{O}(-4))=h^0(\mathcal{O}(-(-4)-2))=h^0(\mathcal{O}(2))=2+1=3$. Compute $h^1(\mathcal{O}(n)) = h^0(\mathcal{O}(-n-2))$.

q: The Picard group $\mathrm{Pic}(\mathbb{P}^1_k)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ is generated by $\mathcal{O}(1)$. The exponential sequence on a smooth projective curve $C$ over $\mathbb{C}$ gives an exact sequence involving $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$. Which statement correctly identifies $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$?
text: The Picard group $\mathrm{Pic}(\mathbb{P}^1_k)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ is generated by $\mathcal{O}(1)$. The exponential sequence on a smooth projective curve $C$ over $\mathbb{C}$ gives an exact sequence involving $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$. Which statement correctly identifies $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$? From the exponential sequence, the degree-zero line bundles form $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)=\ker(c_1)$. The long exact sequence gives $H^1(C,\mathcal{O})\to H^1(C,\mathcal{O}^\times)\xrightarrow{c_1} H^2(C,\mathbb{Z})$, and $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)\cong H^1(C,\mathcal{O})/\mathrm{image}(H^1(C,\underline{\mathbb{Z}}))$, a $g$-dimensional complex torus — the Jacobian of $C$. Pic of $\mathbb{P}^1$ is detected by $H^1$ of structure sheaf cocycles.

title: Groupoids, fibered categories, descent
blurb: Stacks encode objects with automorphisms while preserving gluing across covers.

title: Classifying stacks BG
blurb: BG packages principal G-bundles functorially and acts as a universal quotient object.

title: Quotient stack [X/G]
blurb: The stack [X/G] records a G-action on X while remembering stabilisers; BG is the case X = point.

title: Deligne–Mumford vs. Artin stacks
blurb: Dropping 'étale atlas' to 'smooth atlas' enlarges DM stacks to Artin stacks, accommodating positive-dimensional automorphisms.

title: Deligne–Mumford stacks and coarse spaces
blurb: DM stacks model orbifold-like moduli with étale atlases and coarse moduli maps.

q: Why must a moduli functor $\mathcal{M}:\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$ for elliptic curves fail to be representable?
text: Why must a moduli functor $\mathcal{M}:\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$ for elliptic curves fail to be representable? Every elliptic curve has the automorphism $[-1]$; twisting a family by this automorphism along a cover produces a non-isomorphic family with the same classifying map to any putative fine moduli scheme. Passing to groupoid-valued functors is precisely what records this data.

q: A fibered category $\mathcal{X}\to\mathrm{Sch}$ is a stack (in the étale topology) iff for every cover $\{U_i\to U\}$:
text: A fibered category $\mathcal{X}\to\mathrm{Sch}$ is a stack (in the étale topology) iff for every cover $\{U_i\to U\}$: The stack axiom has two layers: isomorphism sheaves must glue locally, and objects given by compatible local data (a descent datum satisfying the cocycle on triple overlaps) must glue to a global object unique up to unique isomorphism.

q: Let $G$ be a groupoid with three objects, each having automorphism group $\mathbb{Z}/2$, all connected by isomorphisms. How many isomorphism classes does $\pi_0(G)$ have?
text: Let $G$ be a groupoid with three objects, each having automorphism group $\mathbb{Z}/2$, all connected by isomorphisms. How many isomorphism classes does $\pi_0(G)$ have? All three objects are connected by isomorphisms, so they form a single isomorphism class: $\pi_0(G)$ has one element. The three $\mathbb{Z}/2$ automorphism groups are what is lost when one collapses to $\pi_0$.

q: A fibered category $p:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{C}$ satisfies descent for a cover $\{U_i\to U\}$ but the isomorphism-sheaf condition fails for some pair of objects. Which axiom of a stack does this violate?
text: A fibered category $p:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{C}$ satisfies descent for a cover $\{U_i\to U\}$ but the isomorphism-sheaf condition fails for some pair of objects. Which axiom of a stack does this violate? A stack requires two conditions: (1) $\mathrm{Isom}(x,y)$ is a sheaf (morphism sheaf axiom) and (2) descent data for objects glue. Failure of the Isom sheaf condition violates the first axiom while leaving the second intact. Descent is effective only if isomorphism sheaves are actually sheaves.

q: Which of these is a counterexample showing that the naive moduli set-functor $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}^{\mathrm{naive}}:\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$ (assigning iso-classes of elliptic curves) is NOT a sheaf in the étale topology?
text: Which of these is a counterexample showing that the naive moduli set-functor $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}^{\mathrm{naive}}:\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$ (assigning iso-classes of elliptic curves) is NOT a sheaf in the étale topology? Twisting a constant family by a non-trivial $\mu_2$-torsor (via the $[-1]$ automorphism) over an étale cover produces an isotrivial but non-trivial family. The cocycle data exist, but they glue to different isomorphism-class representatives depending on the chosen descent isomorphisms — violating sheafness. Automorphisms collapse objects with $j$-invariant $0$ or $1728$.

q: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be the groupoid with objects $\{A,B,C\}$, morphisms forming a single isomorphism class, and $\mathrm{Aut}(A)=\{e\}$. After passing to $\pi_0$, how many objects remain?
text: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be the groupoid with objects $\{A,B,C\}$, morphisms forming a single isomorphism class, and $\mathrm{Aut}(A)=\{e\}$. After passing to $\pi_0$, how many objects remain? All three objects are isomorphic, so they represent a single isomorphism class. $|\pi_0(\mathcal{C})|=1$. The trivial automorphism groups are irrelevant to $\pi_0$. Automorphism groups give isotropy, not multiplicity.

q: The classifying stack $BG$ is defined by $BG(R)=$ groupoid of $G$-torsors on $\mathrm{Spec}\,R$. Why can $BG$ not be a scheme?
text: The classifying stack $BG$ is defined by $BG(R)=$ groupoid of $G$-torsors on $\mathrm{Spec}\,R$. Why can $BG$ not be a scheme?
