A scheme's functor of points lands in sets, so its geometric points have trivial automorphism groups. $BG$ has automorphism group $G$ at its trivial torsor, which records pure automorphism data and prevents representability by any scheme.

q: How many isomorphism classes of $\mathbb{Z}/3$-torsors are there on $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q$ for a finite field $\mathbb{F}_q$? (Equivalently, $|H^1(\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q,\mathbb{Z}/3)|$, using that the absolute Galois group of $\mathbb{F}_q$ is $\widehat{\mathbb{Z}}$ and $\mathrm{Hom}(\widehat{\mathbb{Z}},\mathbb{Z}/3)=\mathbb{Z}/3$.)
text: How many isomorphism classes of $\mathbb{Z}/3$-torsors are there on $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q$ for a finite field $\mathbb{F}_q$? (Equivalently, $|H^1(\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q,\mathbb{Z}/3)|$, using that the absolute Galois group of $\mathbb{F}_q$ is $\widehat{\mathbb{Z}}$ and $\mathrm{Hom}(\widehat{\mathbb{Z}},\mathbb{Z}/3)=\mathbb{Z}/3$.) $\mathrm{Hom}_{\text{cont}}(\widehat{\mathbb{Z}},\mathbb{Z}/3)\cong \mathbb{Z}/3$ has 3 elements, one for each element of $\mathbb{Z}/3$. Hence $|\pi_0(B(\mathbb{Z}/3)(\mathbb{F}_q))|=3$.

q: For $G=\mathbb{Z}/2$ over $\mathbb{R}$: $\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R})=\mathbb{Z}/2$, so $|\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)|=?$ (this counts iso classes of $\mathbb{Z}/2$-torsors over $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{R}$).
text: For $G=\mathbb{Z}/2$ over $\mathbb{R}$: $\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R})=\mathbb{Z}/2$, so $|\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)|=?$ (this counts iso classes of $\mathbb{Z}/2$-torsors over $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{R}$). $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)=\{0,\mathrm{id}\}$ has 2 elements: the trivial torsor and the non-trivial one corresponding to $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{C}\to\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{R}$.

q: For any algebraic group $G$, the inertia stack $I(BG)=BG\times_{BG\times BG}BG$ is equivalent to which stack?
text: For any algebraic group $G$, the inertia stack $I(BG)=BG\times_{BG\times BG}BG$ is equivalent to which stack? The inertia stack of $BG$ parametrises pairs (torsor, automorphism), which is the same data as a $G$-torsor together with a section of the associated group scheme — i.e., an element of $G$ up to conjugation. This is exactly $[G/G]$ for the conjugation action. The inertia stack parameterizes objects with an automorphism.

q: For $G=\mathbb{Z}/5$ over an algebraically closed field $k$ with $\mathrm{char}(k)\ne 5$, how many isomorphism classes of $G$-torsors are there over $\mathrm{Spec}\,k$?
text: For $G=\mathbb{Z}/5$ over an algebraically closed field $k$ with $\mathrm{char}(k)\ne 5$, how many isomorphism classes of $G$-torsors are there over $\mathrm{Spec}\,k$? Over an algebraically closed field, $H^1_{\text{ét}}(\mathrm{Spec}\,k,G)=0$ for any finite group $G$ of order coprime to the characteristic. So there is exactly $1$ isomorphism class: the trivial torsor. Use $|H^1(\mathrm{Spec}\,k, \mathbb{Z}/5)| = 1$.

q: The correct chain of implications among stack conditions is:
text: The correct chain of implications among stack conditions is: Every scheme is an algebraic space (trivial groupoid structure); every algebraic space is a DM stack (étale atlas, trivial automorphisms); every DM stack is an Artin stack (étale implies smooth). The reverse implications all fail: $B\mathbb{G}_m$ is Artin but not DM; $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_2]$ is DM but not an algebraic space. Separated implies quasi-separated; Artin is weaker than Deligne-Mumford.

q: A $T$-point of the quotient stack $[X/G]$ is:
text: A $T$-point of the quotient stack $[X/G]$ is: By definition $[X/G](T)$ is the groupoid of pairs $(P\to T,\ \phi:P\to X)$ with $P$ a $G$-torsor and $\phi$ equivariant. Setting $X=\mathrm{pt}$ recovers $BG$; setting $G=\{e\}$ recovers $X$ itself.

q: When does $[X/G]$ coincide (as stacks) with the quotient scheme $X/G$?
text: When does $[X/G]$ coincide (as stacks) with the quotient scheme $X/G$? If the action is free, every stabiliser is trivial and no automorphism data needs to be remembered, so $[X/G]$ is the ordinary quotient $X/G$. Non-free actions create non-trivial stabilisers and force stacky structure.

q: For $G=\mu_3$ acting on $\mathbb{A}^1$ by $\zeta\cdot x = \zeta x$, the stabiliser at $x=0$ is all of $\mu_3$ and is trivial elsewhere. What is the order of the automorphism group of the origin in $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_3]$?
text: For $G=\mu_3$ acting on $\mathbb{A}^1$ by $\zeta\cdot x = \zeta x$, the stabiliser at $x=0$ is all of $\mu_3$ and is trivial elsewhere. What is the order of the automorphism group of the origin in $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_3]$? $\mathrm{Stab}_{\mu_3}(0)=\mu_3$ has order $3$. Geometrically this is the stacky enhancement at the fixed point; away from $0$ the quotient is an ordinary scheme.

q: Let $G=\mathbb{G}_m$ act on $X=\mathbb{A}^2\setminus\{0\}$ by $\lambda\cdot(x,y)=(\lambda x,\lambda y)$. The quotient stack $[X/G]$ is equivalent to which classical geometric object?
text: Let $G=\mathbb{G}_m$ act on $X=\mathbb{A}^2\setminus\{0\}$ by $\lambda\cdot(x,y)=(\lambda x,\lambda y)$. The quotient stack $[X/G]$ is equivalent to which classical geometric object? $\mathbb{G}_m$ acts freely on $\mathbb{A}^2\setminus\{0\}$ (no non-trivial stabilisers), so $[X/G]$ is an algebraic space and in fact coincides with the ordinary quotient scheme $\mathbb{P}^1$. The scheme-theoretic quotient is $\mathbb{P}^1$; stacky data is trivial here.

q: A $T$-point of $[\mathrm{pt}/G]=BG$ is a $G$-torsor on $T$. What is the correct chain of equivalences proving $[\mathrm{pt}/G]=BG$?
text: A $T$-point of $[\mathrm{pt}/G]=BG$ is a $G$-torsor on $T$. What is the correct chain of equivalences proving $[\mathrm{pt}/G]=BG$? A $T$-point of $[X/G]$ is $(P\to T, \phi:P\to X)$ with $P$ a $G$-torsor and $\phi$ equivariant. For $X=\mathrm{pt}$, $\phi$ is forced to be the unique map $P\to\mathrm{pt}$, so the data reduces to just the $G$-torsor $P\to T$. Hence $[\mathrm{pt}/G](T)\simeq BG(T)$ for all $T$. Unwind definitions: principal bundle over $T$ is a $G$-torsor.

q: For $G=\mu_2$ acting on $\mathbb{A}^1$ by $\zeta\cdot x=-x$, the coarse moduli space of $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_2]$ is $\mathbb{A}^1$ (via $x\mapsto x^2$). At the stacky point $x=0$, the automorphism group has order?
text: For $G=\mu_2$ acting on $\mathbb{A}^1$ by $\zeta\cdot x=-x$, the coarse moduli space of $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_2]$ is $\mathbb{A}^1$ (via $x\mapsto x^2$). At the stacky point $x=0$, the automorphism group has order? The stabiliser of $0$ under the $\mu_2$ action $x\mapsto -x$ is all of $\mu_2$, which has order $2$. Away from $0$ the stabiliser is trivial. Inertia at the origin is $\mu_2$; elsewhere trivial.

q: Which condition distinguishes a Deligne-Mumford stack from a general Artin stack?
text: Which condition distinguishes a Deligne-Mumford stack from a general Artin stack? Artin requires a smooth atlas and a representable diagonal; DM additionally requires the atlas to be étale, equivalently the diagonal to be unramified. This forces automorphism schemes to be $0$-dimensional (finite and unramified).

q: Which of the following stacks is Artin but NOT Deligne-Mumford?
text: Which of the following stacks is Artin but NOT Deligne-Mumford? $B\mathbb{G}_m$ has automorphism group $\mathbb{G}_m$, which is $1$-dimensional — so it is Artin but not DM. $B\mu_n$ and $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_3]$ have finite automorphism groups, and $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ has finite automorphisms away from char $2,3$.

q: What is the dimension of the automorphism group at the generic geometric point of $B\mathbb{G}_m$?
text: What is the dimension of the automorphism group at the generic geometric point of $B\mathbb{G}_m$? $B\mathbb{G}_m$ has automorphism group $\mathbb{G}_m$ (the multiplicative group) at every geometric point, which is $1$-dimensional. That positive dimension is exactly why $B\mathbb{G}_m$ fails to be DM.

q: Which is the correct order of steps in proving that an Artin stack $\mathcal{X}$ with an étale atlas is Deligne-Mumford?
text: Which is the correct order of steps in proving that an Artin stack $\mathcal{X}$ with an étale atlas is Deligne-Mumford? The standard proof: (1) representability of the diagonal is required for any Artin stack; (2) if the diagonal is also unramified, automorphism groups are finite and unramified (0-dimensional); (3) this is equivalent to the existence of an étale atlas, the defining condition of DM. Find an atlas, then check fibers are etale.

q: Which is a counterexample showing that $B\mathbb{G}_m$ is not Deligne-Mumford?
text: Which is a counterexample showing that $B\mathbb{G}_m$ is not Deligne-Mumford? A stack is DM iff its diagonal is unramified (equivalently, automorphism schemes are 0-dimensional). Since $\mathbb{G}_m$ is 1-dimensional, the inertia stack $I(B\mathbb{G}_m)\cong[\mathbb{G}_m/\mathbb{G}_m]$ has positive-dimensional fibers, so $B\mathbb{G}_m$ is Artin but not DM. The inertia group $\mathbb{G}_m$ is positive-dimensional, failing DM.

q: An Artin stack $\mathcal{X}$ has a smooth atlas $U\to\mathcal{X}$ with $U$ a scheme of dimension $3$ and the fibers of the atlas map have dimension $1$. What is $\dim(\mathcal{X})$?
text: An Artin stack $\mathcal{X}$ has a smooth atlas $U\to\mathcal{X}$ with $U$ a scheme of dimension $3$ and the fibers of the atlas map have dimension $1$. What is $\dim(\mathcal{X})$? For a smooth atlas $U\to\mathcal{X}$ of relative dimension $d$, $\dim(\mathcal{X})=\dim(U)-d=3-1=2$. Here the fiber dimension $1$ corresponds to the $1$-dimensional automorphism group typical of an Artin (non-DM) stack. Dimension of the stack is atlas dimension minus fiber dimension.

q: Which condition separates a Deligne-Mumford stack from a general Artin (algebraic) stack?
text: Which condition separates a Deligne-Mumford stack from a general Artin (algebraic) stack? The defining upgrade from Artin to DM is the étale (versus merely smooth) atlas, which forces automorphism schemes to be finite and unramified. $BG$ for $G$ finite is DM; $BG$ for $G$ positive-dimensional (e.g. $B\mathbb{G}_m$) is Artin but not DM.

q: Keel-Mori produces a coarse moduli map $\pi:\mathcal{X}\to X$ for a finite-type DM stack with finite inertia. Which statement about $\pi$ is true?
text: Keel-Mori produces a coarse moduli map $\pi:\mathcal{X}\to X$ for a finite-type DM stack with finite inertia. Which statement about $\pi$ is true? $\pi$ is a bijection on geometric points and any scheme-valued map from $\mathcal{X}$ factors uniquely through $\pi$. But the fiber of $\pi$ over a stacky point is a classifying stack $B\mathrm{Aut}$, so automorphism data is discarded; e.g. $\mathrm{CH}^*(\mathcal{M}_{1,1})_{\mathbb{Z}}=\mathbb{Z}[t]/(12t)\ne \mathrm{CH}^*(\mathbb{A}^1)$.

q: For $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ over $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[1/6]$, what is the order of the automorphism group of the elliptic curve $E:y^2=x^3+1$ (the $j=0$ point)?
text: For $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ over $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[1/6]$, what is the order of the automorphism group of the elliptic curve $E:y^2=x^3+1$ (the $j=0$ point)? At $j=0$, $E$ has complex multiplication by $\mathbb{Z}[\zeta_3]$ and $\mathrm{Aut}(E)=\mu_6$, of order $6$. After quotienting by the generic $\mu_2$ ($[-1]$), residual automorphism is $\mu_3$, so locally $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ looks like $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_3]$ near $j=0$.

q: The correct sequence of steps in constructing the coarse moduli space via Keel-Mori is:
text: The correct sequence of steps in constructing the coarse moduli space via Keel-Mori is: Keel-Mori: after checking finite inertia and finite type, one locally reduces to a $\mu_n$-gerbe over an algebraic space, handles the tame case via étale-local arguments, then glues the local coarse spaces using the étale topology to produce a global algebraic space (or scheme in good cases). Construct the space, then check universal property.

q: Which is a counterexample showing the coarse space map $\pi:\mathcal{M}_{1,1}\to\mathbb{A}^1_j$ does NOT preserve integral Chow rings?
text: Which is a counterexample showing the coarse space map $\pi:\mathcal{M}_{1,1}\to\mathbb{A}^1_j$ does NOT preserve integral Chow rings? The Hodge line bundle $\lambda$ on $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ satisfies $12\lambda=0$ in $\mathrm{Pic}(\mathcal{M}_{1,1})$ (reflecting the $\mu_2$ automorphism at every point and the $\mu_6$ at $j=0$), giving $\mathrm{CH}^1(\mathcal{M}_{1,1})\cong\mathbb{Z}/12$. Meanwhile $\mathrm{CH}^1(\mathbb{A}^1)=0$, so $\pi^*$ is not an isomorphism integrally. Coarse spaces have different integral classes at orbifold points.

q: For the DM stack $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]$ (with $\mu_n$ acting by scaling), the coarse moduli map $\pi:[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]\to\mathbb{A}^1$ sends the stacky point $0$ to $0$. The local Picard group of the stack at $0$ is $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$. For $n=4$, what is the order of this local Picard group?
text: For the DM stack $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]$ (with $\mu_n$ acting by scaling), the coarse moduli map $\pi:[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]\to\mathbb{A}^1$ sends the stacky point $0$ to $0$. The local Picard group of the stack at $0$ is $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$. For $n=4$, what is the order of this local Picard group? The stacky point $0\in[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]$ has automorphism group $\mu_n$, contributing a local $\mathrm{Pic}\cong\widehat{\mu_n}\cong\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$. For $n=4$ this has order $4$. The coarse map is degree one but ramifies at the origin.

title: Why schemes aren't enough
blurb: Some natural quotients (e.g. $\mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}$ over a field, or non-free group actions on schemes) are not schemes. Algebraic spaces are the smallest enlargement of schemes that closes the category under such étale quotients while keeping a representable definition.

title: Étale equivalence relations
blurb: An étale equivalence relation on a scheme $U$ is a monomorphism $R \hookrightarrow U \times U$ which is an equivalence relation in the categorical sense, with both projections étale. Quotients of these are exactly algebraic spaces.

title: Algebraic spaces as étale sheaves
blurb: An algebraic space is a sheaf $X$ on the étale site of schemes whose diagonal $X \to X \times X$ is representable and which admits an étale surjection $U \twoheadrightarrow X$ from a scheme. Equivalently, $X = U/R$ for an étale equivalence relation $R \rightrightarrows U$.

title: Morphisms of algebraic spaces
blurb: Morphisms of algebraic spaces are morphisms of étale sheaves; étale-locally on the source and target they reduce to scheme morphisms. Most scheme-morphism properties (étale, smooth, proper, finite type) extend verbatim.

title: Examples: free quotients and a non-scheme
blurb: Any scheme is an algebraic space. The free $\mathbb{Z}$-action on $\mathbb{A}^1$ over a field has quotient an algebraic space that is not a scheme (no separated quasi-projective representative exists). Hironaka's example gives a smooth proper algebraic space over $\mathbb{C}$ that isn't a scheme.

title: Algebraic spaces sit between schemes and DM stacks
blurb: An algebraic space is a stack with trivial automorphism groups; a DM stack with trivial automorphism groups is an algebraic space. The hierarchy schemes ⊂ algebraic spaces ⊂ DM stacks ⊂ Artin stacks tracks how much non-trivial isotropy the geometric object can carry.

q: Why does the étale-sheaf quotient $X = \mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}$ fail to be a scheme over a field $k$?
text: Why does the étale-sheaf quotient $X = \mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}$ fail to be a scheme over a field $k$? The pullback of $\Delta_X$ to $\mathbb{A}^1 \times \mathbb{A}^1$ is the equivalence relation $R = \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{A}^1$. For $X$ to be a scheme, $\Delta_X$ would need to be quasi-compact (in fact, locally closed); but $R$ is an infinite disjoint union of $\mathbb{A}^1$'s and so not quasi-compact. The action is free; finiteness of the group is irrelevant; properness of $\mathbb{A}^1$ is unrelated.

q: Which of the following statements about $\mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}$ over $k$ are true? (select all)
text: Which of the following statements about $\mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}$ over $k$ are true? (select all) It is a sheaf (étale-sheafification of the presheaf quotient), and the natural projection $\mathbb{A}^1 \twoheadrightarrow \mathbb{A}^1/\mathbb{Z}$ is étale and surjective; combined with the diagonal pullback $R = \mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{A}^1$ being a scheme, $X$ is an algebraic space. It is not quasi-separated, because $\Delta_X$ is not quasi-compact.

q: Spot the flawed step in the following "proof" that $\mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}$ is a scheme.
text: Spot the flawed step in the following "proof" that $\mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}$ is a scheme. Step 3 invokes "GAGA" — a comparison theorem between projective complex-analytic and projective algebraic geometry — to conclude something is quasi-projective. GAGA does not produce schemes from étale quotient sheaves, and a free étale action on a scheme need not produce a scheme quotient (e.g. $\mathbb{A}^1/\mathbb{Z}$ itself). The correct conclusion is only that the quotient is an algebraic space.

q: Which of the following data does not define an étale equivalence relation on a scheme $U$?
text: Which of the following data does not define an étale equivalence relation on a scheme $U$? Choices A, B, and C are all valid étale equivalence relations: the diagonal (trivial), free finite group action, and the codiagonal (i.e. $\pi_0$ collapses to a point). Choice D fails transitivity — $x \sim x+1$ and $x+1 \sim x+2$ should imply $x \sim x+2$, but $(x, x+2) \not\in R$. To make this an equivalence relation you would need $R = \bigcup_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} \{(x, x+n)\} = \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{A}^1$ — the relation generating $\mathbb{A}^1/\mathbb{Z}$.

q: Match each scheme-theoretic gadget with its role in an étale equivalence relation $j = (s,t)\colon R \hookrightarrow U \times U$.
text: Match each scheme-theoretic gadget with its role in an étale equivalence relation $j = (s,t)\colon R \hookrightarrow U \times U$. Reflexivity = diagonal factors through $R$. Symmetry = swap restricts. Transitivity = the fibre product $R \times_U R$ (composing equivalences) lands in $R$. Étale projections is the topology condition that makes the quotient an algebraic space rather than just any sheaf.

q: Let $G$ be a finite group acting on a scheme $U$. The associated map $G \times U \to U \times U$, $(g,u) \mapsto (u, g\cdot u)$, defines an étale equivalence relation if and only if which condition holds?
text: Let $G$ be a finite group acting on a scheme $U$. The associated map $G \times U \to U \times U$, $(g,u) \mapsto (u, g\cdot u)$, defines an étale equivalence relation if and only if which condition holds? The map $G \times U \to U \times U$ is automatically a monomorphism iff the action is free. Each projection $G \times U \to U$ is a disjoint union of $|G|$ copies of $U$, which is étale exactly when the source is reduced and the action is free in the geometric sense. Abelian-ness, quasi-projectivity, and invertibility of $|G|$ are unrelated to whether the relation is étale; they affect properties of the quotient (e.g. tameness) but not the equivalence-relation structure itself.

q: Which of the following is the cleanest characterisation of an algebraic space?
text: Which of the following is the cleanest characterisation of an algebraic space? By definition (Knutson, after Artin), an algebraic space is exactly a sheaf $X$ on $(\mathrm{Sch})_{\acute e t}$ with (1) representable diagonal and (2) an étale-surjective scheme atlas. Choice A is the definition of (a quasi-affine) scheme. Choice C describes DM stacks. Choice D is the classical definition of a scheme.

q: Arrange the following arrows / objects in the order in which they appear in the definition of an algebraic space (atlas first, equivalence relation second, etc.).
text: Arrange the following arrows / objects in the order in which they appear in the definition of an algebraic space (atlas first, equivalence relation second, etc.). Logical order: first $X$ is required to be an étale sheaf; then $\Delta_X$ is asked to be representable; this turns any test atlas $U$ into a representable picture, with $R = U\times_X U$ a scheme; and finally the existence of an étale surjective $U \twoheadrightarrow X$ closes the definition. (Some textbooks phrase it as $X = U/R$ for an étale equivalence relation; that's the equivalent étale-quotient form.)

q: If $X$ is an algebraic space and $U \twoheadrightarrow X$ is an étale atlas, what is $R := U \times_X U$ as a scheme?
text: If $X$ is an algebraic space and $U \twoheadrightarrow X$ is an étale atlas, what is $R := U \times_X U$ as a scheme? Pulling back $\Delta_X$ along the atlas $U \times U \to X \times X$ gives $R = U \times_X U \hookrightarrow U \times U$. This is the étale equivalence relation, and $X = U/R$ as étale sheaves. It is generally not $\Delta_U$ (that would force the atlas to be an isomorphism) and only equals $U \sqcup U$ in trivial cases.

q: Suppose $X$ is an algebraic space with representable diagonal $\Delta_X$. Which extra hypothesis on $\Delta_X$ recovers the notion of quasi-separated ?
text: Suppose $X$ is an algebraic space with representable diagonal $\Delta_X$. Which extra hypothesis on $\Delta_X$ recovers the notion of quasi-separated ? By definition, $X$ is quasi-separated iff $\Delta_X$ is quasi-compact. "$\Delta_X$ a closed immersion" gives the strictly stronger condition separated ; étale or proper diagonals are different conditions altogether. The example $\mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}$ from §1 fails quasi-separatedness exactly because $\Delta_X$ is not quasi-compact.

q: Spot the flawed step in the following "proof" that every algebraic space $X$ is a scheme.
text: Spot the flawed step in the following "proof" that every algebraic space $X$ is a scheme. Step 3 is exactly where schemes fail: étale equivalence relations of schemes do not in general have scheme quotients — that's the whole reason algebraic spaces were invented. The classical theorem only guarantees a scheme quotient under extra hypotheses (e.g. all orbits in a single affine, or finite flat $G$-action with quasi-projective $U$). Without such hypotheses, $U/R$ is only an algebraic space.

q: Let $f \colon X \to Y$ be a morphism of algebraic spaces, presented by $\tilde f \colon U \to V$ on étale atlases. Which property of $f$ is not determined by the corresponding property of $\tilde f$?
text: Let $f \colon X \to Y$ be a morphism of algebraic spaces, presented by $\tilde f \colon U \to V$ on étale atlases. Which property of $f$ is not determined by the corresponding property of $\tilde f$? Étale, smooth, and locally of finite presentation are all étale-local on the target, so $f$ has the property iff $\tilde f$ does. Separatedness is more subtle: it is the condition that $\Delta_f \colon X \to X \times_Y X$ is a closed immersion, which involves the relation $R \rightrightarrows U$ and is not determined by $\tilde f$ alone.

q: Select all properties of scheme morphisms that are étale-local on the target (and hence transfer verbatim to morphisms of algebraic spaces).
text: Select all properties of scheme morphisms that are étale-local on the target (and hence transfer verbatim to morphisms of algebraic spaces). Étale, flat, proper, finite, and isomorphism are all étale-local on the target. Separated is the exception — it is a condition on the diagonal $\Delta_f$ rather than on $f$ itself, and depends on the equivalence relation in the algebraic-space presentation.

q: Suppose $X$ and $Y$ are both schemes, viewed as algebraic spaces. A morphism of algebraic spaces $f \colon X \to Y$ is the same data as which of the following?
text: Suppose $X$ and $Y$ are both schemes, viewed as algebraic spaces. A morphism of algebraic spaces $f \colon X \to Y$ is the same data as which of the following? The Yoneda embedding (schemes) $\hookrightarrow$ (étale sheaves) is fully faithful, and the further inclusion into algebraic spaces is too. So a morphism between two schemes-as-algebraic-spaces is exactly a morphism of schemes — no extra data, no quotient by anything.

q: Why is the quotient $\mathbb{A}^1/\mathbb{Z}$ over a field not a separated, quasi-projective scheme?
text: Why is the quotient $\mathbb{A}^1/\mathbb{Z}$ over a field not a separated, quasi-projective scheme? Quasi-projective schemes are quasi-separated, so their diagonals must be quasi-compact. But the diagonal of $\mathbb{A}^1/\mathbb{Z}$ pulls back along $\mathbb{A}^1 \times \mathbb{A}^1 \to X\times X$ to $\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{A}^1$ — an infinite disjoint union, not quasi-compact. The other choices are either false ($\mathbb{A}^1$ is affine and one would expect properness to be unrelated to schemehood of a quotient) or red herrings.

q: In Hironaka's smooth proper non-scheme example, what is the obstruction to being a scheme?
text: In Hironaka's smooth proper non-scheme example, what is the obstruction to being a scheme? Hironaka's space $H$ is smooth and proper, but it contains a proper curve $C$ (the descended image of $\tilde C_0$) on which every line bundle has degree zero. This forbids the existence of an ample line bundle, hence projectivity, hence (for proper smooth 3-folds over $\mathbb{C}$) schemehood. The space is reduced, separated, and 3-dimensional.

q: Select all examples of algebraic spaces that are not schemes.
text: Select all examples of algebraic spaces that are not schemes. $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$ and $\mathbb{P}^n$ are obviously schemes. An étale double cover of $\mathbb{P}^1$ is also a scheme (it's a smooth curve). Only $\mathbb{A}^1/\mathbb{Z}$ (not quasi-separated) and Hironaka's $H$ (smooth proper non-projective) are genuine non-scheme algebraic spaces.

q: Let $G$ be a finite group acting freely on a quasi-projective scheme $U$ over a field. Why is the quotient $U/G$ always a scheme in this case?
text: Let $G$ be a finite group acting freely on a quasi-projective scheme $U$ over a field. Why is the quotient $U/G$ always a scheme in this case? The classical theorem (SGA 1 or [EGA III]) says that if $G$ is finite and every orbit on $U$ is contained in some affine open (which is automatic for quasi-projective $U$, since one can take a $G$-stable affine using ample-line-bundle averaging), then $U/G$ exists as a scheme. The Zariski-local structure is $\mathrm{Spec}(R^G)$. Without quasi-projectivity, the conclusion can fail.

q: Spot the flawed step in this attempted proof that Hironaka's space $H$ is projective.
text: Spot the flawed step in this attempted proof that Hironaka's space $H$ is projective. Step 3 is false. Ampleness is not preserved under proper birational pullback — it can become only nef, and in Hironaka's example precisely this happens: the pullback restricts to degree zero on the descended curve $C$ that obstructs schemehood. Chow's lemma exists for algebraic spaces (giving a projective birational cover), but the pulled-back line bundle is only nef, not ample, so the conclusion fails.

q: Which condition characterises algebraic spaces among Artin stacks?
text: Which condition characterises algebraic spaces among Artin stacks? An Artin stack is an algebraic space iff every geometric stabiliser is trivial — iff $\Delta_{\mathcal X}$ is a monomorphism. Choice A characterises DM stacks; choice C is necessary but not sufficient (DM stacks also have étale atlases by schemes, e.g. $B(\mathbb{Z}/2)$ has the trivial étale cover from a point times $\mathbb{Z}/2$); choice D is unrelated.

q: Match each rung of the hierarchy with the condition on its diagonal.
text: Match each rung of the hierarchy with the condition on its diagonal. The hierarchy is fully captured by progressively weaker conditions on the diagonal: locally closed immersion (schemes) $\Rightarrow$ monomorphism (algebraic spaces) $\Rightarrow$ unramified (DM) $\Rightarrow$ representable (Artin). Each weakening corresponds to allowing more structure in the geometric stabilisers (none, none, finite étale, any algebraic group).

q: Let $\mathcal X = [\mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}/2]$ where $\mathbb{Z}/2$ acts by $x \mapsto -x$. What kind of geometric object is $\mathcal X$?
text: Let $\mathcal X = [\mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}/2]$ where $\mathbb{Z}/2$ acts by $x \mapsto -x$. What kind of geometric object is $\mathcal X$? The action is not free: the origin $0 \in \mathbb{A}^1$ is fixed, with stabiliser $\mathbb{Z}/2$. So $\mathcal X = [\mathbb{A}^1/(\mathbb{Z}/2)]$ has nontrivial isotropy at the origin, making it a DM stack, not an algebraic space. (The naïve geometric quotient $\operatorname{Spec} k[x^2] \cong \mathbb{A}^1$ is the coarse moduli space , which loses the stabiliser information.)

q: Suppose $\mathcal X$ is an Artin stack with diagonal $\Delta$. Which characterisation is correct?
text: Suppose $\mathcal X$ is an Artin stack with diagonal $\Delta$. Which characterisation is correct? The clean characterisation is: $\mathcal X$ is DM $\Leftrightarrow$ $\Delta_{\mathcal X}$ is unramified $\Leftrightarrow$ all geometric stabilisers are finite étale group schemes (and an étale atlas exists). Choice B is wrong: algebraic spaces have $\Delta$ a monomorphism , not étale (étale + mono = open immersion, too strong). Choices C and D are unrelated to the standard characterisations.

q: Which of the following are DM stacks but not algebraic spaces?
text: Which of the following are DM stacks but not algebraic spaces? $B(\mathbb{Z}/n)$, $\overline{\mathcal M}_{1,1}$ (with $\mathbb{Z}/2$ stabiliser at the $j=1728$ point), and $\mathcal M_g$ for $g \ge 2$ are all DM stacks with nontrivial finite stabilisers, hence not algebraic spaces. $\mathbb{P}^1$ is a scheme. $BGL_n$ has positive-dimensional stabiliser $GL_n$, so it is an Artin stack that is not DM.

title: Algebraic cycles and rational equivalence
blurb: An algebraic $k$-cycle on $X$ is a finite formal $\mathbb{Z}$-linear combination of closed subvarieties of dimension $k$. Two cycles are rationally equivalent if their difference is the principal divisor of a rational function on a $(k+1)$-cycle — the right replacement for homotopy in the algebraic setting.

title: Chow groups $A_*(X)$
blurb: The Chow group $A_k(X)$ is the free abelian group on $k$-cycles modulo rational equivalence. The total Chow group $A_*(X) = \bigoplus_k A_k(X)$ is the algebraic analogue of singular homology — it sees algebraic but not transcendental cycles.

title: Intersection product on smooth $X$
blurb: On a smooth variety $X$, two transverse subvarieties $Y, Z$ of complementary codimension intersect in finitely many points; the count gives the intersection number $Y \cdot Z \in A_0(X)$. The product extends to all of $A_*(X)$ via moving lemmas, making $A^*(X)$ a graded ring.

title: $A^*(\mathbb{P}^n) = \mathbb{Z}[h]/(h^{n+1})$
blurb: The Chow ring of projective space is generated by the hyperplane class $h$ with relation $h^{n+1} = 0$. Bezout's theorem $C \cdot D = (\deg C)(\deg D)$ for plane curves is the case $n = 2$ — multiplication of degrees in $\mathbb{Z}[h]/(h^3)$.

title: Chern classes
blurb: A vector bundle $E$ of rank $r$ on a smooth $X$ has Chern classes $c_i(E) \in A^i(X)$ for $i = 0, \ldots, r$, encoding obstructions to $E$ trivializing. The total Chern class $c(E) = 1 + c_1 + \cdots + c_r$ is multiplicative in short exact sequences.

title: Grothendieck–Riemann–Roch (preview)
blurb: GRR replaces the classical Riemann–Roch with an equality in $A^*(X) \otimes \mathbb{Q}$ involving the Chern character $\mathrm{ch}(E)$ and the Todd class $\mathrm{td}(T_X)$. It packages every Riemann–Roch in algebraic geometry — for curves, surfaces, and beyond — as instances of one functorial statement.

q: Which of the following is the cleanest definition of rational equivalence on $Z_k(X)$?
text: Which of the following is the cleanest definition of rational equivalence on $Z_k(X)$? Rational equivalence is an algebraic homotopy: $\alpha - \beta = \sum \mathrm{div}_{W_j}(f_j)$ with $\dim W_j = k+1$. Singular homology is a coarser invariant; degree is much coarser still. Think of $\mathbb{P}^1$ as the algebraic interval — divisors of rational functions are the connecting homotopies.

q: Match each cycle on $\mathbb{P}^1$ with the unique class it represents in $A_0(\mathbb{P}^1)$.
text: Match each cycle on $\mathbb{P}^1$ with the unique class it represents in $A_0(\mathbb{P}^1)$. $A_0(\mathbb{P}^1) = \mathbb{Z}$ via degree. A degree-$0$ cycle is $0$; a degree-$1$ cycle is $[\mathrm{pt}]$; a degree-$2$ cycle is $2[\mathrm{pt}]$. Compute the degree of each right-hand cycle.

q: Find the flawed step in this attempted proof that $[P] - [Q] = 0$ in $A_0(C)$ for a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g \ge 1$ and any two points $P, Q$.
text: Find the flawed step in this attempted proof that $[P] - [Q] = 0$ in $A_0(C)$ for a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g \ge 1$ and any two points $P, Q$. Step 1 is the bug: a rational function with divisor $[P] - [Q]$ exists iff $[P] = [Q]$ in $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$, i.e. iff $P, Q$ have the same image under Abel–Jacobi. For $g \ge 1$ the Jacobian is non-trivial, so generic $P \neq Q$ are inequivalent. Step 4 thus fails: $A_0(C) \cong \mathbb{Z} \oplus \mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$. On $\mathbb{P}^1$ every degree-$0$ divisor is principal — but $\mathbb{P}^1$ is the genus-$0$ exception.

q: What is the rank of $A_*(\mathbb{P}^4)$ as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module?
text: What is the rank of $A_*(\mathbb{P}^4)$ as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module? $A_*(\mathbb{P}^n) = \bigoplus_{k=0}^n \mathbb{Z} \cdot [L^k] \cong \mathbb{Z}^{n+1}$, with one generator in each codimension $0, 1, \ldots, n$. For $n = 4$ that's $5$. Linear subspaces of dimension $0, 1, \ldots, n$ generate the group, one per dimension.

q: What is $A_*(\mathbb{A}^n)$?
text: What is $A_*(\mathbb{A}^n)$? Every cycle on $\mathbb{A}^n$ of dimension $< n$ is rationally equivalent to $0$ via a translation family pushing it to infinity. So $A_*(\mathbb{A}^n) = A_n(\mathbb{A}^n) \cong \mathbb{Z}$, generated by the fundamental class $[\mathbb{A}^n]$. Affine space is contractible in the rational-equivalence sense.

q: Which of the following are true about Chow groups $A_*(X)$ for smooth quasi-projective $X$?
text: Which of the following are true about Chow groups $A_*(X)$ for smooth quasi-projective $X$? Push-forward (covariant, proper) and pull-back (contravariant, flat) are the two basic functorialities. The cycle map exists but generally fails to be either injective or surjective — Griffiths' work on Fano threefolds gives counterexamples to injectivity, and transcendental Hodge classes obstruct surjectivity. Simple-connectedness has no bearing on $A_*$.

q: What is the rank of $A_*(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1)$ as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module?
text: What is the rank of $A_*(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1)$ as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module? $A_*(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1) = \mathbb{Z}[h, k]/(h^2, k^2)$ has $\mathbb{Z}$-basis $\{1, h, k, hk\}$, so rank $4$. (Equivalently, the Künneth formula: $A_*(X \times Y) \cong A_*(X) \otimes A_*(Y)$ when one factor has a cell decomposition; rank $2 \cdot 2 = 4$.) Künneth + the answer for $A_*(\mathbb{P}^1)$.

q: Two distinct lines $L_1, L_2$ in $\mathbb{P}^2$ meet in $[L_1] \cdot [L_2] = ?$ point(s) (counted with multiplicity).
text: Two distinct lines $L_1, L_2$ in $\mathbb{P}^2$ meet in $[L_1] \cdot [L_2] = ?$ point(s) (counted with multiplicity). Each line has class $h \in A^1(\mathbb{P}^2) = \mathbb{Z}$, so $[L_1] \cdot [L_2] = h \cdot h = h^2 \in A^2(\mathbb{P}^2) = \mathbb{Z}$, with degree $1$. Two lines in the projective plane meet in exactly one point.

q: On $\mathbb{P}^3$, a smooth quadric surface $Q$ (degree $2$) and a smooth cubic surface $S$ (degree $3$) intersect generically in a curve of degree $\deg(Q \cdot S) = ?$.
text: On $\mathbb{P}^3$, a smooth quadric surface $Q$ (degree $2$) and a smooth cubic surface $S$ (degree $3$) intersect generically in a curve of degree $\deg(Q \cdot S) = ?$. $[Q] = 2h$ and $[S] = 3h$ in $A^*(\mathbb{P}^3) = \mathbb{Z}[h]/(h^4)$. Their product is $[Q] \cdot [S] = 6 h^2$, the class of a curve of degree $6$ (the degree of a $1$-cycle in $\mathbb{P}^3$ is the coefficient of $h^2$ when paired with the line class $h$). Multiply degrees: this is the projective Bézout in $\mathbb{P}^3$.

q: Why does the moving lemma matter for defining $[Y] \cdot [Z]$ when $Y, Z$ do not meet transversally?
text: Why does the moving lemma matter for defining $[Y] \cdot [Z]$ when $Y, Z$ do not meet transversally? On smooth quasi-projective $X$, the moving lemma (Chow, Roberts) lets you replace either factor by a rationally-equivalent cycle in general position with the other; transverse-only intersections then define the product on classes. On singular $X$ the moving lemma fails; one falls back to the bivariant theory of Fulton–MacPherson. Definitions need representatives in 'general position'; moving moves them there.

q: On $\mathbb{P}^2$, a smooth conic $C$ (degree $2$) and a tangent line $L$ at one of its points: what is the intersection multiplicity $i_p(C, L)$ at the tangent point $p$?
text: On $\mathbb{P}^2$, a smooth conic $C$ (degree $2$) and a tangent line $L$ at one of its points: what is the intersection multiplicity $i_p(C, L)$ at the tangent point $p$? $\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^2, p}/(F, G)$ has length $2$ when $L$ is tangent to $C$ at $p$ — equivalently, two transverse intersections collide as you specialise from a secant to a tangent. Bézout still gives $\deg(C \cdot L) = 2$, but the intersection is concentrated at one point with $i_p = 2$. Bézout gives the total — what makes the count survive when two transverse points collide?

q: What is the self-intersection $h \cdot h$ on $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$, where $h = [\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathrm{pt}]$ is the class of a horizontal ruling?
text: What is the self-intersection $h \cdot h$ on $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$, where $h = [\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathrm{pt}]$ is the class of a horizontal ruling? Two horizontal rulings $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \{a\}$ and $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \{b\}$ never meet for $a \neq b$, so by the moving lemma $h \cdot h = 0$ in $A^*(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1) = \mathbb{Z}[h, k]/(h^2, k^2)$. Only $h \cdot k = [\mathrm{pt}]$ is non-zero. Two parallel rulings of the same type don't meet — move one to verify.

q: In $A^*(\mathbb{P}^5) = \mathbb{Z}[h]/(h^6)$, what is $h^3 \cdot h^2$? (Enter the integer coefficient if the result is $c \cdot h^k$ for some $k \le 5$, or $0$ if the product vanishes.)
text: In $A^*(\mathbb{P}^5) = \mathbb{Z}[h]/(h^6)$, what is $h^3 \cdot h^2$? (Enter the integer coefficient if the result is $c \cdot h^k$ for some $k \le 5$, or $0$ if the product vanishes.) $h^3 \cdot h^2 = h^5 \in A^5(\mathbb{P}^5)$, the class of a point — coefficient $1$. (We didn't hit the relation $h^6 = 0$ because $3 + 2 = 5 \le 5$.) Add the exponents and check whether you exceed $n = 5$.

q: In $A^*(\mathbb{P}^4)$, what is $h^3 \cdot h^3$?
text: In $A^*(\mathbb{P}^4)$, what is $h^3 \cdot h^3$? $3 + 3 = 6 > 4$, so $h^6 = 0$ by the relation $h^{n+1} = h^5 = 0$ which forces all higher powers to vanish. Check whether the sum of exponents exceeds the dimension of the ambient space.

q: Order these computations from smallest to largest as integers (degree of the resulting $0$-cycle on the indicated $\mathbb{P}^n$).
text: Order these computations from smallest to largest as integers (degree of the resulting $0$-cycle on the indicated $\mathbb{P}^n$). First: $h^2 \cdot h^2 = h^4 = $ class of point on $\mathbb{P}^4$, degree $1$. Second: $(2h)(3h) = 6 h^2$, degree $6$ on $\mathbb{P}^2$. Third: $(4h)(5h)(6h) = 120\, h^3$, degree $120$ on $\mathbb{P}^3$. So the order $1 < 6 < 120$. Compute each as an integer first, then sort.

q: What is the total Chern class $c(\mathcal{O}(2) \oplus \mathcal{O}(3))$ on $\mathbb{P}^2$?
text: What is the total Chern class $c(\mathcal{O}(2) \oplus \mathcal{O}(3))$ on $\mathbb{P}^2$? By Whitney multiplicativity: $c(\mathcal{O}(2)) = 1 + 2h$, $c(\mathcal{O}(3)) = 1 + 3h$. Product: $(1 + 2h)(1 + 3h) = 1 + 5h + 6h^2$ in $A^*(\mathbb{P}^2)$. $c(\mathcal{O}(d)) = 1 + dh$ for line bundles; multiply for direct sums.

q: On $\mathbb{P}^2$, what is $c_2(T_{\mathbb{P}^2})$ as an integer (writing it as $c_2 = N \cdot h^2$, give $N$)?
text: On $\mathbb{P}^2$, what is $c_2(T_{\mathbb{P}^2})$ as an integer (writing it as $c_2 = N \cdot h^2$, give $N$)? Euler sequence $0 \to \mathcal{O} \to \mathcal{O}(1)^{\oplus 3} \to T_{\mathbb{P}^2} \to 0$. By multiplicativity (after tensoring properly): $c(\mathcal{O})\, c(T_{\mathbb{P}^2}) = c(\mathcal{O}(1))^3 = (1+h)^3 = 1 + 3h + 3h^2 + h^3$. Truncate at $h^3 = 0$: $c(T_{\mathbb{P}^2}) = 1 + 3h + 3h^2$, so $c_2 = 3$. Use the Euler sequence and read off the coefficient of $h^2$ in $(1+h)^3$.

q: Which of the following are axioms of Chern classes (i.e., used to characterise them, not derived)?
text: Which of the following are axioms of Chern classes (i.e., used to characterise them, not derived)? Naturality, Whitney sum, and the line-bundle normalisation are Grothendieck's three axioms; vanishing $c_i$ above the rank is a consequence (every rank-$r$ bundle has all non-trivial Chern classes in degrees $\le r$). The axioms must be enough to compute everything; the rank vanishing is forced by the splitting principle.

q: On $\mathbb{P}^3$, compute $c_2(T_{\mathbb{P}^3})$ as the integer coefficient of $h^2$.
text: On $\mathbb{P}^3$, compute $c_2(T_{\mathbb{P}^3})$ as the integer coefficient of $h^2$. Euler: $c(T_{\mathbb{P}^3}) = (1+h)^4 = 1 + 4h + 6h^2 + 4h^3 + h^4$. Truncate at $h^4 = 0$: $c(T_{\mathbb{P}^3}) = 1 + 4h + 6h^2 + 4h^3$. So $c_2 = 6$. Generalize the $\mathbb{P}^2$ Euler-sequence calculation: $(1+h)^{n+1}$.

q: Why does the splitting principle work? (i.e. why can we pretend a rank-$r$ vector bundle $E$ splits as a direct sum of line bundles when computing Chern classes?)
text: Why does the splitting principle work? (i.e. why can we pretend a rank-$r$ vector bundle $E$ splits as a direct sum of line bundles when computing Chern classes?) The flag bundle $\mathbb{F}(E)$ parametrises complete flags in fibres of $E$; on it the pulled-back $E$ acquires a tautological filtration with line-bundle quotients. The pull-back $A^*(X) \to A^*(\mathbb{F}(E))$ is split injective (Chow's projective-bundle formula iterated), so any identity true on $\mathbb{F}(E)$ — where $E$ does split — descends to $X$. Vector bundles emphatically do not split in general (e.g., $T_{\mathbb{P}^2}$). Look at the flag bundle of $E$ — it adds the splitting data without changing the Chern classes.

q: By Riemann–Roch on a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g = 3$, compute $\chi(C, \mathcal{O}(D))$ where $\deg D = 7$.
text: By Riemann–Roch on a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g = 3$, compute $\chi(C, \mathcal{O}(D))$ where $\deg D = 7$. $\chi(C, \mathcal{O}(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g = 7 + 1 - 3 = 5$. $\chi = \deg D + 1 - g$ for any divisor on a smooth projective curve.

q: Compute $\chi(\mathbb{P}^2, \mathcal{O}(4))$ via Riemann–Roch / GRR.
text: Compute $\chi(\mathbb{P}^2, \mathcal{O}(4))$ via Riemann–Roch / GRR. $\chi(\mathbb{P}^2, \mathcal{O}(d)) = \binom{d+2}{2} = \binom{6}{2} = 15$. (For $d \ge 0$ this also equals $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^2, \mathcal{O}(d))$ since higher cohomology vanishes.) GRR on $\mathbb{P}^2$ for $\mathcal{O}(d)$ gives $(d+1)(d+2)/2$.

q: Arrange the steps of GRR on $X = \mathbb{P}^2$ for the sheaf $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{O}(d)$, in the order one carries them out.
text: Arrange the steps of GRR on $X = \mathbb{P}^2$ for the sheaf $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{O}(d)$, in the order one carries them out. Compute each ingredient, multiply, then take degree (= push-forward to a point). This is the universal recipe for Hirzebruch–Riemann–Roch on $\mathbb{P}^n$. First gather the ingredients (ch and td), then combine them, then specialise.

q: On a smooth projective surface $S$ with $K_S$ trivial (e.g. a K3 surface, where $\chi(\mathcal{O}_S) = 2$), compute $\chi(S, \mathcal{O}(D))$ for a divisor $D$ with $D \cdot D = 4$. (Use the surface Riemann–Roch $\chi = \tfrac12 D \cdot (D - K_S) + \chi(\mathcal{O}_S)$.)
text: On a smooth projective surface $S$ with $K_S$ trivial (e.g. a K3 surface, where $\chi(\mathcal{O}_S) = 2$), compute $\chi(S, \mathcal{O}(D))$ for a divisor $D$ with $D \cdot D = 4$. (Use the surface Riemann–Roch $\chi = \tfrac12 D \cdot (D - K_S) + \chi(\mathcal{O}_S)$.) With $K_S = 0$: $\chi = \tfrac12 D \cdot D + \chi(\mathcal{O}_S) = \tfrac12 \cdot 4 + 2 = 4$. $K_S = 0$ kills the $-D \cdot K_S$ term; just plug into the formula.

q: Why is $\mathrm{td}(T_X)$ — rather than just $\mathrm{ch}(T_X)$ — the right correction term in GRR?
text: Why is $\mathrm{td}(T_X)$ — rather than just $\mathrm{ch}(T_X)$ — the right correction term in GRR? Hirzebruch chose td precisely because $\mathrm{td}(L)$ for $c_1(L) = \alpha$ equals $\alpha / (1 - e^{-\alpha})$, whose coefficients reproduce $\chi(\mathbb{P}^n, \mathcal{O}) = 1$ in all dimensions. Equivalently, $\int_{\mathbb{P}^n} \mathrm{td}(T_{\mathbb{P}^n}) = 1$, which uniquely characterises td among multiplicative classes. Test the formula on $\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^n}$ — what value of $\chi$ does it predict?

title: Finite étale covers
blurb: A finite étale cover $Y \to X$ is a morphism that is finite, flat, and unramified — the algebro-geometric replacement for a topological covering space. The category $\mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X$ of finite étale covers is the right combinatorial object on which to define a fundamental group.

title: The fiber functor at a geometric point
blurb: Choosing a geometric point $\bar{x}: \mathrm{Spec}\,\bar{k} \to X$ gives a fiber functor $F_{\bar{x}}: \mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X \to \mathrm{FinSet}$ sending $Y \to X$ to the underlying set of the fiber $Y_{\bar{x}}$. The étale fundamental group is the automorphism group of this functor.

title: $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X, \bar{x}) = \mathrm{Aut}(F_{\bar{x}})$
blurb: $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X, \bar{x})$ is the profinite group $\mathrm{Aut}(F_{\bar{x}})$, with topology induced by the pro-finite-set structure of fibers. It classifies finite étale covers via $\mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X \simeq \pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X, \bar{x})\text{-FinSet}$ — Grothendieck's Galois theory.

title: $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\mathrm{Spec}\,k) = \mathrm{Gal}(\bar{k}/k)$
blurb: For a field $k$, the étale fundamental group of $\mathrm{Spec}\,k$ is the absolute Galois group $\mathrm{Gal}(\bar{k}/k)$. Finite étale covers $\mathrm{Spec}\,L \to \mathrm{Spec}\,k$ are exactly finite separable extensions; the equivalence is classical Galois theory.

title: Frobenius generates $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q)$
blurb: $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q) = \hat{\mathbb{Z}}$, topologically generated by the Frobenius $\mathrm{Frob}_q\colon x \mapsto x^q$. Every cover of $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q$ is determined by the action of $\mathrm{Frob}_q$ on a finite set.

title: Comparison with topological $\pi_1$
blurb: For a smooth complex variety $X$, the étale fundamental group $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X)$ is the profinite completion of the topological $\pi_1(X(\mathbb{C}))$ (Riemann existence). For varieties over fields of characteristic 0 this captures all finite-cover information.

q: Which conditions together define a finite étale morphism $f\colon Y \to X$ of schemes?
text: Which conditions together define a finite étale morphism $f\colon Y \to X$ of schemes? Finite étale = finite + flat + unramified. Equivalently, finite + smooth of relative dimension 0. Properness is implied by finiteness; surjectivity is not in the definition (a finite étale cover may have multiple connected components mapping to a connected base). There are exactly three independent local conditions.

q: Let $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Q}[t,t^{-1}] = \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathbb{Q}}$ and let $Y = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Q}[s,s^{-1}]$ with $s^5 = t$. What is the degree (i.e. rank of $f_*\mathcal{O}_Y$) of this finite étale cover $f\colon Y \to X$?
text: Let $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Q}[t,t^{-1}] = \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathbb{Q}}$ and let $Y = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Q}[s,s^{-1}]$ with $s^5 = t$. What is the degree (i.e. rank of $f_*\mathcal{O}_Y$) of this finite étale cover $f\colon Y \to X$? $\mathbb{Q}[s,s^{-1}]$ is free of rank 5 over $\mathbb{Q}[t,t^{-1}]$ with basis $1, s, s^2, s^3, s^4$. The fiber over any geometric point has 5 elements (the 5 fifth roots of the chosen value of $t$). Count a free basis of $\mathbb{Q}[s,s^{-1}]$ over $\mathbb{Q}[t,t^{-1}]$.

q: Where does this argument that $f\colon \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_p[s] \to \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_p[t]$, $t = s^p$, is finite étale go wrong?
text: Where does this argument that $f\colon \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_p[s] \to \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_p[t]$, $t = s^p$, is finite étale go wrong? Step 3 is wrong. $\Omega^1_{Y/X}$ is a free rank-1 $\mathbb{F}_p[s]$-module (generated by $ds$), not zero — that is the opposite of unramified. The map $s \mapsto s^p$ is purely inseparable in characteristic $p$, hence not étale; the argument that 'one generator means small' is bogus. Look carefully at the relation $d(s^p) = p\,s^{p-1}\,ds$ in characteristic $p$.

q: For $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Q}$ and $\bar{x} = \mathrm{Spec}\,\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$, what is the cardinality of $F_{\bar{x}}(\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2}))$?
text: For $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Q}$ and $\bar{x} = \mathrm{Spec}\,\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$, what is the cardinality of $F_{\bar{x}}(\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2}))$? $F_{\bar{x}}(\mathrm{Spec}\,L) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbb{Q}}(L, \overline{\mathbb{Q}})$, which has cardinality $[L:\mathbb{Q}] = 3$ for $L = \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2})$ (the embeddings send $\sqrt[3]{2}$ to $\sqrt[3]{2}$, $\omega\sqrt[3]{2}$, or $\omega^2\sqrt[3]{2}$). Note that $L/\mathbb{Q}$ is not Galois — $|\mathrm{Aut}(L/\mathbb{Q})| = 1$, but the fiber still has 3 points. Count $\mathbb{Q}$-algebra embeddings of $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2})$ into $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$.

q: Which of the following is NOT a property of the fiber functor $F_{\bar{x}}\colon \mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X \to \mathrm{FinSet}$?
text: Which of the following is NOT a property of the fiber functor $F_{\bar{x}}\colon \mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X \to \mathrm{FinSet}$? Faithfulness, exactness, and finite values are exactly the axioms making $\mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X$ a Galois category. $F_{\bar{x}}$ is only pro-representable in general — by the universal cover viewed as a pro-object of $\mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X$ — not representable by a single finite étale cover (covers of $X$ by simply-connected schemes don't exist when $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}$ is infinite). What kind of object would represent it, and does it live in the category $\mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X$ itself?

q: Match each cover of $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{R}$ with $|F_{\bar{x}}(\,\cdot\,)|$ for $\bar{x} = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{C}$.
text: Match each cover of $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{R}$ with $|F_{\bar{x}}(\,\cdot\,)|$ for $\bar{x} = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{C}$. $|F_{\bar{x}}(\mathrm{Spec}\,A)| = \dim_{\mathbb{R}} A$ for a finite étale $\mathbb{R}$-algebra $A$. Trivial cover: 1. $\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R}$: 2. $\mathbb{C}\times\mathbb{R}$: $2+1 = 3$. The fiber sees the underlying rank, not the field-theoretic structure. $|F_{\bar{x}}(\mathrm{Spec}\,A)| = \mathrm{rank}_{\mathbb{R}}(A)$ for finite étale $A$.

q: Two geometric points $\bar{x}, \bar{x}'$ over the same scheme-theoretic point of $X$ generally give non-equal fiber functors $F_{\bar{x}}, F_{\bar{x}'}$. What is the precise relationship between them?
text: Two geometric points $\bar{x}, \bar{x}'$ over the same scheme-theoretic point of $X$ generally give non-equal fiber functors $F_{\bar{x}}, F_{\bar{x}'}$. What is the precise relationship between them? Any two fiber functors at geometric points are (non-canonically) isomorphic — this is the analogue of 'change of basepoint' in topological $\pi_1$. The set of isomorphisms is a torsor under either fundamental group, and choosing one identifies the two groups up to inner automorphism. Compare with the classical fact that based vs. free homotopy classes of paths give a torsor under $\pi_1$.

q: Where does this 'proof' that the fiber functor on $\mathrm{Spec}\,k$ is representable by a single finite étale cover go wrong (assume $k$ is a field with infinite absolute Galois group)?
text: Where does this 'proof' that the fiber functor on $\mathrm{Spec}\,k$ is representable by a single finite étale cover go wrong (assume $k$ is a field with infinite absolute Galois group)? Step 3 is wrong. $P$ exists only as a pro-object : $P = \varprojlim_{L} \mathrm{Spec}\,L$ over all finite separable $L/k^{\mathrm{sep}}$. This is $\mathrm{Spec}\,k^{\mathrm{sep}}$, which has infinite-dimensional structure sheaf when $k^{\mathrm{sep}}/k$ is an infinite extension (e.g. $k = \mathbb{Q}$). It is not a finite étale cover. The closure-under-products argument fails because finite étale covers form a category closed under finite products but not arbitrary pro-objects. Could $P = \mathrm{Spec}\,\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$ ever be finite étale over $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Q}$?

q: Arrange the steps of Grothendieck's construction of $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}$ in their logical order.
text: Arrange the steps of Grothendieck's construction of $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}$ in their logical order. Geometric point → fiber functor → automorphism group → topology → equivalence of categories. Each step uses the previous; the equivalence is the payoff. What's the very first datum you choose, and what's the last theorem you prove?

q: Under the equivalence $\mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X \simeq \pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X,\bar{x})\text{-FinSet}$, what corresponds to a connected finite étale cover $Y \to X$ of degree $n$?
text: Under the equivalence $\mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X \simeq \pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X,\bar{x})\text{-FinSet}$, what corresponds to a connected finite étale cover $Y \to X$ of degree $n$? Connected covers ↔ transitive actions on finite sets ↔ open subgroups (the stabilizer of a fiber point), index = degree. Normality is the extra condition that singles out Galois (regular) covers, where the cover is itself a torsor under $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}/H$. Transitive actions on $n$-element sets ↔ stabilizer subgroups of which index?

q: Which of the following are properties of $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X, \bar{x})$ for $X$ a connected scheme?
text: Which of the following are properties of $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X, \bar{x})$ for $X$ a connected scheme? Profinite by construction (inverse limit of finite groups), so equal to its own profinite completion (a profinite group is its own profinite completion). Choice of basepoint changes the group up to inner automorphism. Continuous action on each fiber is the equivalence-of-categories statement. NOT finitely generated: $\hat{\mathbb{Z}}$ is not finitely generated as an abstract group (only topologically). $\hat{\mathbb{Z}}$ is uncountable — could it be finitely generated as an abstract group?

q: Let $Y \to X$ be a Galois cover with Galois group $G$. What is the relationship between $G$ and $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X, \bar{x})$?
text: Let $Y \to X$ be a Galois cover with Galois group $G$. What is the relationship between $G$ and $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X, \bar{x})$? A Galois cover with group $G$ corresponds to an open normal subgroup $H \triangleleft \pi_1^{\acute{e}t}$ with $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}/H = G$. So $G$ is a finite continuous quotient — exactly the way $\mathrm{Gal}(L/k)$ is a quotient of $\mathrm{Gal}(k^{\mathrm{sep}}/k)$. Galois covers correspond to open normal subgroups; what does the quotient look like?

q: Prove that $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}$ of a connected scheme is profinite. The next step after the given ones is:
text: Prove that $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}$ of a connected scheme is profinite. The next step after the given ones is: Each naturality condition $F_{\bar{x}}(g)\circ\sigma_Y = \sigma_{Y'}\circ F_{\bar{x}}(g)$ is a closed condition (an equality of permutations in a finite group — its complement is open in the product topology). Intersection of closed sets is closed. A closed subgroup of a profinite group is profinite. Naturality conditions are equalities — what's the topological character of an equality cut?

q: What is $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{R})$?
text: What is $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{R})$? $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\mathrm{Spec}\,k) = \mathrm{Gal}(k^{\mathrm{sep}}/k)$. For $k = \mathbb{R}$, $k^{\mathrm{sep}} = \mathbb{C}$ and $\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R}) = \mathbb{Z}/2$, generated by complex conjugation. The only nontrivial connected finite étale cover of $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{R}$ is $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{C}$. List the finite separable extensions of $\mathbb{R}$.

q: Match each open subgroup of $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb{Q}}/\mathbb{Q})$ with the connected finite étale cover of $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Q}$ it corresponds to.
text: Match each open subgroup of $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb{Q}}/\mathbb{Q})$ with the connected finite étale cover of $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Q}$ it corresponds to. Open $H \le \mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb{Q}}/\mathbb{Q})$ ↔ finite separable $L \subset \overline{\mathbb{Q}}$ via $L = \overline{\mathbb{Q}}^H$, with $[L:\mathbb{Q}] = [\mathrm{Gal}:H]$. Note that $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2})/\mathbb{Q}$ is not Galois — the corresponding subgroup is not normal — but the equivalence works on all (connected) covers, not just Galois ones. Index of $H$ = degree of the cover.

q: How many connected finite étale covers does $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_5$ have of degree dividing 4 (counting up to isomorphism, i.e. counting subgroups of $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb{F}_5}/\mathbb{F}_5)$ of index dividing 4)?
text: How many connected finite étale covers does $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_5$ have of degree dividing 4 (counting up to isomorphism, i.e. counting subgroups of $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb{F}_5}/\mathbb{F}_5)$ of index dividing 4)? Connected covers of degree $n$ ↔ open subgroups of index $n$ in $\hat{\mathbb{Z}}$, of which there is exactly one (corresponding to $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_{5^n}$). Indices dividing 4: $n=1,2,4$, giving 3 covers (no $n=3$ contribution since $3 \nmid 4$). How many open subgroups of $\hat{\mathbb{Z}}$ have index $n$ exactly?

q: Why do we use $k^{\mathrm{sep}}$ rather than $\bar{k}$ in defining the geometric basepoint $\bar{x} = \mathrm{Spec}\,k^{\mathrm{sep}}$?
text: Why do we use $k^{\mathrm{sep}}$ rather than $\bar{k}$ in defining the geometric basepoint $\bar{x} = \mathrm{Spec}\,k^{\mathrm{sep}}$? Finite étale extensions of fields are exactly finite separable extensions. Purely inseparable extensions $k(t^{1/p})/k(t)$ in characteristic $p$ are not étale — they fail unramifiedness ($\Omega^1 \ne 0$). So the étale fundamental group only sees finite quotients of $\mathrm{Gal}(k^{\mathrm{sep}}/k)$; the difference between $k^{\mathrm{sep}}$ and $\bar{k}$ disappears for perfect $k$ (e.g. characteristic 0 or finite fields).

q: What is $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q)$ as an abstract topological group?
text: What is $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q)$ as an abstract topological group? $\mathbb{F}_q$ has a unique extension $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q$ in each degree $n$, and they fit into a tower with $\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q) = \mathbb{Z}/n$ (generated by Frobenius). The inverse limit is $\hat{\mathbb{Z}}$. Note: NOT $\mathbb{Z}$ — $\hat{\mathbb{Z}}$ is the profinite completion of $\mathbb{Z}$ and is uncountable. Take an inverse limit over all finite extensions of $\mathbb{F}_q$.

q: Frobenius $\mathrm{Frob}_q$ acts on the geometric fiber of $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_{q^{12}} \to \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q$. How many orbits does $\mathrm{Frob}_q^4$ have on this 12-point fiber?
text: Frobenius $\mathrm{Frob}_q$ acts on the geometric fiber of $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_{q^{12}} \to \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q$. How many orbits does $\mathrm{Frob}_q^4$ have on this 12-point fiber? Frobenius $\mathrm{Frob}_q$ acts as a 12-cycle on the fiber (the cover is connected, of degree 12). Its 4th power $\mathrm{Frob}_q^4$ acts as a permutation with $\gcd(4, 12) = 4$ disjoint cycles, each of length $12 / 4 = 3$. So 4 orbits. $\mathrm{Frob}_q^a$ on a $\mathbb{Z}/n$-cycle has $\gcd(a,n)$ orbits.

q: Which integers $a \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ have the property that $\mathrm{Frob}_q^a$ generates the same cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}/6$ as $\mathrm{Frob}_q$ on the 6-point fiber of $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_{q^6} \to \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q$?
text: Which integers $a \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ have the property that $\mathrm{Frob}_q^a$ generates the same cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}/6$ as $\mathrm{Frob}_q$ on the 6-point fiber of $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_{q^6} \to \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q$? $\mathrm{Frob}_q^a$ generates $\mathbb{Z}/6$ iff $\gcd(a, 6) = 1$, i.e. $a \in \{1, 5\}$ (these are the units mod 6). For $a = 6$, $\mathrm{Frob}_q^6$ is the identity, not a generator. For $a \in \{2, 3, 4\}$, the action has multiple orbits — the cover decomposes when restricted to $\mathbb{F}_{q^d}$ for $d = 6/\gcd(a,6)$. Generators of $\mathbb{Z}/n$ are exactly the units mod $n$.

q: For a smooth complex variety $X$, what is the relationship between $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X)$ and $\pi_1^{\mathrm{top}}(X(\mathbb{C}))$?
text: For a smooth complex variety $X$, what is the relationship between $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X)$ and $\pi_1^{\mathrm{top}}(X(\mathbb{C}))$? Riemann's existence theorem: every finite topological cover of $X(\mathbb{C})$ is the analytification of a unique finite étale cover, so finite quotients agree. Taking inverse limits: $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X) = \widehat{\pi_1^{\mathrm{top}}(X(\mathbb{C}))}$. They are NOT isomorphic in general — $\pi_1^{\mathrm{top}}(\mathbb{C}^\times) = \mathbb{Z}$ while $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathbb{C}}) = \hat{\mathbb{Z}}$. Profinite completion always loses non-finite-quotient information.

q: Match each smooth complex variety with its étale fundamental group.
text: Match each smooth complex variety with its étale fundamental group. $\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}$ topologically $\simeq \mathbb{C}$ is contractible: $\pi_1^{\mathrm{top}} = 1$, so $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t} = 1$. $\mathbb{G}_m$: $\pi_1^{\mathrm{top}} = \mathbb{Z}$, completion $\hat{\mathbb{Z}}$. Elliptic curve: $\pi_1^{\mathrm{top}} = \mathbb{Z}^2$, completion $\hat{\mathbb{Z}}^2$. $\mathbb{P}^1_{\mathbb{C}} = S^2$: simply connected. Compute the topological $\pi_1$ first, then take the profinite completion.

q: Where does this 'proof' that $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{F}_p}) = 1$ go wrong?
text: Where does this 'proof' that $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{F}_p}) = 1$ go wrong? Step 3 is wrong. $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}$ is highly sensitive to the base field. Over $\mathbb{F}_p$, $\mathbb{A}^1$ admits Artin–Schreier covers $y^p - y = f(x)$ for any $f \in \mathbb{F}_p[x]$ — these are étale, connected, and Galois with group $\mathbb{Z}/p$. So $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{F}_p})$ is enormous (a huge profinite group with infinitely many $\mathbb{Z}/p$ quotients). Riemann existence applies only over $\mathbb{C}$. Have you ever seen $y^p - y = x$? Is it étale?

q: Let $X$ be a smooth proper variety over $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$. Embed $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}\hookrightarrow\mathbb{C}$ to form $X_{\mathbb{C}}$. What is the relationship between $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X)$ and $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X_{\mathbb{C}})$?
text: Let $X$ be a smooth proper variety over $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$. Embed $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}\hookrightarrow\mathbb{C}$ to form $X_{\mathbb{C}}$. What is the relationship between $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X)$ and $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X_{\mathbb{C}})$? For a geometrically connected proper variety over an algebraically closed field, étale base change to a larger algebraically closed field is an equivalence on $\mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X$ (this uses properness — without it, base change can introduce new covers, e.g. $\mathbb{A}^1$ acquires more covers under $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}\hookrightarrow\mathbb{C}$ via transcendental extensions). So $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X) = \pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X_{\mathbb{C}})$ for $X$ proper smooth. Properness is essential — what fails for $X = \mathbb{A}^1$?

title: Smooth projective curves and their genus
blurb: A smooth projective curve over an algebraically closed field $k$ is a smooth projective integral $k$-scheme of dimension 1. Its genus $g$ is the dimension of $H^0(C, \Omega^1_C)$ — over $\mathbb{C}$ this matches the topological genus of the Riemann surface $C(\mathbb{C})$.

title: Divisors on curves
blurb: A divisor $D = \sum n_p [p]$ is a finite formal $\mathbb{Z}$-sum of points. The degree is $\deg D = \sum n_p$, and $\mathrm{Pic}^d(C)$ classifies linear-equivalence classes of degree-$d$ divisors. $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$ is the Jacobian — an abelian variety of dimension $g$.

title: Riemann–Roch on curves
blurb: For a divisor $D$ on a smooth projective curve of genus $g$, $h^0(D) - h^0(K - D) = \deg D - g + 1$, where $K$ is the canonical divisor. Riemann–Roch is the curve-level explanation of why genus controls the dimension of linear systems.

title: The canonical embedding
blurb: If $g \ge 2$ and $C$ is non-hyperelliptic, the canonical divisor $K$ embeds $C \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^{g-1}$ as a curve of degree $2g - 2$. The image is the canonical model — the most natural projective realization of an abstract curve.

title: Hyperelliptic curves
blurb: A curve of genus $g \ge 2$ is hyperelliptic if it admits a degree-2 map to $\mathbb{P}^1$. Equivalently, $|K|$ does not separate points. Hyperelliptic curves form a codimension-$(g-2)$ locus in $\mathcal{M}_g$ — most curves of genus $\ge 3$ are not hyperelliptic.

title: Moduli of higher-genus curves $\mathcal{M}_g$
blurb: $\mathcal{M}_g$ is the moduli space (DM stack) of smooth projective curves of genus $g$. For $g \ge 2$ it has dimension $3g - 3$; the Deligne–Mumford compactification $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ adds stable nodal curves at the boundary.

q: Let $C$ be a smooth projective curve of genus $g = 5$ over $\bar k$. What is $\dim_k H^0(C, \Omega^1_C)$?
text: Let $C$ be a smooth projective curve of genus $g = 5$ over $\bar k$. What is $\dim_k H^0(C, \Omega^1_C)$? By definition the genus equals $h^0(\Omega^1_C)$. So $\dim H^0(C, \Omega^1_C) = g = 5$. The genus is the dimension of the space of regular 1-forms — that's the definition.

q: A smooth projective curve $C(\mathbb{C})$ has topological Euler characteristic $\chi_{\mathrm{top}} = -8$. What is its genus $g$?
text: A smooth projective curve $C(\mathbb{C})$ has topological Euler characteristic $\chi_{\mathrm{top}} = -8$. What is its genus $g$? $\chi_{\mathrm{top}} = 2 - 2g$, so $-8 = 2 - 2g \Rightarrow g = 5$. Use $\chi = 2 - 2g$.

q: A degree-3 cover $\pi\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^1$ has $b = 8$ simple branch points (each contributing $1$ to the ramification sum). Compute the genus of $C$ by Riemann–Hurwitz.
text: A degree-3 cover $\pi\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^1$ has $b = 8$ simple branch points (each contributing $1$ to the ramification sum). Compute the genus of $C$ by Riemann–Hurwitz. Riemann–Hurwitz: $2g - 2 = d(2 g_{\mathbb{P}^1} - 2) + \sum (e_p - 1) = 3 \cdot (-2) + 8 = 2$, so $g = 2$. $2g - 2 = d(2 g_{\mathbb{P}^1} - 2) + b$ for simple branching.

q: Which of the following is FALSE for a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g$ over an algebraically closed field?
text: Which of the following is FALSE for a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g$ over an algebraically closed field? Only hyperelliptic curves have a $g^1_2$ — for $g \ge 3$ the generic curve is non-hyperelliptic. The Brill–Noether-style gonality bound $\lfloor (g+3)/2 \rfloor$ is correct. Most curves of genus $\ge 3$ do NOT admit a degree-2 map to $\mathbb{P}^1$.

q: On a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g = 4$ over $\mathbb{C}$, what is the complex dimension of the Jacobian $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$?
text: On a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g = 4$ over $\mathbb{C}$, what is the complex dimension of the Jacobian $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$? $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C) = \mathbb{C}^g / \Lambda$ has complex dimension $g$. So for $g = 4$, $\dim_{\mathbb{C}} \mathrm{Pic}^0(C) = 4$. $\dim \mathrm{Pic}^0 = g$.

q: Let $D = 2[p] - 3[q] + [r]$ on a smooth projective curve. Compute $\deg D$.
text: Let $D = 2[p] - 3[q] + [r]$ on a smooth projective curve. Compute $\deg D$. $\deg D = 2 + (-3) + 1 = 0$. Sum the integer coefficients.

q: Two divisors $D, D'$ on a smooth projective curve are linearly equivalent iff:
text: Two divisors $D, D'$ on a smooth projective curve are linearly equivalent iff: Linear equivalence is by definition: $D \sim D'$ iff $D - D'$ is a principal divisor, i.e. the divisor of some rational function. This is the equivalence relation defining $\mathrm{Pic}(C)$.

q: On a smooth projective curve of genus $g = 3$, take $D$ with $\deg D = 5$. What is $h^0(D)$? (Use the high-degree vanishing: $\deg D > 2g - 2$.)
text: On a smooth projective curve of genus $g = 3$, take $D$ with $\deg D = 5$. What is $h^0(D)$? (Use the high-degree vanishing: $\deg D > 2g - 2$.) $\deg D = 5 > 2g - 2 = 4$, so $h^0(K - D) = 0$ and Riemann–Roch gives $h^0(D) = \deg D - g + 1 = 5 - 3 + 1 = 3$. Past the threshold $\deg D > 2g-2$, $h^0(D) = \deg D - g + 1$.

q: On a curve of genus $g = 2$, $D = K_C$ (the canonical divisor). Compute $h^0(D)$.
text: On a curve of genus $g = 2$, $D = K_C$ (the canonical divisor). Compute $h^0(D)$. $h^0(K_C) = g = 2$ — this is the definition of genus restated, and Riemann–Roch with $D = K$ recovers it: $h^0(K) - h^0(0) = (2g-2) - g + 1 = g - 1$, with $h^0(0) = 1$ giving $h^0(K) = g$.

q: On a curve of genus $g = 4$, take $D$ with $\deg D = 3$. Suppose further that $h^0(K - D) = 2$. What is $h^0(D)$?
text: On a curve of genus $g = 4$, take $D$ with $\deg D = 3$. Suppose further that $h^0(K - D) = 2$. What is $h^0(D)$? Riemann–Roch: $h^0(D) - h^0(K - D) = \deg D - g + 1 = 3 - 4 + 1 = 0$. So $h^0(D) = h^0(K - D) = 2$. Just plug $\deg D = 3$, $g = 4$, $h^0(K-D) = 2$ into RR and solve for $h^0(D)$.

q: On a non-hyperelliptic genus-$3$ curve $C$, let $D = p + q + r$ with $p, q, r$ three distinct points whose canonical-embedding images are NOT collinear. Riemann–Roch then gives $h^0(D) = 1$. Compute $h^0(K - D)$.
text: On a non-hyperelliptic genus-$3$ curve $C$, let $D = p + q + r$ with $p, q, r$ three distinct points whose canonical-embedding images are NOT collinear. Riemann–Roch then gives $h^0(D) = 1$. Compute $h^0(K - D)$. Riemann–Roch: $h^0(D) - h^0(K - D) = \deg D - g + 1 = 3 - 3 + 1 = 1$. With $h^0(D) = 1$ this gives $h^0(K - D) = 0$. Equivalently: $K - D$ has degree $4 - 3 = 1$, but the three points are not collinear in $\mathbb{P}^2$, so no canonical hyperplane (= line in the canonical $\mathbb{P}^2$) cuts out $D$, and $|K - D|$ is empty. Use $h^0(D) - h^0(K-D) = \deg D - g + 1$ with $h^0(D) = 1$, $g = 3$, $\deg D = 3$.

q: On a curve $C$ of genus $g$, the linear system $|D|$ defines a morphism $\varphi_D\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^{r}$ where $r = h^0(D) - 1$. For $|D|$ to give a closed embedding, the standard sufficient condition is:
text: On a curve $C$ of genus $g$, the linear system $|D|$ defines a morphism $\varphi_D\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^{r}$ where $r = h^0(D) - 1$. For $|D|$ to give a closed embedding, the standard sufficient condition is: On a smooth projective curve, $\deg D \ge 2g + 1$ guarantees very ample (and $\ge 2g$ guarantees base-point-free + injective on tangent vectors generically). The standard cleanest threshold is $2g + 1$ ⇒ very ample, so 'at least $2g$' is the correct intuition. Of the four options, (a) is the canonical sufficient condition for very ampleness.

q: On a smooth projective curve of genus $g = 5$, what is $\deg K_C$?
text: On a smooth projective curve of genus $g = 5$, what is $\deg K_C$? $\deg K_C = 2g - 2 = 2 \cdot 5 - 2 = 8$.

q: If $C$ is a non-hyperelliptic smooth projective curve of genus $g = 4$, into what projective space $\mathbb{P}^N$ does the canonical map embed $C$? Give $N$.
text: If $C$ is a non-hyperelliptic smooth projective curve of genus $g = 4$, into what projective space $\mathbb{P}^N$ does the canonical map embed $C$? Give $N$. $\varphi_K\colon C \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^{g-1} = \mathbb{P}^3$ for $g = 4$. So $N = g - 1 = 3$. Canonical map lands in $\mathbb{P}^{g-1}$.

q: A smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g \ge 3$ is non-hyperelliptic if and only if:
text: A smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g \ge 3$ is non-hyperelliptic if and only if: Max Noether/Enriques–Babbage: $\varphi_K$ is an embedding ⇔ $C$ is non-hyperelliptic (assuming $g \ge 2$). For hyperelliptic $C$, $\varphi_K$ factors through the $2:1$ map to $\mathbb{P}^1$ followed by the rational normal curve.

q: Which of the following is the correct genus-$3$ canonical model dichotomy?
text: Which of the following is the correct genus-$3$ canonical model dichotomy? For $g = 3$: non-hyperelliptic curves embed as smooth plane quartics ($\deg = 2g-2 = 4$ in $\mathbb{P}^{g-1} = \mathbb{P}^2$); hyperelliptic curves have $\varphi_K$ factoring through $C \xrightarrow{2:1} \mathbb{P}^1 \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^2$ as a smooth conic. Choice (c) is the genus-4 dichotomy.

q: A smooth canonical curve of genus $g = 6$ in $\mathbb{P}^5$ has degree:
text: A smooth canonical curve of genus $g = 6$ in $\mathbb{P}^5$ has degree: Canonical degree is $2g - 2 = 2 \cdot 6 - 2 = 10$. $\deg K = 2g - 2$.

q: How many branch points does a hyperelliptic cover $\pi\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^1$ have when $C$ has genus $g = 5$?
text: How many branch points does a hyperelliptic cover $\pi\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^1$ have when $C$ has genus $g = 5$? For a hyperelliptic curve of genus $g$, the degree-$2$ cover has $2g + 2$ branch points (Riemann–Hurwitz with $d = 2$, simple ramification: $2g - 2 = 2(-2) + b \Rightarrow b = 2g + 2$). For $g = 5$: $b = 12$. Number of branch points is $2g + 2$.

q: Select all curves which are hyperelliptic. (Assume each is smooth projective over $\bar k$ and the equations are written affinely.)
text: Select all curves which are hyperelliptic. (Assume each is smooth projective over $\bar k$ and the equations are written affinely.) (a) $y^2 = $ (degree 5) is hyperelliptic of genus 2. (c) $y^2 = $ (degree 7) is hyperelliptic of genus 3. (d) every genus-2 curve is hyperelliptic (canonical map is the unique $g^1_2$). The smooth plane quartic in (b) is the canonical model of a non-hyperelliptic genus-3 curve (generic case). Hyperelliptic ⇔ $y^2 = f(x)$ with $f$ separable of degree $2g+1$ or $2g+2$, OR genus $\le 2$.

q: On a hyperelliptic curve of genus $g = 4$ with hyperelliptic involution $\sigma$, how many fixed points does $\sigma$ have on $C$?
text: On a hyperelliptic curve of genus $g = 4$ with hyperelliptic involution $\sigma$, how many fixed points does $\sigma$ have on $C$? Fixed points of $\sigma$ are exactly the ramification points of $\pi\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^1$, which sit one above each branch point. Number = $2g + 2 = 10$. Ramification points = fixed points of involution = $2g + 2$.

q: What is $\dim \mathcal{M}_g$ for $g = 7$?
text: What is $\dim \mathcal{M}_g$ for $g = 7$? For $g \ge 2$, $\dim \mathcal{M}_g = 3g - 3 = 3 \cdot 7 - 3 = 18$. Use $\dim \mathcal{M}_g = 3g - 3$.

q: What is the codimension of the hyperelliptic locus $\mathcal{H}_g \subset \mathcal{M}_g$ for $g = 5$?
text: What is the codimension of the hyperelliptic locus $\mathcal{H}_g \subset \mathcal{M}_g$ for $g = 5$? $\mathcal{H}_g$ has dimension $2g - 1$ inside $\mathcal{M}_g$ of dimension $3g - 3$, so codimension $(3g-3) - (2g-1) = g - 2$. For $g = 5$: codim $= 3$. Codimension is $g - 2$.

q: The Deligne–Mumford compactification $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ adds boundary divisors $\Delta_0, \Delta_1, \ldots, \Delta_{\lfloor g/2 \rfloor}$. The divisor $\Delta_0$ parametrises:
text: The Deligne–Mumford compactification $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ adds boundary divisors $\Delta_0, \Delta_1, \ldots, \Delta_{\lfloor g/2 \rfloor}$. The divisor $\Delta_0$ parametrises: $\Delta_0$ is the boundary divisor of irreducible nodal curves: pinch a non-separating loop in $C$ to a node, get an irreducible curve whose normalisation has genus $g - 1$. The arithmetic genus stays $g$. Pinch a non-separating loop on $C$ — what stays connected?

q: For $g = 5$, how many distinct boundary divisors $\Delta_i$ does $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ have? (Count all $\Delta_i$ with $0 \le i \le \lfloor g/2 \rfloor$.)
text: For $g = 5$, how many distinct boundary divisors $\Delta_i$ does $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ have? (Count all $\Delta_i$ with $0 \le i \le \lfloor g/2 \rfloor$.) Boundary divisors are $\Delta_0, \Delta_1, \Delta_2$ (since $\lfloor 5/2 \rfloor = 2$ and $\Delta_i$ for $i = 0, 1, 2$ are distinct: $\Delta_0$ irreducible nodal, $\Delta_1$ the (1, 4)-split, $\Delta_2$ the (2, 3)-split). Total $= 3$. Count $\lfloor g/2 \rfloor + 1$ for $g$ odd.

q: The codimension of any single boundary divisor $\Delta_i \subset \overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ is:
text: The codimension of any single boundary divisor $\Delta_i \subset \overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ is: By definition / construction $\Delta_i$ is a boundary divisor — codimension $1$. Pinching $k$ disjoint cycles produces a stratum of codimension $k$; pinching exactly one cycle (the generic point of $\Delta_i$) produces codim 1.

title: What is a group scheme?
blurb: A group scheme over $S$ is an $S$-scheme $G$ equipped with multiplication $m: G \times_S G \to G$, inverse $\iota: G \to G$, and identity $e: S \to G$ satisfying the group axioms diagrammatically. Equivalently, a group object in the category of $S$-schemes.

title: Examples: $\mathbb{G}_a, \mathbb{G}_m, \mu_n, \alpha_p$
blurb: $\mathbb{G}_a = \mathrm{Spec}\,k[t]$ (additive), $\mathbb{G}_m = \mathrm{Spec}\,k[t, t^{-1}]$ (multiplicative), $\mu_n = \mathrm{Spec}\,k[t]/(t^n - 1)$ (roots of unity), and in characteristic $p$ the infinitesimal $\alpha_p = \mathrm{Spec}\,k[t]/(t^p)$. The four basic affine group schemes.

title: Affine group schemes ↔ commutative Hopf algebras
blurb: An affine group scheme $G = \mathrm{Spec}\,A$ is the same data as a commutative Hopf algebra $A$: comultiplication $\Delta: A \to A \otimes A$, counit $\epsilon: A \to k$, and antipode $S: A \to A$ from the multiplication, identity, and inverse maps.

title: Étale, connected, and infinitesimal pieces
blurb: Over a perfect field, every finite commutative group scheme decomposes canonically as $G$ as a semidirect product of a connected (infinitesimal) piece $G^\circ$ and an étale piece $G^{\acute{e}t}$, with $G^\circ$ connected (infinitesimal) and $G^{\acute{e}t}$ étale. In characteristic 0, $G^\circ$ is trivial and group schemes reduce to ordinary discrete groups; in char $p$, $\alpha_p, \mu_p$ live in $G^\circ$.

title: Lie algebra $\mathrm{Lie}(G)$
blurb: $\mathrm{Lie}(G) = \ker(G(k[\epsilon]/\epsilon^2) \to G(k))$ — the tangent space at the identity, with bracket from the adjoint action. Recovers the classical Lie algebra of a Lie group when $k = \mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$ and $G$ is smooth.

title: $G$-torsors and $H^1(X, G)$
blurb: A $G$-torsor over $X$ is a scheme $P \to X$ with a free transitive $G$-action that is étale-locally trivial. Isomorphism classes of $G$-torsors are classified by the étale cohomology $H^1_{\acute{e}t}(X, G)$ — generalizes Galois cohomology and the Picard group ($G = \mathbb{G}_m$).

q: Which of the following are group schemes over $\mathbb{Z}$?
text: Which of the following are group schemes over $\mathbb{Z}$? $\mathrm{GL}_n$ and $\mathbb{G}_a$ are group schemes (one comes with matrix multiplication, the other with addition).
