text: Let $E(\mathbb{Q})$ have rank $1$ with generator $P$ satisfying $\hat h(P) = 0.5051$. Compute $\hat h(3P)$. $\hat h(3P) = 3^2 \cdot \hat h(P) = 9 \times 0.5051 = 4.5459$. The canonical height is exactly quadratic: $\hat h(nP)=n^2\hat h(P)$.

q: Which property distinguishes the Néron–Tate canonical height $\hat h$ from the naive Weil height $h$ of the $x$-coordinate?
text: Which property distinguishes the Néron–Tate canonical height $\hat h$ from the naive Weil height $h$ of the $x$-coordinate? The Weil height $h(P)$ satisfies $h(nP) = n^2 h(P) + O(1)$. Tate’s limiting process $\hat h(P) = \lim_{n\to\infty} h(2^n P)/4^n$ removes the $O(1)$ error, making $\hat h$ exactly quadratic. Crucially, $\hat h(P)=0$ iff $P$ is torsion.

q: A rank-$2$ curve has height-pairing matrix $\begin{pmatrix}4 & 2\\ 2 & 4\end{pmatrix}$. Is this matrix positive definite? (Enter $1$ for yes, $0$ for no.)
text: A rank-$2$ curve has height-pairing matrix $\begin{pmatrix}4 & 2\\ 2 & 4\end{pmatrix}$. Is this matrix positive definite? (Enter $1$ for yes, $0$ for no.) Check: $\det = 4\cdot 4 - 2\cdot 2 = 12 > 0$ and the leading entry $4 > 0$, so Sylvester’s criterion confirms positive definiteness. The canonical height pairing on the free part is always positive definite.

q: An element of $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ corresponds geometrically to:
text: An element of $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ corresponds geometrically to: $\Sha = \ker\bigl(H^1(\mathbb{Q},E) \to \prod_v H^1(\mathbb{Q}_v,E)\bigr)$ classifies principal homogeneous spaces for $E$ that are everywhere locally trivial but globally nontrivial — failures of the Hasse principle.

q: If $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ is finite, the Cassels–Tate pairing forces $|\Sha|$ to be a perfect square. Which of the following integers CANNOT be the order of a finite $\Sha$?
text: If $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ is finite, the Cassels–Tate pairing forces $|\Sha|$ to be a perfect square. Which of the following integers CANNOT be the order of a finite $\Sha$? Cassels–Tate gives $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ a non-degenerate alternating pairing, which forces its order to be a perfect square. Among the listed values, $1=1^2$, $4=2^2$, $9=3^2$, and $25=5^2$ are all squares; $6$ is not, so it is forbidden. Which of the options isn't a perfect square?

q: The $n$-descent sequence reads $0 \to E(\mathbb{Q})/nE(\mathbb{Q}) \to \mathrm{Sel}_n(E) \to \Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})[n] \to 0$. If $|\mathrm{Sel}_2(E)| = 32$ and $|E(\mathbb{Q})/2E(\mathbb{Q})| = 8$, then $|\Sha(E)[2]|$ is:
text: The $n$-descent sequence reads $0 \to E(\mathbb{Q})/nE(\mathbb{Q}) \to \mathrm{Sel}_n(E) \to \Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})[n] \to 0$. If $|\mathrm{Sel}_2(E)| = 32$ and $|E(\mathbb{Q})/2E(\mathbb{Q})| = 8$, then $|\Sha(E)[2]|$ is: From the exact sequence, $|\Sha[2]| = |\mathrm{Sel}_2|/|E(\mathbb{Q})/2E| = 32/8 = 4$. This is the standard way $2$-descent pins down $|\Sha[2]|$ even when the full group is out of reach.

q: The Cassels–Tate pairing on $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ is alternating and non-degenerate (when $\Sha$ is finite). Which of the following is the correct logical chain forcing $|\Sha|$ to be a perfect square?
text: The Cassels–Tate pairing on $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ is alternating and non-degenerate (when $\Sha$ is finite). Which of the following is the correct logical chain forcing $|\Sha|$ to be a perfect square? A finite abelian group with a non-degenerate alternating bilinear pairing to $\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ is self-dual, and the alternating condition forces it to be isomorphic to $B \oplus B$ for some $B$. Hence $|\Sha| = |B|^2$. An alternating form pairs every element with itself to zero — what does that force on the group?

q: A genus-$1$ curve $C/\mathbb{Q}$ represents a non-trivial element of $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$. What must be true of $C$?
text: A genus-$1$ curve $C/\mathbb{Q}$ represents a non-trivial element of $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$. What must be true of $C$? $\Sha$ consists of everywhere-locally-trivial torsors: $C$ must be locally solvable (points over every $\mathbb{Q}_p$ and $\mathbb{R}$) yet have no global $\mathbb{Q}$-point. This is a pure failure of the Hasse principle, with no single local obstruction.

q: Suppose $|\mathrm{Sel}_3(E)| = 27$ and $|E(\mathbb{Q})/3E(\mathbb{Q})| = 3$. Using the descent exact sequence $0 \to E(\mathbb{Q})/3E(\mathbb{Q}) \to \mathrm{Sel}_3(E) \to \Sha(E)[3] \to 0$, compute $|\Sha(E)[3]|$.
text: Suppose $|\mathrm{Sel}_3(E)| = 27$ and $|E(\mathbb{Q})/3E(\mathbb{Q})| = 3$. Using the descent exact sequence $0 \to E(\mathbb{Q})/3E(\mathbb{Q}) \to \mathrm{Sel}_3(E) \to \Sha(E)[3] \to 0$, compute $|\Sha(E)[3]|$. From the exact sequence, $|\Sha[3]| = |\mathrm{Sel}_3|/|E(\mathbb{Q})/3E(\mathbb{Q})| = 27/3 = 9$. This is consistent with $|\Sha|$ being a perfect square.

title: The Frey curve
blurb: From a hypothetical solution $a^p + b^p = c^p$ to Fermat, Frey attaches $E_{a,b,c}: y^2 = x(x - a^p)(x + b^p)$. Its discriminant is $2^{-8}(abc)^{2p}$: a perfect $2p$-th power away from 2, which makes the curve semistable and forces very mild ramification.

title: Modularity theorem
blurb: Every elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$ of conductor $N$ corresponds to a weight-2 newform $f_E \in S_2(\Gamma_0(N))$ with matching Frobenius traces $a_p(E) = a_p(f_E)$. Wiles + Taylor–Wiles (1995) handled the semistable case; BCDT (2001) extended to all $E/\mathbb{Q}$.

title: Ribet's level lowering
blurb: Ribet's $\varepsilon$-theorem: if $\overline\rho_{E,p}$ is modular of level $Np$ and unramified at $p$, it is modular at level $N$. Applied to the Frey curve this collapses the level to $2$, where $S_2(\Gamma_0(2)) = 0$ contradicts the Fermat solution.

title: Deformation rings
blurb: Given a residual $\overline\rho\colon G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$, Mazur's universal deformation ring $R_\Sigma$ represents lifts to complete Noetherian local rings with prescribed ramification $\Sigma$; it is the formal moduli of Galois deformations.

title: The R = T theorem
blurb: Wiles' lifting engine identifies $R_\Sigma$ with the localized Hecke algebra $\mathbb{T}_\Sigma$ on weight-2 cusp forms; the Taylor–Wiles patching argument upgrades $R_\Sigma \twoheadrightarrow \mathbb{T}_\Sigma$ to an isomorphism, so every deformation is modular.

q: Let $a,b,c$ be a hypothetical coprime solution of $a^p + b^p = c^p$ with $p \ge 5$ prime. The Frey curve $E_{a,b,c}: y^2 = x(x-a^p)(x+b^p)$ has discriminant equal to which of the following?
text: Let $a,b,c$ be a hypothetical coprime solution of $a^p + b^p = c^p$ with $p \ge 5$ prime. The Frey curve $E_{a,b,c}: y^2 = x(x-a^p)(x+b^p)$ has discriminant equal to which of the following? For $y^2 = x(x-\alpha)(x+\beta)$ with $\gamma = \alpha+\beta$, the discriminant is $16(\alpha\beta\gamma)^2$. With $\alpha=a^p,\beta=b^p,\gamma=c^p$ this gives $16\,(a b c)^{2p}$; after the standard change of variables to minimal model the factor of $2^{8}$ drops out, leaving $\Delta = (abc)^{2p}/2^{8}$.

q: The Frey discriminant is $\Delta=(abc)^{2p}/2^{8}$. Suppose $\ell$ is an odd prime dividing $abc$ exactly once (i.e. $v_\ell(abc)=1$), and take $p=5$. Compute $v_\ell(\Delta)\bmod p$.
text: The Frey discriminant is $\Delta=(abc)^{2p}/2^{8}$. Suppose $\ell$ is an odd prime dividing $abc$ exactly once (i.e. $v_\ell(abc)=1$), and take $p=5$. Compute $v_\ell(\Delta)\bmod p$. At an odd prime $\ell\mid abc$, the factor of $2^8$ doesn't affect $v_\ell$, so $v_\ell(\Delta)=2p\cdot v_\ell(abc)=2\cdot 5\cdot 1=10$, and $10\equiv 0\pmod 5$. This divisibility by $p$ is precisely what makes $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ unramified at $\ell$, enabling Ribet's level-lowering to strip $\ell$ from the level. $v_\ell(\Delta)=2p\cdot v_\ell(abc)$ at odd $\ell$; what is that value mod $p$?

q: The prime factorization of the Frey discriminant is a perfect $2p$-th power away from $2$. This forces the Frey curve to be:
text: The prime factorization of the Frey discriminant is a perfect $2p$-th power away from $2$. This forces the Frey curve to be: A prime $\ell \mid abc$ with $\ell$ odd appears in $\Delta$ to multiplicity $2p$, i.e. a perfect square. By Tate's algorithm this gives multiplicative reduction, so the curve is semistable; the conductor $N_E$ is squarefree away from $2$.

q: The Frey curve $E_{a,b,c}$ is constructed so that its conductor $N_E$ is divisible only by primes dividing $2abc$. Which property of the discriminant $\Delta = (abc)^{2p}/2^{8}$ forces the conductor to be squarefree at odd primes?
text: The Frey curve $E_{a,b,c}$ is constructed so that its conductor $N_E$ is divisible only by primes dividing $2abc$. Which property of the discriminant $\Delta = (abc)^{2p}/2^{8}$ forces the conductor to be squarefree at odd primes? Tate's algorithm: if $v_\ell(\Delta) > 0$ but the curve has a node (not a cusp) after minimal change of variables, reduction is multiplicative, contributing $\ell^1$ to the conductor. The $2p$-th power structure in $\Delta$ at odd primes ensures exactly nodal reduction, giving squarefree conductor at those primes. Think about the reduction type when $v_\ell(\Delta)$ is a multiple of $p$.

q: For the formal Frey discriminant $\Delta = (abc)^{2p}/2^8$, compute $v_5(\Delta)$ (the $5$-adic valuation) when $a = 5,\ b = 4,\ c = ?$ and $p = 7$. (Only $a = 5$ contributes a factor of $5$; treat $b,c$ as coprime to $5$.) Enter the integer value.
text: For the formal Frey discriminant $\Delta = (abc)^{2p}/2^8$, compute $v_5(\Delta)$ (the $5$-adic valuation) when $a = 5,\ b = 4,\ c = ?$ and $p = 7$. (Only $a = 5$ contributes a factor of $5$; treat $b,c$ as coprime to $5$.) Enter the integer value. $v_5(\Delta) = 2p \cdot v_5(abc) = 2 \cdot 7 \cdot v_5(5) = 14 \cdot 1 = 14$. This is a perfect $2p$-th power valuation, ensuring multiplicative reduction at $5$.

q: Serre's original conjecture (now Khare–Wintenberger theorem) predicts that every odd irreducible $\overline{\rho}: G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$ is modular. In the FLT proof, which weaker statement about $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ actually suffices to apply Ribet's theorem?
text: Serre's original conjecture (now Khare–Wintenberger theorem) predicts that every odd irreducible $\overline{\rho}: G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$ is modular. In the FLT proof, which weaker statement about $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ actually suffices to apply Ribet's theorem? Wiles proves $E$ is modular (as a full $p$-adic representation), from which the residual representation $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ is automatically modular. Ribet's theorem then applies to lower the level. The full Serre conjecture is not needed.

q: Which space of modular forms is matched to an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$ of conductor $N$ by the modularity theorem?
text: Which space of modular forms is matched to an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$ of conductor $N$ by the modularity theorem? Modularity: $E$ corresponds to a weight-2 newform $f_E \in S_2(\Gamma_0(N_E))$ with $a_p(E) = a_p(f_E)$ for all good primes $p$. The weight is $2$ because $E$ has dimension $1$ (H^1 is 2-dimensional).

q: Cremona's curve $E: y^2 + y = x^3 - x^2$ has conductor $11$. Its associated newform has the $q$-expansion $q - 2q^2 - q^3 + 2q^4 + q^5 + \cdots$. Independently, one can verify $\#E(\mathbb{F}_2) = 5$. What is $a_2(f_E)$? (Use $a_p(E) = p + 1 - \#E(\mathbb{F}_p)$.)
text: Cremona's curve $E: y^2 + y = x^3 - x^2$ has conductor $11$. Its associated newform has the $q$-expansion $q - 2q^2 - q^3 + 2q^4 + q^5 + \cdots$. Independently, one can verify $\#E(\mathbb{F}_2) = 5$. What is $a_2(f_E)$? (Use $a_p(E) = p + 1 - \#E(\mathbb{F}_p)$.) $a_2(E) = 2 + 1 - 5 = -2$. Modularity says $a_2(f_E) = a_2(E) = -2$, which matches the $-2q^2$ coefficient of the newform $\eta(\tau)^2\eta(11\tau)^2$.

q: Wiles and Taylor–Wiles (1995) proved modularity for semistable elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$. Who extended this to every elliptic curve over $\mathbb{Q}$?
text: Wiles and Taylor–Wiles (1995) proved modularity for semistable elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$. Who extended this to every elliptic curve over $\mathbb{Q}$? The full modularity theorem was completed in 2001 by Breuil, Conrad, Diamond, and Taylor, handling the remaining cases of additive reduction at $3$.

q: The newform $f_E$ associated to the curve $E: y^2 = x^3 - x^2 - 10x - 10$ of conductor $N = 20$ satisfies $a_3(f_E) = a_3(E) = 3 + 1 - \#E(\mathbb{F}_3)$. Given $\#E(\mathbb{F}_3) = 4$, what is $a_3(f_E)$?
text: The newform $f_E$ associated to the curve $E: y^2 = x^3 - x^2 - 10x - 10$ of conductor $N = 20$ satisfies $a_3(f_E) = a_3(E) = 3 + 1 - \#E(\mathbb{F}_3)$. Given $\#E(\mathbb{F}_3) = 4$, what is $a_3(f_E)$? $a_3(E) = 3 + 1 - 4 = 0$. By the modularity theorem, $a_3(f_E) = a_3(E) = 0$, meaning $3$ is a root of the $L$-factor at $p = 3$ in the newform's $L$-function.

q: The modularity theorem implies that the $L$-function $L(E, s)$ equals the $L$-function $L(f_E, s)$ of a weight-2 newform. Which analytic property does this immediately grant $L(E, s)$?
text: The modularity theorem implies that the $L$-function $L(E, s)$ equals the $L$-function $L(f_E, s)$ of a weight-2 newform. Which analytic property does this immediately grant $L(E, s)$? Hecke's theory gives $L(f, s)$ an analytic continuation and functional equation for any newform $f$. Via modularity $L(E, s) = L(f_E, s)$, the elliptic curve $L$-function inherits both properties — this is essential for BSD and other applications.

q: The space $S_2(\Gamma_0(N))$ has dimension given by the genus formula. For which level $N$ does one first get a nonzero space $S_2(\Gamma_0(N)) \neq 0$?
text: The space $S_2(\Gamma_0(N))$ has dimension given by the genus formula. For which level $N$ does one first get a nonzero space $S_2(\Gamma_0(N)) \neq 0$? The genus of $X_0(N)$ (hence $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(N))$) is $0$ for small $N$. The first $N$ with genus $\ge 1$ is $N = 11$, where $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(11)) = 1$, spanned by the newform $\eta(\tau)^2\eta(11\tau)^2$.

q: Ribet's level-lowering, applied to the mod-$p$ Galois representation of the Frey curve, lowers the level from the Frey conductor down to the squarefree part of $\{2\}$: the final level is $N = 2$. What is $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(2))$?
text: Ribet's level-lowering, applied to the mod-$p$ Galois representation of the Frey curve, lowers the level from the Frey conductor down to the squarefree part of $\{2\}$: the final level is $N = 2$. What is $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(2))$? The genus of $X_0(2)$ is $0$, and $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(N)) = \mathrm{genus}(X_0(N))$. So $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(2)) = 0$: there are no weight-2 cusp forms of level 2. This is the contradiction that finishes FLT.

q: Ribet's theorem is sometimes called the ``$\varepsilon$-conjecture.'' Conceptually, what principle does it embody?
text: Ribet's theorem is sometimes called the ``$\varepsilon$-conjecture.'' Conceptually, what principle does it embody? Ribet proved that superfluous primes in the level can be removed whenever the residual Galois representation is unramified there. Applied to the Frey curve, this strips the level all the way down to $2$.

q: Put the pieces together. Assuming $a^p+b^p=c^p$ (with $p\ge 5$, $abc\ne0$), the Frey curve $E_{a,b,c}$ is semistable, hence modular (Wiles), and Ribet's theorem lowers the level of $\overline\rho_{E,p}$ to $2$. Why is this a contradiction?
text: Put the pieces together. Assuming $a^p+b^p=c^p$ (with $p\ge 5$, $abc\ne0$), the Frey curve $E_{a,b,c}$ is semistable, hence modular (Wiles), and Ribet's theorem lowers the level of $\overline\rho_{E,p}$ to $2$. Why is this a contradiction? After Ribet, $\overline\rho_{E,p}$ would come from a weight-2 newform in $S_2(\Gamma_0(2))$. But $X_0(2)$ has genus $0$, so $S_2(\Gamma_0(2)) = 0$. No such newform exists — contradiction, and therefore $a^p+b^p=c^p$ has no nontrivial integer solutions.

q: Ribet's level-lowering requires the residual representation $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ to be irreducible. Why does this hold for the Frey curve when $p \ge 5$?
text: Ribet's level-lowering requires the residual representation $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ to be irreducible. Why does this hold for the Frey curve when $p \ge 5$? Mazur classified rational isogenies of prime degree on elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$; semistable curves with a rational $p$-isogeny are excluded for large $p$. The Frey curve's special form and $p \ge 5$ guarantee irreducibility, which Ribet's theorem requires.

q: In Ribet's proof, one removes a prime $q \mid N_E$ from the level by showing $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ is unramified at $q$. Which property of $q$ in the Frey setting makes this possible?
text: In Ribet's proof, one removes a prime $q \mid N_E$ from the level by showing $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ is unramified at $q$. Which property of $q$ in the Frey setting makes this possible? The key: $v_q(\Delta_E)$ is divisible by $p$ for every odd prime $q \mid abc$. This means $E$ has multiplicative reduction at $q$, and the Galois representation on $E[p]$ is unramified at $q$ (it looks like an unramified twist). Ribet's machinery then removes $q$ from the level. When $p \mid v_q(\Delta)$, the Galois action on $E[p]$ at $q$ acts through a quotient that doesn't see ramification.

q: The conductor of the Frey curve $E_{a,b,c}$ is $N_E = 2^s \cdot \prod_{\ell \mid abc,\ \ell\text{ odd}} \ell$ for some $s \ge 1$. After Ribet lowers the level by removing all odd primes, the resulting level is a power of $2$. Given that $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(2^k)) = 0$ for $k = 1, 2, 3$, what is $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(8))$?
text: The conductor of the Frey curve $E_{a,b,c}$ is $N_E = 2^s \cdot \prod_{\ell \mid abc,\ \ell\text{ odd}} \ell$ for some $s \ge 1$. After Ribet lowers the level by removing all odd primes, the resulting level is a power of $2$. Given that $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(2^k)) = 0$ for $k = 1, 2, 3$, what is $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(8))$? $X_0(8)$ has genus $0$, so $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(8)) = 0$. This is consistent with the fact that all levels $N = 2^k$ for $k \le 3$ yield genus-$0$ modular curves, and the contradiction in FLT holds regardless of which power of $2$ the level reduces to.

q: Mazur's universal deformation ring $R^{\mathrm{univ}}$ of an absolutely irreducible residual representation $\overline\rho : G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$ represents which functor?
text: Mazur's universal deformation ring $R^{\mathrm{univ}}$ of an absolutely irreducible residual representation $\overline\rho : G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$ represents which functor? A deformation of $\overline\rho$ to $A$ is a continuous lift to $\mathrm{GL}_2(A)$ reducing to $\overline\rho$ mod $\mathfrak{m}_A$, up to conjugation by the kernel of $\mathrm{GL}_2(A) \twoheadrightarrow \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$. Mazur proved this functor is representable when $\overline\rho$ is absolutely irreducible.

q: The mod-$\mathfrak{m}^2$ tangent space of the deformation ring $R_\Sigma$ is computed by which cohomology group?
text: The mod-$\mathfrak{m}^2$ tangent space of the deformation ring $R_\Sigma$ is computed by which cohomology group? The mod-$\mathfrak{m}^2$ (Zariski) tangent space of $R_\Sigma$ is naturally the Selmer group $H^1_\Sigma(G_\mathbb{Q}, \mathrm{Ad}\,\overline\rho)$: first-order deformations are exactly adjoint cocycles satisfying the chosen local conditions.

q: Suppose $R_\Sigma$ is a quotient of the power series ring $\mathbb{Z}_p[[x_1, x_2, x_3]]$ by a regular sequence of length $1$. What is the Krull dimension of $R_\Sigma$?
text: Suppose $R_\Sigma$ is a quotient of the power series ring $\mathbb{Z}_p[[x_1, x_2, x_3]]$ by a regular sequence of length $1$. What is the Krull dimension of $R_\Sigma$? $\dim \mathbb{Z}_p[[x_1,x_2,x_3]] = 1 + 3 = 4$ (the $1$ for $\mathbb{Z}_p$); killing a regular element drops the dimension by $1$, giving $\dim R_\Sigma = 3$.

q: The tangent space of $R_\Sigma$ computes $H^1_\Sigma(G_\mathbb{Q}, \mathrm{Ad}\,\overline{\rho})$. In the Taylor–Wiles method, one adds auxiliary primes $Q_n = \{q_1, \ldots, q_n\}$ to enlarge $\Sigma$. What is the net effect on the tangent space dimension?
text: The tangent space of $R_\Sigma$ computes $H^1_\Sigma(G_\mathbb{Q}, \mathrm{Ad}\,\overline{\rho})$. In the Taylor–Wiles method, one adds auxiliary primes $Q_n = \{q_1, \ldots, q_n\}$ to enlarge $\Sigma$. What is the net effect on the tangent space dimension? Each Taylor–Wiles prime $q \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ with $\overline{\rho}(\mathrm{Frob}_q)$ having distinct eigenvalues adds one dimension to the unrestricted local $H^1$ at $q$, enlarging the Selmer tangent space by $1$. Choosing $n$ such primes balances the obstruction count in the numerical criterion.

q: Suppose $\overline{\rho}: G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$ is reducible. Why does the universal deformation ring $R^{\mathrm{univ}}$ fail to exist in the same sense?
text: Suppose $\overline{\rho}: G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$ is reducible. Why does the universal deformation ring $R^{\mathrm{univ}}$ fail to exist in the same sense? When $\overline{\rho}$ is reducible, the group of units $1 + \mathfrak{m}_A M_2(A)$ acting by conjugation is too large relative to the stabilizer of $\overline{\rho}$; the functor of conjugacy classes of lifts may fail to be representable. Mazur's representability theorem crucially requires absolute irreducibility.

q: In the minimal deformation problem for $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ at a prime $p$ where $\mathrm{Ad}^0\overline{\rho}$ has $H^0 = 0$, Euler characteristic considerations give $\dim H^1 - \dim H^2 = -\chi$. If the local Euler factors at $p$ and $\infty$ contribute a net correction of $-1$, and the global $\chi(G_\mathbb{Q,\Sigma}, \mathrm{Ad}^0\overline{\rho}) = -3$ before local correction, what is $\dim H^1_\Sigma - \dim H^2_\Sigma$?
text: In the minimal deformation problem for $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ at a prime $p$ where $\mathrm{Ad}^0\overline{\rho}$ has $H^0 = 0$, Euler characteristic considerations give $\dim H^1 - \dim H^2 = -\chi$. If the local Euler factors at $p$ and $\infty$ contribute a net correction of $-1$, and the global $\chi(G_\mathbb{Q,\Sigma}, \mathrm{Ad}^0\overline{\rho}) = -3$ before local correction, what is $\dim H^1_\Sigma - \dim H^2_\Sigma$? The Euler characteristic formula: $\dim H^1_\Sigma - \dim H^2_\Sigma = \chi(G_{\mathbb{Q},\Sigma}, \mathrm{Ad}^0\overline{\rho}) + \text{local correction} = -3 + (-1) = -4$. A negative value means obstructions outweigh tangent dimensions, which the Taylor–Wiles patching resolves by adding auxiliary primes.

q: In Wiles' modularity-lifting setup, what are the two rings in the identity $R_\Sigma = \mathbb{T}_\Sigma$?
text: In Wiles' modularity-lifting setup, what are the two rings in the identity $R_\Sigma = \mathbb{T}_\Sigma$? $R_\Sigma$ is the Galois-deformation side: all $p$-adic lifts of $\overline\rho$ with prescribed ramification. $\mathbb{T}_\Sigma$ is the automorphic side: Hecke operators on weight-2 cusp forms, localized at the maximal ideal matching $\overline\rho$. R = T says the two moduli spaces coincide.

q: Why is the surjection $R_\Sigma \twoheadrightarrow \mathbb{T}_\Sigma$ automatic, and what technique upgrades it to an isomorphism?
text: Why is the surjection $R_\Sigma \twoheadrightarrow \mathbb{T}_\Sigma$ automatic, and what technique upgrades it to an isomorphism? Modular eigensystems visibly produce lifts of $\overline\rho$, giving a natural surjection $R_\Sigma \twoheadrightarrow \mathbb{T}_\Sigma$ by the universal property of $R_\Sigma$. Taylor–Wiles patching chooses auxiliary 'nice' primes $Q_n$, matches dimensions on both sides level by level, and takes a compatible limit to collapse the surjection into an isomorphism.

q: What is the arithmetic consequence of $R_\Sigma = \mathbb{T}_\Sigma$ for a given residual representation $\overline\rho$?
text: What is the arithmetic consequence of $R_\Sigma = \mathbb{T}_\Sigma$ for a given residual representation $\overline\rho$? R = T means the map from Galois lifts to Hecke systems is a bijection: every $p$-adic lift of $\overline\rho$ with the prescribed local conditions comes from a weight-2 cusp form. For a modular residual $\overline\rho$, this promotes modularity from the residual level to every characteristic-zero lift — the whole point of modularity lifting.

q: The Taylor–Wiles patching argument constructs inverse systems $R_{Q_n} \twoheadrightarrow \mathbb{T}_{Q_n}$ over auxiliary sets $Q_n$. In the limit, one obtains an isomorphism $R_\infty \cong \mathbb{T}_\infty$ of power series rings over $\mathbb{Z}_p$. Which property of the auxiliary primes $q \in Q_n$ is essential for the patching to work?
text: The Taylor–Wiles patching argument constructs inverse systems $R_{Q_n} \twoheadrightarrow \mathbb{T}_{Q_n}$ over auxiliary sets $Q_n$. In the limit, one obtains an isomorphism $R_\infty \cong \mathbb{T}_\infty$ of power series rings over $\mathbb{Z}_p$. Which property of the auxiliary primes $q \in Q_n$ is essential for the patching to work? The conditions $q \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ and distinct Frobenius eigenvalues make the local deformation ring at $q$ isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}_p[\![x]\!]/(x^{p-1}-1)$ locally, which is formally smooth after base change. This provides the free variables needed to patch the modules compatibly.

q: Arrange the key steps of Wiles' proof of Fermat's Last Theorem in their logical order.
text: Arrange the key steps of Wiles' proof of Fermat's Last Theorem in their logical order. W1 (semistable Frey curve) is the hypothesis that lets Wiles' modularity lifting engine apply. W3 (R=T via Taylor–Wiles) is the technical heart, which combined with residual modularity yields W4 (Frey is modular). Then W2 (Ribet) lowers the level of $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ from the Frey conductor down to $2$, and W5 closes the contradiction: no weight-$2$ cusp form of level $2$ exists. Semistability is the hypothesis that enables the modularity engine; Ribet acts after modularity is known.

q: At the numerical criterion step in the R = T proof, one shows $\ell(\ker(R_\Sigma \to \mathbb{T}_\Sigma)) = 0$ using the equality of Fitting ideals. If the Hecke algebra $\mathbb{T}_\Sigma$ has $\mathbb{Z}_p$-rank $d$ and the deformation ring satisfies $\mathrm{rank}_{\mathbb{Z}_p} R_\Sigma \le d$, what is $\mathrm{length}_{\mathbb{Z}_p}(R_\Sigma / \mathbb{T}_\Sigma)$ when the numerical criterion holds with equality?
text: At the numerical criterion step in the R = T proof, one shows $\ell(\ker(R_\Sigma \to \mathbb{T}_\Sigma)) = 0$ using the equality of Fitting ideals. If the Hecke algebra $\mathbb{T}_\Sigma$ has $\mathbb{Z}_p$-rank $d$ and the deformation ring satisfies $\mathrm{rank}_{\mathbb{Z}_p} R_\Sigma \le d$, what is $\mathrm{length}_{\mathbb{Z}_p}(R_\Sigma / \mathbb{T}_\Sigma)$ when the numerical criterion holds with equality? The numerical criterion: when $\#\Phi_\Sigma = \#\Psi_\Sigma$ (the Selmer and congruence numbers match), the surjection $R_\Sigma \twoheadrightarrow \mathbb{T}_\Sigma$ is an isomorphism, so the length of the cokernel/kernel is $0$. This is the definitive check that R = T.

title: Étale morphisms
blurb: Étale = smooth + unramified + flat; the algebraic analog of a local homeomorphism. Covers like z↦z^n are ramified; those like t↦t^2 on G_m (char≠2) are étale.

title: ℓ-adic cohomology
blurb: H^i_ét(X, ℤ/ℓ^n) and H^i_ét(X, ℚ_ℓ) as an inverse limit; works in characteristic p as long as ℓ≠p, and recovers classical Betti numbers for a smooth projective variety.

title: Grothendieck–Lefschetz fixed-point formula
blurb: For an endomorphism f whose graph meets the diagonal transversally, #Fix(f) = Σ(−1)^i Tr(f* | H^i_ét). Applied to q-power Frobenius it counts 𝔽_{q^m}-points — the engine behind rationality and the functional equation.

title: Weil conjectures and Frobenius trace
blurb: Point counts are an alternating sum of traces of Frobenius on ℓ-adic cohomology: #X(𝔽_q) = Σ (−1)^i Tr(Frob_q | H^i_ét). Eigenvalues of Frobenius lie on the circle |α|=q^{i/2} (Deligne).

title: Comparison theorems: étale vs. singular, de Rham, crystalline
blurb: Artin's theorem identifies H^i_ét(X_ℂ, ℚ_ℓ) with H^i_sing(X(ℂ), ℚ)⊗ℚ_ℓ; crystalline and p-adic Hodge theory (B_dR, B_cris) fill the ℓ=p gap, so étale, singular, de Rham, and crystalline are facets of one Weil cohomology.

q: Which of the following is **not** part of the definition of an étale morphism of schemes?
text: Which of the following is **not** part of the definition of an étale morphism of schemes? Étale = flat + unramified (equivalently: smooth of relative dimension $0$). Properness is a separate condition; étale morphisms are by definition of finite presentation but need not be proper.

q: Consider the map $f\colon \mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}\to \mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}$, $z\mapsto z^5$. Away from the origin it is étale. Over a point $t\neq 0$, how many geometric points does the fibre have?
text: Consider the map $f\colon \mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}\to \mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}$, $z\mapsto z^5$. Away from the origin it is étale. Over a point $t\neq 0$, how many geometric points does the fibre have? The fibre over $t\neq 0$ is $\{z : z^5 = t\}$, i.e. $5$ distinct fifth roots, so the étale degree is $5$. Over $t=0$ the fibre collapses to a single ramified point $z=0$.

q: The map $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_p[t,t^{-1}]\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_p[t,t^{-1}]$ sending $t\mapsto t^n$ is étale exactly when:
text: The map $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_p[t,t^{-1}]\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_p[t,t^{-1}]$ sending $t\mapsto t^n$ is étale exactly when: The derivative $nt^{n-1}$ must be invertible on $\mathbb{G}_m$; over $\mathbb{F}_p$ that holds iff $p\nmid n$. Otherwise the map is inseparable (Frobenius-like) and ramified.

q: Which is the correct order of steps in verifying a morphism $f:X\to Y$ is étale using the infinitesimal lifting criterion?
text: Which is the correct order of steps in verifying a morphism $f:X\to Y$ is étale using the infinitesimal lifting criterion? The standard verification: $\Omega^1_{X/Y}=0$ detects unramifiedness; unique lifting of $T\hookrightarrow T'$ (any nilpotent thickening) gives formal étaleness; adding finite presentation gives étaleness. Flatness follows automatically from formal étaleness + finite presentation. Unramifiedness then flatness, then finite type.

q: Let $f:\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}[[t]]\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}[[s]]$ be given by $s\mapsto t^2$. This map is **not** étale at the closed point. Which failure is the direct obstruction?
text: Let $f:\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}[[t]]\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}[[s]]$ be given by $s\mapsto t^2$. This map is **not** étale at the closed point. Which failure is the direct obstruction? $\mathbb{C}[[t]]$ is flat over $\mathbb{C}[[s]]$ (torsion-free over a DVR). The residue fields are both $\mathbb{C}$, so separability of the extension is not the issue. The obstruction is that $\Omega^1_{\mathbb{C}[[t]]/\mathbb{C}[[s]]}$ is generated by $dt$ with relation $2t\,dt=0$; since $2t$ is not a unit (it lies in the maximal ideal), $dt\neq 0$ and the module is nonzero — the map is ramified. Check for ramification: $s = t^2$ has nontrivial differential.

q: The Artin–Schreier map $\wp:\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{F}_2}\to\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{F}_2}$, $x\mapsto x^2-x$, is a $\mathbb{Z}/2$-torsor (hence étale). How many geometric points lie over $0\in\mathbb{A}^1(\overline{\mathbb{F}_2})$?
text: The Artin–Schreier map $\wp:\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{F}_2}\to\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{F}_2}$, $x\mapsto x^2-x$, is a $\mathbb{Z}/2$-torsor (hence étale). How many geometric points lie over $0\in\mathbb{A}^1(\overline{\mathbb{F}_2})$? The fibre over $0$ is $\{x\in\overline{\mathbb{F}_2}: x^2-x=0\}=\{x(x-1)=0\}=\{0,1\}$, giving $2$ geometric points. This confirms the étale degree is $p=2$ and the torsor is everywhere unramified. Artin-Schreier is a separable covering of degree $2$.

q: Why do we take the prime $\ell$ distinct from the characteristic $p$ of the ground field?
text: Why do we take the prime $\ell$ distinct from the characteristic $p$ of the ground field? In characteristic $p$, the étale cohomology $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Z}/p^n)$ is pathological (one uses crystalline cohomology instead). For $\ell \neq p$ the theory is well-behaved and gives the right Betti numbers.

q: Let $E$ be a smooth projective curve of genus $g$ over an algebraically closed field. What is $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}_\ell} H^1_{\text{ét}}(E, \mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ for $g = 3$?
text: Let $E$ be a smooth projective curve of genus $g$ over an algebraically closed field. What is $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}_\ell} H^1_{\text{ét}}(E, \mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ for $g = 3$? For a smooth projective curve of genus $g$, $\dim H^1_{\text{ét}}(E,\mathbb{Q}_\ell) = 2g$. With $g=3$ this is $6$ — matching the classical Betti number $b_1 = 2g$ for a complex curve.

q: The $\ell$-adic cohomology $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X, \mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ is constructed as:
text: The $\ell$-adic cohomology $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X, \mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ is constructed as: One takes the inverse limit over torsion sheaves $\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n$ to land in $\mathbb{Z}_\ell$-modules, then tensors with $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$ to get a finite-dimensional $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$-vector space.

q: Why does the naive colimit $\varinjlim_n H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n)$ fail to give a useful $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$-vector space, and what goes wrong?
text: Why does the naive colimit $\varinjlim_n H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n)$ fail to give a useful $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$-vector space, and what goes wrong? The direct limit inverts $\ell$ and yields a $\mathbb{Z}[1/\ell]$-module, which loses the $\ell$-adic topology and completeness structure. The inverse limit $\varprojlim_n H^i(X,\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n)$ instead gives a profinite $\mathbb{Z}_\ell$-module; tensoring with $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$ then yields the correct finite-dimensional $\ell$-adic cohomology. Colimit does not respect the torsion-free tower structure.

q: Let $X = \mathbb{P}^3_{\overline{\mathbb{F}_q}}$. Using $\dim H^{2k}_{\text{ét}}(\mathbb{P}^n,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)=1$ for $0\leq k\leq n$ and $0$ otherwise, compute $\sum_{i=0}^{6}\dim H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$.
text: Let $X = \mathbb{P}^3_{\overline{\mathbb{F}_q}}$. Using $\dim H^{2k}_{\text{ét}}(\mathbb{P}^n,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)=1$ for $0\leq k\leq n$ and $0$ otherwise, compute $\sum_{i=0}^{6}\dim H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$. For $\mathbb{P}^3$ the nonzero cohomology groups are $H^0,H^2,H^4,H^6$, each of dimension $1$. All odd-degree groups vanish. The total is $1+1+1+1=4$. Sum $\dim H^{2k}$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3$.

q: Which chain of constructions correctly assembles the Tate module $T_\ell(E)$ of an elliptic curve $E$ over $\overline{\mathbb{F}_p}$ (with $\ell\neq p$) into $H^1_{\text{ét}}(E,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$?
text: Which chain of constructions correctly assembles the Tate module $T_\ell(E)$ of an elliptic curve $E$ over $\overline{\mathbb{F}_p}$ (with $\ell\neq p$) into $H^1_{\text{ét}}(E,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$? The Tate module is the inverse limit $T_\ell(E)=\varprojlim_n E[\ell^n]$ under the multiplication-by-$\ell$ maps — this is a free $\mathbb{Z}_\ell$-module of rank $2g=2$. Inverting $\ell$ gives $V_\ell(E)$, which is the dual of $H^1_{\text{ét}}(E,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ (or canonically $H^1$ depending on sign conventions). Take inverse limit over $n$ of $E[\ell^n]$ and tensor with $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$.

q: For an endomorphism $f\colon X\to X$ of a smooth projective variety with $\Gamma_f$ transverse to the diagonal, the Grothendieck–Lefschetz trace formula says $\#\mathrm{Fix}(f)$ equals:
text: For an endomorphism $f\colon X\to X$ of a smooth projective variety with $\Gamma_f$ transverse to the diagonal, the Grothendieck–Lefschetz trace formula says $\#\mathrm{Fix}(f)$ equals: The count is an **alternating** sum of traces: odd-degree cohomology contributes with a minus sign, matching the classical topological Lefschetz formula $L(f) = \sum (-1)^i \mathrm{Tr}(f^*\mid H^i)$.

q: Apply the trace formula to $X = \mathbb{P}^2_{\mathbb{F}_q}$ with $q=3$ and $f = F_q$ (geometric Frobenius), so $F^*$ acts as $q^i$ on $H^{2i}$ and odd cohomology vanishes. Compute $\#\mathbb{P}^2(\mathbb{F}_3)$.
text: Apply the trace formula to $X = \mathbb{P}^2_{\mathbb{F}_q}$ with $q=3$ and $f = F_q$ (geometric Frobenius), so $F^*$ acts as $q^i$ on $H^{2i}$ and odd cohomology vanishes. Compute $\#\mathbb{P}^2(\mathbb{F}_3)$. $\sum_i (-1)^i \mathrm{Tr}(F^*\mid H^i) = 1 + q + q^2 = 1+3+9 = 13$, matching $\#\mathbb{P}^2(\mathbb{F}_3) = (3^3-1)/(3-1) = 13$.

q: Why is transversality between $\Gamma_{F_q}$ and the diagonal automatic in characteristic $p$?
text: Why is transversality between $\Gamma_{F_q}$ and the diagonal automatic in characteristic $p$? The derivative of $x\mapsto x^q$ is identically zero, so the tangent space of the graph at a fixed point is just $T_x(\Delta)$'s complement inside $T_x(X\times X)$ — the two subspaces are transverse, and every fixed point contributes $+1$ to the intersection.

q: Using the Lefschetz trace formula, compute $\#\mathbb{P}^3(\mathbb{F}_2)$. Frobenius acts as $q^k$ on $H^{2k}$, odd cohomology vanishes, and $q=2$.
text: Using the Lefschetz trace formula, compute $\#\mathbb{P}^3(\mathbb{F}_2)$. Frobenius acts as $q^k$ on $H^{2k}$, odd cohomology vanishes, and $q=2$. $\#\mathbb{P}^3(\mathbb{F}_2)=1+q+q^2+q^3=1+2+4+8=15$, which equals $\sum_{k=0}^{3}(-1)^{2k}q^k=\sum_{k=0}^{3}q^k$ since only even-degree cohomology contributes. Compute $1 + 2 + 4 + 8 = 15$.

q: Suppose $f:X\to X$ has $\Gamma_f$ **not** transverse to $\Delta$. Which statement about applying the Lefschetz trace formula is correct?
text: Suppose $f:X\to X$ has $\Gamma_f$ **not** transverse to $\Delta$. Which statement about applying the Lefschetz trace formula is correct? The trace formula computes the intersection number $[\Gamma_f]\cdot[\Delta]$ in $H^*(X\times X)$. Without transversality this intersection number can exceed the naive count of fixed points (each non-transverse fixed point contributes a local intersection multiplicity $>1$). Transversality (guaranteed by $dF_q=0$ for Frobenius) ensures each fixed point contributes exactly $1$. Non-transverse intersection needs multiplicity corrections.

q: Which step in proving the Lefschetz trace formula for the Frobenius endomorphism uses a **cohomological** computation rather than a geometric one?
text: Which step in proving the Lefschetz trace formula for the Frobenius endomorphism uses a **cohomological** computation rather than a geometric one? The geometric step is identifying fixed points with $\mathbb{F}_q$-rational points and checking transversality. The purely cohomological step is the application of Poincaré duality on $X\times X$: it converts the intersection number $[\Gamma_f]\cdot[\Delta]$ into a trace formula via the Künneth decomposition of the diagonal class. Frobenius eigenvalue computation uses the comparison theorem.

q: Let $E/\mathbb{F}_q$ be an elliptic curve and write the Frobenius eigenvalues on $H^1_{\text{ét}}$ as $\alpha, \beta$ with $\alpha\beta = q$. If $q = 5$ and $\alpha + \beta = 3$, compute $\#E(\mathbb{F}_5)$.
text: Let $E/\mathbb{F}_q$ be an elliptic curve and write the Frobenius eigenvalues on $H^1_{\text{ét}}$ as $\alpha, \beta$ with $\alpha\beta = q$. If $q = 5$ and $\alpha + \beta = 3$, compute $\#E(\mathbb{F}_5)$. $\#E(\mathbb{F}_q) = q + 1 - (\alpha+\beta) = 5 + 1 - 3 = 3$. The trace $\alpha+\beta$ is the standard $a_q$.

q: For a smooth projective variety $X$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$, Deligne's theorem says each eigenvalue $\alpha$ of Frobenius on $H^i_{\text{ét}}$ has $|\alpha| = q^{i/2}$. Give one allowed eigenvalue (as a complex number $a+bi$) on $H^1$ for $q = 4$, assuming the eigenvalue lies on the positive imaginary axis.
text: For a smooth projective variety $X$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$, Deligne's theorem says each eigenvalue $\alpha$ of Frobenius on $H^i_{\text{ét}}$ has $|\alpha| = q^{i/2}$. Give one allowed eigenvalue (as a complex number $a+bi$) on $H^1$ for $q = 4$, assuming the eigenvalue lies on the positive imaginary axis. On $H^1$ with $q=4$ we need $|\alpha| = \sqrt{4} = 2$. On the positive imaginary axis that is $\alpha = 2i$.

q: Which statement is part of the **Weil conjectures** for a smooth projective $X/\mathbb{F}_q$ of dimension $d$?
text: Which statement is part of the **Weil conjectures** for a smooth projective $X/\mathbb{F}_q$ of dimension $d$? Rationality, functional equation, and the analog of the Riemann hypothesis are the three Weil conjectures (with Betti numbers matching topology a fourth). Étale cohomology with its Frobenius action is what makes them provable.

q: Let $E/\mathbb{F}_3$ be an elliptic curve with Frobenius eigenvalues $\alpha,\beta$ on $H^1_{\text{ét}}$ satisfying $\alpha\beta=3$ and $\alpha+\beta=-2$. Compute $\#E(\mathbb{F}_9)$ using $\#E(\mathbb{F}_{q^2})=q^2+1-(\alpha^2+\beta^2)$.
text: Let $E/\mathbb{F}_3$ be an elliptic curve with Frobenius eigenvalues $\alpha,\beta$ on $H^1_{\text{ét}}$ satisfying $\alpha\beta=3$ and $\alpha+\beta=-2$. Compute $\#E(\mathbb{F}_9)$ using $\#E(\mathbb{F}_{q^2})=q^2+1-(\alpha^2+\beta^2)$. $\alpha^2+\beta^2=(\alpha+\beta)^2-2\alpha\beta=(-2)^2-2\cdot 3=4-6=-2$. Hence $\#E(\mathbb{F}_9)=9+1-(-2)=12$. Use $\alpha + \beta = a_3$ and $\alpha\beta = 3$.

q: For the Artin–Tate conjecture, the zeta function $Z(X,t)$ of a smooth projective curve $C/\mathbb{F}_q$ of genus $g$ factors as $Z(C,t) = \frac{P_1(t)}{(1-t)(1-qt)}$. What is $\deg P_1(t)$?
text: For the Artin–Tate conjecture, the zeta function $Z(X,t)$ of a smooth projective curve $C/\mathbb{F}_q$ of genus $g$ factors as $Z(C,t) = \frac{P_1(t)}{(1-t)(1-qt)}$. What is $\deg P_1(t)$? $P_1(t)=\det(1-tF^*\mid H^1_{\text{ét}})$ and $\dim H^1_{\text{ét}}(C,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)=2g$, so $P_1$ has degree $2g$. Its roots are the reciprocals of Frobenius eigenvalues, all of absolute value $q^{1/2}$ by the Riemann hypothesis. Factor $Z(C, t) = P_1(t) / ((1-t)(1-qt))$ with $P_1$ of degree $2g$.

q: Deligne's proof of the Weil Riemann hypothesis ($|\alpha_{i,j}|=q^{i/2}$) proceeds by which high-level strategy?
text: Deligne's proof of the Weil Riemann hypothesis ($|\alpha_{i,j}|=q^{i/2}$) proceeds by which high-level strategy? Deligne (Weil II, 1980) chooses a Lefschetz pencil $X\to\mathbb{P}^1$, applies the Picard–Lefschetz formula to control monodromy, invokes the hard Lefschetz theorem to upgrade a weight bound on the generic fibre, and runs an induction on dimension. The key input is that the local monodromy operators are unipotent, which forces the eigenvalues of Frobenius to have the correct absolute values. Deligne used geometric monodromy and the Leray-type argument for weights.

q: Artin's comparison theorem states that for a smooth projective variety $X$ over $\mathbb{C}$ and any prime $\ell$:
text: Artin's comparison theorem states that for a smooth projective variety $X$ over $\mathbb{C}$ and any prime $\ell$: Artin's theorem: étale cohomology of a complex variety agrees with topological singular cohomology after tensoring to $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$. In particular $\dim H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ is the classical Betti number $b_i$, independent of $\ell$.

q: Using the comparison theorem, compute the $\ell$-adic Betti number $b_2 = \dim_{\mathbb{Q}_\ell} H^2_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ for $X = \mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$ over $\mathbb{C}$.
text: Using the comparison theorem, compute the $\ell$-adic Betti number $b_2 = \dim_{\mathbb{Q}_\ell} H^2_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ for $X = \mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$ over $\mathbb{C}$. By Künneth and comparison, $H^2(\mathbb{P}^1\times\mathbb{P}^1) = H^2(\mathbb{P}^1)\otimes H^0(\mathbb{P}^1) \oplus H^0\otimes H^2$, giving $b_2 = 1+1 = 2$. The two classes are the pullbacks of the hyperplane class from each factor.

q: Over $\overline{\mathbb{F}_p}$, why can we NOT use $\ell = p$ in $\ell$-adic cohomology, and what replaces it?
text: Over $\overline{\mathbb{F}_p}$, why can we NOT use $\ell = p$ in $\ell$-adic cohomology, and what replaces it? Étale cohomology with $\mathbb{Z}/p^n$ coefficients sees Frobenius-kernel pathology in characteristic $p$. Crystalline cohomology (Berthelot–Grothendieck) replaces it, producing a $W(k)$-module with semi-linear Frobenius whose characteristic polynomial matches the $\ell$-adic ones for $\ell\neq p$.

q: Artin's comparison isomorphism $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\cong H^i_{\text{sing}}(X(\mathbb{C}),\mathbb{Z}/n)$ requires $X$ to be defined over $\mathbb{C}$. Which step in the proof is the key algebraic–topological input?
text: Artin's comparison isomorphism $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\cong H^i_{\text{sing}}(X(\mathbb{C}),\mathbb{Z}/n)$ requires $X$ to be defined over $\mathbb{C}$. Which step in the proof is the key algebraic–topological input? The core of Artin's theorem is that analytification gives an equivalence between finite étale covers of $X$ (in the algebraic category) and finite topological covers of $X(\mathbb{C})$. This makes the étale site over $\mathbb{C}$ equivalent to the site of locally contractible opens on the analytic space, from which the comparison of cohomology with $\mathbb{Z}/n$ coefficients follows. Proper smooth complex schemes are needed for the comparison.

q: Let $X$ be a smooth projective K3 surface over $\mathbb{C}$. The singular Betti numbers are $b_0=b_4=1$, $b_1=b_3=0$, $b_2=22$. Using the comparison theorem, compute $\chi_{\ell}(X)=\sum_i(-1)^i\dim H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$.
text: Let $X$ be a smooth projective K3 surface over $\mathbb{C}$. The singular Betti numbers are $b_0=b_4=1$, $b_1=b_3=0$, $b_2=22$. Using the comparison theorem, compute $\chi_{\ell}(X)=\sum_i(-1)^i\dim H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$. $\chi_{\ell}(X)=b_0-b_1+b_2-b_3+b_4=1-0+22-0+1=24$. By comparison with singular cohomology, $\chi_{\ell}$ equals the topological Euler characteristic $\chi(X(\mathbb{C}))=24$ for any $\ell$. Sum Betti numbers: $1 + 0 + 22 + 0 + 1 = 24$.

q: Which statement correctly describes a **failure** of the comparison theorem outside its hypotheses?
text: Which statement correctly describes a **failure** of the comparison theorem outside its hypotheses? For smooth **projective** varieties over $\mathbb{C}$ the comparison holds in all degrees. For non-proper (quasi-projective) varieties it can fail: étale cohomology with constant coefficients $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$ computes the cohomology of a singular complex, which need not agree with singular cohomology in high degrees (one needs compactly supported or mixed Hodge-theoretic corrections). The theorem in its clean form requires properness. Characteristic-$p$ issues break the comparison outside characteristic zero.
